What words can I use to sum up the emotional experience that Undertale is...?  Well Undertale is an indie RPG inspired by classics such as Earthbound and Chrono Trigger but is distinct enough to stand on it's own as a classic in it's own right. On the surface, Undertale looks like a traditional JRPG, but digging deeper you find a very melancholic but positive outlook on real life itself, the friends you meet on your journey, and being able to stay determined even when things start to look grim...at least that's what I got out of it, to me that's Undertale's greatest strength. Besides a really meaningful message, the game has a great story, memorable characters, hilarious sense of humor, and an amazing soundtrack.  The story is about a boy, nameless protagonist *insert name here*, who falls from the human world into a land that's filled with monsters, and as the nameless protagonist *insert name here*, you must journey through this land and eventually find your way back to the human world. Of course you encounter enemies, bosses, memorable characters, skeletons that make hilariously bad puns, and dogs along the way that it's hard not to get engrossed in this world and actually start caring about the people you meet along the way.  While I highly recommend sticking with the Pacifist run and experiencing the amazing Pacifist True Ending, there's also a Genocide run you can do which basically means instead of negotiating and sparing everyone you come in contact with, you play it in a more traditional way and massacre everyone you meet. I have yet to do this and I'm not sure if I could do this. I've grown to love the characters so much I couldn't even think about doing the Genocide run... this game is really good at making you care about the in-game characters.  The battle system is very unique, encouraging you to not kill enemies but instead trying to negotiate your way to sparing your enemy. The enemies themselves have a very fun and unique way of attacking, spraying bullet-hell type attacks in a way that almost feels like Wario Ware's micro games. Through the battle system you learn about the enemies, and come to understand them.   The characters themselves are very memorable, my favorites being the bone headed brothers, Sans and Papyrus, who are literally skeletons. Characters you meet and befriend are mostly bosses who want to kill you but with your determination, eventually they give up and instead help and encourage you along your journey to get back to the surface and return to the human world.   The soundtrack as a whole is amazing, sporting many catchy songs, atmospheric melodies, and many mood setting pieces. There's a really good song that to me sums up the game's atmosphere perfectly. 'Memory' does a great job at setting the melancholic but positive tone of the game.  Some moments in this game are really psychological, messing with player interaction as well as breaking the fourth wall to a degree that not even Psycho Mantis from Metal Gear Solid could manage. This game does a great job of just playing with your mind even if you don't even realize it's playing with your mind.  It's hard to explain in detail what makes this game so good. In short I'd say experience it for yourself, try the demo if my review hasn't piqued your interest. But seriously do yourself a favor and play this game, it's only $10 and what you get in this game is more than what most Triple 'A' developers offer for $60 games these days.   I can safetly say this game is highly recommended and give it a definite 10/10, I'd definitely play this game again.

Where has this game been all my life? I have fallen in love 100% with this title; even with graphically superior titles in my library such as  Fallout: New Vegas , this just ends up being the more... fulfilling game? It's strange; Undertale seems to be equal parts RPG, decision making simulator, and SHMUP (which gets very bullet hell the further in you go), but it's also pretty simple and satisfying... and your choices   matter  . Pay attention to what the trailer for the game said: NO ONE HAS TO DIE. There's a way to ensure you don't kill a single thing if you play your cards right, but it's not really all  that  simple; even I needed help at certain parts, but I'm just about at the end of the game. Already, though, I feel like if I beat it, it'll still be  too soon , the game was just  that good .

This game is so rough around the edges I nearly gave it a thumbs down. A host of technical problems likely due to the PC port (lots of z-fighting textures in the early levels plus frequent crashes) almost caused me to quit an hour in out of frustration.   BUT ... I'm glad I didn't give up. If you can make it past embarassing technical problems (the streaming backend for the live action episodes is seriously attrocious), you'll discover one of the best game stories of all time. This thing is a (quantum) leap forward in terms of plot structure, characterization, and performance for the entire medium. I've always loved Remedy's work, but was a little underwhelmed by how 'video game-y' Alan Wake was in its writing and execution. QB addresses that campiness and then some. The time-travel plot sturcture is Primer-level in its internal consistency, and the characters and action have taken the best of the JJ Abrams school of storytelling to craft a well drawn, exciting, emotionally engaging story experience.   The live action episodes have wonderful performances and good writing wrapped in 'above average student film' production value. The vaunted choice driven plot structure isn't really all that revelatory ... but I do have to admit that I love the way the mixed media presentation in this game allows audiences to experience the characters from multiple angles. The end result of the hybrid approach winds up being satisfying because of the committment to story, despite the technical failings.   Be warned: the final boss encounter is infuriating. It nearly shut down all my good will for this game, and I had a serious debate about giving it a thumbs down as a result. After all the technical flaws present in the PC version, the design of the final boss encounter (one shot kills, poor visual feedback, mandatory cutscene before final checkpoint) was the (enourmous chronon) straw that nearly broke this camel's back.   BUT ... the story, overall visual design, respect for the craft of acting ... it all gels into something that transcends its parts.   Remedy needs to get their ♥♥♥♥ together on a technical level for future releases, and if they ever design a boss encounter this infuriating again ... well ... I'll probably write more angry things on the internet. But despite all those flaws: Quantum Break is a classic. Remedy have pushed forward interactive storytelling in a way that few others, outside of Naughty Dog, are even attempting.   Remedy grabbed the brass ring on this one - barely - but they did it. Recommended.

app_name: Warhammer 40,000: Dawn of War II - Retribution
review_text: I think I played it enough to write a review. First of the all the game is amazing, there are 6 factions with which you can play with in the Single player and these factions are: The space marines, Chaos space marines, Imperial Guards, Orcs, Elder and the Tyranids. The campaign doesn't change between the races, but every time although you go through the same places and fight vs the same enemies it is still really fun and feels a little bit different!. Unlike in the previous games such as, Dawn of War: Soulstorm or Dawn of War 2 Chaos Rising, here you have 4 commanders which you control and you also construct an army but not a base, you get your units by capturing a beacon or barracks (depends on your race) and from there you can deply troops into the battlefield.  The multiplayer is pretty active, a few cutom games here and there and it seems like people can't stop playing in the last stand! (The last stand is a game mod in which you group up with 2 more players and try to survive waves of enemies up to 20 waves) The game also has many dlc's many of them are skins for the multiplayer or some wargear for the single player, except for two DLC's which are the Tau commander and the Necron Overlord, these DLC's might be expensive, but if you really like the last stand which I mentioned above, you might want to consider buying it. The DLC will give you a new commander to play with in the last stand and he is damn powerful, but as powerful as he is, it doesn't mean he is more powerful than the other commanders, he has his pros and cons.  Overall the game is amazing, the story is nice, really fun single player campaign and the multiplayer is active.

What can I say about ORION: Prelude? Let's start with what it's  not ...  It's not a perfect game. It's not 100% bug-free. It's not a contender for Game Of The Year.  But more importantly, let's go on to what it  is ...  It's extremely fun. It's very well supported. It's extremely fun. It's got a great community. It's extremely fun. It's amazing value for money. Did I mention that it's extremely fun?  For the price, you wouldn't believe that this is a fully-fledged multiplayer FPS with solid progression, numerous weapons and power-ups, a decent array of vehicles and goddam  dinosaurs  to gun down! But that's exactly what it is. You probably won't put hundreds of hours into it but it certainly doesn't feel like a £0.79p / $0.99c game. In fact, I reckon this is the single best value for money of any game in my entire collection. I've certainly got my 79p worth and then some - and I still keep coming back for more!  If you like multiplayer FPS and you're not literally penniless, why are you still reading this review? At this price, just buy it already!

Hello, if you're viewing this then hopefully you're contemplating buying Terraria. If you are then please let me inform you. Terraria is nothing like Minecraft, regardless of what you have heard. It's a 2-D sidescroller however you will at least get used to if not prefer that camera angle. You can also get alot of things in Terraria that are fun like boss trophies or mob banners. You can also get many types of armor and weaponry, from your normal iron armor to molten lava and even an armor made of turtle shells. The weapons can be amazing for example the Crystal Storm or the gun shoved down a sharks mouth, the Mini and Mega sharks. I have played Terraria for a long time and even after getting all of the best things in the game and having hundreds of hours under my belt I can safely say that it is still entertaining. If you happen to run into any problems I, or many others, can be of service. Just Private Message me and I will help. All in all I think you should buy Terraria as the game is worth more than $100, 10x its original amount.

Gunpoint is a clever and unique puzzle game that employs stealth mechanics within a familiar, though modernized private eye narrative. My God is this game amazing. Let's start with the music. David Housden did a fantastic job with the soundtrack. I am a sucker for a good soundtrack. I love how characters that are nothing but a square or rectangle are more than just that. They have unique mindsets, personalities, and ablilities. The gameplay is enjoyable, but pretty easy. Levels towards the end can be hard enough to make you google how to get past the level. Overall, the gameplay has easy mechanics, good for all types of gamers, and is very enjoyable. The narrator adds so much to the story. He is funny and witty, just like the narrator of 'The Stanley Parable' he has quotes such as 'Thomas knew he'd never meet Nathan Fillion, or a transformer. He'd never visit Gotham city or eat at Mooby's. It didn't matter. If he and his friends jumped into the creation matrix, they could give everyone else in the simulation a chance to.' or 'Thomas was connected to the internet for 12 seconds. And he had seen everything. He had seen the cats who couldn't spell, he had heard of the arrow through the knee. He felt that there was a thing called cake, but that it was a lie' The game is all about friendship, sacrafice, and betrayal. It explores the natures that make up who we are. And for this reason, and many more I give this game 9.7/10

Feel free to disregard this review, I think I played this for all of 20 minutes, but let me tell you - that was enough.   I don't care how much Tom Savini and Lloyd Kauffman you wave in people's faces, a bad game is a bad game. The humour feels utterly forced and the 'wackiness' and 'zaniness' is just too much to bear.  The game itself looks and sounds pretty nice I suppose, but the 'gameplay' as far as I got was press A, then X a bunch of times, then A if something flashes! Oh, you can hold down B for little while now. If you like. No. No, I don't like. Add in that you trail along a character who only speaks Spanish, so you have to read the subtitles while trying to play. Flip this game. Flip it right up its gaping bottom.  You may say that it's my fault for not playing on - you'd be right, but consider that anyone designing a game should make someone want to keep playing. This made me want to gnaw my own wrists.

Great game. I got this on release and have clocked a lot of hours on it, but I waited to recommend it. I waited because it had a few balance and playability issues otu of the box, which have been adressed with patches and the Dev's listening to the community.  Pros - Good division between the classes and races, everyone is good at, and weak at, something. This makes the choice meaningful. - A style which is both functional and cool looking. - Excellent in-game encyclopedia, much refined over the patches. Likely inspired by Civ, but not a bad inspiration at that. - Excellent tooltips. Every unit has all its abilities and current stats available at a click, no searching through menus or having to remember who does what. This lets you focus on stratagy, and not memory tests. - Unit models. Personally I love how there is a version of each class unit for every race, they didn't skimp out on the design budget by making generic models. Also, Goblin Angels!  Cons -The campaigns are not the greatest feats of storytelling. But they do give a good vehicle for the stratagy, and a reason for the objectives. - The first expansion campaign (I haven't played Eternal Lords yet) was frustrating because it was basically a long tutorial for the new features, when I would rather have had all those new features through the campaign. Except possibly the seals, which make sense at the point they are at (I'm not giving any spoilers here!) - There doesn't seem to be much rhyme or reason to how nuetral factions act towards you. This may be addressed with the proposed changes to races in Eternal Lords. - The randomly generated maps do produce some wierd anomalies from time to time, but I believe these are being refined.  Over all, this game deserves its 80% approval rating, and I heartily recommend it to both fans of the series and other turn-bases stratagy or empire building fans.

Prepare to die. A lot. Normal mode pits you against enemies levels higher than you are for the completion of your first mission. Early archers and mages are OP with far too many status effects.  The vendor system is awful, too. Whoever heard of vendors that have 0 gold perpetually?   Getting gear you don't need is easy and abundant. Getting gear you can actually use? Forget about it.  Grinding to help level up? Nope. Enemies will grind on you instead.  I'm going to keep playing in the hope that it will get better. After all, the game has won a lot of awards for a reason and maybe I'm just not getting good drops which will help smooth out the rough spots. And yes, for those who are wondering, I am fighting the 'easier' mobs first, but the mobs I need to fight still outlevel and outnumber me.  ****EDIT****  I restarted the game with new characters, one of which was a healer. I experienced the same issues but I was able to work past them and get into the second area (Luculla Forest). I stand by my original review: the enemies outnumber you and they begin the combat by locking down your party with knockdown, freeze, cripple, and so on, even with points into the saving abilities (like Bodybuilding and Willpower).   It's no fun to be a punching bag.  ****EDIT****  Eventually the difficulty does even out a little. For me, that happened around level 13-14 (out of 20 max) when I was able to use the more powerful weapons and when I finally found the summoning skillbooks I needed (having summons to absorb damage and status effects helps a lot). Getting to this point has been incredibly frustrating.  ****FINAL EDIT****  I have beaten the game. There was just enough playablity for me to slog my way through, but I can't say I enjoyed it and I doubt I'll play it again.

It's just... Bland. Sure it's fun at first, but it only took eight missions for me to be absolutely sure I did not like the game.  Even if I COMPLETELY disregard the fact that pretty much 85% of the content is pay-to-use (big surprise, considering this is a 'free to play' game), the remaining content you get is just not enough. And honestly, don't even try throwing the whole 'game is free, the developers need to make money somehow' argument, because there are SO many other games that can keep a good free-to-play backbone while offering premium content on the side.  Maybe there was an error somewhere in the game, but I entered a level 5-6 area hoping to do a mission whilst me myself being level 10, and the enemies absolutely steamrolled me, coming in groups of eight continuously. What REALLY killed me though, after reviving five times, is the fact that after excrutiatingly overcoming impossible odds and coming within millimeters of completing this ridiculous mission, I ran out... Need more revives? They cost real money to buy... And you'll NEED them in some of these poorly designed levels...  And boy, the money to content ratio in this game is laughably poor. I looked at the $100 pack that steam offers, and boy have I not laughed that hard in a long time...

I have played warframe for much longer now and my feelings about it have vastly improved. Since last reviewing this I have continued to improve and have found myself enjoying the game so much more. Warframe feels like a home, the game I always come back to after playing other games and it always has something to offer me.  At this current time I own at least one of every warfame, either primed or non-primed versions, and have enjoyed playing them all. Note that most of my game experience has been had while playing as either Excalibur, Volt Prime or Frost Prime.  I feel now that warframe is not only greatly customisable, and still Improving, that I feel I can now specialise myself in different ways. All my loadout slots have a speciality or an archetype. You may say I'm limiting myself but this is how I choose to play. I said in my original review that there wasn't quite enough to specialise in, but I now feel otherwise.  Something that feels perfect to me now is Warframe is getting more cinematic and lore friendly quests, mainly beginning with Natah, and I feel as though there is a much better narrative to the game, that while I did not feel it was required, I do love the addition of.  The pay model is my favourite thing as it is basically 'Pay if you are not patient' as it still seems to me that you can get everything you want, save maybe a few cosmetic items, by just playing the game and knowing where to be and when.  To my favourite point about this game, Digital Extremes is still keeping me, personally, hyped. Usually what they add is something that I have been thinking 'Warframe would be so cool if it had X' for example the upcoming weapon sheathing customisation. While a small thing to mention it does make me excited for almost every update, the only one letting me down being Sands of Inaros, but following such a big update as The Second Dream this couldn't really be avoided.  Pros: A great game to play alone A great game to play with friends Fantastic customisation to warframes and their colours Fun Weapons Fun Warframes Easy enough for new gamers Challenging enough for veterans  Cons: Conclave can be toxic (I do not blame DE for this one) Dark Sector as they are now, and as it seems they are to become New Warframes appear to be stagnating slightly  Overall I reccomend Warframe to try, while it may take a while to really feel it you will feel like this game is everything you want. It's a love it or hate it game I say. Well done DE, well done.

You come home, nobody is there to greet you. Just you, in a stormy night in an empty house.  I always found the aspect of exploring an empty house of a completely unknown family exciting. Who is living there? How are the relationships in that family? How do they live their life?  The house, its environment and the family living in it is absolutely believable. If you grew up during the 90s you'll find a lot of nostalgia of that decade. Whether its the oversized phones, VHS tapes or SNES cartridges you'll remember those times.  You can interact with most things you find and either put them back to where they were or wherever you feel like it.  Read notes, documents, journals to discover the life of the family and progress the story.  There are a few minor things that somewhat break the immersion, like the doors that swing both ways, things clipping, some of the overlayed text disappearing and a few typos.   All these things can easily be overlooked because the atmosphere of the house and the story its telling is going to keep your attention.
Really cool concept that is fun to experience the progress of gaming throughout the years.

I really like waz hack. it has a ton of replay value and is a nice easy pace hack and slash rpg rouge like. its like a singleplayer side scrolling dungons and dragons game. i have beat the game twice already but have not done so on steam there are a lot of improvements from the desura version.  EDIT: 7-11-16  I finally beat Wazhack for the first time on the steam version! Woohoo!  I feel that give me a good enough perspective to re-write my review of Wazhack. The first time i beat the game was with a knight decked out with dragon armor and Excalibur.  The second time was with a wizard who could teleport and shoot lightning bolt and had crazy mana regeneration. this third time I had beat the game was with a rogue who wielded two legendary curved swords and a suit of crystal armor.  Wazhack has filled a role in my game collection as an exceptional side-scrolling adventure game. it can be unforgiving at times but I have always been rewarded by thinking critically and trying to utilize all avenues. the main game play is great at the beginning there are usually a dozen or so options to dispatch enemies and some even offer some interesting interactions. the middle of the game sometimes is a bit of a slug fest you really need to make sure your armor class is high and your weapons hit bonus is decent. the end game is frustratingly simple you need to ready yourself to face monsters that are directly countered by items found early in the game.  some of the late game monsters can feel really unfair due to luck and their sheer power.  I would like to seem some puzzle elements like codes or ciphers.  a quest giver would be cool. With quests like bring back 3 rubies or the head of a named monster for a ring in return. More unique items something like the Midas would be really cool. like the Horadric cube or a portal gem that goes somewhere else.  In conclusion Wazhack has always delivered and more. I have played a lot of Wazhack and died a lot too but the randomly generated levels always leave me coming back for more. I would recommend this game to anyone who likes single player dungeon adventure rouge-like games.

Shadowrun: Dragonfall is effectively a giant DLC, the size of another game, for Shadowrun: Returns. Because of this, most praise and criticism in  my review  of Shadowrun: Returns applies to this game as well. This review will primarily focus on the changes.  Some aspects of the design were improved.  Shadowrun: Dragonfall is less linear than Shadowrun: Returns, and most important decisions you make will affect the ending.  Several new weapons and spells have been added.  You can now save at will, outside combat and conversations.  Other aspects of the design are disappointing.  I was hoping for more new weapons, spells, and armor, and perhaps other positive changes in the combat system (more statuses, called shots, a matrix that feels different from meatspace, etc.).  Most music is reused from Shadowrun: Returns. Yes, it was good. Yes, some new tracks were added. Yes, they are of similar quality. It still feels disappointing.  The Bloodlines quest has an infuriating stealth segment. Guards literally seem to have eyes in the back of their head, such that you will be spotted even if they are facing away from you, unless there is a wall in-between. The guards patrol very quickly, making it hard to get past them in time, even when you know what needs to be done. Worst of all, your train of companions cannot be directly controlled and will frequently get spotted. This should have either been turn-based, with proper vision cones, or removed.  The developers have clearly favored a small number of possible builds for the main character and party. Players without clairvoyance will be punished for deviating from the favored builds mid to late game. Having to start over, after investing so much time, is likely to prevent many people from enjoying the game.  Since you now have a large number of companions, you will want to play a build that is not redundant. The only main character builds that are not redundant are adepts (still somewhat redundant due to Glory) and mages (recommended).  You better buy and keep any tasers you find, and any other action point damaging weapons or spells. Be sure that most if not all party members (not just your main character) have a way to cause AP damage. It will be almost impossible to win the final battle otherwise.

This review is aimed at rhythm-game fans like myself. Please have that in mind while i describe the game as 'way too easy' and such.  First off i have to say, that i just skipped through every cutscene in the game. They just feel super cheasy but take a lot of time to sit through. I think the development time spent on them might have better been used for improving the core gameplay. Speaking of which, what does it offer?  Well, sadly not that much. The game gives you 7 keys to work with, 4 in the lower row and 3 in the upper row (as black notes), which is not enough. 4 keys in the lower row mean, that you can´t simulate chords propperly, which i felt was missing from this game. Having 5 keys instead to play chords wouldn´t make a huge difference tho, as there is no second hand to accompany it. With only one hand, the game is just way too easy. It took me 3 levels to get used to the pattern and after that, i felt that there really wasn´t anything to improve on. Thats even more troubling as the game lacks the courage to put notes onto the playfield. Instead of using fewer notes for the easier difficulties and then gradually increasing the notes in the higher ones, all difficulties have the same set of notes. There were so many times that i felt they could have easily tripled the number of notes to make me keep wanting to play the game and improve, to make meactually feel like really playing the song...  that said, there are some good notes here. The music is really cool and the overall design feels very smooth. Tho what i took from this game was starting on the lowest difficulty to get used to the gameplay, finding it too easy, going into a higher difficulty and then figure out that i´ve basically seen it all. Now i had fun in this game for a few minutes, so if you´re really bored just go for it, but there are better, free games out there with way more content that i to this day didn´t get tired of.  So yeah, even tho i liked the game, i can´t recommend it...

Because there's a lot of misinformation and reactionary reviews to the game's rather poor launch state, I've decided to write a review, covering the game's pros and cons in the most objective fashion possible. No aggressive bashing, no unwarranted praise.  I want to begin with a very simple fact - the game is free. That means that you don't have to spend a dollar (or euro) to try it out and see if it fits you. This fact, of course, does not shield the game from criticism, but it's a thing to remember - as long as you're having fun with it, you got your 'money's' worth. To add to that, the monetization system is super-fair. Unlike other games, where you'll keep opening repeats of cards you already own over and over again, in MDO (Magic Duels: Origins) each pack will contain only cards you don't own a full set of yet. According to some (yes, I'm too lazy to make the calculations, but if you're interested, look it up), this puts the pricetag on the entire set of cards of $70. That is laughably cheap, both compared to it's competitors and 'real' Magic. I'll come back to this system further into the review, but the overall impression is extremely positive.  For the list of gameplay pros, we have to start with the obvious - it's Magic the Gathering. One of the best TCG games on the market, going strong for over 21 years. Whether it's THE best, that's up to your opinion, but if you love the game and want to have the option of playing it any time - it's here. If you haven't tried it out yet and are looking for a great introduction - the Duels of the Planeswalkers series (to which this game kinda belongs to) was always an amazing learning tool. And now, it's free.  As an additional bonus, the game features a surprisingly great soundtrack. While it doesn't seem energetic at first, you might quickly find that it puts you in the right kind of magical atmosphere.  Now, for the 'controversial one', or the one that's neither an obvious pro or a con, the game only features cards from the newest MTG set - Magic Origins (and also some common cards from other sets, as well as cards from some expansions in story missions). It actually seems to lack some cards from that set as well. Additionally, as you craft your deck, you may only put one of each Mythic-rarity card into it, two of each Rare and three of each Uncommon, instead of the standard maximum of four for each card. While that may seem like a con - and it definitely is for people who were looking for a 1-to-1 recreation of Magic - I feel like it's just a change. If you ever felt like paper Magic was about slamming the most expensive rares and mythics into your library, well, here you only need a couple of copies and these rare cards have an added downside of being less reliable than common ones. Since everyone sticks to the same rules - your games will be balanced and Magic Origins, as a set, is made a bit like a 'new' Magic - the card's balance has been shifted to promote more thoughtful, more strategic play than before.  Now it's time for cons. I'm going to intentionally skip over connection issues and some bugs - if you want to get the full, accurate picture, do your research, as any complaint about them might have been fixed by the time you're reading this or any other review. Also, the game might be developed further, which means that even the complaints I'll post below might get fixed. Again, do your research or just try the game yourself. Developers promised updates with new cards from new sets - it might also extend to Quality of Life improvements and bonus features. First of all, the UI is not very user-friendly. There are numerous issues with it - it doesn't do a great job of telling you in which part of the turn you are. The button placement is questionable. Once you grab a card, there is no way to back off, you have to play it. Questions about decisions are displayed as huge pop-ups that cover your entire vision, which is annoying if the decision regards stuff that's on the battlefield. Manually tapping lands is broken. The list goes on and you'll probably come up with your own complaints along the way, like having the 'skip attack' button appear in the same place, where 'continue' button was a second ago - I never had a problem with it, but I saw people that had. Second of all, there isn't much content in the game yet. The AI fights and the ladder system give you the option of playing 'pure' Magic, but the game lacks any fun modes, like Commander or even competitive ones like Draft or Sealed, featuring only barebones two-headed giant. If these two available modes won't suit you, or will just bore you after a while, you might not find anything else in the game to entertain you. However, I'm going to immediately discredit the complaint about the game lacking a chat feature - many competing games also lack it, because even I can sometimes feel the salt coming from my opponent. I don't want to read it as well. Third of all, and my final complaint, is that the game is overly simplistic with it's rules. While I like the fact that it's made with beginners in mind, it actually doesn't even feature some elements of the turn, like upkeep - a moment between untapping and drawing, where you should be able to cast spells. I don't think that the game's direction will allow for it's inclusion, or any increase in difficulty, unless they'll features something like a 'Pro' queue. That said, if you do feel comfortable with the rules, remember to go to the game settings and uncheck all the features that will basically make the game do some decisions for you, like deciding blocking order.  In the end, I'd like to, again, come back to the monetization system. After you finish the campaigns and loot all of the possible gold coins from Skill Quests, you'll gain 5, 10 or 15 gold for a win against AI (depending on it's difficulty), or 20 against a real opponent. A pack containing six cards costs 150 gold, which you'll get after eight won duels online, which, again, is really fair compared to it's competition. There's also a standard quest system, which nets you bonus coins from time to time, although it's pretty broken at the time of writing this review, for instance quests that require you to play with a set archetype only work with decks created using Deck Wizard. Again, the developers claimed to be working on it right now, so check for yourself.  Should you spend real money on it? In the state it is now - no. But if you're reading this two months into the future and at least half of my complaints are fixed - sure, I'll drop a buck. Try it out and see if it fits you.

This game has some truly good potential. So why am I not recommending this game?  Here is the answer: - Been trying to do several runs in story mode now but the game automatically shuts down after the transfer between new areas (each shutdown differs between areas) so I've not even gotten the chance to complete Akihabara, the first stage. The fact that this is possible to happen when it's sold for real money is really bad. I can play the arcade mode just fine though. - It has one of the worst 'controller friendly' settings I've seen. I've tested it with various different third-part controllers but none of them are playable. (They work but the settings are off by far) And you can only change the 4 different punch/kicks/special controls. On top of this it also has a horrible keyboard layout which you cannot change either.  Positive things: The combat seems really smooth and satisfying. The reward system of gaining levels which allows you to improve your character is also something I think is a cool addition to a 2d fighting game.  So what would it take for me to recommend this game? - The controller settings needs to be fixed ASAP (So that you can adjust even a third part controller, or atleast your keyboard to make it enjoyable). At the moment I'd say that it's only enjoyable with supported controllers. - Enable multiplayer for online-gaming. Nowadays 99% of the people who play games at a computer either do it solo or online. The odds that a person has 2 PC controllers that support this game and friends who actually wants to play this game with them are rather slim.

Despite having played 444 hours in Elite: Dangerous, I have played another 100 hours in Frontier's own launcher, outside steam. From what I've read, most of the reviews are against DLC's... yes, AGAINST THE DLC... The DLC isn't that bad, but its overrated, since it only allows you to land on airless planets. If what they say its true, then I will buy the next DLCs and I wont stop here. They advertised FPS on stations, multi-crew, atmosferic landings, etc.  Let me state the obvious thing. I own this game since quite a lot, and I didn't play a lot on the first time. First 40-50 hours are the best time you will have, but after that its all the same... you are now bored... A aisling commander scooped me out of this boredom once he got me into his growing player group. I was lucky to be one of the first, and travelling with a T7 covering my 6, better yet :) . After 300 hours of gameplay, I got myself a T7... here the boredom restarted. I am more of a fighter than a trader, but bounty hunting didnt get me much prizes. After almost 600 hours of gameplay, I got myself a Python. Once you have a multi-role, capable of trading, exploring and jumping into combat, it all gets awesome. You wont feel the same as I if you are not in a player group, since we help eachother, and we do all sorts of thing you never imagined, from manipulating Background Simulation to expanding your leader's area of influence.  TL;DR Good game if played with other players, not so good to play alone.   Also, go Horizons, not this one... Its not worth buying this right now...

Imagine waking up, not entirely sure where you are, but knowing it's not quite where you're supposed to be. Nyctophilia runs somewhere along those lines, but it's not entirely clear whether you should be where you end up, either. A tale of parallel lives and mental mysteries, this game provides a bit to chew on mentally despite its rather short play time.  In Nyctophilia, you assume the role of a young man who doesn't entirely want to return to the game's primary setting, a house from his past, but who seems to have little choice in the matter. Over a series of days, you explore the house and what was left in it, including shadows of the past that grow increasingly menacing. At the same time, you explore the main character's past, only to find why the game's called Nyctophilia in the first place.  As a caveat, I need to point out that I played this game back when the English translation was rather rough, and things were a bit buggy; I had a game-ending issue about 1/3 of the way through and had to replay the first day or so. Even then, I enjoyed what was presented, getting enough out of the translation to sort of know what was going on, and probably wondering more about the rest than I would have with a straight translation. There was enough weirdness going on to creep me out, though the sudden shift in location and direction toward the end threw me off a bit. I'm eager to give it another go with the updated translation sometime soon so I can figure the rest out.  The game itself is rather short, taking maybe an hour or so to finish, but it's still not bad for free. I particularly enjoyed some of the quips your character makes upon inspecting things around the house; these change each day, so be sure to try interacting with everything. I also liked some of the more trivial-seeming options, such as what drink to start the day with. Personally, I'm a kegs and eggs kind of guy, and I'm glad that was available (and I didn't run out).  So yeah, it's a bit rough around the edges, but it's free and it's only an hour. Give it a chance.

To be honest the only reason I bought this game was because 4 days had gone by and there still wasn't a review on the store page. I was kinda hoping for a quirky underrated gem, I guess. I was half-right; boy it sure is quirky. I mean, when the dev popped into the corner of the screen in the form of a youtube-esque facecam in a lazy costume in a poorly-lit environment recorded on a below-average quality webcam, I knew I was in for something at least DIFFERENT, and I'll admit, the gag made me laugh. The music isn't bad either, it's a bit short so the loops gets old quickly but it's nothing too unbearable.   Unlike the graphics. I don't think I've ever purchased a game on Steam with artwork this terrible. 90% of the time, I have no ♥♥♥♥ing clue what the hell I'm looking at. Now to be fair, if I tried to make a game and do the artwork myself, it would probably look just as bad or worse, which is why I would stick to what I'm good at, and find people who are good at what I'm bad at to do the rest. The environment is a total clustercuss of indistinguishable assets and the characters themselves might actually make you vomit. I've seen better character concepts on the average 14 year olds Deviant Art page. Water tiles are literally nothing more than 4 lines made with the pencil tool in a couple different shades of blue, it's aMAZing. The games sense of humor seems to revolve around the idea that it doesn't actually care, at least I think that's what it's going for, I couldn't tell how sincere it was to be honest, so its okay to be ugly as long as it plays nice, right? but it doesn't.  The controls, oh my god, the controls. So basically it's a non-traditional platformer. Your basic jump ability is a little weak so you have to draw a line from your character to the spot you want to jump and he flings in that direction, which sounds easy, but you don't get a great sense of exactly where you're going and you have to correct your course once you're in the air, but it's so hard to control because your character flies through the air so quick that it's really difficult to process what's going on. You really have to plan ahead before each jump, it's so disorienting. You'll probably be stuck in the intro for a little while before you adjust to simply jumping from platform to platform, and even though I did get the hang of it, it never actually felt natural, merely understood. You also have this teleport ability that can teleport you a short distance, but it doesn't seem to make any sense to me. It's supposed to go where your cursor is, but sometimes it would work and sometimes it wouldn't and I couldn't make heads or tails on how the mechanic functioned. Eventually I figured out which parts of the level wanted me to teleport and to where, but that was just from looking at the level design, I never actually understood it. It's like someone saying 'press this button' without being told what the button does.  There's combat too, if you can call it that. You have this tiiiiiny little white line called the chow-blade, or something to that effect, which is kinda ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t when virtually every enemy has ranged attacks and you practically have to hump their face to damage them. It was clear to me that this was not a combat-oriented game so I avoided the monsters, but the platforming was frustrating and didn't make sense to me so I avoided that too and got a refund instead.

I am still playing (~4h), but but so far I really like the game.   BTW. I do not agree that combat is sooo basic. Saving bullets, deciding which one take down by hand, how to shoot exploding ones if there are surrounded, utilizing throwable elements, understanding what you can do with different wepons, and when just sneak or run by, it all adds for IMHO nice experience.

I really REALLY wanted to like this game. And honestly, I DO. But can I 'recommend' it? No, not at all.  Now you may ask why? Well here is where the fun starts... Or stops I guess I should say.  First, let me start off by saying the story itself is great, the idea and concept, the characters, narrator, ect ect. It's all great. And it saddens me the only way to really experience it is through the interactive medium of video games. Because THIS is where Stories comes SOOOO close and falls just short.  If I were to sum up the entire game in one word, it would be 'C-L-U-N-K-Y'. Seriously, it is SO clunky. I could sit here and tell you about how the levels and combat is repetitive (which they are after the third or fourth, hour long play through) but I think what really kills this title is just how non consistant the gameplay is. To those of you who've played Assassin's Creed or the Arkham games, you'll be familier with the combat, enemies circle you and have big obvious tells for attacks, and you must dash, block, parry, counter, ect your way through every fight, which is fine... If the controls worked more than half the time.  I can't count how many deaths I've had due to pressing dash and my character on screen simply dashing to nowhere and standing in the exact spot he was already in, or clicking on enemies to block and just sitting there unresponsive, or even attempting to ATTACK them instead and being hit first instead of blocking, because oh yeah, ALL your combat options are done through ONE button, making combat entirely context sensitive. Which means the game sometimes has no idea what you actually want to do when you press this singular button.  Movement is governed by your 2nd mouse button and suffers a similar fate. If your curser is JUST over the invisible walls that line EVERY path, the game has no idea where you want to go and you'll just run in seemingly random directions or just stop dead in his tracks until you correct it. (Oh yeah, sometimes the movement sticks OR the floor randomly turns into an ice level and I don't know why and you can't stop until you hit a wall.)  Speaking of walls, this is a game with no jump or otherwise, acknowledgement of a 3rd dimension, still HAS one. Meaning if you're not careful, you can fall off your horizontal layer and get stuck under the floor. (It would have been better to just make the game borderless and the fall killed you rather then keeping you stuck in 3D purgatory IMO.)  And speaking of design, I have no idea what they were doing with the optimization. Don't let the late PS2-esque graphics fool you, anything above 'VERY LOW' in the settings will probably make your computer feel like a toaster. It did for me.    Okay, enough bashing the game for now... SO WHAT GOOD CAN YOU SAY ABOUT THIS GAME??? you might ask.  Well, apart from the chug-enducing graphics, they are quite appealing to look at. The art-design and overall aesthetic is beautiful, I'd say the game itself is well-written enough, there's a response to pretty much EVERYTHING you do from the narrator, which is fun. It's short but replayable with interesting characters, it's funny and good-humored. Overall, I'd say this game is quite charming and actually pretty fun when it works. Just a shame about there being so much wrong with the 'game' portion of it...  Vinal verdict? 6ish out of 10? Not worth 15 dollars. You might, atmost, get 10 hours out of it. If you can get it on sell for 5 or less though, go for it, it's a fun time waster on a dry weekend and it's worth it for the laughs alone.
Unless you run a VR demo business, this is not worth $10. But if you can snag it for $1-5, it's cool to relax in for 5-10 minutes for each of the three experiences.

Gemini Rue is an smart, interesting, and worthwhile little point and click adventure game. I hadn't played the genre for quite some time before this, but there was something about a cyberpunk noir dectective title that I couldn't avoid. And it delivered. The ambiance was exactly what I was looking for, providing a deep sci-fi mystery thrown over a gritty urban locale. Mix in a decent soundtrack reminiscent of a mellow jazzy detective story combined with Mass Effect or BladeRunner and it hits all the right beats.  The story was woven brilliantly; you playing the part of Azrial Oden, ex-assassin on the hunt for his missing brother. I found myself following the pace very well, putting the parts of the mystery together on my own just as the story was delivering the outcomes. This meant, at least to me, that the story was smart, but not too smart. The gameplay is similar enough to other point and click games however the use of Azrials commlink to call associates or the terminals being used to look up vital information kind of gave the sci fi narrative a much more immersive feel.  I guess a for and against this game would be it's length. If there was one complaint I would have it's that the story was a bit on the short side and that I didn't get to see enough of this universe. One or two other locations to further flesh out this world would have been great to see. I finished the game in around 8 hours at a pretty relaxed pace, not completing all (maybe a third) of the achievements. This to me was a bit too brief but the tight story and atmosphere more than make up for it.  I thoroughly enjoyed Gemini Rue. So much so that I'm looking to try some of the other point and click titles from Wadjet Eye Games; the next being the Blackwell saga. If your looking for a good point and click game with elements of noir detective mystery combined with a great sci fi angle, then I'd suggest giving this game a try.

Ghostbusters: The Video Game may very well be the only thing we will ever see resembling a (real) third movie for the series. The story picks up where the second movie left off with an all new disturbance that sets off a chain of events requiring the ghostbusters to spring into action once again, this time with a new recruit (you) in charge of testing new and extremely dangerous equipment. You also spend some time re-visiting locations from the previous movies as well as a few familiar faces and specters.   Gameplay is similar to any over-the-shoulder style shooter and your character handles pretty well, especially when he dodges. Ghosts operate on a sort of pokemon logic where you have to damage them until their health meter dips into the red before wrangling them with the capture beam into your ghost trap. You get a variety of different weapons and purchasable upgrades that all add some sort of utility in addition to being a different style of damage (slowing monsters, tethering things, slamming objects, etc). Most of the weapons are actually useful for specific situations and I didn’t find myself using only one type of attack for the majority of the game, which was a welcome bit of variety since in a lot of games with multiple weapon types it’s typical for a good chunk of them to be totally unused or ignored.  The game throws a few boss battles at you here and there and they’re generally a bit challenging, but what is unfortunately more challenging is the absolutely lackluster AI. I probably spent 1/3 of the game reviving my teammates and if you play on any higher level difficulty you will likely be doing the same. The other ghostbusters have a bad habit of getting knocked down when out of sight or in the middle of a swarm and while they can raise each other, you will absolutely need to pitch in once the second one drops or it will start a chain reaction of repeat incapacitation and reviving, ending in you being the only one left alive. Once you’re all down it’s back to the last checkpoint and this can be particularly frustrating if you were near the end of an encounter or boss fight. I hear the original release of this game on console had multiplayer and I really wish they hadn’t stripped it out of the PC version because this game would be loads of fun to play with friends instead of barely-functional AI.  Despite the flawed AI, and a few performance issues with the final battle (massive FPS drop), I have absolutely no other criticisms of this game. The characters are acted out perfectly by their original actors, something unheard of in most movie-video game adaptations. The combat is actually fun and challenging, and once you figure out how to babysit the AI they become less of a hindrance and more of a “get out of incap free” card. The graphics are pretty decent for a game released in 2009 and you can tweak a settings file to put in some anti-aliasing if you feel like it. Considering the fact that you can pick up this game for less than $5 during sales, you’d be doing yourself a real disservice if you were to pass up on such an underrated gem.

Seriously, it's just like the matrix - you can manipulate physics in the environment, jack out and in of the matrix, and interact with your 'handler' while inside.  Co-Op is pretty great, however, it feels more like two people who are in the same map but aren't necessarily required to help each other with the mission. The city is extensive so you can both be doing challenges in different places - which makes it more like multiplayer than true co-op.  Bad guys are tough - but with a wide selection of awesome weaponry including the ability to drive just about every vehicle you see by hijacking it - it's an absolute blast to play. Highly recommended fun!

Decent game, horrible port.  Viking: Battle for Asgard has a potential concept: a conflict between the Viking and the Legion Army. You a.k.a the chosen warrior will build up the Viking army and lead them into the war. This game has all essential factors for a great mini sandbox hack-n-slash game: combat feels good, although it gets a little bit repetitive after 3 hours, gore effect are sick, impressive large scale battles, great characters' design and some lite RPG elements. However, it has some shortcomings such as lack of enemies' variety, boring boss battles and running from a camp to another site feels tedious (you can fast travel at some locations called Lairstone or Laystone or what-ever-the-hell-it-is), also navigating the map is a pain in the ♥♥♥. I would have given this game a 6.8/10.  ...but  Let me remind you the origin of this game:  Viking: Battle for Asgard was released on PS3 and Xbox 360 in 2008...  ...and PC version got released in 2012. What do we have ? Enhanced graphics and kb/m support.  Keyboard and mouse work great. Graphic is somewhat mediocre but it didn't bother me at all.  But there's one funny thing is that the dev - Hardlight Studio made effort in enhancing graphics but they didn't UNLOCK FRAMERATE.  This game is hard locked at 30FPS, there's no way to unlock it. I'm personally fine with 30FPS, but the problem is framerate drops very often, which makes combat less accurate. Once framerate drops below 20, the game runs in slow motion, YES FULL SLOW MOTION, and this happens randomly. For example, I was talking to an innkeeper, after 2 seconds, framerate drops to 15 for no good reason at all. In large scale battles with a large numbers of soldiers on screen, my laptop still can keep the framerate at 30, but when I get into a cave, which has less objects to render, framerate drops and the game goes into slow motion for 5 or 6 seconds. I don't understand what happened, why did the dev made efforts in making kb/m compatible and enhancing graphics, but managed to ♥♥♥♥ up the framerate ? I just don't get it.  Viking: Battle for Asgard is a decent game ruined by technical problems. I had a good time chopping dudes' heads off and I'd say this game's well worth $4, but this horrible port is one of a hundread reasons why PC gamers are boycotting SEGA (Rome 2: Total War I'm looking at you). Hardlight, next time please don't ♥♥♥♥ up another game.

It's been a long, long time since I picked this game up. I remember watching my friend play in total awe as he flew through the atmosphere and mined out little rocky satellites in orbit. Well, the game did lose some of what made it as purely unique and awe-inspiring as it was before 1.0. However, it is still an enjoyable game for its incredibly diverse open universe and its sensible progression system. There were some things that didn't translate well into a story-based game, such as the more shoehorned bosses like the mech and dreadwing, but the story did add numerous things which I feel helped it in so many ways. A collection of likable characters, if minimally involved, still seem to be relatable in some ways. Aside from the numerous bugs and gltiches upon the full release, many of which remain a problem, Starbound will be a game I will fondly remember decades down the line. I hope Chucklefish continues to nourish this game for a long time, and eventually I hope it remains an Indie classic among the likes of Minecraft and Terraria. Also, it did No Man's Sky better than No Man's Sky, so there's that.

After reading all the other reviews I brazed myself, bought and installed the game, updated my display drivers and pressed play, ready to be disappointed: But I wasn't.  The game ran smooth 50-60fps on high, very little stuttering near crowds, no crashing, no graphical or connection issues found (i5 3,4GHz, 970GTX, 8mb RAM).  The game feels like a Blood Money 2, which is everything I've ever wanted from a Hitman game. Each level feels like one big puzzle with multiple ways to approach and complete. Instincts can be turned off (important) and the disguise system works wonderfully. Overally a very enjoyable experience, and I haven't even finished the Paris yet. After that: Contracts!  Can't wait for more!

My favorite AC title just because of the ships, after ACIII I wanted to do more with the sailing and this answered that prayer. I enjoy just sailing and listening to the same repetitive shanty songs over and over and just attacking other ships. As far as story goes its unique and interesting, the out of Animus reality gameplay is spread out and not intrusive like previous AC titles where you were mad you had to do real life tasks instead of progress the story inside the Animus. Graphics are not very beautiful on lower and medium settings but gorgeous on high. I had to turn settings and draw distances down to run higher than 40 fps on my low end machine (AMD A8 6500 3.5Ghz 16GB DDR3 RAM GTX750ti 2GB DDR3 VRAM.) On highest settings the game bogged badly to teen fps and had bad lag. On medium settings I'd compare it to playing on xb360. Lots of pop up too, annoying, you see characters render directly in front of you while walking down the street in a village, or boats pop up 20 feet away while at sea. But, its playable on my budget PC, and despite all of that I still enjoy it greatly as my favorite AC title. I probably put 50 plus hours into the xb360 game, and just restarted again on PC when game was on sale. I'm still happy I bought it.

A great game for city builder enthusiasts.  Set in the XVIIIth century, you are a little colony settling in an inhospitable land. Build you town to survive the winter, and expand it to welcome more and more people. Become the best city in the New World !  Featuring beautiful graphics, this game also has a very intricate economic system, which will not hesitate to slap you on the wrist for the slightest mistake. You will need to manage every resource at your disposal to survive, from wood and stone to herbs and clay. Catastrophes can stem from the smallest errors and snowball very rapidly, often resulting in comedic losses in no time.  The community is active and mods are available, the best being Colonial Charter, which expands and adds vast amounts of content.  Highly recommended game, but be careful, it will eat up your free time.

I really wanted to like this game, the art style is kind of captivating, i find the pirates squeaky voices kind of cute, it has a neat little rpg system for your pirates! it basically checks all the boxes i need! I 'beat the game' or at least, i killed the 4 legendary pirates, i believe there is an end game screen if you clear the entire map as well, but i don't think i really enjoyed it.  Even though it is officially out of early access now, it should not be. The UI is confusing, there are a series of buttons at the bottom of the screen that control everything, only several of them don't actually do anything (most notably, the attack button seems to be completely non-functional, you attack just by moving your units to a hostile), and the ones that do let you accomplish things are explained very badly or not at all (even in the games help system, it kind of just assumes you already know what you're doing). It took me hours before i realized how to select my hired crew of pirates (you click on a set of bars with pirate group names on them at the top, when clicked the bar will change from black to a very dark maroon), i still am not sure how to change the groups my pirates are in to make them more easily selectable.   These problems continue in every part of the ui, the shipbuilding screen is hard to use, and obscured by unnecessary parts of the ui (if your captain is nearby a shopkeeper, you cannot build your ship without also viewing the shopkeepers inventory), on top of that, there is a grey overlay that persists while trying to build your ship, also, if you remove a part, it does not go back to your inventory until you leave the screen and come back. The crew screen is bad at presenting information, some of my crew have what appears to be medals on their portraits, i cannot find out what these mean, there is no further info on them, some pirates have them, some don't, no mouseover/extra info as to why. When on an individual crewman's page, skills are learned through books, which after much trial and error, it was found that to use them, you have to put the book in the crewmans equipment slot? somewhat confusing, improving stats of a crewman is done by using TP (gained from leveling) and then clicking on what appears to be a golden poop, i assume this was meant to be an arrow up, but it looks nothing like one. Its not apparent how skills work, there is a skill called cannon mastery, my ship has several piles of ammo and plenty of cannons, but despite the fact that all 10 of my crewman have the skill, they almost never decide to use it, and almost never more than once, also i've never seen anyone even try to use the swivel gun. That brings to my next point about the crew! despite their morale being maxed out, along with full bellies, ample supplies of rum and food, sometimes they'll just go crazy and shoot someone, just for kicks, no warning! one second you'll have a crew of 10, the next, they've all shot each other! and despite the captain still being alive, its game over anyways. Also, the entire game is covered in a weird blur effect, i guess it was an artistic choice, but it honestly makes the game look drastically worse than the screenshots you see above, you can turn the effect down in your settings, but not completely off. The rest of the ui is at least mediocre, the world map is kind of boring, but functional, the animals screen, i have not been able to figure out as of yet, sometimes the buttons just don't do anything at all, sometimes they start working if you click on them several times. The game makes this absolutely horrible high volume noise at the intro whenever you load it (at the Quadro-Delta developer logo), i actually almost quit right there.  In conclusion, this could be at least a good game if they completely throw out every last aspect (seriously, every single bit, the ui is horrible, redo every single screen) of the ui and start from scratch. The actual game is lacking in variety in some areas, there are not very many different ships you will be fighting along with every island being nearly the same, and there are a few things that i don't think add to the experience, like pirates pooping everywhere which was cute for about a minute (with the constant influx of new pirates after they randomly shoot each other, i get tired of teaching each one to poop). But, still the game behind that miserable ui seems like it might be at least a little fun after completely starting over on the ui (100% redo of the ui, every part is bad).  08/01/14: I've heard that the UI was recently updated? I never played when the game was in early access under the old ui, but whatever was changed, change it back, it cannot be worse.

Naissancee  is a first person view platformer game with puzzle elements, set within an abstract, dystopian futuristic, dark industrial, three dimentional environment. It involves interesting, innovative mechanics - first time, as far as my memory goes, as a digital player have I been actively responsible for the protagonists breathing control. The damage tolerance system is thought out well, being mostly forgiving, while still is it possible to perish due to unwise or unlucky move. Fortunately, the game does auto-save, but unfortunately, the savegames are available only at given checkpoints.  The platforming aspect is swift and featuring proper challenges. The exploration aspect - as plenty of pathways and vistas are provided to be discovered - is very good as well. The quality of puzzles involved is noteworthy even when compared to  Antichamber , nevertheless the ones present are oftentimes too focused on platforming to call  Naissancee  a proper puzzle game in the vein given.  I have yet one complain about  Naissancee . Namely, it visually becomes deliberately overtuned at times, making it hard to distinguish which way to go certain places. Albeit, since I have written a recommending review, it must be bearable.  7/10 wHATs grade. Expect between ten and fifteen hours of honest singleplayer experience. By the way, check  The Matrix  once more before beginning  Niassancee .

The Evil Within, or 'Why Is This Game 40 GBs I Want To Delete It But Must Finish It'   Pros:   On the physical release box, the cover is holographic. Holographic stuff is great. Wonderful job, guys.  There's a guy named Sebastian. For someone who has the last name 'Breedlove', I enjoy seeing ♥♥♥♥ing ridiculous and dumb names in video game characters. It really just heightens the experience.  The game looks great, for about a chapter.  To upgrade the protagonist's abilities, you have to shock them in the brain. That really helped me get through and finish the game, worst case scenario I could just rush and pray a save spot with upgrade facilities would be near and voila. It would be there, I'd laugh and walk this dumbass to the chair. It was very therapeutic.   The feeling I got when I deleted this game off my hard drive was wonderful. There's nothing like seeing 40GBs off your hard drive in one fell swoop.    Cons:   The story goes nowhere and to make things worse, started off in 'nowhere' and ended up 'nowhere' as well. It might be the worst story I have ever seen in an AAA Horror game, no hyperbole. Maybe in general, for all games, of the last 5 years at the very least.  The characters are so bad, you will hope they do die. It seems like the idea here was for gamers to read and listen to all of the hidden bits of story and that would get the characters 'over'. It's worked for many other games but definitely did not here. Oh, the main character is an alcoholic, I would be too if I was that damn boring and never questioned why a guy with a microwave for a head is trying to kill me.  The worst camera I have seen since the god damn Blair Witch Project.  Letterboxing, fixed FPS, the ♥♥♥♥ing mouse cursor can't even work correctly in the title screen, there are so many little problems with the PC port of this game. I'm running a great machine and if it's this poor, I shudder to think how it's functioning for most people. Absolutely no excuse for this at all.  It's Resident Evil 4 with a bottle of Aunt Jemima poured over it and placed in your gutter with leaves flying everywhere. You're pretty sure that Resident Evil 4 is somewhere below that mass of leaves and high fructose corn syrup but who has the ♥♥♥♥ing time?  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  Do not buy this. I know, the creator of Resident Evil made this and it looks interesting but please don't. I love horror games, RE: Code Veronica on the Dreamcast was probably my favorite game of all time. I loved Resident Evil 4, all the other games in the series, I even put up with the dumb chick in RE5 who spoke like she played for Newcastle. This isn't someone saying that it wasn't scary enough, or that it's stupid, it is just literally an absolutely horrible video game.  This game, without a discount goes for $59.99 but I had bought the game in a physical edition on Amazon for $10. It wasn't worth the $10. To have this game list for sixty dollars is not only insulting but really does feel like theft. It is halfbaked, there are so many other better horror/action-adventure hybrid games out there and even then, who says your computer will be able to run it at an even below average rate?     Score:  1.5 out of 10

I'm going to go ahead and recommend this game for anybody new to VR.  Firstly let's talk about what the game isn't (and what people complain about). It's not complex, there are very little (or no) puzzles.   The game is all about the polished visual element. You've survived a disaster in space and you have to make your way from one checkpoint to another. This game is about potential I think for what games could be, which is more about 360 degree story telling and some low to mid level user interaction.   The controls are awkward, as they should be since you're floating in a cumbersome space suit.   So for the brief amount of time that I've played this, I feel like I've gotten the jist. I did have to stop though because I'm prone to nausea with locomotion games, but I am definitely compelled to pick it up again. It's a beautiful game.  I'd say pick it up on the steam sale. Its easily one of the top experiences out right now for VR.

Luckily I was the former and enjoyed the view and great control system using a gamepad. Performance was perfect, extremely smooth constant 60FPS on a 2560x1080 monitor (which was without fault during gameplay) running on a GTX 970.

I very much like the concept of the game, but the novelty wears off and then it's just an FPS. And if you're doing an FPS, replayability depends on the gameplay. I'm sorry to say I felt Screencheat doesn't deliver enough in that department. Walking, aiming and shooting just doesn't go smoothly enough. As such it is an interesting idea, but not that great of a game.  You've got to applaud the courage to put out a four player local multiplayer PC game, though. We don't see enough of those.

Warning, possible spoilers   Since the beginning, what I've come to expect from this was a story relating to an almost over abused type of telling, with a just as common anime like twist to its nature. The story was short and sweet yet.... I'm not sure. Now, I know I can't expect some great novel for the price of ten bucks, but from what I've read, its intended purpose is to suck in a few interested viewers, for the next volume to come. Yet, the story I was given so far, felt so independent in its role, I felt less of a linger for more, and more of a disappointment that this was all I was given.  Now this isn't to say this is a bad story for its price, rather the opposite. But for those interested in purchasing this story segment, I give warning, that you'll be getting less of an actual story, and more of a prologue of what is to come. I truly hope that when/if part two comes out, there will be more to cling onto, more to enjoy.   One particular note, though for those who have read up on this game, probably already know this. This is a story, nothing more, nothing less. The most interaction you will ever see is one option somewhere within the midst of the game's dialogue, nothing more (though I've chosen only one of the two options, so maybe the second option has more to follow, who knows). But this is definitely something that is meant to be enjoyed for the story in its entirety. If this is something that turns you off, maybe wait until part two, or just pass on it. If you're still interested, have at it, and have fun.

review_text: If you wish to get into strategy games then I would recomend starting here, it is a RTS strategy not like the Total War series which is a turn based strategy with many things such as polotics, but Star Wars Empire At War dosen't have any of that stuff. Its just a plain and simple game with hundreds of hours of fun and maybe more. It dosen't say I have that because I played it years ago not on steam. Very good game, but with some flaws. This game is a great story-based RPG. If you like Undertale, Earthbound, or OFF, definitely get this game.  +++Pros+++  Strong Narrative, very dark Great use of permadeath Solid Art style, mutants are very well designed This game will destroy your soul.  ---Cons---  Walking off cliffs is an instant game-over. Hold left or right a second too long, and you could find yourself getting kicked back to the last save you made. Very annoying, and it will happen at least once Game is rough around the edges in some parts  Overall, I'd say definitely get this game if you can handle occasional frustration.  NOTE: Selecting to play the game in Pain mode makes every save point work only once. It makes the game very tense, as all your actions last unless you go back to your last save, and it adds a kind of survival-horror element to the game. I played through the game like this, and found it rewarding but very annoying at points. Think carefully if you want to play the game this way.

It feels like this game could have been so much more with just a few adjustments. I really like the idea of buying and selling to make a profit, upgrading your ship, crossing swords with pirates and navies alike, and an intriguing match 3 system. Ultimately though it seems like everything comes up short.  The end result feels like a poorly made mobile port.  The game holds your hand on the trading portion of the game by telling you what is a good buy/sell instead of letting you figure it out on your own, or even tracking what you paid per unit. The result of which just leads to simple grinding to make gold.   Questing feels rather shallow with the standard fetch and deliver or bounty tasks. Some complexity or variety could definitely help in this regard.  Sailing in itself is a bit of a chore as well. The game could stand to gain from a mini-map (or even, in keeping with the theme, a map item in your inventory that would also serve to track quests), and landmasses seem a bit sticky especially with a lack of path-finding.   Accumulation of land also seems like a bit of wasted potential. Why not have it so you can use the land to set up some sort of sugar cane farm or distillery in order to produce your own goods?  The match 3 portion has a rather unique twist to it, and this was probably my favorite part of the game, though I feel like this should be tweaked a bit as well. I won't nitpick here though.  I respect this developer for his continued involvement and dedication towards improving his game. He seems to be very active in engaging the players and responding to feedback.  Can I recommend this as it stands? It's an average time waster if you don't mind the grind, and some might find the match 3 portion rather fun. As for me, I really tried to like it, but is was mediocre at best and just couldn't hold my attention.

If you are an old time gamer who loved the original King's quest back in the 80's, then this King's Quest may feel a bit of a let down. I liked the open end worlds of the original series. You could make mistakes and get yourself in a corner where you couldn't win. It was a challenge, especially back then because you didn't have the internet to help you cheat through the puzzles. Also in the old games Graham was a bit of a badass. In this one he is a little clumsy dork who has no business being in an adventure game. The puzzles were too easy and pretty much impossible to screwed up if you click enough buttons. The game play is linear so there are only so many options. It was actually more of a movie than a game. For me this sort of ruined the game and didn't keep true to the orginal series.  If you were never a fan of the original series, you were part of the entitled generation who feels like they should be a king even though they can barely walk without tripping over their own goofy feet, and you like simple puzzles that you can't fail at then maybe this game is for you.  I am on the fence at how to rate this... It would be a pretty good game for little kids, but it isn't a KIng's Quest game.

Spent 155 hours playing around before I finally beat the game. Upon the successful completion of the final main story quest I was greeted with a 30 second cutscene. It took me a further five minutes to realize that what I had just seen was the actual ending of the game. It was a bigger letdown than the ending to Mass Effect 3 and left me quite unsatisfied.  On a personal/nitpick note, the lack of Ron Perlman voice acting, forced player character voice over (I like my Fallout PC quiet) and a conversational UI that doesn't tell me anything about what I am about to say adds up to my inability to recommend this game; especially at the release price.   The overall gameplay is quite solid beyond a few bugs and the environment is colorful and can be quite engaging. At least half of all my played hours thus far are from playing farmville with my settlements.   Would recommend for ~$25, not $60.

I also LOVE the way he sometimes makes mistakes while typing, then he erases the ♥♥♥♥ he typed.  *types* 'He just sounds like a ♥♥♥♥ing ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.' *deletes* '...like a jerk.'  I played the game three times, in hope for an alternate ending. First time i played it as a really nice guy, but Emily seemed to be a ♥♥♥♥♥ and don't give a ♥♥♥♥ about what i felt. The second time, i was a kinda nice guy, but told her she deserves better, and still tried to ♥♥♥♥ her in my room when she wanted to come over, but she still complained about nothing one year later.  The third time is different: i was the soul-less ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ who didn't care about anything. It's a nice little game, a 2D parkour platformer based on Tibetan Buddhism and the concept of the Bardos. The gameplay is fair, but the art, atmosphere, and cryptic narrative is where the story really shines. I won't spoil much, but basically you guide a little totem(which represents the soul of a recently dead person) through a series of ethereal landscapes to collect the totems needed to unlock the gates to the next life, scattered around the map. The artstyle and music is enchanting, and the strange poems around the map hint at a broader theme of rebirth and renewal. I'm not sure if I'd buy this(I think it deserves money but I'd probably overlook it if it had a price) but it's certainly worth getting, especially with its low price of $0.00. As a Buddhist myself, this game interested me and I now am more curious about the real-world teaching of the Bardos.
