A tower defense game mixed with RPG elements and a 3rd person perspective on your hero. A really enjoyable game where you are a young wizard defending the onslaught of orcs. You set up each mission by placing your 'towers' (in this games case, traps) to slow the orcs down. This is only your first line of defense as you control the young wizard once the round starts. Cast magic, go melee, fix traps, and much more.  I would recommend getting this game before purchasing the sequel. Sure the second one has two player co-op, but this will be cheaper and allow you to get a feel for the game. If you really enjoy the game and, like myself, find yourself wanting co-op play to add new deminsions and strategy, grab the second one. If you are quick, grab both games from Humble Bundle (as of this posting 12/10/13) and get both games, all DLC, for $4.25.  I highly recommend giving it a try. Watch the video/trailer to see if the playing style is what you are wanting too. If not, then don't waste your time because you will not be satisfied. If you are intrigued or thinking, 'yeah I will give it a try. looks fun!' then you should buy this right away.

app_name: South Park™: The Stick of Truth™
review_text: Spoiler warning ---------------- ----------------   When 'South Park' came out in the late 90's, people's reactions equal part shock and euphoria. It was an adult comedy series that joked coarse and tasteless with everyone and everything, while it was sharp social satire far more daring than 'The Simpsons'.  Although it has dropped a couple of 'South Park' games, none of those have had the blessing from creators Matt Stone and Trey Parker. that's one of the reason they set out to create 'South Park: The Stick of Truth ,' in which they both wrote the script.  In the Colorado town of South Park played out a bizarre story, where the TV show's most prominent figures - Cartman- Kenny - Stan - Kyle - playing fantasy war. On the 'magician Cartman's' backyard, people have their base while ' Elvenking Kyle' harboring elves, and they fight over a tree stick called 'The Stick of Truth .' You play a newly arrived nine year old who ends up in the middle of this death serious fantasy LARP . In typical 'South Park' spirit the story takes an extra absurd turn when the town get's hit by a Nazi zombie virus.  'The stick of truth' is eerily like the original, with its expressive 2d graphics and its austere character animations. It really feels like 'South Park ': during the game, I get to interact with all the familiar characters, from Al Gore and goth gang to the Canadian duo Terrence and Philip.  Although the story is wicked enough to be directly taken from the TV series, and scripted authors presence is palpable. The story is a mix of a sometimes incredibly entertaining operation with video-game conventions, social satire, American politics, infantile fart humor and tasteless joke.  Sometimes it hits the humor just right and I choke with laughter: as when you finally get access to Canada and it appears to exist in 8-bit graphics, according to the old 'The Legend of Zelda ' formula. the jokes Sometimes miss the banter with the mile-long distance.   The game has been censored in many countries worldwide. In particular two sequences , one of which takes place at an abortion clinic, and the other one which deals with ♥♥♥♥. i did expect 'South Park' to trample over all boundaries regarding which substances may be making fun about, but in the absence of satirical sophistication, as on these occasions, it becomes just sickening.  It turns out to still be the meta fun and sometimes sharp script, along with the familiar characters carrying the game. Despite its twisted storyline and sick humor is 'The Stick of Truth ' namely a surprisingly standardized role play, with turn-based battles and focus on upgrading your armor. Although the childish armor and weapons makes me gigg. the fights quckly become repetitive.   The fighting takes a long time, and I tire soon to dull stare at the same attacks again and again. The missions you should perform is even more monotonous, as most is to knock down a certain number of enemies or collect a certain number of gadgets. It is a boring and totally uninspired design.  Although the game has its brilliant moments, it is uneven. Sure it's fun to watch 'South Park' realized properly in the game medium, but it nowhere near as clever and thoughtful as I d expected.

It's not a bad game per se, but after totalling my underwear multiple times playing the first one, this was a real letdown. It's creepy for a while, but for whatever reason, it ceases to be frightening after you encounter the pig monsters, and after that it gets rather dull with only very occasional frights. The story is a intriguing at first, but by the end, it's wandered off the deep end. Gone is the ability to toss just about any loose objects around, which helped to make the first more immersive and more enjoyable. Very little can be manipulated in this game unless you're supposed to do it to progress. There is no inventory, which really limits and hampers the level design and puzzles. Your lantern lasts forever. It's too simplistic and linear. Many of the gameplay elements that helped make the first (and the Penumbra games before it) more fun have been removed for reasons unknown. There's some decent design in here with some foreboding machinery, but it gets a bit one-track after a while.  Pick it up if it's on sale and you're interested, but don't expect to get the same experience as the first. It doesn't come close.

It isn't streamlined, minor tasks are difficult, and you will find yourself save scumming. It took me forever to get through the inital quest, but something kept me playing. I realized it was the story. An interesting world, fascinating systems, and a dark grim world that is a joy to explore. Add in detective elements as you play the titular inquisitor, and you have yourself something not quite polished, but unique and fascinating. If you can brave the lackluster gameplay, you'll find yourself drawn to the dark world surrounding it.  That being said, 10 dollars is a lot to ask, wait for a discount.

(I have not yet completed this game and this is thus only a first impressions review)  One of the most fun and prettiest 2D platformers I've played in recent memory. The game is a 2D beat em' up platformer, mixing combat with nice and fun to explore enviroments, that reward you for taking your time to look around (and often also test your speed). Even when I've died multiple times to the same boss I never feel cheated or loose interest in the game, I want to get back in and prove my skill.  The game is not afraid of shoving bosses at you from the very start, with a nice balance in difficulty, where even normal might prove a challenge for you if you're not used to this type of game. The music is simply beautiful, and fit well into the games atmosphere. For me the game was worth it's price and I almost can't wait to play more of it. The only thing I've been missing is co-op, which is a very small complaint, if you can even call it that, since it's just me wanting to share experience that is this game with some friends.  (Should most definitely be played with a controller)

Double Dragon Neon (PC version) is a very hard game to review. On one hand the gameplay itself is varied, and offers more ways to defeat enemies than have ever been possible in the franchise (Super Double Dragon or Battletoads/Double Dragon Ultimate Team). Wayforward defintely has its heart in the right place. On the other hand, the game is frustrating to play for all the wrong reasons and will probably turn away potential players.  For what it gets right, Double Dragon Neon is a very stylish nod to the 1980's and all the cheesiness bravado that was found in arcade video games during this era. DD Neon employs an experience system and offers shops to spend money/ore you collect in levels, much like the Game Boy Advance version of TMNT via UbiSoft. The PC version offers various resolutions, and offers very smooth 60 fps beautiful cell shaded animation and graphics. The soundtrack here is probably the best original score for its release back in 2012, and offers throwbacks to the 80's with very catchy lyrics accompanied by synthesizers and drum machine beats. The game also is very leniant with how combos work, allowing for mid-air catches and throws while allowing for moves that involve running or crouching. Overall, the combat (and screen clearing attacks) are more reminscent to Streets of Rage than classic Double Dragon (or its many sequels or spinoffs).  For what the game gets wrong is heartbreaking. Billy and Jimmy Lee are incredibly sluggish when controlling around, and its made more frustrating when you have to dodge telegraphed boss attacks that can (and will) juggle you in the air or in the corner; effectively draining your health and losing a precious life (lose all your lives and restart the entire mission over). There are sections in the end where its uncommon to fall off cliffs because you weren't fast enough to dodge a whirlwind attack and lose precious health (losing lives would have been much worse).  The coop aspect makes more sense when playing local coop, because the coop here is very weak especially in this 'definitive edition' PC port. Stealing lives, sharing diet coke health pickups, and allowing the other player to collect 1-ups is essential for this very hard game (even when using the 'training wheels' tape)....so its too bad none of this can be done effectively in the PC port. The PC version supports no text or voice chat whatsoever, so playing with a random person in Steam matchmaking is hit or miss.   When joining as a client, it's no help that the game still has issues with latency (overall performance on the zombie forest stage is horrible even in single player)...online play is functional....but reduces frame rates to about 25ish with all the stuttering and teleporting (online code as of 7/2/14).   Abstration Games have pretty much done little work to get this port to the PC, as evident in the very confusing key assigning in the options (good luck seeing that its Tab key to even get in there with keyboard controls) or the obtuse indicator for reviving your coop buddy via rewinding his tape (good luck seeing that its done with your throw key). Save yourself the frustration and don't bother playing this game with a keyboard, because the devs clearly just pushed this out the door with an Xbox controller in mind.  Overall, this game just barely wins a recommendation from me clearly on the presentation and solid combat while playing single player or local coop. Game offers a lot of little fun things to do with a coop partner (one player beatboxes and the other breakdances), and its clearly meant as a local coop game. The problems from the console release are here for the most part, and I can't really recommend this version of the game if you want to play online (while your online partner is very far away or has a subpar internet connection). If anything, pick this game up when it goes on Steam sale.

I went into this with very low expectations. I honestly wasn't expecting much, but here goes.  This is the WORST video game I've ever played.  THE WORST.   Now, why am I complaining so much? Let me start with the technical issues. 1. I was greeted with a resolution setting that only goes up to 1680 x 1050. Not even 1080. That ♥♥♥♥♥♥ me off. 2. The graphics. This looks like Half-Life 1, and for a game that is awfully new, it should looks somewhat okay, but it doesn't. This game tries to shove in your face that it has bloom and SSAO in it, but seriously, even with them, the game still looks like ♥♥♥♥. 3. Voice Acting. Dreadful. 4. Highest mouse sensitivity is still really slow for a person like me who likes to have a high mouse sensitivity.   Alright, the story. Nothing special. And by that I mean pretty much non-existent. You and a bunch of 'teammates' go to the castle, after it has been attacked. Turns out the girl of your dreams has been taken. Now you have to get her back. And there you go. I'm not gonna complain about the small amount of story I've been given, because honestly, I just want to try the hack and slash.  The first level. Why they made it so complicated did not make any sense to me. The first level started off with a mother♥♥♥♥ing keyhunt. It made no sense. It confused the living hell out of me. I needed to use a walkthrough, on the FIRST LEVEL. That's just awful. The area was big and the objects were all over the place. The controls were poor and didn't really help this game out at the slightest. Everything about this game just screamed wrong. For everyone asking about the keyhunt, imagine trying to find keys that do not even show up. That's right, you can't actually see them. Not to mention, you need a torch to even find one of them, which is just silly, because if there's a key there, then I wouldn't need a torch to actually see the key. It's a pointless quest to go find keys to open chests with other keys. But have I mentioned the fact that you literally have to walk on the walls to get an item you need? Yes, you do. You walk on the walls. YES. You literally jump on a small side railing on the wall, basically hovering to the side of it when you walk on it. I'm sorry, but I would never be able to figure that out because in a video game, I've never had to do that to actually get something. I just found that bizarre.  Then the main section of the game. The fighting. Oh my.  OH MY. OHH. You get three attacks. All of which are ♥♥♥♥ and don't feel natural. Combat was godawful. All you do is stab the enemy, move back, then just keep doing that until it's dead. That's it. That's the game. Oh my god. The attacks are all mapped to mouse buttons, including the middle mouse. I went immediately to change it, but WAIT, you can't change the controls. But for some reason, they have options for Bloom and SSAO instead of options for controls. Come on. Congratulations, you have shader effects. Yay.   This is the worst. Day One, Walking Dead Survival Instinct, and Postal 3, are all better than this. I didn't want to say this, but I had to. I'm here for you so you can save money. I would not wish this on my worst enemies. My point has been made. Nothing in this game works properly. Everything is bad.   0/10. This is a technical nightmare. Even if this game looks fun to you (which it really shouldn't), remember that it is 10 bucks, and you can do so much more with the ten bucks. I'm genuinely upset that I spent nine dollars on this.

I'm only two hours in.,.. I don't know where the game ends but so far after a dozen or more apartment visits it feels like a whole lot of nothing going on. The character ruminates on events happening outside of her control, but I don't know if there is a challenge or fail state. It just keeps moving forward, even without completing all the apartment work. So I'm not getting much out of this right now. You do only two things: Look around and 'Click 'x' to sympathize', to borrow from Call of Duty Advanced Warfare. And the looking around is excruciating because for a game with little environmental scenery this game moves like a sloth. Even on lowest graphic settings it has the same frame rate drops as the highest setting. It's clear that this game needs graphical performance tuning.  (ADDENDUM, June 22): It turns out that I'm missing half of the ideas in the game just by doing the chores alone. Angela expresses her thoughts in a the form of a diary and the way to see that is to sit down in the one lounge chair with a sheet over it. There are a lot of striking ideas being expressed in this moment that it is a shame if you miss it... And it's easy to miss because if you're out of time then you won't sit.   (FINAL UPDATE, JUNE 24): Finished the game. There is an intellectualism in the writing, but the game is boring and frustrating. I enjoyed playing the vinyl records because they broke up the otherwise dire soundscape. Moving around was always a problem and on more than one occasion I found a chore item impossible to do: Thoroughly scoured the apartment but didn't find the objective. I appreciate the idea of trying new things in gaming, and there is one crucial diary entry that illuminated why this game exists, but otherwise the game was thematically all over the place and full of mood swings. It felt more like a gaming transposition of someone's blog than an actual story. In that regard I felt as though I had been tricked into someone else's indulgence.

TL;DR - A horrible port of an otherwise amazing game - still worth your time.  FFXIII Has received a lot of criticism from people over being 'Too linear' or 'not final fantasy enough' - and while i can't disagree with linearity, i can safely state that this is one of the most fun games i've ever had the privilege to play. Whether you're a die hard fan, or someone new to the genre, i bet you will find yourself immersed regardless.  A story that might a bit confusing at times, but puts a lot of effort into character development - this is the ONLY final fantasy in which i cared for the characters. Sure, there is *that one character* who everybody hates (you will know immediately), but other than that, i really enjoyed watching how characters behaved. Not only that, but this game is actually quite long. I am currently on my 7th playthrough, and 16 hours into the game (knowing where to go / what to do / etc) i just reached halfway point marker. The 'sandbox endgame' is not as wide as some of the other jrpgs, but it does offer some stuff to do after you 'beat the game' - but even if you're just interested in seeing the story, you will have a long journey.  Graphics are absolutely amazing - although you might have difficulty noticing that with how ATROCIOUSLY this game was ported. Honestly, if you own PS3 or Xbox360, do yourself a favor and buy this game for the console. The port is really, really bad. For example, as part of gameplay you can gather data on enemies that shows stuff like resistances, important notes ('Can self-heal'), etc. Only due to some weird bug, this extra data will NOT be visible if you play using a resolution higher than 720p. Yeah, thats right - you have to choose to either get access to full game UI (but run it at 1280x720) or run it at full resolution but not be able to access enemy data. There are workarounds (google for FF13 GeDoSaTo), but overall this port is a massive, massive fail.  Soundtrack is fantastic. Both voices and game sound are very well done and fit properly. I have FF13 soundtrack in a boxed edition, and absolutely love listening to it.  World design and animation quality deserves its own spot. Its very easy not to notice just how realistic characters are animated - how they twist their hands, move their jaws, etc. But when you start paying attention, you realise just how much detail went into getting it to work - and its astounding. The world is just as awe-inspiring, with lots of design and art process clearly involved in its creation. Sure, there are 'simple' areas like that green grass field you can see on example screenshots - but there are also places which look damn fabulous, and there are more of those by far.  Overall, i would absolutely hands-down no-discussion recommend this game. I owned it on PS3 back during its original release, and got it on Steam during one of the sales - it is easily my favourite FF game, and one of most favourite games of all time. Don't listen to the haters (if you see any), go and try it for yourself - i promise, you won;t be dissapointed!

As someone who enjoys both Choose Your Own Adventure novels and sci-fi RPGs (Bioware's games in particular), I'm finding myself pleasantly surprised with this title. While I have no prior experience with the IP, the ambience and story are vaguely reminiscent of Star Trek and the like in terms of audio and overall presentation.   It's worth noting that this game/novel is quite unique among its genre since most of the CYOA stories I've read are bereft of any graphical (i.e., artwork injected into the text) and/or musical representations. Having these additions is a nice change of pace and helps to establish the atmosphere the developer is trying to create. Furthermore, this also includes a visually appealing GUI along with some customization options, which is yet another treat in this genre.  EDIT: The Star Trek references littered throughout this novel are kind of amusing. For example, you carry 'phasers,' 'beam down' to planets, have warp drives, wear the same uniform colors as the classifications in TOS/TNG, and when you select your ground team, the choice is labeled, 'Make it so!'  Will update once I've finished my playthrough in its entirety.

This is a horror/adventure/point & click game. Reminds me a lot of Neverending Nightmares.   You are a little girl, some bad things happened to you, you are in a bad mental state, surrounded by hallucinations and only want to find your kitty and go back home. There are good and bad creatures, strange places, etc. So the story is basically a slightly darker version of Alice in Wonderland.   Positive: -The characters are nice. The graphics are nice. The mini games are nice. The puzzles are OK, although they can be sometimes too frustrating or too obvious.  Negative: -I can't give a good rating to this game mainly because of the extremely long and dull dialogues. There are a lot of characters that you come accross and you have to talk to almost all of them. Most of these conversations are meaningless and helpless. They talk nonsense and they contribute very less to the story because the story is not clear at the end. Believe me, they are VERY long, and VERY boring. Creatures, demons, magic, terrible dreams and a lot of 'I miss my home and my cat' cryings. You have two choices in converstaions and they have no effect at all. You always have to click LMB to go on with the conversation. For god's sake, at least give me a keyboard button or something.   - The gameplay lacks logic sometimes, which can be really frustrating. At one part of the puzzle, you try to combine two objects but it doesn't let you do it. Then you pass that stage, you do a few other things, then you have to combine those two objects this time to continue. But since you think they can't be combined, you don't bother trying the same thing again, which is the normal human behaviour. So you get stuck there just for nothing.   For those who played the game, I'm talking about    the crayon and the insect executor card. You can't combine them before you try the ticket you stole from the bar
Honestly, it's way too addictive and the real game is trying to escape. I'm warning you now, don't play this game.

From the get go, you are pigeonholed into the role of a generic straight man on an insanely pedestrian quest to avenge a dead wife and a kidnapped son. And the truth is, I just don't care. It feels so contrived. It takes away from the immersion because I can hardly roleplay as the character I would like to be.  If the tradeoff for full voice acting is a shoehorned story and the loss of most RPG elements, then it's clearly not worth it. It's probably the first game I've ever modded from the get-go because the 'vanilla' experience is so excruciatingly dull.  The only thing that this game has going for it are the graphics, which are a massive improvement over previous entries in the series. I'd recommend picking up  Fallout 3  or  Fallout: New Vegas  (my favourite) instead of Fallout 4 because this buggy mess isn't really worth it even on sale at -75%.  Oh, and one last thing... In 2016, it's simply unacceptable to be so behind the times as to not to include a single LGBT character in a role-playing game. No, wait, there are two lesbian NPCs, but they can't be interacted with, and they literally get nuked roughly 2 minutes into the game. Aside from that, in the year 2287, there are no gay characters. None. Zero. Nada. Sure, your character and companions can get a little bi-curious, if you catch my drift, but come on... Would it have been really so hard to redub just a handful of lines to allow for a gay or lesbian main character?  But given this is a game in which the inclusion of a female playable character was a last minute afterthought due to public backlash, none of this is surprising. Stay away from this lazy attempt at an RPG unless you've already beaten the previous two entries in the series, and you're in desperate need of some more post-apocalyptic gameplay.

A worthy successor to the reimagined Tomb Raider series.  First and foremost, compared to the last Tomb Raider, this game is definitely less 'button-mashy' -- which is a very good thing. For better or for worse, most of my deaths are caused by falls, instead of not hitting the right combination of buttons. Kudos to the developers for avoiding that fatal flaw from the first game. Not having to mash buttons makes me very happy!  Let's start off with some criticisms: The puzzles aren't as challenging or as interesting as before, which is kind of a bummer. Most of the game relies on a series of jump puzzles and slowly diving into more complex movement mechanics. It leaves the game feeling a tad bit more linear -- don't know where to go? Look for a rock wall or an ice wall, or a place where you can shoot a rope arrow. Yawn.  And while most of these acrobatics feel somewhat satisfying, the gimmick gets old. And you can't help but wonder: How the hell do other people make their way around this landscape? Is everyone in this game a parkour expert slash freescale mountain climber? Sometimes I just wish there was a dirt path that I could just run around on, even if such a path was extremely dangerous. But this is Tomb Raider, and somehow you end up at your destination through a series of fortunate ledges and ropes and ice walls. There doesn't seem to be multiple routes to reach the same destination, which adds to the sense of linearity.  The crafting system is OK. Crafting and leveling up is really not that interesting, though it's better than nothing. I just am not a fan of devs adding 'crafting!' to everything under the sun.  Also, a lot of artifacts are just plain useless and uninteresting, and are only useful for 'completion' bragging rights. Oh cool, so you found an ancient dagger handle made of bone from the Mongolian era? In the end, it doesn't add anyting to the gameplay. Nothing. Zilch.  And upgradable executions that have no bearing on the gameplay? Quit trying so hard to make Lara cool. She doesn't need to execute people in a variety of ways to be interesting.  All of that being said, this is a very good game, even if I do have my gripes. If you're willing to suspend your disbelief, there's a lot of fun to be had.   Some of the many pros:   * There are some intense action sequences that bring some adrenaline (granted, some of them are a little 'out there' and cheesy).   * Great graphics. Some of the locations were incredible detailed! I also enjoyed the sound immersion. Some of the locations felt haunting, and I love that.  * Lara doesn't die as often in grisly ways, compared to the first game. I always found her deaths to be so disconcerting (granted, this does make her feel less vulnerable, which is something I enjoyed from the first game).  * Lara is an extraordinarily likable protagonist, and the facial animations are excellent. The voice acting is excellent. She is a strong heroine, not some sexualized bimbo. Yay!  * Fun and dynamic locations with good map design.   * Some of the scripting is intense. There are even moments where the suspense is palpable!  Overall, RIse of the Tomb Raider is a fun play, and absolutely worth it if you enjoyed the last game. It's a slightly different exprience, but still very enjoyable and still very satisfying. Would recommend.   8.5/10

In the mindset of an average RPG, entering this game was such a breath of fresh air. You could go the typical way and go genocidal. You could go through the game in a neutral fashion, doing either/or. Or you could even go merciful as shown in the trailer. The game has so many ending, so many pathways it can go. With that being said, the battles are so meaningful. Every. Single. One. Notice how in the average RPG, battles are just a way of grinding levels, adding playtime to the game as a whole, or just as an unnecessary obstacle (most of the time). In Undertale, every battle means something. You're meating a new creature from this whole new world beneath our feet. It makes everyone from the game feel...real. Everyone seems genuine, even the 'enemies'. It's a beautiful experience that came at a time where games aren't seen as an artistic medium. At a time where it may not be socially accepted by everybody. At a time where games strive to be taken seriously. Using fantastic writing, lovable characters, and unique game design, maybe it will be able to change minds.  In short, I ♥♥♥♥in' love this game. If you haven't tried it yet, play it or atleast watch a Let's play or two about it. The best mistake I've ever made. 10/10

I love a good puzzle game, and ZeGame is a very good puzzle game. The concept is simple-yet-innovative, the controls are intuitive, and the design is soothing. When I first downloaded ZeGame, I only meant to play long enough to get a feel for the environment. Several hours later, I was fully immersed in solving a particularly frustrating level.  Level progression is well thought out, introducing new concepts in each of the eight worlds, then gradually increasing difficulty as you progress through the world's twenty levels. If you make it to the end of it, you are left feeling both triumphant and shell-shocked, and you probably don't care that you didn't achieve three stars on every level (there's a time component in the scoring!), or that you weren't able to complete all the levels without 'dying'. Or you do care, and you're going to be in for quite a challenge.

Although ACIII has been criticized A LOT by  'real fans'  of the franchise I don't think of it as a disappointment. Having had purchased the Freedom Edition together with my brother, I now even got myself a second copy of this what I think  truly brilliant experience in gaming . I'm just going to outline what it is that I enjoyed so much about the fifth part of the Assassin's Creed series. Let's start with the  story , as this is what I believe to be the one red thread throughout all the games and thus should be paid special attention to. Assassin's Creed III has a  very promising  story being set in the time of the American Revolution. As a great fan of history this already got me very excited for the game. My hopes weren't disappointed either. After a couple of plot twists we encounter Connor and follow him throughout his childhood until he meets his teacher and becomes an Assassin. His story is incredibly  touching and thrilling , his character devekopment shows a lot of  depth  as we see him change throughout the story. But it's not just Connor we love about the game. The  sidecharacters  we meet and interact with all are created with great detail, we get to hear plenty of stories and yes, there are some  extremely sad moments , too. But enough with the story, let's take a look at what als Connor/Haytham/Desmond's adventures offer us. Assassin's Creed games have always been nice to look at and 3 definitely has some  breathtaking graphics . The different seasons, night and day, the deep forests and dirty towns as well as characters and animals are well animated and look  extremely realistc . What I especially liked here is the  light and shadows , that add flavour to the already great graphics. The most beautiful part probably is when you take a black horse at night when it's snowing, ride into the woods and have it rear right in front of the full moon;  it's incredible . Lastly, let's take a look at the  game-play  and the  controls . I've heard alot of people being unhappy about these, but as I only ever play games with my XBOX 360 controller for Windows (if possible), I was okay with it. The  controls are different  though, which felt a  little weird  from time to time, but is something you get used to easily. The introduction of a bunch of cool,  new skills and weaponry  also make these new controls forgiveable. Combat is fluid and well-paced. Another very interesting new addition to the game are the naval missions.  'Real Fans'  who have already played Black Flag are probably going to tell you how crappy these are, but you mustn't forget that III actually was released  before  IV. They are of course not as fantastic as they are in the latest Assassin's Creed game, but since they are  not a key element  and you're  not playing as a pirate , they are still pretty fun and a  refreshing difference . Personally I wasn't a fan of them at first, but after a while even I started to love them. Assassin's Creed III is a  great gaming experience  in so many a way. It has so much to offer and there's  no way  you won't enjoy it. I can only recommend it.

Hotline Miami, a game full of neon, blood, and a lack of story.  ------------------------------PROS------------------------------  -Music. Obviously the strongest point of this game is its music. Besides being a great soundtrack on its own, the music fits perfectly into the game, accenting every murder with brutal beats and carnivorous drops.  -Gameplay. The game has you fight the goons of the Russian Mob with baseball bats, golf clubs, machetes, knives, and even katanas. The combat is fast paced, to say the least. The enemies ASWELL as the player can only sustain one hit before they perish, forcing you to hide around corners, blitz gunmen, and dodge lead pipes to survive. The unnamed anti-social protagonist bludgeons, stabs, and rips his way through countless mobsters leaving the player with more satisfaction than sex with Kate Upton.  -Visuals. A perfect blend of pixel graphics and top down perspective let the player imagine worse fates for the deceased mooks and inject vivid neon and gratuitous blood spatters directly into your eyes.  -Difficulty. It's a hard game, that much is for sure, but not an impossible one. You have to work on ways to improve your skill set, find weapons you're familiar with. This leads to players preferring certain abilities and weapons.  -Story. You may have noticed the message at the top of this review. This game lacks story in all the best ways. Remember when you first played chess? Remember how it didn't need a story? Think of that when you play this game. It didn't need it because it was just that kind of game. Of course there is a bit of story here and there, but the point is for there to be a lack of story. To make players imagine, guess, and assume are not bad tools for a game to utilize. Sure, you are a masked man running around murdering people, and that masked man has reasons for doing it, but you don't NEED to know them. That's why this game is so amazing.  ------------------------------CONS------------------------------  -AI. Yes, yes. The most common problem for a game to have. The AI acts very idiotically in this game. They ignore dead bodies like nothing ever happened, run right after you and into a trap, sometimes they even get stuck on doors. Fortunately, as odd as it sounds, this combat based game barely even needs the AI. All it needs to do is spray and pray.  -Replayability. Personally, I only come back to this game every year or so. It doesn't drag me back often like GTA 3 did or how MGSV could convince me to try to be a total ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ one more time. I suppose it wasn't meant to do that though.  ------------------------------TLDR------------------------------ A solid, 9.5/10 rating from me. Play this game at midnight with noise cancelling headphones. It's the goriest rave you'll ever go to.

In my opinion this game is definately not ready for release as a finished game.  It has far too many issues to just accept that it has teething troubles.   I accept that the developers are continuously dealing with problems as they are raised but to me it should not have been released as anything other than a beta with all the outstanding issues.  The actual concept is great and if the developers do eventually ever deliver a product which matches the rhetoric, I believe it will be a great game however as it currently stands, it is impossible for me to recommend it.  Only the extremely patient should even consider buying this game.  You will have to be patient waiting for the developers to deliver the things that were said to be features that made this game special, such as smarter AI rather than just bonused AI.  Some bonuses, such as AI seeing entire map (ie.where best to colonize, harvest resources and all movements of competitors) are ridiculous!  Unfortunately you will also have to be patient in regard to not having adequate sorting or filtering tools.  What this means in practise is that if you play on a large map, when it gets to late game stage, when you have several hundred colonies, shipyards and ships, it can easily take over an hour to complete one turn because you have to scroll thru so many ships to find the ones you want to build.  An inadequate guide is also frustrating. Try using the guide to find out which are the buildings you can only build one of! The quickest way to find out is by trial and error!   To summarise I would advise anyone considering the game to view the forums and wait until they see that most of the issues are dealt with before they make the purchase.

I've spent over 50 hours playing receiver and I still have mixed feelings about it. The reloading mechanics are fantastic and I hope to one day see the same mechanics in a more fleshed out game. Sadly past the reloading mechanics receiver is quite a shallow game. You only ever come across 2 types of enemy; turrets and drones, neither are practically fun to fight. There is only a small number of possible rooms so after you've played for a few hours the level design becomes very repetitive. The only objective is to find the tapes which can either be very tedious or ridiculously easy depending on the random level generator. Most of receivers problems come from the fact the bulk of the game was made in 7 days with only one real update after it's release, It's just a shame no more content was added afterwards.   I hope one day a game developer will notice Receiver and make a properly fleshed out game built around It's gun mechanics. but until that game comes out I'll just have to keep playing reciever and you will.

The game is cute, if limited in scope. The story of the game itself is necessarily short due to the fact that it is 'emergent' but ultimately I don't think I like having 100 different choices over 10 different choices in a more gripping, original story. The story is no more complex than a kids' cartoon, you and some other people are stranded on a desert island and have to survive. It feels more like a really nice looking and sounding tech demo.   Easily worth the amount I paid for it in the Humble Bundle but I don't think I would buy it at full price. The decisions you make are incredibly arbitrary, much like a typical Choose Your Own Adventure book where what seems like the right choice will suddenly lead to tragedy for no reason. With choices like that, it's more like trial and error to get the best possible ending, as you will likely not get it on your first try because it's just so random.  While I am sure a ton of work went into the game, it's really more like a mildly interesting diversion than something you would call incredible.

If you've played those this ones camera issues aren't as bad in comparison.

Also needs more different enemy types since you don't see 7 out of the 9 different types that are already in the game that often, pretty sure they can get some ideas from the warhammer fantasy units and put them into the game? When the game gets up and running i'd love to see an ork or chaos DLC or even ork and chaos heroes.

Very unsure about this product when i first saw it. I mean there must be huge down sides to a game engine that is drag and drop, right?  Wrong, its fantastic. My heart will always lie with programming your own games, but THIS is amazing for making smaller 2d games, even prototyping ideas with rapid iteration time.  You literallty game make a whole game in an hour with this :)   Im sure it has limitations, but none i have found yet :)  //**** edit ****  I found some issues:  - The standard version (not developer) when used with the Android exporter has some limitations, mainly to do with monetization.  - To use Google Admob advertising you HAVE to have the developer version (this is not clear on the product comparison page on their website)  - You CANNOT provide ANY in app purchase functiionality without the developer version.  I have seen other people ask how to use admob only to be told they need the dev version. Its just not clear enough when buying the Standard edition that those things arent included.  The comparison between versions is ONLY available on the Developer version page. It should be on the standard page as well, because the people buying standard need to know what they are NOT getting, just as much as the people buying the developer edition need to know what they ARE getting.

The basic story's very simple but it's interesting enough to keep your attention as you keep playing the game. There are some parts that take a lot of hard work to complete but what game doesn't have that? It would get boring pretty fast if you didn't work at it! I feel this is a game that's clearly meant to be played for fun, and it does this job well!  Plus, drawing stick figures of anything you can think of is always awesome.  Highly recommended for those with an art background (casual or pro, doesn't matter.) Also recommended for those who only know how to draw stick figures and/or don't mind just having fun.

This is a must-buy for anyone who is into action roguelike games and doesn't mind or likes trashy humor.  The game is crazy action-packed fun. You're Mark Skid and your goal is to get to a concert. On your way you'll pick up hitchhikers, gear them up with weapons, upgrade your car and kill absolutely everything and everyone that gets in your way.  You can unlock a lot of different cars, each being different than the other. Some give passive bonuses/penalties, some are equipped with saws or even with a freaking rocket launcher.  You can equip your hitchhikers with melee or ranged weapons which you can buy in shops. In those shops you can also buy food to heal your characters, upgrades to your car, drugs to boost stats of your characters, repair your vehicle, all kinds of special items and of course tickets to the concert!  There are also all kind of mini-games that you can enter during your trip. There's a russian roulette mini-game where if your selected character loses he dies but if he wins his stats get boosted greatly. There's also a slot machine if you feel like gambling, workshop where you can upgrade your weapons and other stuff.  There's a decent number of bosses that you can encounter and they get randomly selected for each run. In each run you will face a total of 5 bosses, including the final one but they'll always be different (even final bosses change each time). They all of course have their own attacks and patterns that you'll have to learn if you want to beat them.  The game is pretty challenging but fun and addictive. The RNG can help or screw you over at times like in any game from this genre and I have to admit that when I started playing I thought it's unfair and impossible to win. But as I continued to play I realized that's not the case. Once you get skilled enough at the game and know how everything works you can win almost every run.

Dead Rising is a classic standing shoulder to shoulder with the likes of Left 4 Dead and Resident Evil.  Unlike the Zombie fodder that comes out nowadays, Dead Rising 1 is more than just another crowded room full of braindead trash mobs that you spam X on for five minutes before getting bored, it doesnt spoon feed you or treat you like a dribbling idiot either.   The game is challenging and fun to learn, the timer makes the zombies a formidable obstacle, forcing you to plan the safest and quickest routes with whatever weapons and health you can find along the way. As the timer ticks down and the player gets stronger and more knowledgeable, the game switches things up with stronger zombies, psychos and new enemy types. The story unravels at a perfect pace, filled with memorable characters, secrets, great music and nail biting tension.   Overall i think the game is a must own for people who like survival games.

This game was fun for its time, but it hasn't aged well. It's a game full of cliches that comes with having Czech developers write a New York (or Empire Bay) mafia story. For starters, the timelines for the war are very loopy. Vito goes to Sicily in 1943 and then they say 'a few years later, I got shot.' 1947 would have been after the war ended. They then backtrack on that and say he got shot 'three years' after his drop in Sicily (1946, again after the war ended). Then it says 1945 when the story begins. They really can't keep to the story.  But it's really one cliche after another. All the mobsters are New York wise guys with trademark New York accents. The story is about Vito's rise in the Empire Bay mafia scene. You can travel around Empire Bay (pretty much 1940s NYC) in between missions and do some side quests, but the story is pretty linear and it forces you to go in a singular direction. I played this on the XBox 360 a while ago and I forget if the story forces you to reset (lose all of your weapons/cars/etc.) twice, or if that was Godfather II: The Game. Either way, it just kind of slogs along.  It is kind of fun just cruising around in Empire Bay in old-time cars that handle like giant refrigerators and listening to old-timey music on the radio with period advertisments that say smoking is good for you. It's not worth full price, even if it is $20, so just wait for it to go on sale and if you've never played it before and like a decent mafia story to burn some time, give it a play.

Despite the 'Not Recommended' Rating, this is not a bad game.   It's just the kind of game that you either get, or you don't.  Some people will go into it and get lost in the narritive, the music, the sights and little extra pieces of handiwork that show that the developers put love and effort into it. They will connect with the game, and may even feel emotional when it ends.  Others, like myself, will groan 'why can't I go faster' after you've held down the W button for 30 minutes and listened to a bunch of well-meaned but at front nonsense that the narrator spouts. They will feel absolutely nothing when it ends, and feel puzzled at any attempt to make sense of the experience.   This is not a bad game. If you want to know whether you'd like it, I'd suggest watching 5-10 minutes of a letsplay. Do not buy this game just because people are raving about it; look into it and make sure this is something you 'get'

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Dark Souls is a challenging experience set in what has been described as a 'heavy metal fantasy world' by some. And they couldn't be any more correct. Take one look at monsters like the Gaping Dragon and you can tell that this isn't some candy-♥♥♥ elf paradise. You come into Lordran with those pansy-♥♥♥ ears and those pathetic noodles you call arms and you'll get ripped into pieces faster than you can cry for mercy to your pathetic tree god. Seriously, ♥♥♥♥ elves.  But I'm getting side-tracked. Dark Souls is a unique experience, in that it's amazing storyline and stage progression is presented with no dialogue whatsoever. The only real hints you get is that some whining little ♥♥♥♥ tells you to ring a bell, and a snake thing with a mustache made of flesh tells you to kill more bosses. For starters, you're going to want to learn that sorcery is for ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Even though there's no difficulty setting available in Dark Souls, if it was based on classes, then the Sorcerer is easy mode. All you do is stand at a distance and click on things with your ♥♥♥♥♥♥ magic walking stick. There's no challenge to it whatsoever, and it isn't even fun. I couldn't get past the second boss without giving up on the character out of sheer boredom. But magic is extremely powerful and can do devestating damage from a distance without risking getting a blade in your skull. Now, that's all fair and good if you're looking to be casul scum, but if you want to be a man and git gud you'll get yourself a sword or polearm. I reccommend the Halberd. ♥♥♥♥'s amazing. But if you must go for the less hack-and-slash type of playstyle, then Pyromancy or Miracles are a good choice. Pyromancy is useful as a secondary damage source with the benefit of not scaling it's damage with any stat, so as long as you keep upgrading your Pyromancy Flame, you won't have to worry about levelling your Intelligence like some big gaping ♥♥♥♥♥♥. And Miracles are always a respectable path to take. You can heal yourself or give yourself a magic barrier, which helps to deal with magic-using ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, and the Lightning Spear is always fun to use, especially since Solaire uses it, and you using it alongside him will only deepen your strength as sunbros. Just don't pick Sorcery. I cannot stress this enough, it's cowardice incarnate. You'll enjoy the game so much more once you've figured out what you're doing and you've remembered to stay away from anything that has to do with sorcery. Except the Crimson set. But that's only if you have no curse protection.  Overall, this has been one of the most unique, frustrating, and entertaining gaming experiences I've ever had. I haven't had this much fun with a game since when I first played Fallout 3.  9.5/10 Just enough magic casuls to give it an imperfect score.

Hotline Miami appears to be a suprisingly simple 2D game in terms of its gameplay, but don't be fooled so easily. This action packed and vibrant top-down shooter brings a wonderful combination of music, gore, and retroactivity that gives the game an unparalleled design.   The combinations and tactics for combat can vary for each level, from being the stealthy melee takedown ninja or to the rambo-like gunblazer. Players will have to plan out their paths of murder and hope to get a lucky run, often with great satisfaction upon completion. You can also select different animal masks that have different abilities that are very useful for different playstyles.  If you enjoy quick-paced top-down action with a lot of gore, get Hotline Miami!

Requires enough skill to scratch the gaming itch, but not as much time and effort as larger titles. The physics is grand, the tables all have a distinct personality, and the gameplay is incredibly entertaining.

There have been much better on-rails action games (e.g. the Uncharted series), but the world, story, graphics, and atmosphere all make this worth recommending if it's on sale. Score: 7/10.  Positives: + The graphics are great and the world of Neo-Paris is beautifully realized. + An interesting storyline that kept me engaged for the 12 hour runtime. + Good sound design and music. The way the music reacts to your fights is a nice feature. + Combat is imperfect but largely satisfying and the boss battles are all really cool and well-staged. + Memory remixing (Inception-like changing of people’s memories) is an original and well-implemented gameplay mechanic, but a little underused. + Frequent save points and a dynamic and immersive HUD that only shows when useful.  Negatives: - Extremely linear and on rails, to the extent that there are glowing markers telling you which ledge to grab or pipe to climb; this is a very closed-off world with nothing to explore or interact with. - Although the story is good, the dialogue and voice acting are both underwhelming. - The controls during climbing/platforming are a little fidgety. - The camera is passable, but I’ve seen much better in other games. - Some unnecessary shortcuts were made, such as having the TV screens showing still images instead of videos.

app_name: Eador. Masters of the Broken World
review_text: It's hard to play a game over 100 hours and then not recommend it. And yet, I can't. I wan't to - but I can't.  You've heard the saying that 'the sum is greater than the parts'; in this game, the parts are greater than the sum. There is such a good game here waiting to 'happen'. Unfortunately, the overall game design is flawed.  Specifically, the size of the 'shards' and the mechanics of the shards hurt the overall game. Because of this, you won't even use most of the best units/buildings/etc. In essence, you've got all this outstanding content that never comes into play bacause of the mechanics of the 'meta-game'.

NPC's are boring and flat, world design is drab (there's no color like there was in the originals), and for some reason they tried to pull like a reverse borrow by giving Garrett supernatural powers similar to Corvo's and a plague. Also Garrett is now some kind antihero and lacks any of the character of Stephen Russell's version. I want to compare the level design to the Dark Project, but thats an insult even to that game, it's just so linear and boring and I can't focus on any of it for more than 5 minutes. There's no keepers no pagans no Hammerites, there's nothing here but tiny sneaky boring thief man.

I like this game a lot. There is a wide variety of game modes that include coop, pvp, and invasions. The first two are self-explanatory but the invasions are similar to dark souls. You can invade another persons single player game and take control of one of the AI wizards. You can also volunteer to become an ally instead. Keep in mind you can disable this completely if you want to play without interruptions.  You control wizard(s) and cast spells in a turn-based format. The objective is to kill the enemy wizards. The gameplay is simple and well explained in the expansive tutorial; however, the point of contention is the RNG or luck. Everyone has 1 hit point and your spells have percentage chances to cast. This makes the game feel like a mix between Chess and Poker. The movements of the wizard and casted minions are strategic, but you must manage your RNG appropriately. You need to make the right decisions and try your best to account for mistakes. 1 HP means that any attack landing will be a death.  This is a remake to the classic game Chaos and it lives up to its namesake. You need to manage your luck, not control it and take your losses on the chin. There is a ton of replayability, between realms and the various pvp. The bar for entry is very low and not intimidating, so it's worth giving it a shot if you enjoy turn-based games heavily wrapped in luck. RNGesus take the wheel!

We Knights of the Round now. The voice work is pretty damn good, the VO Director was Kris Zimmerman which means you'll be hearing Colonel speaking sass in Liquid's ear, you'll be protecting Naomi Hunter from the forces of evil within her (foreshadowing too many years in the making? I highly doubt that this game requires any further discussion in regards to the gameplay and appeal--instead, this game has a contrasting value of what it means to have, not what it means to play and by means in particular to Steam community.   'Now I am become death, the destroyer of worlds.' --Julius Robert Oppenheimer, quoting Bhagavad Gita  The game itself is a mere part of the Steam store which is already saturated by far more playable and valuable games that people are actually appreciating to have downloaded, played and reviewed on it. At the very bottom of this meticulous spire of gaming industry within Steam, Bad Rats is at the lowest part of the chain, including both in quality and price. What is interesting, however, is its comparison from others within the same pit of fate, such as Def Gravity, Gearcrak Arena, etc. Curiously enough, one has comprised of reviews that mark its incompleteness, though it does argue that game didn't put much effort to expand beyond its generic concept. The other one was known for cheapest game in the Steam store, marked mainly as 'made to be cheap.' Bad Rats on the other hand is in stuck between--its crude quality and blunt effort made into the game keeps this game's value in between being poorly made and best at its value--It's just absolutely BAD. Is there a mystery to it? To answer your question, assuming your eyes aren't sore already after my first sentence, this game is a monumental symbol of where it is at--to take this seriously is up to how you perceive my opinion below.   Bad Rats is arguably one of those memetic infectious existence being in our game library. Aside from that guy who bought the Bad Rats first before others, we simply didn't buy the game because we bought it for what games usually are consumed as--for fun. Probably the main reason why it sits at your game library is because:  1. You heard about it and you want to know about it.  2. Your friend gifted it to openly express that your friend hates you.  3. You spent all your money on good game and the left over in your Steam Wallet can actually afford Bad Rats.  4. Other reasons. (I don't know--fan of rats? fan of murdering cats in most sinister rendition?)  After a careful thought, I have come to a conclusion that Bad Rats, if not already explicitly clear, represents the Plague of our realms in the gaming history. It is no easy matter that the video game history has reflected an interesting parallel reflection of what human race has endured. The quality control ever since the crash of video game market and grim turns of gaming industry has taken in nowadays is strangely similar. Where have these games sprouted in the Steam store and how they managed to root into our game library? For some reason, it is eerie to actually know why this game was made to exist in reality. However, one can assume that this is the creation by the human being and worst part of the reality is that anyone can make such cancerous being in the world--even that 5-year-old Tommy in your neighbourhood.  Let's trace ourselves back to the grim time of Plague, where people simply died without explanation but only the sign of death. most historians would agree that the main reason why Plague deeply damaged the human population was due to lack of understanding how it was transferred and how to maintain hygiene to prevent the spread. Such mysterious yet deadly disease brought a great panic across the commonwealth, causing confusions and ultimately the fear of what it could do to a human being. It wasn't exactly the issue of death because death was inevitable--the only difference was to die happily or cursed. When the demonic black-red rings of death appeared on one's body, you could had only assume that the person was the sinner and has received its terminal punishment. In result the person would be damned by his friends, family and even the doctors and priests garnered in infamous crow masks.  In our game library, thing is little different than back in the middle age--our game library has been under control in lieu of our judgements, sometimes making foolish mistakes that we would regret. However, Steam store brought a prosperity to literally anyone's game library, even if you had no money to spend on any games. With its constant flow of new and old games easily discounted, our game library can easily grow into bloating collection of games yet to be installed or even be recognized. This easily develop-able habit of buying games on discounts, particularly in Steam sales, symbolizes the hardly-controllable growth of infectiously bad games. the numbers slashing across games' presenting values soften our grip on our wallets and easily be fooled into buying the game because it is on sale--such same devious illusion shapes poorly-developed early access games and falsely satisfying your desire of getting ahead of the hastely-expanding (or seemingly so) video game market via pre-orders. Our senses are dulling and our judgements are loosen. It would be no big surprise that we're heading toward the second great video game market crash. Some may see this as hollow as the Mayan's 2012 Apocalypse while some take it as seriously as whatever the guy with big fat white beard said.   Bad Rats in such sense symbolizes our rustic taste of video games nowadays, taking the spoon into the depth of excrete flakes floating on the soup of urine. It is a fair warning that the developer of this game must had in mind, to become the epidemic death crawling within our steam library. I dare say that it is a volatile revolution against the undesirable direction that the video game industry had been indulging out of false satisfaction of gamers. (Press X to Continue) The gameplay and the setting was mere way to present it as the game because what the game symbolizes does not require a weight to its presence--its true value, if not sole purpose, is to nest within our Steam library along with many other games that we have bought and carelessly thought if we were really going to play back in the time we bought it.   The overture of pandemic has just started--and what's really scary thing is that we're snickering and pointing fingers at the upcoming horror against what we love to play. When the chain on the bellowing beast's neck be broken? It is truly depressing statement to say that no one really knows where this is going. However, it is crystal clear that the Bad Rats catalysed the march of bad games once again after its predecessors.

Don't mind the hours, I have 230% what is shows here, thanks to horizons.  What people don't understand, is that this game isn't for everyone. If you're expecting to get 24/7 action, and mercy, you're in the wrong place. Let me tell you about the game itself, and how it might play out for you.  TL;DR: This game is only as good as the people who play it, help them become better men. It is made to be played together. Go watch streams and sympathise, teach and get taught, Don't be afraid of others, and be nice to newcommers.  I started the game on my own. In my little Sidewinder, I couldn't go far, or do much, but I felt that this game had potential. I did missions, explored my surroundings, untill I could get another ship, and repeated the process to get my first good ♥♥♥♥: the Cobra. After that I really started to enjoy the game. I could go far, and do a lot. I started to fight, and soon after I bought an ASP. With my ASP I fought, traded, smuggled, explored... But it got quite boring, so I stopped playing for a few months. When I came back, I deleted my character and made a new one. Then I really started to see the game as a new experience, and now that I knew what to do I made it to the ASP much quicker, but doing the same things as before became a lot more interesting, and you know why? Because I started to play with my friends. I taught some of them how to play. I learned a lot from others. And still today my eyes shine when I see them in their brand new ship, be it a Cobra, a Pyrhon, or a Corvette. I won't go into detail too much, but what makes games good are their communities, and this gale has one of the best. It has flaws, but it has enough to compensate.

Mushihimesama is a japanese shoot'em up where you have to kill insects ( mushi means insect ) and dodge tons of missiles. If you read recommandations, it might means that you are hesitant to buy these kind of games because they are usually really hard if you want to play correctly. Honestly, as a casual shoot'em up player, i really enjoy this game. The different difficulties permits you to enjoy the game and progress step by step.   Pros: Good graphics for a shmup - Graduable difficulties - You can play with a controller ( i use the Xbox 360 controller and it works well) - Customizable game : if you prefer vertical or horizontal gameplay - Infinite continues , for discovering the game and permits you to play at your level   Cons : The price  - Only one character, you have 3 differents shots that you can choose at the beginning, but others character instead would be nice ( my opinion)  So if you want to discover these kinds of game, but are afraid about this 'elite' genre, i'd recommand you Mushihimesama. If you are an advanced gamer, Mushihimesama is still good in many ways, there is many challenges ( no continues , have fun for the true last boss ! ;) )

Firewatch, thanks for these 5 hours of gaming, that I shall never forget.

I love puzzle games, but this one's not for everyone.  After the greatly enjoyable easy and medium levels, you're graced with the irritating hard mode difficulty. I felt that solving a lot of the problems didn't involve skill but continuous trial and error. It ended up overstaying its visit and without tools like slowing down, pausing, or many ways to see what went wrong. In the end, it means you're shuffling tiles, clicking the go button, and then trying agian.  But that's not where my big complaint is. It has a 'Beat the level for me' button. If you're stuck, press the hint button. Go on, there's no repurcussion. In fact, click it as many times as you want. It'll solve the level for you with enough clicks. This might sound like a good thing, but it makes solving a puzzle so unsatisfying. You work for an answer that you can get the same results from just letting the game tell you were to put it all. And (outside of achievements) There's 0 drawbacks. In a game that gives you few tools to diagnose your problem, it puts a bandaid on a bullet wound by solving the problem for you.  My last complaint is that it's just so heartless. Until you get to the glados levels (which are the best part of the game that remove the F***it button and add some flavor to the game) You're playing a game about cubes. Emotionless, boring, cop-out cubes. Minimalistic? Yes. But like modern art, if I have no reason to be interested in these cubes, they're just cubes. I'm just solving puzzles and not engaged with anything put on the screen. It's a very subjective opinion, though. If you're here for puzzles more than engagement, you'll probably like it.  All in all, it's a great game that spikes in difficulty a little too quick and with a few too many tools to help you figure out problems. If you enjoy games that require 1 answer, don't mind cubes, you'll probably enjoy this game. Otherwise you may want to look elsewhere.

I love the artwork, the story, the music, everything about it, but I find myself rage-quitting after trying to solve the same puzzle over and over and over again with perfectly-timed double- and triple-jumps, using air-dashes and targetted dashes... Forget playing with the keyboard and mouse. Even though it's more precise than a controller pad, it's just too complex. I should note, I'm playing on Easy difficulty. God help me if I try playing any other difficulty! I've had to restart after getting myself stuck in areas I was unprepared to play, as I didn't have enough energy orbs to open doors that were absolutely necessary. All in all, I have to give this game two thumbs up for making me cry in the opening cinematic.

I tend to go with a rather simple list of criterias when I rate artistic media. It usually revolves around the likes of  'Did this media make me feel satistfied?  Did it make me laugh until attaining stomach ache?  Cry somber, truly heartfelt tears?  Fear from the depths of my soul?  Rage, with good reasoning?  Did it leave me with a feeling of newfound revelation, and philosophical pondering?  Did leave me staring blankly at the media for a long amount of time after said media was, per se, over?' Usually, I am able to tick off a few of these, and that usually already leaves a pretty acceptable media.  Undertale, however, left me questionning my standards for these criteria.

Helldivers is a top-down twin stick shooter from developer Arrowhead, on the surface this description is underwhelming but through a combination of diverse enemies and addictive gameplay it has the potential to be the very best in its genre.   On starting the game you are greeted with a slick recruitment video giving you all the motivation you need to drop into enemy territory. You are a Helldiver and that means making the safety of Super Earth your personal responsibility, using a huge range of weapons you will spread “managed democracy” across the galaxy one bullet (or laser or toxic goo or missile or fire etc…) at a time. It takes a very satirical approach to the messaging reminiscent of 1998 masterpiece Starship Troopers.   First up is the graphics, they are not genre defining and by no means the selling point of the game but Helldivers is pretty to look at. By utilising vivid colors and an almost cartoon quality Helldivers is easy on the eye, throughout my time with Helldivers I experienced no graphical or frame rate issues. Most impressive are the weapon effects, each weapon handles very differently and you can plainly see the time and effort that has gone in to the various animations. Bullets from a LMG spray across the screen with smoking shells flying and laser guns overheat with smoke rising from the weapon, it is this attention to detail that really gives this game an edge. The only weapon that left me slightly disappointed was the flamethrower, the first spews out in a steady stream rather than the sustained wide destruction you would want from such a weapon especially as the effects on all other weapons are done so well. Sound next, whilst lacking in variety the score is a perfect representation of how quickly things can turn bad for a Helldiver. As you land on the planet and are exploring you will be treated with a quiet mellow tune, this will quickly change when you are greeted with waves of enemies. There is nothing like standing back to back with your squad awaiting extraction as dramatic music reminiscent of final battles in big budget Hollywood movies plays, again it is these moments that set Helldivers apart from other top down action games.   Now the gameplay, this is where Helldivers really shines and this is what will keep you coming back to play it again and again. The premise is simple, you with up to three others drop on an enemy planet and you are given a series of objectives that you can complete or fail. Failing a single objective does not fail mission, you will still get the chance to extract but will be penalised when it comes to receiving XP. Helldivers is simple on paper but I cannot stress enough, this game is addictive as hell! I can’t remember the last time a game completely consumed me and I have not been able to put it down since purchasing it.   You will face three different alien races as a Helldiver. The Illuminate are an advanced bunch, capable of futurist feats and able to kill you in a single blow with a shot from one of their laser cannons. Next are the Cyborgs, these guys are heavily armed and to face them you should be to, unless you want to end up as roadkill underneath one of their hulking tanks. Finally, my personal favourite, the Bugs. These come in all shapes and sizes and can prove quite deadly to an inexperienced squad.   How you tackle these three enemies differs massively, having a single load out will simply not cut it. Take the time in the lower difficulty zones to see how the enemy operates and find their weaknesses. This should adequately prepare you for what is to come but you can never be sure and believe me when I say everything in Helldivers is out to kill you but more on that later.   There is simply so much to this game that completionists will easily be able to squeeze 100 hours of gameplay from Helldivers. You collect research points which allow you to upgrade weapons or stratagems, you get these by either collecting samples found in the map and by levelling up your character. When you have collected enough you are presented with so many options it can be a tough choice. Do you upgrade your machine gun to hold more rounds? Do you upgrade your APC to give more protection to your team? The list goes on but it is important to say that all of the upgrades have meaning and will change the way you play, nothing feels tacked on for the sake it.  It is a good idea to make your character strong as mentioned previously everything in Helldivers is out to kill you. Not only is friendly fire always on but the pods your teammates arrive in, defence turrets, mines or vehicles will instantly kill you if you are not careful. Even trying to play the game defensively can quickly end badly. Death for a single Helldiver is not the end of the mission however, as long as one teammate is alive (and the stratagem isn’t on cooldown) your dead teammates can be dropped back into the action…just be sure to not stand underneath them when they do.  Helldivers does a very good job of making you feel part of an army with the galactic war set up. The war is fought on three sides representing the three different alien races, as you complete missions and secure planets in a particular zone you will eventually reach the alien home world. This will start a timed event calling all Helldivers to focus their efforts to defeat the enemy. Alternatively if there are not enough Helldivers fighting the alien scum the war can reach Super Earth, much like reaching a home world all Helldivers will be called to defend their home.   Besides the small selection of music and underwhelming graphics the one major downside to this game is not capitalising on its humorous tone and Starship Trooper inspiration. Whilst your character will occasionally shout hilarious comments as they fight the enemy this is all we see, I would have loved to see more cutscenes and perhaps be introduced to interesting characters within the universe.   Helldivers gets a score of 90/100. Besides its lack of story content, uninspired visuals and relatively small selection of music the gameplay easily makes you forget all of that. In fact without those minor issues Helldivers would be a perfect game. Helldivers provides some of the best white knuckle co-op action I have ever played and there is no better feeling than making it off a planet swarming with enemies with your whole squad. This game is definitely worth a full purchase as you will want to keep coming back…and bring friends, it gets dangerous out there!

Very fun, well put together game. Plenty of stuff to do in the vanilla game, with hundreds of mods that range from unit skins and textures to entirely new maps and stories. The vanilla version has hours upon hours of playability, and high replayability as well. You throw in mods, and you get even more hours out of it.   You create a hero and embark on a journey to Calradia, the main continent in the game. From there, you begin a tutorial quest (which is optional) and from there, the world is your oyster. Do you become a noble hero, fighting off bandits and war criminals and hunting down wrong doers? Or do you become a cutthroat bandit, raiding villages and peasants? Will you become a lord in service to one of the many kings? Or will you set out to become king yourself? The possibilities are endless, only limited by your imagination.  Combat in my opinion is smooth and works well, and there are a multitude of mods to change it should you not like it. Graphics are decent, and again there are many texture, model, skin, animation, and map mods available. The world is large enough to be interesting to explore and take time to travel, but small enough that you won't get lost and it doesn't take too long to get from point A to point B.   There is multiplayer, and although it is not what I wish it was (a bordelands-ish invite friends to your world) it is very fun, as you engage in battle and siege mode and lead soldiers with and against other players. There is also MP dlc, which places you in the era of muskets and cannons for multiplayer battles. Very fun when you are in the mood for it.  Here you have a great game with many hours of gameplay and high replayability, with heavy mod support and a multiplayer feature that works well. All in all, I'd give it an 8/10 personally, and would highly reccomend it.

This review is not for the current build, as I haven't played the game in some time. When I played this I broadcasted a hardcore play through, with the goal of defeating every boss in the game without dying once or having played the game prior to the challenge. I looked nothing up, no walkthroughs or tips / spoilers, and it was one of the best times I've had in any game I've played. I lost a few hardcore characters (some 40 hours, another 60 hour character), before finishing the challenge. I love the simplicity on the surface of the game, yet the deeper you dig beneath the surface (get the pun?) the more in-depth and challenging it gets. Building traps and shelter, hiding out during the night cycle, and making a hellivator are just a few of my beloved memories of this game. So many games have tried to emulate Terraria, but there is only one, and to this day I stand by saying it is still the best of its kind.   I give Terraria 10/10 pants
