Probably one of the best games I've ever played. The story was chilling and epic, and was accompanied by beautiful cinematics and music. It's one of the few games that really get's you inside of it's grasp. The graphics were made even more lifelike in the PC port (with directX features that were not available on the xbox). However, combat was, to put it frankly, pretty awful. Slowly, painfully, you burn out the darkness in each enemy before shooting them several times with a revolver. It's a big step down from your traditional shooter. Despite this, the amazing story keeps you pushing for the end, which turns out to be just as crypic as, well, most of the other stuff in the game.

When there is a 2010's 'Indie Game Hall of Fame', The Binding of Issac will be one of its first inductees. Ed McMillen and Florian Himsl's top down rougelike dungeon crawler is a zebra in a field of wild horses. Its themes are what videogames and films try to avoid like the bubonic plague. In fact, I wouldn't be suprised if one of the bosses in this game has the bubonic plague (I'm looking at you, Gurdy). Infanticide, abortion, child abuse, and the idea that religion brainwashes would have some marketing departments cleaning out their desks. But this beautful departure isn't where The Binding of Issac shines. The gameplay is solid gold. The Legend of Zelda is a huge influence, and the game touts it well. Replayabilty is off the charts thanks to randomized levels, items, and boss fights. The pacing of difficulty and item unlocks as you progess feels reasonable. The soundtrack composed by Danny Baranowsky provides an added dimension into the hell that is Issac's basement, and its a damn good one. The community surrounding this game is extremely dedicated, constantly creating lore for a game that leaves a lot of blanks. The 'Wrath of the Lamb DLC' (2012), and recently added 'Eternal Edition' (2015) added substantial content to the game. Do yourself a favor and play this game until your eyes bleed (I hear toothpicks for your eyelids will help).

One of the best games I have ever played. This game is one of those rare indie gems that pops up out of nowhere. Where do I even begin...  If you still haven't picked this game up, stop what you're doing, go grab 5 to 7 bucks from someone, and do yourself a huge favor and go buy this game.  The gameplay is quick and fun. Easy to pick up, yet hard to master. The game will test your reflexes, quick decision making and your patience. The randomly generated rooms, monsters, and pick ups makes sure that every run is unique. Speaking of monsters, there's a wide variety of them to discover on your trip through the basement, which increases even more if you buy the Wrath Of The Lambs DLC along with it, which I also recommend( but be warned, it makes the game tougher than it already is). With 100's of items to collect, a multitude of endings, characters to unlock, and challenges to beat, this game has the ability to keep you playing for hours, soon enough you'll be racking up 100+ hours. The replayability is very high on this title, and is definately the best game coding using flash.

Payday 2 is not fit for the people with a budget of 10 (or less) dollars. The game is decent for some time but after that you notice that more than half of the game is in dlcs which you need to pay for and it costs alot if you are considering purchasing more than 1 dlc  which is usualy the case. However, the game is pretty fun if you can spend those extra dollars for dlcs.  A few more things to consider: 1) A lot of unbalanced weapons, alot of weapons (from both dlc and default) are unbalanced to the point of being useless in higher end difficulties, and some might be even difficult to use on mid range difficulties. 2) The game contains bugs in the code and each update (extra dlc) they add more bugs into the code, expect more crashes in this game than your entire time playing games before hand. 3) First thing when trying the game is to check if alot of people from your area play the game, if not, refund. You might ask why refund?, to that I shall answer with one word 'desync' which translates to pain and suffering caused from lag and what you see on your screen will be different from the host's screen, some might argue that there are mods that can fix this, to that I shall say NO, these mods may work sometimes and sometimes not, its not guranteed. If you ever played cs go, imagine having 200 ping, do you have fun playing competitive with that ping in high ranks? I doubt that. 4) The game pays for itself with time with safes being dropped frequently ingame after completing a heist, so you might be able to afford some dlcs (after 200 hours) 5) Important to point out the fact that this games has a diverse community which is rare.  Overall mark, is it worth buying ? only when on sale, same with the dlcs, if its more than 5 dollars, don't.

At the start of this year, Fallout: New Vegas was hands-down the game I'd put more time into than any other, at least within the last ten years. I'd put more than four hundred hours into the Xbox version over the course of several playthroughs. After my time with the Xbox version, I would still hear the Mojave's siren call as I wandered the tundras of Skyrim, the streets of Columbia, the alleys of Dunwall, and many other fantastic places games took me. Recently, I returned to walk the wastes as The Courier. And according to Steam, I've put over 120 hours into my latest playthrough, all within a month's time.  Given all this, I feel comfortable saying that I'm crazy about this game. It isn't perfect. It can be frustrating. Even three years after its release, it's still a buggy mess at times. Nonetheless, I love this game.  My experiences aside: I'd encourage you to take a visit to the Mojave wastes. Maybe our paths will cross along the Lonesome Road in a land beset by war. And as we all know - war: war never changes.

Behind it's cute, warm humor and life lessons, Octodad will ignite a fury inside of you that can only be tamed by smashing your Sunday morning coffee cup against your monitor. The problem is that after you've broken anything and everything around you, you are so pumped up on adrenaline that you feel like you can take on anything life throws at you. So you jump back into Octodad, ready to make it your little ♥♥♥♥♥. And then the cycle repeats itself...with the addition of a few tears and mental breakdowns.  And in your darkest moments of gameplay, when all hope of completing the daunting tasks laid before you seems lost, Octodad's adorable little mustache grabs a hold of the remnants of your soul, and he embraces you with the cold, slimy arms of a man that's unwilling to give up the fight for his family. In that moment, you realize that this little sea creature needs you now more than ever. How could you possibly abandon him?  Now all of this may sound like I'm telling you to stay far, far away from this game, and in all honesty, if you value your sanity, you probably should avoid Octodad. But this is one of the most fun and memorable games I have played in a long time despite the pain and frustration. Sure, Octodad's blubs of *forced casualness under duress* haunt me in my sleep, but so does Jason Statham's glorious chest. And you don't see me complaining about either.

Cubemen 2 is a strategy game where you use several types of cubemen to defeat your opponent. The types of cubemen range from soldiers with pistols to flamethrowers to rocket launchers to sniper rifles to ones that turn into walls and land mines. To unlock these units during a game, you buy them with cubes which you get for defeating enemies. Each time you buy a new unit, the price for that unit goes up one cube. For example, if you buy a flamethrower cubeman for 20 cubes the price goes up to 21 and so on. There are a few different game modes like defense, skirmish, rescue, and capture the flag. The game also comes with a level editor so you can make new levels and upload them so other people can play and rate them. Because of this, you may be finding yourself finding lots of interesting levels that you can play other than the ones that come with the game. This game also has an awesome techno soundtrack.  I recommend this game, but I think $8 is a little too much for this game. If this game interests you, I would suggest waiting for it to go on sale. I wouldn't say you'd play this for hours straight, but it's a good game to get if you're looking for something new and interesting to keep you occupied for a few minutes.

This game is trash, let me tell you why.  There's an ingame cash store where you can buy any weapon or ammo you want at any time, completely removing the need to preserve items. Bodies disappear immediately after KO/kill, meaning that killing over silent takedowns is worthless as enemies cannot be woken up. Graphics are horrible, looking very much like a PS2 game; no graphic options other than resolution and AA, and all animations that aren't directly ripped from Human Revolution look stiff and janky. Levels are tiny and linear compared to the original Deus Ex, a game released  14 years ago.  No way to rebind keys, period. UI and menus are clearly designed for tablets, are incredibly clunky, and don't make proper use of the many keys a keyboard has. The game babies you to the point of  telling you how to walk before allowing you to do so.  The story isn't even half as interesting as either Deus Ex or Human Revolution... oh, and it's unfinished, telling you to wait for a second episode that is likely never coming.   This doesn't even cover the myriad of bugs that The Fall has, but merely the 'intended gameplay experience' that the people at Eidos thought everyone would eat up and love.  Don't buy this unless you are morbidly curious at what a bad Deus Ex game looks like.

This game is well worth the asking price.   A 'roguelike' game that is actually  Balanced!!!   Most devs think that's an oxymoron and use 'roguelike' as an excuse to be lazy and put out a sub-standard product, but not the dev of this game. You're totally free to blunder into areas and enemies way out of your league and get horribly murdered, as I just did, but you can expect  most  of the enemies in an area that are your level to be beat-able.  But even if you go to another starter area you might get swarmed and over-confidently die. It's tough and challenging but with the right tactics, equipment, power selection, and a little luck you can make it fairly far and play for a while. Then you'll open a sealed door with an unspeakable horror too powerful for you on the other side and die, but be so amused and enjoy it so much you pick another class and try again.

It's about exploring the once-inhabited-now-lifeless world you're on and that's the best part of it. Exploring the unknown. The graphics are really good as well as the sound. The gameplay is fine (could be a bit smoother in some cases but nothing serious) and it's as 'repetitive' as every other game. I'd recommend it to anyone who likes SciFi, either if it's literature, movies or games.

You'll be quick to notice the downsides of this game. The graphics, controls, and features are very limited. There is no multiplayer or achievements. The game functions all around as if you were playing it on a DOS OS. While it may be nostalgic for many, the rest will find it unnecessarily limiting. The game  could  be better by implementing modern capabilities of computers.  However, these limitations aside, it is a very solid and fun game. If you can look past these design flaws, there is a great game behind them. It left me wondering what this game would look like as a triple A title, or if it were designed for a SNES instead of DOS. There was great potential for this game, and what little it does encompass is amazing. If it is your type of game, and you can look past the deliberate retro nerfing, you will love it.

Pros:  Good fun in short bursts  Can be pretty damn scary at times  Fun to play as monster  Great atmosphere  Cons: No public party system (It is being worked on though)  Repetitve  Too unfair for survivors, they are easily found, and don't blend in well. The maps are filled with crows that give away your position too often and easily. The generators take way too long to start at least to me but I understand if it was fast, survivors would win instantly but still a nusence.   Fairly buggy but I'm sure they are being worked out   Overall it is great fun with friends for great scares and mindless enjoyment, but at $20 ($16 sale price), I just don't see that the content given is worth the price. This is a better version of Dead Realm but has the same problem that game did, there is not enough content/replay value to justify the price tag. As updates roll out, maps are added, new skills are added, monsters, etc. this would be worth the buy but at current state, it is not in my opinion.   Ultimately these kinds of games are down to preference so if you love horror this is great for you, if you look for content and replayabilty this is a hit and miss. Don't be afraid to try it out, if you don't like it refund it.

Old school 2D platform games have certainly been in the forefront in recent years. Games like Shovel Knight, Mighty No. 9, and Azure Striker Gunvolt are some great examples of how this seemingly “old school” type of gaming is more popular than ever. Trying to join in on the excitement is The Legend of Dark Witch. Witchcraft combined with a bit of customization allows this game to have a unique spot in this genre of games for the Nintendo 3DS.  Circle Entertainment is a fairly new developer and publisher located in China that was founded in 2006. The company takes pride in themselves for utilizing the latest technology while keeping pace with the videogame industry. In addition, Circle Entertainment boasts that the average age for the company is only 25. Some of Circle Entertainment’s recent games include Theological July, Bridal June, Knytt Underground, and Demon King Box.  Right off the bat, the storyline is not explicitly told. Players just start playing and eventually get snippets of what is going on in the world they’re playing in. Essentially, Syega Crystals, items that provide the power to all magical creatures in the world have gone missing. Your character, Zizou, a dark witch herself, must retrieve the missing Syega Crystals to bring balance to this mystical world. Unfortunately, the story isn’t anything new and is very cursory. Little to no depth of it is explored, and quite frankly, unentertaining.  However, what was entertaining was the presentation of the game. The simple, yet well crafted, graphics of the game makes it at the very least a nice game to look at. Each stage has its own theme (library, fighting arena, etc.), with specific enemies, hazards, and unique music that directly tie into the stage you are currently battling through. The first stage, for example, is the library. Some enemies you face include a librarian, falling books, and a one-eyed book who launches pages at you. The music is pretty casual with nothing fast-paced. I’ve heard that libraries were a place of terror, so I suppose casual music with attacking books sounds about right.  Since this is a ported game from Japan, there are voice actors who, logically, speak in Japanese. This is nothing new since it is very expensive to hire more voice actors. Consequently, the text that translates the banter back-and-forth works just fine.  Like any typical 2D platform game, the objective is to get from one side of the stage all the way to the other. Sounds easy, right? Well if it does, you can adjust the gameplay with three different difficulties - Easy, Normal, and Lunatic. In addition, players can decide how they want to “install” upgrades to their character - auto (game selects which attributes to boost), semi-auto, and manual (complete autonomy). These customizations of gameplay, right in the beginning, can offer unique experiences to each player, in addition to some nice replayability.  Players then use the A/X/B/Y buttons to either jump, attack, switch attacks, or utilize a special attack. At first, I was a little annoyed by the pre-designated button assignments, but quickly found out that you can customize it. The top screen offers the actual gameplay of your character, the world, and enemies. The bottom screen shows your health, lives, Tres (the currency you obtain from defeating enemies), and time. The presentation of the bottom screen was perfectly done - it’s a great layout that ensures that the top screen isn’t cluttered with an abundance of information. In addition, there is an electronic manual that you can access throughout the game - a handy thing in case you want some clarification for something on the screen.  Something that irked me about the gameplay is that you can just hold down the attack button and destroy any enemy without thought. The button-smashing-esque combat can get very old very quickly. If you like that type of gameplay, then this is fine, but for wants a little bit more thought in their fighting can find this terribly boring. There are also no opportunities to replenish your health. Ever. So you either have to die to get full health for the boss, or go through the level without taking damage….an obvious flaw and annoying concept for sure.  Unfortunately, I was disappointed by The Legend of Dark Witch. The 2D platform action genre has some innovative and creative games. I always want to give indie games a chance, but the lack of story, intrigue, and overall appeal with this game simply made it unintriguing. The customization is a nice touch, but ultimately cannot save the game from a poor overall performance. Overall 5/10.

This game is a modern horror classic. In addition to this, however, it is a very good adventure game. These two genres are blended together very well. Frictional Games has truly inspired game design. Many puzzles are clever and satisfying. This is one of the scariest games ever; sound and visual effects are implemented masterfully and instill undeniable dread in the player. That there is no combat further reinforces the player's vulnerability and encourages you to play intelligently to survive. Play it with friends for a fun time. If you like this game, check out Frictional's earlier games, the Penumbra series, which are games in much the same horror-adventure style.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight: Jedi Academy™
review_text: Even better than its predecessor (Jedi Outcast) and a little less challenging. But more customiazble, you start off by choosing what species you character can be, and your single lightsaber, (Blade colour and hilt) and start off. Enter Yavin 4 and the Jedi Academy, progressing through your first force trials and chatting to good ol' Luke Skywalker. You then get sent off to your frist set of 5 mission, (only 4 needed to be completed to pass through to the 1st story level but you can complete all 5) At the beginning of each mission, you can choose to level up a force power, both light and dark. It will be upgraded by 1 point, with a max of 3 points all over to get the ability to its full potential. Your character then gets more focused on the storyline, with a 'story mission' where you will go to a strong point of the Disciples of Ragnos. This happens all the way through the game. This game has some epic lightsaber battles later in the game, and also has a wide variety of weapons and force powers. This game and Jedi Outcast both have multiplayer with some great maps and games, such as capture the flag and free for all. Recommended to anybody looking for a cool adventure, and fans of star wars.   Worth its price.
A must buy.

Some of the reviews for this game (the negative ones) surprise me. This is a really wonderful game and one of my favorites of the 'last' generation.  It's a very simple premise. You are a sniper with the United States military and are tasked with going deep into the heart of Germany during WWII and sniping people. It's a very good looking game with robust sound and graphical effects with the kill cam being a highlight. It's from the third person perspective and I found the controls to be very responsive.  The game is not without its faults. A sniper is basically a stealth unit, killing people at long range. The fault with this game is that I find the AI's ability to locate you after one sniper shot (even if change positions) relatively easily. It can get 'annoying' (for lack of a better word) and it seems that the entire German army (and some Russians) are also crack shots. While this is a hindrance occasionally, it never took away the fun of the game for me.   The story itself is not very deep, but you're not playing this game for the depth of its story. You're playing it to snipe nazis from afar. I think of this game as a very good B-movie. Its not perfect, but you'll want to play it more often. Heck, even the DLC is fun!

This was amazingly fun with a full group of 4 people when I first started it. Very fun way to handle the tower defense genre. There is your standard mage with towers, but also interesting ideas like monk with damage/debuff bubbles, archer using traps for support type defense, and a tanky type knight using walls and ballista. You can increase either your obstacles' effectiveness, your actual hero effectiveness, or a mix of the two.  It got too difficult to keep up with much of the harder content without getting some amazing gear, which was only obtainable very briefly via some hardcore exploitation. By the time we got to that point in the content, there was no legit way to get the gear yourself without trading for exorbitant prices. Was fun while it lasted, and definitely worth some time just to get through the original/non-DLC part of the game to level up and try it out. Even more so if you've got a group of buddies.   Have not played in a long time so some of the negative aspects may already be addressed by now, but at this point I'm not likely to return.

Over 1100 hours played, in two years of owning this game, and I believe I have barely scratched the surface of this game. I have discovered many locations, have achieved high levels of gameplay (albeit a lot of that with installed mods), but I truly believe that a person could grow old playing this game, and not 'complete' it, as such. I am not very good at this review-writing, but the company behind the name, Bethesda, truly made a work of art here, and I do hope so much that, going forward, they can continue to update and otherwise add to this game, and not let it simply die out. With that being said, they are about to release Skyrim Remastered, or whatever the 'official' working title is, and I am chomping at the bit in anticipation of the release date, which I believe is the 28th of this month (October).  You go, Bethesda! Don't the haters stop you from continuing to provide quality entertainment.

Saints Row 2 was once upon a time one of my favorite console games. Infact I'd say it was the main reason I bought an xbox 360 and all the experiences I had I'll cherish for the rest of my life.  However, I will be the first to admit this game has not aged well by any stretch of the imagination. The animation work is simply amazingly bad, the voice acting is terrible and the modelling work is simplistic. However, the game world still features a significant amount of depth. With numerous collectables and secret areas that litter the landscape of Stilwater. The player character customization was only reduced in SR3 to put it into perspective.  The biggest problem with this game today is the PC port, which is today known as the worst PC port in the history of gaming. You would normally require an extensive knowlege with hex editors inorder to run the game natively. But thankfully the modding community has released 'Gentlemen of the Row' which fixes pretty much all the problems and even adds functionality to the game that didn't previously exist in the 360/PS3 release. If your at all curious about SR2 it's pretty much mandatory to run this mod.

I'm not a frequent reviewer here on Steam but I feel compelled to write my thoughts on what so far has been a very divisive game on PC. On one hand, the game itself is fantastically well written and is the culmination of 6 years of Batman games. The Arkham series has never before looked or played better; and Arkham Knight is the ultimate Batman gaming experience. Then, on the other hand, is the god-awful launch and myriad of technical problems that have plagued the PC version for months. Only recently in October did Warner Brothers finally put the game back up for sale on Steam and made the season pass content available to every PC player who bought it.  So, how is the game performing now? There's honestly no easy answer, some players, myself included, have encountered minimal technical issues in its current state while others are still reporting vast drops in framerate and slowdown.  Ultimately, whether or not to purchase Arkham Knight comes down to the individual. If you're a big fan of Batman and the Arkham games like I am, I'd say its worth the purchase, and if the game doesn't run as well as you'd like on your setup, there's always the Steam refund policy. When it comes down to it, Arkham Knight is still an amazing game and great sendoff for Rocksteady's Arkham trilogy, one of the best trilogies in modern gaming history.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Dark Souls has often led me to question my own sanity. Why do I continue playing a game that clearly takes such great pleasure in murdering me constantly? The answer is beacuse the sense of accomplishment you feel after conquering an area/boss that previously seemed impossible is immense. After reaching a high enough level (80+) the difficulty begins to level out, and the challenge subsides somewhat, allowing you to feel like a predator when once you were the prey. Enemies will still break off your kneecaps and beat you with them if you're not cautious, but the challenge slowly erodes as you eventually become one badass ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. There are drawbacks, such as a complete lack of story/cutscenes, an annoying checkpoint system, and some bosses that feel exceptionally cheap. Still, if you're into slamming your head into a brick wall until the puny brick crumbles underneath your magnificient cranial assault, then you've found the game for you.

Not recommended. The game had a lot of potential, but all the inherent issues I first found when playing the game are still present. First person isn't viable, warhammers are overpowered and kill people with one attack, daggers and shortswords in general are very imbalanced.   And then we get into the match making system, which doesn't exist. Its the classic pick a server and wait for it to fill. Sadly, it doesn't have team balancing at all. You can balance the teams for a fair fight yourselves, or steam roll your enemy with more numbers when a few people from the other team take their leave.  To be a good game, at the very least this needs forced team balancing. And then the way in which you accumulate expereince needs to be changed as well.  Overall not recommended, while its initially fun, once you start to notice the drawbacks and ♥♥♥♥ups that could've easily been fixed by now, you won't be willing to let it go. 5/10, won't buy any more of their products. They fail to listen to the community and fix their issues, like most other companies these days.

review_text: I had already played through this game on PS3, but it was so glitch ridden that i got frustrated while trying to go through most of the DLC and eventually gave the copy away. I tried a few of the fixes i found, run as admin, compatibility mode, changing some lines in fallout3.cfg and none of them worked. I just now stumbled across the fix, i use dual monitors (monitor and big picture on my TV) apparently that causes the game to crash upon startup, if you run it windowed mode it should work or disable the second monitor. Mine worked in windowed mode...hopefully now i can enjoy the already amazing game!

No I don't reccomend this game. Not in its current state. It's not fun after a short while. and the bugs become overwhelmingly frustrating. Not to mention the loot system is absolutely ♥♥♥♥ing horrific. Which is the only replayability it has.   Here's the short version: The game is bugs bugs bugs, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ mechanics and bad Ai, with more bugs on the side, on top of that it has become extremely laggy to the point the playerbase has dwindled as anything above hard becomes nearly impossible. This also makes half the bugs and crap mechanics even worse. Bad hitboxes even worse and rats attack range problem even worse. Since rats can easly damage you from 3 times the distance their weapon landed, during lag you will get struck even further when you think your opponent is staggered, in cata this is 30% of your health from the simple mobs. The developers knew this was an issue back in the beta and yet still haven't amended it.  I'll say this before all the whiney babbling:  You have Warframe, or you have this. What are their differences you ask?  Warframe is third person; this is first. Warframe is free; this costs £20 Warframe has 3000 missions; this has 12. Warframe has an endless supply of different enemy archtypes, each with a subtype: this has the same 8 types every mission. Warframe has blood gore, awesome combat: This has blood gore, awesome combat ruined by bugs, lag crap hitboxes and terrible mechanics. Warframe has 30 classes, new ones released often; this has 5. In Warframe you can choose how you setup your class from its weapons to its looks to all the addons and ability benifits. To the point you can choose between how long an ability lasts or how much damage it will deal: In this, you (don't) get new weapons. (The loot system is crap) In Warframe you don't even have to spend a penny as it has a crafting system and loot is golden: This has a random roll changer which requires you to destroy 3 items to craft 1 new random item that won't be what you want. And you can use 5 items to upgrade them into one better item that will have terrible addon rolls. Warframe has extremely fun character progression and amazing replayability: This has neither, the former doesn't help the latter as it gets stale after 20 hours. What else... Did I mention Warframe is free to play and thus has 300x more players? That half of this game is waiting in the lobby for a decent team or failing missions over and over again because of its numerous issues? Seriously. Leads me to the last most important thing... Enjoyability, risk reward and just reward in general.  In Warframe you can spend 6 hours 'grinding'. Which won't feel like grinding, because you'll be having fun during it, you'll complete endless missions and thus progress your character in a spectrum of different ways. And after the grinding is done? You know you'll be rewarded with what you wanted and will be happy with what you have achieved, you have a visible goal and can choose what to aim for.  In this game you will feel like you have wasted those six hours because it will have been split between waiting in the lobby doing nothing whilst playersr join you and then leave straight away, whether through lag, game crash, or because it isn't the group they wanted... Failing missions over and over through faults that aren't your own, or any of your team mates and instead are completely uncontrollable circumstances. You may complete a mission or two, and what will you have to show for it after that? Nothing, because there is no progression or sense of achievement and you won't have gotten any decent loot after all of it because the system is completely random.  *Wow you spent hours grinding that one mission on cataclysm because it's one of the few that are playable currently? Okay that's brilliant, Here's some 3's 2's and 1's on the loot rolling. You get nothing. See you tomorrow*  *You attempt to repeat another impossible mission over and over and over and over for 36 hours straight because the Ai kept spacking out, you kept getting ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t impossible circumstances even though you were playing with the best team you have ever met; you all lagged out or got kicked from the game randomly and *Insert 20 other issues here*. Did you finally... Actually complete it? On cataclysm of all things? Did it take 3 ♥♥♥♥ing days of your fun time? Did you actually get all tomes and all grims? Jesus you did as well. Okay, well, here's a 2 dice roll.*   The Ai is absolutely horrific as well: You may get attacked by an assassin rat, Whom will stand there walking three feet to the left and three fight to the right over and over and over again unable to get hit and unable to be damaged, when suddenly he will jump at you, you doge his lunge. He will then fly half way across the map through every wall and dissapear only to suddenly appear behind you again later. (This isn't lag this is a 'mechanic'... Lag just makes it worse.) Globadeers may stand there climing over walls only to climb back over the wall and then back over the wall and then wander around outside of the map only to throw a grenade at you through a wall. Rat gunners may attack from outside the map at a place you can't aim because of all the invisible walls. All rats can attack you through solid surfaces so you need not wonder why your health is disapearing randomly. And Rat ogres can fit through any gap regardless their humungous size, can punch through walls and glitch around constantly.  All in all its brilliant game ruined by so many easy to fix problems, crappy mechanics and lack of progression.  Once you've finished the 12 missions (yes only twelve) You then have to grind over and over each mission to not get the gear you want. So realistically there is no point in playing because you never get what you work for. Let's put it this way, You have 3 trinket slots once you open them. I have only ever found one trinket, and that gives me 2% chance to poison on an attack. Big ♥♥♥♥in whoopideedoooo. I'm ecstatic.  Want to backpedal and die because your character hit an invisible wall that is an inch high on the floor? Want to fire your rifle and be unable to switch to melee for 6 seconds whilst you spam the button you have bound for sword? Only for your guy to spack out and do nothing? Want the game to crash sixteen times? Want to have to replay missions over and over without actually completing them because you got unlucky with mob spawns? Because it's sure as hell not fun when the game spawns a wave, an ogre, two rat catchers and 3 assassins... at the same damned time. No amount of co-ordination can deal with that on cataclysm  And when you finally, finally finish a mission after 3 hours of bugged spawns what are you rewarded with? A green drop because the dice rolls are rigged, I've finished missions on cat with 3 tomes and two grims and landed a two. You know why? I have been using PlayClaw 4 and 5 for a while now. PlayClaw 5 contains many options for recording video and audio, such as video and audio codecs, resolution options, audio channels, webcam support, streaming support, and useful overlays.  I used to really like using this software for recording games for my YouTube channel, but the software started to screw up my recordings. For some reason now, PlayClaw 5 will cause the audio to become out of sync throughout the recordings, which becomes a major pain to fix. I am not the only person who has this problem, and the developers tell users to use their 'Fast Codec' to work around this issue, but this codec is not very good.  I would recommend using this software if they would fix the syncing issue, but I would strongly recommend Dxtory, which is a much better recording software with more options.

Barring the fact that this game cemented the style that Nifflas apparently refuses to stop using, I was pretty enamored with Knytt Underground. With past experience playing both Knytt and NightSky, you could be forgiven for saying that KU is something of a midway point between the two, with the sprite design of the former and background and atmosphere of the latter.  The controls take a bit of getting used to, but are eventually smooth and fluid and make for a lot of moments that make the player feel like a badass. There is a bit of flab in the plot, and I eventually defaulted to skipping the majority of the dialogue, but there's something innately satisfying about exploring, pulling off jumping puzzles, and finding all the little nooks and crannies in the map that would otherwise go unexplored. The tone isn't too light-hearted but also(thankfully) doesn't feel the need to take itself seriously, and the background design has this amazing, almost Pikmin-esque quality to it that looks incredible.   EDIT: I wrote this review right as I was beginning to explore the full map that's revealed to the player a fair bit into the game. I will say that the map is kind of ridiculously huge, and the fetchquests get way, way too numerous to care about or keep track of. Also, something I failed to mention in the beginning is that the sprites look pretty odd, and it doesn't seem to be a stylistic choice. In-game character sprites all have this very weird look to them that makes it hard to take any serious moments seriously.  HOWEVER. Everything I already said still stays true. Satisfying mechanics, beautiful backgrounds and atmosphere. Buy it anyway despite its occasional flabbiness.

NEStalgia   This game is a game I have dreamed about since youth, an MMO RPG with Monster collecting. With steam flooded with subpar JRPGS, NEStalgia is a diamond among coal. Whether you play in your own world with friends using the super simple server hosting or you play on the Official Multiplayer Server this game is fun for all.   Pros ~~~ The Official Server is by far one of the best thing about this game, being able to connect with people anywhere at any level is fantastic. The community for this game is wonderful. Everyone is helpful and pleasant. Not only can you PVP, and party up to PVE, there are several other multiplayer features that rise NEStalgia above others including a players market, guild creation, private local world guild and party chat, daily events, and much more.  Pets!!! Farm monsters to fight along side you or give reserve bonuses just from being there. 2 ways to use the pets allows most if not all to be relatively useful. Unlike some games where only a handful of classes/ monsters matter because of strength differences.   Extensive gameplay and character design. Skill trees are extensive, the mathematics behind the game tweaked with updates to create fair and balanced gameplay between classes and the rest of the world. Even after reach max level there are still a plethora of things to do and accomplish and several end game dungeons meant for characters not only of max level but max gear and strategy.  Nice map design. As you progress through the story and the map unfolds it seems vast, but was designed so that travel between areas takes no time at all once quests are completed. Revisit old places and areas with ease while exploring a vast world with many secrets and puzzles.   Promises of updates, even if they are slower than hoped.  (also seems like this dev actually cares about their game :)  Cons ~~~ You will have to grind against random number generators for items, and then gold/exp of course. (But I mean that’s what sets rpgs apart from point and click >.>)  You will have to grind allot more if you want to survive in later parts of the game on your own. It can be done, especially early - mid game, but I do find myself wishing for a single player area.

Simply put, this is the best game I've ever played, and I've played tons of them over the span of 20+ years of gaming.  I'm a huge RPG fan. Easily my favorite genre. I've heard a lot about Bloodlines and people saying how great it was but I didn't get a chance to play it until recently. I'm so glad I finally played it because it is superb.  It's got an incredible, believable atmosphere of dread that draws you in completely and hooks you from the first minute of the game. It's got a great story with plenty of intrigue, interesting characters, some dark humor and excellent RPG systems under the hood that all come together to provide a fantastic gaming experience.  Your choices actually matter and different things will happen based on how you play the game. Unexpected outcomes can happen if you make the 'wrong' decision. The game always keeps you on your toes.  It's also got it's share of creepiness. You'll explore some levels that will scare you, and it also explores some dark and disturbing themes.   As I said at the beginning, it's the best game I've ever played. Highly recommended.   10/10

review_text: I had never heard of the game before but I bought it for less tha 5 € on Steam. Oh boy was it the deal of the year ! This is a 2D platform game. This is a story driven game that will make you attached to the main characters from minute 1 of the game ! 4 stories, 4 heroes, 4 destinies meeting during the First World War. The graphism are great especialy if you like the comic strip style. The puzzles are not too difficult but you'll sometimes find yourself wondering what to do. Steam needs a 'maybe' option. I've been playing Tex Murphy games since the mid-90s. To this day I will still occasionally throw in Under a Killing Moon or The Pandora Directive and thoroughly enjoy them. Tesla Effect will be this one playthrough.  It's a Unity game and it shows. The amount of audio channels is limited (engine issue) and will occasionally cause some artifacting. The graphics are worse than what a lot of indie developers are putting out these days. The worst--and most unforgivable--part of this is on Chandler Avenue.  Chandler Avenue is the main setting for the game and at least half your playtime will be spent there. That's fine for a Tex Murphy game, they've all been like that, but what is not fine is that their 3D artist had no concept of LOD. The entire street has one LOD and it all seems to be loaded even if you're on the other end of the block. This produces a ridiculous amount of frame rate loss and is rather inexcusable for a game these days.  The story was convoluted to the point where Kafka might have ghost-written it. Characters are completely re-written and made into new mechanations that are not true to the decade, or so, of writing that made them.  A lot of the puzzles are obtuse and needlessly gimicky.  The pros:  The game still has the Tex Murphy charm which is worth the price alone. The acting is fantastic (in it's intentionally over-the-top fashion) and it was fun to see the old characters back again even if a lot of who they are has changed.  The overall sound design stayed true to the original games. I got giddy when I first heard 'You have a fax.'  Most importantly, the game knows what it is and is true to itself for the most part.  If you played the games from the 90s then you should be able to spend $20 on a trip through memory lane. It's less than the cost of a movie and you will get at least a dozen hours out of it. It won't all be great but there is enough to justify the price.

The port is fairly well done, though default keyboard controls feel a bit odd, however are all fully rebindable. The controller support feels slightly off, but it isn't a big issue since you have all the time in the world in combat. Otherwise, the port is very functional, and the ability to use the mouse in selection screens is a rather nice plus. The actual game itself is somewhat of a unique take on a JRPG, with high customization elements, an ultra cute cast and a rather large amount of content, especially with the DLC bringing up the level cap to 999. The combat system is very simplistic, but because of the simplicity, it makes the game rather casual. That being said, the balancing of the foes in the game feel slightly strange, with a large gap between the strength of regular monsters and boss monsters. Just about everything is customizable, from your character outfits, attack combos, monster difficulty and loot obtained and the dialogue includes lighthearted humor and much fourth wall breaking. The voice overs are available in both English and Japanese and all Music and Artwork are unlocked as the game progresses for viewing at any time in a town.  Overall, Hyperdimension Neptunia Re;Birth1 is a unique JRPG that fans of the series or JRPGs in general may enjoy.

As fun as it is in places, for people who don't spend as much as i do on video games. It's just not worth it. Wait for the inevitable activision week discount.  This'll be a zombies focused section of the review for those who like me ( yes, i know. We exist.) who buy call of duty exclusively for the Zombies gamemode. I've sank nearly 36 Hours into the current map, Zombies in Spaceland. Digging right into it with tibia and teeth and while i've nearly done all that it has to offer aside from the soul and career challenges.   First, Zombies in Infinite Warfare is a more bulletspongy affair from their Black Ops bretheren and let's be honest here, there are alot of carry overs from the Zombies series in Black Ops. Perk Drinks are replaced with Candies, you buy barriers with points, you buy guns from walls, you use barriers as a cheap way of making a couple of extra points. It's essentially the same thing, and while alot of people are going to decry that as a bad thing, it stops ZIS from becoming Extinction, the gamemode from Call of Duty Ghosts. The formula works, but there are defintely a couple of new twists that from first glance seem to make the game easier but make it ALOT harder.  Speaking of Perks, there are bunch of new additions and old friends that make a comeback through different faces. I won't list all of them here for fear of spoiling it for you but they offer interesting strategies,although some (*cough* blue bolts *cough*) need a serious buff or quirk for me to consider using them for high round.  Instead of bleeding out and dying, having to wait until next round you can get back into the round by playing small minigames in an arcade. At first glance this might seem to make the game easier but this taxes you even more than the original way of doing things and it's all because of lost and found. While the option to buy back your weapons is great, what isn't so great is that you essentially fall back into the map naked. Without your perks, your weapons or anything actually. Your first instinct is obviously to grab your weapons but on a high round this is nearly suicidal unless your at a skill level where you can abuse the hell out of sliding. If you die during that time, you lose your weapons and they can be progressed pretty high and considering Arcane Cores, which can upgrade your guns and you have to jump through hoops to get and upgrade can lost with them, it means you could potentially be jumping through many many many hoops in order to get the gear you previously had.  This difficulty is bolstered by the fact that in comparison to black ops 3, the weapons scale off alot more harshly as the rounds go on, even with upgraded weapons you'll start to find them getting weaker and weaker towards round 20 and then becoming peashooters even with the additional core at 30 essentially meaning that Wallbuys (guns you buy on the wall) are the kings of running high round in zombies. You don't even need to upgrade them AT ALL, and there are so many great places to run zombies around in circles that you'll always be making points back while gradually killing the hordes. It takes FOREVER to progress through the rounds but i ended up with upwards of 100k in points through the 20s alone for you to recover from potential losses of perks and weapons.   What is probably the worst part of this iteration of zombies is that so much of the weapon progression ingame is locked behind the Easter Egg and the boss is no joke, arguably the hardest in zombies history. I spent 3 runs, about total of about 6-8 hours setting up only to fail each time because he hits so hard,is very very VERY bulletspongy and can regenerate a whole phase of damage which is unacceptable when max ammo hardly drops.Hell, i got Gorod Krovi on my first go but this ♥♥♥♥ ain't no cakewalk!  Without the Easter Egg being completed in that very game, you are locked out of a second upgrade for your weapon and the final upgrade for Arcane Core, which i couldn't get. These upgrade unlocks don't persist, you need to do the easter egg everytime you want to get those upgrades, which if your aiming for high round can compromise the above strategy i mentioned. You'll have more powerful weaponry, absolutely but all of that weaponry eventually will scale down in damage until your essentially firing a glorified peashooter and making less points for it. Avoid the Dischord, get pretty much every other buildable weapon.  A final note, I literally wrung every little bit of content i could out of ZIS and unless you are absolutely nuts and plan on grinding all 400 Levels in Zombies on the first map, i really really wouldn't recommend it at full price point just for zombies. Just wait and get Black Ops 3 packed with the DLC on a key site if you don't have that already, you'll have mods and all DLC content to enjoy while Zombies in IW atm is absolutely bulimic, and only worth enjoying after all 4 dlc packs come out with the game on discount with it.

Let me begin by saying that Tengami is absolutely beautiful. As an origami enthusiast, I adored the premise that the world was made of paper. Watching the game's landscapes literally unfold themselves hearkened back to my experiences with the graphics of the Paper Mario series, which are still some of my favorite graphics of all time.   That said, Tengami is a very atmospheric and calm, albeit very short, experience. The music suits the game's various environments perfectly, and (in my opinion) the puzzles felt very clever, particularly the last few. Though I was able to finish the whole game in one sitting, I would nonetheless strongly recommend playing it on the basis of its creativity alone. Really, it was that great! :D   Length : ~2 hours  Difficulty : Easy (but tricky; requires thinking!)

This is an old game released in the late 90s and for some reasons resurfaced in a bundle. Good memories in mind, I go ahead and purchase the bundle (and paid far less than the $1.99 on Steam) for it.   Crazy chicken is a pretty basic game where you have to shoot as much as chicken possible in 1:30 min. The control somehow seems to have changed from the .EXE standalone and using the left mouse button for both shooting and moving the mouse is not a good control design.   The game might be good if you have like 5 min to kill or trying to release some steam at work (assuming you can install Steam on computer at work), but besides that... the game becomes really boring really fast especially the extra funny stuff in the background tend to repeat and are not longer funny (or weird if you caught the sheeps fornicating in the back).   The only use I could see for this game as some sort of entertainment is for drinking party... Get drunk and see how you and your guesses handle shooting chicken while being impaired. I am not sure that a valid use to spend $1.99.

I heard about this game on Wyoming Public Radio and wanted to check out a game set in my home state. It's unorthodox, for sure, and in this case that's a good thing. As others have mentioned, the game is heavily dialogue driven, and plays like an interactive novel. The plot is generally good, getting a little hokey at moments. But it comes together at the end. The game is rather short, but the pacing is just about right. Contrary to what some say, I thought the end was brilliant and moving on a number of levels. Not to mention gorgeously portrayed (visually). It probably doesn't have much replay value, as the main reason for doing so would be to play with the dialogue options. But overall, this is a very strong showing for a brand new developer, and I'll be keeping an eye out for them in the future. A beautiful little game that is unexpectedly human.

The playerbase was as unfriendly then as it is now, but nothing was more fun than walking through a field of standing bodies during pvp and slaughtering everyone you could before they dissappeared due to an entire country disconnecting from the game.   Plenty of good memories.. like instant mage casting with the skeleton scroll.. aoe groups, rogue kiting like a pro.. christmas events.. but it's definitely not a game I'd reccomend to anyone in this day and age. I honestly can't believe it's still up and running. That horse has been dead for a very long time.

Zaton, Misery.  Walking. Watching the sun slowly rise, it could've been a scene from a thousand years ago- with no cars, no lights, no noise but the animals and wind through the trees.  Human interference seemed so minute, I barely noticed the subtle gunfire in the background, the cracked and blistered road I walked across.  Then suddenly-  CRACK.  Something small and fast whizzes past my head like a bee.  No time to check.  I hold shift and book it.  There's a van nearby. Maybe two metres, maybe four.  I make a line straight for it.  CRACK.  I hear the bullet thump into the road. A plume of dust floats past the van i'm hiding behind.  I pull my gun out from it's holster.  It's a 357 magnum revolver. Condition is terrible, and rust visibly coats the gun like fungus on a tree.  Three shots. Better make them count.  Pull the hammer back, ready to fire.  I hold it steady, creeping my way to the edge of the van.  I peak out the side. Slowly. Carefully. Don't make it obvious.  Binoculars out.  In front of me is a hill. Tall and green, with a thick layer of grass and foliage covering the top like mould.  Something flashes in my eyes. Movement. I track it like a sniper.  The grass is moving, alive like some unknown mutated creature of the zone.  I look closer.  Whoever it is, his outift blends in well with the greenery. He evidently hasn't noticed me yet, and he's moving down the hill no doubt for a better view.  On his back is a long gun. Maybe a rifle, maybe a shotgun. It's metallic sheen stands out like a sore thumb amongst the natural hues surrounding it.  I check my gun again. Yeah. Definitely just three shots.  I wait.  He reaches the edge of the hill. Cautious. Careful.  Starts running.  Not close enough yet.  As soon as he goes past the van, that's when I'll start moving.  From over his shoulder he reveals his gun to be a shotgun. The long barrelled 'over-under' type.  It'd be deadly if I give him the chance to use it.  His aproach is swift, not wanting to be caught out in the open road, completely disregarding the van I could be hiding behind.  Now.  I slowly creep to the back of the van.  Small, miniscule steps mark my way as I go, gun aimed with an intense trigger finger.  It finally centres over his head.  I stand up.  Calmly walk out into the road. Gun still trained on his head.  I wait until he notices something. The subtle echo of footsteps, or just good situational awareness, he eventually hazards a look behind him.  'ANNU CHEEKI BREEKI IVV DAMKE,' I scream in real life, as I hit mouse1.  The gun jerks back as a plume of smoke appears at the front end of the barrel, and my vision goes hazy at the edges for a moment.  Then it happens again. Then again. What? What the hell is happening? The same sounds, the same bullet sound, over and over again until -   'X-ray engine has stopped working. Windows is trying to find a solution to the problem,'  10/10 Would have the game crash on me again.

Gameplay wise it's excellent but content and option wise, it leaves a lot to be desired. I wouldn't mind it so much if there was actual options to modify the gameplay. Perhaps a more relaxed setting, for the individual who just wants to take down the pace a notch or two. A setting to only have targets appear in one direction, rather than in a 270* arch. Even settings for the size of the target, requiring more precise aim to hit them, stuff like that.   For now, it's an extremely basic experience for the price, where you literally can only press start and be thrown into a very stressful experience where you are worn out after 3-4 attempts.  So with some more options and variety, it'll be great but right now, it's just too limited and gets boring too easily.   However, I won't give it a thumbs down due to the price. It's staying well below the current price-average of most VR games and there's enough fun in the game for the given price. But it could be so much more!

This game is as entertaining as it is creepy and suspensful. You wander the 2D world environment in its ever spooky atmosphere with the D-pad, and use the Action Button on various items and objects to help you clear puzzles. Though don't let this single-button game get the better of you...  You'll have to think fast on your feet in the world of LIMBO if you want to survive long. Death is bound to happen on certain puzzles, though thankfully you get an unlimited amount of lives. The only thing that can deter you from solving that pesky puzzle is how much patience you're willing to devote to this game.  While not -quite- on par with Portal 2 or some of those other action-y, high end puzzle solvers, LIMBO is definitely worth giving a try if you have ten bucks to spare, and an aching to dodge and escape flinging bear traps, shadowy figures with blow darts, an oversized arachnid and more, all hell bent on sending you to an early, prepubescent grave.

The first thing you will notice are the vastly outdated graphics, which just hurt your eyes and are nothing like the trailers and the screenshots. Everything is downgraded as usual. However, this is not the biggest of surprises in the game industry these days, so in my generosity, it can be forgiven. What I and many others fail to understand is the concept of the game, for it simply isnt very well thought out before put into use. This is very obvious when you really start to understand the gameplay and try to execute the things you observe the game showing you, which eventually feels clunky, outdated and sometimes, unfortunately, even flat-out depressing.  Gameplay-wise, the game holds your hand almost all the time and it becomes really annoying, when you start to notice you have no real difference in the course of action. There are moments where you just feel completely lost and get annoyed at how everything doesnt quite fit together.  The controls dont feel fluid at all, and occasionally you feel like you're either stuck in the mud or glue, or the game intentionally was made hard and frustrating to control. Why would anyone want to offer the players of their game an experience like this?  And what's really frustrating are the sounds. It's like someone literally wanted to hire one of the worst composers, voice actors and the sound designers for this kind of a game! Could it be simply a bad decision from the lead of this project or very, very dark humor from the entire team of the game developers? You decide! And dont even get me started on the AI. It's rather non-existent. Some games are famous for their silly bot-AI that keeps doing mistakes and failing at the only thing it was designed to do. The only difference between those games and this is that the AI here isn't even funny. It just wants to make you shout at it's actions in utter rage.  The performance of the game tends to be lackluster. Ive seen it perform badly on many different specs and machines, which cannot garner much respect, but at this point it doesn't really surprise, either. All this could be fixed with at least a long, interesting story that would give content for your money and/or time, but it doesn't really offer either.  When you thought it couldn't get any worse, the game has more bugs here and there, hindering your already non-existent progressing and sucking the last drops of joy out of the experience On top of all of this, the game is insanely difficult to progress in. Everything has been made non-intuitive for the average gamer, to the point even hardcore difficulty masochists will find it unpleasant to experience.  It all makes you think: Where is the gaming industry heading these days?

You should play this game. Briefly, here's why:  1) It is fun. Not content to simply shoehorn mediocre gameplay into the gaps between story, the developers have managed to make a game where simply BEING Corvo is fun. Running around, double-jumping, and using Blink to explore rooftops... these are actions that would be enjoyable even in the absence of story entirely, and other powers continue to add interesting ingredients as you unlock them. That they THEN wrapped this gameplay in an interesting story is the proverbial icing on the cake.  2) The art. LOVE the art. The character designs are in my personal Top 3 (with Brink and DE: Human Revolution) and the environments are beautiful too. I could (and probably have) spent hours just appreciating the visual style.  3) Open to different play-styles. You can treat it like a shooter and kill everyone, or you can finish the entire game without killing a single person. (Or somewhere in between.) Furthermore, the environments themselves offer great flexibility in terms of your desired approach, instead of just being narrow corridors funneling you along the intended path, like many other games I won't name here.  4) It is a polished, AAA, single-player-only, original IP. If that sentence doesn't make you nod with respect, you haven't been paying attention to the past few years of AAA games and the epidemic of 'Sequelitis.' It takes real stones to make a game like Dishonored happen in an age where publishers are VERY reluctant to take risks. The fact that Arkane and Bethesda stepped up to the plate is admirable, and the fact that the resulting game is actually GOOD is something worth supporting.

It can be hard trying to nail a good puzzle/stealth game. As of any game it should highlight its key features to make it stand out. Well my god does this stand out. While the story isn't super gripping you are a free lance spy contracting yourself out. In your arsenal you have nothing but those stretchy pants and what might be the most awesome tool ever, the Crosslink. It's probably what makes this more fun and interesting. Being able to rewire things trick security and be able to mess with all the hard ware in a building feels gratifying. It's not so say that there isn't a challenge to it. After an hour of playing it seems that the levels are fair and not overly difficult all though you can run into a bit of wall when you get stuck, but after a moment of clarity you can piece it together and get the data and get out. The game rewards you with 'XP' for how silent, quiet, and being unseen as well as no violence....if that is possible. The nice jazz too also helps and keep you laxed as you are playing through which is always a plus. At a staple price of $9.99 , it's a game well worth spending my time on and should be yours as well.

This point-n-click adventure is very well built, and it's a pity that it hasn't been released in the Super Nintendo era, because it would have gotten much more attention back then.  By today's standards, the sounds and graphics are outdated, but the music and story are still better than most games have to offer today (AAA titles included).  'To the Moon' is proof that you don't need a high budget and guns and explosions to create something great. You can't die or lose in any way in this game. It's a point-n-click story that will give you what you came for: 'The Feels'.  I'd recommend this to anyone who has nostalgia for old-school point-n-clicks, and to those who prefer a beautiful story to high-end graphics.  My score: 7/10

Excellent tactical RPG action.   The fights are pleasingly balanced push/pull affairs that require you to press every advantage you can get to cross over the knife-edge to victory, and the variety of enemies and bosses are enough to keep things fresh for a long time. as is playing around with the various party arrangements. The hassle-free respeccing between fights lets you really get experimental with ways to counter your opponents.  There are some alright non-combat puzzle elements there, and the story/voice acting is surprisingly enjoyable too, but the combat is the real gem of this game.  As for downsides: The RPG elements are pretty basic, but it wears that on its sleve. And the item/trinket system isn't very interesting and can it get kind of annoying to trawl through all the junk loot for marginal stat improvments.

Fun/Interesting story, Great Sound track, Hand drawn art, Fun Gameplay, Voice acting, Funny sidekick. What else could you want.   STORY- *without spoilers* The story follows the main character Dust on his to quest to find his identity. Kept me on my seat trying to unravel the mystery.   SOUNDTRACK- All 37 tracks are well done and great at capturing the feel of the game. I havent liked a soundtrack this much since to the moon. Most of the time id rather have a good soundtrack then good graphics.   GRAPHICS- This game is truely gorgeous. Its hand-painted and it moves very well. Its very easy to forget its hand drawn.   GAMEPLAY- Just so you know i used a controller. I feel the game was made to work with one since it was origanaly a xbox live game. The fights make you feel like a Bad A**. So many Slashes, and explosions its sometims hard to see what happened untill the Dust settles (See What I Did There) or as Fidget says 'DID YOU SEE THE SCREEN'    VOICE ACTING- Some people say the voice acting is not to good. I really liked the voices expetaly Fidgets voice and Dusts voice is good at capturing his lost feeling. Its awesome that it even has voices, i somtimes get tired of reading text after awhile. So dont think the voice acting is bad till you hear it.     Must play, That is unless you dont care for Storylines and only like 3D games like Call of Duty.

I will recommend Hand of Fate, but certainly not without qualification. Many elements of this game involve the RNG - the success/failure card picks, the combination of enemies you face in a given encounter (and the environment in which you face them), the weapons/magic/blessings/armor you have access to in a given game. All of this can lead to a very frustrating experience. You may desire to complete a 'quest,' only to find that you can't seem to get the 'success' card multiple times in the card game. Sometimes, you won't even see the quest card that game. Perhaps I should've seen all this coming? I enjoyed the Arkham Asylum-esque combat, and the ability to create both your equipment and encounter decks. My main recommendation would be simply to quit after you finish the campaign. Tying up the lose ends, even if you enjoy the game, may be more trouble than it's worth.

Valkyria Chronicles was my favorite game on PS3. I was always sad they never released achievements but i completed the game several times over anyway. When I heard it was coming to PC I was overjoyed. Valkyria chronicles is a tactical, turn based strategy game with a anime-esque art style and is extremely fun.   The game can be played many ways (and I have yet to play it on the harder difficulties, looking forward to that) and offers some interesting strategic decisions. Do you let your scouts outpace your heavy infantry and try to pick off the enemy at range? Do you spend some valuable command points to bring your tank forward to smash through a heavy concentration of infantry? Do you sit back and let your snipers pick off the enemy one-by-one while your engineers keep them supplied with ammo? There are many ways to play this game and make it fun; and if all else fails you can always play it slow and safe to finish a hard mission. Another dimension of depth is added by Potentials (unique traits that have a chance to activate during combat) that each character has as well as what other characters they like, which grants them other bonuses when they are together.   However, perhaps the best part of the game is its story. Each of the main characters has a beautifully crafted personality and you really get to know them throughout the game. Some people skip dialogue when they play games. If you do this in Valkyria Chronicles, you will miss so much that this game has to offer. As the story goes on, you fall in love with the characters. They will make you laugh, and sometimes even cry.   As someone who has played everything from Fire Emblem to XCom to Starcraft, I would DEFINITELY recommend this game. It will always hold a special place in my heart for its gameplay and its story that tugs at emotions. 20/10 would buy twice.

This is simply put one of (if not the best) starship game out there.   Yes, it has a steep learning curve but after a little training and referencing the keybindings you can easily get the hang of it and once you do, it'll get it's tentacles into you and you can't stop playing.   Once I learned how to be a cargo hauler I started my illustrious career of hauling (mostly) illegimate goods across conflict sectors where you have to learn how to evade security and avoid interdiction attempts (which is an interesting game dynamic as they WILL try to pull you out of supercruise speeds).   After a while of that I had enough saved for a shiney new ship which I then armed with the best weapons I could afford (at the time). From there, it was bounty hunting and hit jobs for sector baronies. Aka, my in game persona wasn't looking like a good guy by any stretch of the civilized mind.   Oh yeah, and once you get into playing the game you'll quickly realize the flow of controls and processes that make a good captain. It may seem inaccesible at first, but after one day of playing you'll most likely feel like you got the hang of it.   Highly recomend this game, and can't wait for the expansions.

I've played, or at least ATTEMPTED to play, quite a few point-and-click adventures in my time, and the sad truth is I either give up on most of them before I reach the end, or I cave at some point to the temptation to look at a walkthrough, and from pretty much that point onward, find my lazy ♥♥♥♥ returning to said walkthrough so frequently that I probably could have just watched someone else's YouTube video and saved myself all that RSI from clicking. There's only a few point-and-click adventure games which I've seen through to their conclusion WITHOUT constant deference to a walkthrough, and Stasis is one of those games. (Well, okay...a few walkthrough consultations, to be sure, but not THAT many considering, haha!)    A lot of people downright believe that this genre of gaming should have gone the way of the Dodo a long, long time ago. I mostly agree. But it's the rare game like Stasis which completely validates the genre's ongoing existence. STASIS is just modern enough - with its gorgeous graphics, atmospheric sound, excellent voice acting and generally high level of immersion - while still evidencing the kind of story-telling and puzzle-solving logic which surely only the very best point-and-click games, of any era, are able to boast. It's completely 'real-time', you CAN die, and on the whole it's about as 'cinematic' as a point-and-click adventure can be without ever fully crossing over into the 'action-adventure' stakes. It's even a tiny bit scary, in its way, but not enough to make it a tie-breaker as to whether or not one should purchase it (i.e. don't buy it expecting Dead Space 1, but likewise don't be discouraged from trying it if you 'don't normally like' scary games).    So who would I recommend this to? Anyone, anyone at all, who thinks they might be in the market for a high-quality, moderate-difficulty, ultra-atmospheric, surprisingly emotional and, okay, rather 'dark' point-and-clicker. If you don't like reading and/or don't like games where you don't get to shoot loads of things, then please, KINDLY ♥♥♥♥ OFF. Then again, if you don't like reading things, WHY ARE YOU READING THIS REVIEW?! So, er...if you managed to get all the way to the end of this review, I guess I'm recommending it to YOU, whoever you are. It's damned good. Trust me. I don't even usually dig this kinda game all that much, and even I think it's staggeringly good. That's gotta count for something, surely...   Verdict: 9.5/10.     (PS I do have to say, however, that the puzzle where you 'refine' a body part into a useable blood-and-tissue sample by SMASHING IT TO A PULP WITH AN EMPTY PISTOL was a little on the ludicrous side. Seriously. I even thought of using the pistol on the body part before I consulted a walkthrough - since they were the only two items left in my inventory at that point! - and wrote the notion off as kinda ridiculous. But MOST of the puzzles are still logical enough, hahaha...)

As a 10 years player of FFXI Online, I never bothered with this game until now, after buying it on it's first release day. Obviously it wasn't a full game then. And it certainly is now.  Well I got a new bells and whistles computer. This game runs 100% maxed and it looks beautiful for it.  Pros?  It looks absolutely stunning. There is loads and loads to do. Good value for your subscription once your free 30 days are up. 40 characters allowed for that one fee per month.  Cons? Nope sorry, it reminds me of FFXI back when it was too annoying.  Pay the £10 or whatever it will cost you, to run around for those 30 days by all means. Because you'll love the scenery as you run around. But if you're like me you will be well bored of the constant grind after 3 days playing. It's actually slower to level jobs than FFXI Online was when it was grindy. I mean 2 hours a level grind. In which time you could *literally* be level 30 on a character on FFXI Online from starting a new character these days. Or level 10 back in the bad old days when leveling was uber slow and annoying.  Summary? If you liked FFXI Online back in 2005 or so, you will LOVE this. If you like FFXI Online how it is now, leveling every mob at the beginning and hitting level 99 over a weekend, this game is going to really wind you up quickly.

Cool game. Very pretty, supports 144Hz monitors, and is pretty fun. Very very annoying that you have to hold R1 while not moving in order to pass time, though. In my (4.4 hour) playthrough, I found myself never needing nor wanting to use any crafted gadgets, because enemies were always easily dispatched with the dagger. For this reason, despite the rather grim setting, I can't help but think this game's place is for playing with your kids, as an entryway to stealth games.  The controls need a lot of work. When you're in the crafting menu, you use left and right on the D-pad to cycle through schematics, and then use the analog stick to select ingredients for the schematic, which is just kind of bulky, and I think they should have made it so that you select a schematic, and then use the d-pad again. As stated before, time stops when your character stops moving, unless you hold R1. I found my right index finger getting fatigued due to constantly holding R1 waiting for guards to move around and such.  Lastly, this game is very, very short. It took me 4.4 hours to go through the full game (15 levels) once, that's an average of 17.6 minutes per level. If you're looking for a stealth game that takes patience and skill, go play Thief 1, 2, and I dare say 3. Not the reboot though, the reboot sucks.  Whether or not a game of this length is worth $17 to you is ultimately your decision. I enjoyed the game, but, honestly, I'm having a hard time recommending it due to its short length, and I simply don't think I would purchase it again. I will, however, leave it as a 'yes' because I prefer to err on the side of positivity, and because the Trine devs are pretty cool.

Civ V was my first turn-based strategy game, and my first Civilization series game, and on both counts, I couldn't be happier. The strategy is compelling and detailed, with many options and playstyles available. The variety of bonuses and unique units available to each civilization allows for a great deal of replay value, and randomized elements in the maps and AIs contribute to a fresh start with every game.  There are some downsides, however. Although one of the biggest draws to Civ V is the complex and highly variable strategy options, the AI programming leaves much to be desired, as an AI simply cannot account for the many different levels of strategy needed to achieve victory. As such, the difficulty settings feel somewhat lackluster, as they simply give the AI greater and greater early advantages, leading to a very different gameplay at high levels compared to a truly 'even playing field'.   Combat, like with many games, is clunky and time-consuming, and although there are elements of strategy and maneuvering, it can be boiled down into essentially a numbers game of tech and manpower, rather than true strategic prowess. Multiplayer is also less than ideal, particularly in a mixed human/AI environment, as AI features are severely nerfed or nonexistent.   In terms of fluff, I find the pseudo-historical theme to be a huge benefit, as I'm just enough of a history/linguistics/geography nerd to find it interesting, but not condescending or painfully inaccurate. (The 'alternate history' line allows for a lot of freedom, when of course the game will not reflect a real-life history.)  Ultimately, though, I've sunk many, many hours into this game and I can see myself continuing to play well into the future. Especially considering the price is quite reasonable for comparable game quality and style, I would recommend this game to anyone who likes strategy, history, or just having a good time. I particularly recommend it as a solo game - for those of us who don't care for online multiplayer, Civ V is by far the most rewarding single-player Steam title I've come across.

Empire: Total War takes us away from the battlefields of both Ancient and Middle Aged Europe and Middle-East and takes us to the Empires of Europe during the 18th Century. Muskets and Cannon now determines who is the most powerful in all of Europe, Improved graphics and gameplay makes the game more exciting and stunning for Total War fans. Campaigns follow George Washington as he revisits the past from the development of Jamestown (1607), to the French and Indian War (1740's to 1750's), and to the American War of Independence (1775-1783). Not only can the battles between the European powers take place in Europe and the Middle-east, but also the Colonies of North America and South America and to the Colonies of India. Trade and expansion is key to your nation's victory, expanding out to colonies and your Empire's borders are crucial. The century turns to the the advancement of technology and development of new forms of Government. A time when the system of Monarchy seems to crumble while Republics and Democracies rise. Revolution and Colonization has made the 18th Century one of the world's most crucial centuries in all of the World's History. Will you reign or will you revoutionize a new Empire, the choice is yours. The times of Royal familes and difference in social class lays in your hands, will you preserve it or will you destroy it?
