I have always wanted to play this game, so when i built my new rig i bought this game straight away, regardless of price. The game is an interesting take on 1940's Los Angeles. You play Detective Cole Phelps who is a rising detective, solving many cases. Preivously an officer in the army and having returned from WW2 I feel his character has many flaws within his personalty.During the flashbacks that occur after each case he comes acrross as someone who is arrogrant and see's the world through rose tinted glasses. Basically he's too uptight.. This is evident when he is thrust upon the grim business of crime solving. He is sharp, but also cold hearted during interviews and questioning of suspects. But this is one of the things i love about this game, the dialogue is spot on for what i expect from 1940's America, Take Two has hit the sweet spot in producing a realistic portrayal of 1940's Los Angeles. Overall i would give this a sold 8/10, with the story/world/characters being excellent. In my opinion i mostly say that gameplay comes ahead of story within a game, but is it really appropiate with this game? I would therefore recommend this tomature gamers.

Mark of the Ninja is a game. By that, I'm not trying to state something obvious, I'm saying playing Mark of the Ninja actually feels like playing a game, which is something I rarely feel these days.   Mark of the Ninja isn't story/art/experience first and gameplay second like gameplay is this random, minor, unimportant part of a game. Mark of the Ninja is all about the gameplay. There is a great story with a pretty awesome twist and the game does boast some excellent atmosphere and sound, but by far the biggest strength of this game is in how it actually plays.  It plays great. The controls are precise and you never feel like they are at fault for you failing. The goals and objectives are varied and interesting and the game provides a steady increase in challenge, which keeps you hooked all the way through, and even past that.  Past that? Well, yeah. Once you complete the game, you unlock an extra difficult mode which you'll be itching to immediately try out and then probably continue with completing it one more time.  But what is it? It's a proper stealth game. It's possible to kill enemies withouth stealth, but you won't score well, you won't get far and you'll miss out on upgrades and objectives. Instead, you're supposed to assess the situation, make a plan and execute it without being seen, like a real ninja is supposed to do it.   Mark of the Ninja is by far the best stealth game I've played in over a decade and one of the better games I've played in years. I highly recommend it.

I'd like to start saying my hours played isnt very accurate. I played via Wakfu launcher for much more than i did here.  This game is a remake of a popular game known as Final Fantasy Tactics. a Square grid based, turn based tactical game.  There are some great features of the game but most are overshadowed with extreme lacklustre support and game management. Ranging from hacks and exploits never being dealt with causing the entire auction house(market) to flood kamas into the game nulifying the years spent aquiring them. The good:  This is a really cool, really fun, die and retry game. It's got a lot of cool elements that make you think 'I can do better on this next run'... Very addictive. Plus, there is a bunch of disturbing pixel gore mixed with an unending 80's style acid trip. Mix this with the crazy fast pace the game takes and you've got something exceptional.  The bad:   The levels seem to get a little monotonous after playing for too long. Aside from that, the only bad thing I can say is that the story is a little lacking. Still, those things are fairly minor in my mind.  The ugly:  The music in this game is by far the strangest I've heard in any video game. It's actually quite annoying at times, but it fits so perfectly that I'm not sure I'd have it any other way. It really does add to the hallucinogenic atmosphere of the game.

Well I have been a fan of Don't starve for quite some time. Pretty much back when it was in beta, back before caves, back before rabbits had an ai. Watching it progress has been interesting. So this game is a survival... sorta horror game? It is hard to say it has things happen that are kinda creepy. The point of this game is to try to stay alive for as long as you can. It is easier said than done. Throughout the game you will have problems trying to stay alive. Hazards include hunger, wetness, heat, freezeing, monsters, giants, the dark, sanity, seasons, thunder, and fire. So basicly you start the game if you can survive long enough you will be able to get a little camp going and have enough food to live off of. But things in this game never go your way. Random mobs sometimes attack at the worst times, and bosses can randomly come and destroy your 'house' and set your crops a blaze. This is basicly one of those games where you can be having fun and then your fun will be ruined and you will go sour on the game. Pretty good for an indie game, I give it...8.5/10.  Ah, almost forgot,   console commands!   [dont-starve-game.wikia.com] (`) So you can cheat your heart out and not starve!

I would score this highest out of any of my racing games, a true petrol heads dream, if you are on the fence, dont be, go for it, you won't regret it if you are a true car/motorsports fan, thumbs up

review_text: Short but sweet: Graphics, gameplay (local vs.) and fun factor are high. To date, I have never connected to a single online game. If you're getting this solely to play with a buddy online, skip it. If you want a fun single player or local machine match, go for it.

This is a real city (or town) builder. There is no silly monsters or other form of combat, your only interaction with the world at large is through traders, and the occasional nomads. Banished is a brutal, unforgiving game. You will likely have many villages where your entire population dies from starvation, cold, or both, before you learn how much food and firewood you need to sustain your population. And then still disaster can strike if someone in the food or wood industry dies, and you don't have a new citizen to take over.  Banished is one of those time sink games you simply cannot put down. I recommend this to everyone who likes the city building genre even a little bit, but be prepared for a harsh learning curve.

Telltale Studios came into the spotlight with their Walking Dead game — which went on to win many, many game of the year awards and the praise of consumers and critics alike. It was also a game that I didn't particularly care for. Admittedly, I only played the first two chapters and the voice acting and gameplay was quite good, but the story itself did not grab me like it seemed to do to others. I'm over the whole zombie thing. However, with The Wolf Among Us, Telltale has successfully recaptured my attention. They've produced a high-quality game with careful attention to good storytelling. While the gameplay might be minimal, the performances, direction, and script will keep you invested till the last moment.  If I were to describe The Wolf Among Us, I'd say it's equal parts Drive, and Chinatown with the aesthetic of something like Only God Forgives. It's a intricate detective story (an immediate interest for me) following the Big Bad Wolf (now just called Bigby, in his human form) as he teams up with Snow White in the search for a serial killer who's recent actions have disturbed the 'peaceful' community of Fabletown — a community of fairy tale characters located in the Bronx New York. However, as Bigby progresses, he tests the limits of himself (physically and mentally), his friends, and the community as he unravels a plot much bigger than himself. The plot starts off great and just keeps getting better. There's a constant sense of mystery, a legitimate interest in the results of the case, and the identity of the perpetrator. Telltale's script is tight and focused, yet allowing for ample amounts of flexibility and player choice. The dialogue is perfectly delivered by the accomplished voice actors, adding to the gritty mix of realism and fantasy. The story is just plain great — it checks off all the important boxes for success and even adds a few more. While the ending may feel a little messy in its ultimately satisfying payoff, the plot is riveting from start to finish. It clocks in at a solid length too, it can probably be finished in anywhere from 8-12 hours, depending on how much stuff you investigate. The replayability comes almost entirely from the choice based gameplay — allowing players to play again, choosing different dialogue paths. In addition, there are a few hidden 'Book of Fables' pages that can be unlocked from different choices and investigations. It's an engaging ride, and one that keeps you thinking and wanting for more.  Like I said, the gameplay is pretty minimal. When boiled down, it's comprised of quicktime combat events and investigation scenes in a mix of classic point-and-click gameplay and L.A. Noire investigation. I would've been perfectly happy with The Wolf Among Us being a TV miniseries, but it works very well as a game. The choice aspects and quick-time combat involve the player enough to be a true gaming experience. The combat is pretty fun, and adds to the game viscerally, but there are the occasional 'mash Q' button prompts that are impossible to win (according to the story). It's a tad frustrating, but not a big gripe. Overall, the gameplay is mostly comprised of ticking dialogue boxes, but the aforementioned high production values keep the game interesting.   The graphics are simply gorgeous. The game's aesthetic is alluringly saturated, giving off a distinct 80s/neo-noir vibe. The comic book mixed with realism is a style Telltale is known for, and it works just as well as it did in The Walking Dead. It's also clear that Telltale's trademark aesthetic is not a cheap way of using lower quality assets, but a deliberate artistic choice that wouldn't have worked in any other way. The Wolf Among Us is slick, gorgeous, and tremendously rendered.   It's also nice to know that Wolf doesn't use its fairy tale setting as a gimmick. This story could have only been told with these characters. It could've ended up as a lame detective story with normal characters reskinned as fables. However, the tales and the characters involved ingrained in the story's core so as to where they're integral. Good on Telltale for that one.   All in all, I'd be surprised if The Wolf Among Us is not among the ranks of my 'best of 2014' list when that day comes. A twisted, dark fairly tale mystery with immaculate visuals and superb voice acting, writing, and direction. Here's hoping we get a season 2.  4/5

An astonishingly beautiful, deeply complex space simulator. The X series have always ruled the economy-based space sim genre and Reunion is no exception. It provides a large universe swarming with life as tons of AI controlled ships each mind their own bussiness and freighters trading wares between stations, affecting the ware prices, which makes the economy quite dynamic and feeling real. Your actions will always have consequences- if you destroy a trader ship, it will not arrive to it's destination and the station will not receive the wares it needs to construct it's products. These were always the core features of X games, just like it's amazing and calming soundtrack, detailed and unique sector backgrounds and intricate 3D models, making it a piece of art. You will notice many tracks ported from the previous X games, aswell as many new ones- my favourite is Home of Light, Kingdom's End and Seizewell sector music.  So what's unique about Reunion, when compared to the other X games? Well to be honest, I enjoyed the older X2: The Threat way more. It's atmosphere was just a bit different, it's interface control was quicker to control, you weren't forced to use a mouse occasionally and could do everything much quicker through keyboard or a flightstick/gamepad (which I really recommend for X3). It's a-life also seemed more detailed as every station had it's set of fighter and freighter ships, each with their corresponding orders. Here in Reunion, everything is replaced with universe traders, which are randomly spawned freighters with commands to endlessly seek the best deals and don't really belong to anyone. After I couldn't find a single station that owns at least one ship, I thought it's a bug. The interface of Reunion is difficult to get around at first and lacks propper organisation. Widescreens weren't so common back then so the windows aren't taking much space either, although there are no issues (except the main menu). There are also no station hangar interiors like they were in X2. Ships now usually dock from outside instead, into a limited number of ship holders. The speed of all spaceships is also reduced so the usage of the time acceleration becomes a must.  Don't forget to visit the Egosoft forums, for mods and gameplay guides. X-Tended Mod (XTM) is probably the biggest one, but if you want to play the X games sequentially and take on Terran Conflict later, then it may spoil you lots of content as it ports most of the ships and stations from it. The cockpit mod is also really good because defaultly there are no cockpits in Reunion, unlike at X2. New Horizons seems like a nice alternative to XTM. Another great mod is CMOD II, which adds more immersive sounds and Military HUD which.  If you're looking for a good space sim gameplay and don't care about the X story, skip this game and go directly for X3 Albion Prelude. If you haven't played the previous X games before, then Reunion is probably the worst one to start with and I would rather try the X2 for it's in-game tutorial that the X3 games lack. X3TC/AP could also be easy to start with if you don't mind reading some user made guides instead.  Speaking about the whole X series, Reunion is probably the worst one. The only good thing it brought to the series were updated graphics, which can still be called quite eye-candy today. Other than that, it's campaign is is about 20-40 hours long (depending on your X game experience) and I recommend playing it if you want to follow up with the story.

http://youtu.be/4-V8lrk-iWQ  I played for about 15 minutes.   Very, very basic graphics. But it’s meant to be an ‘indy’ game. It doesn’t look awful which is a good thing. It’s got a comical style I guess.  It is a platforming game that starts at the final boss. The game is put in reverse. You need to unkill squashed enemies, put coins back where you got them and catch back the fireballs you used to incinerate enemies. All the while avoiding creating a time paradox e.g. picking up a coin that you never picked up. The same music plays over and over again.  You have no control over directional movement. You character moves left continually while you tell it when to jump and when to grab fireballs. The time I played was about enough time to complete the first of 3 worlds. So it looks like if you want a few hours out of the game you would probably need to download other user made levels. Apparently they can be published on the steam workshop. I tried out the level editor briefly. It’s simple and easy to use.  At the rate I was going I would have completed the game in less than an hour. I don’t really think it’s worth the price. Even at the current sale price of $6.39 on steam. For the entire duration that I played the game all I did was press 2 buttons.  Click  here  for more let’s play videos =)

app_name: You Have to Win the Game
review_text: For the first few minutes of playing the game, I got addicted already.  A MUST HAVE.

First off, my ideal space game would be the flight and combat mechanics of Evochron Mercenary coupled with the depth and complexity offered in the free form sandbox of the X series. I believe this is the closest thing I've found to date and though it has it's issues and may put people off for now it's certainly one to keep an eye on. Despite the drawbacks which I explain further below I am still happy to have made the purchase and I plan to play often, it's still fun but mostly I want to keep tabs on the fixes and updates before I make serious run at the campaign and building my empire. More conservative space fans might want to wait it out.   The game has a lot of the old systems of the X games but right now they're mostly inaccessible due to bugs and design flaws but they are there and I have no doubt this will become one of my favorite games. It has that much potential but keep in mind that it's potential. I actually appreciate the new direction of the series and think that once the bugs are handled and some optimization is done more and more people will forget about the launch troubles and begin enjoying it themselves. My favorite comment on the design and a great description of what I love about the change was that they wanted to make traveling more fun, much like the GTA series where traveling is an extremely action oriented element of gameplay. I believe they have done that and I love the new travel system with highways and drafting, it's really cool and far more fun to use than gates at NSWE. The controller use in space is great and makes all spaced based elements look and feel more like a sim than a RTS as in previous X games where all the flying was mostly done on drop down menu's except when in a fight for brief periods. Traveling was never fun and took way too long. Make no mistake, X could never get by without menus and they're still around, using the controller is the downfall here as with limited options you have select categories and follow the branches although the menus seem to make things easier to manage and operate like property, market and crew functions even if their navigation is less than perfect. All in all it's a huge change that incorporates a lot of the depth of the X series although not as micro manageable as some seem to prefer it but it's packaged in an action sim which is my dream come true. One of the newest features is the ability to visit and walk in stations which I believe is a great idea and it looks good. It's not amazing by any means but for a feel of actually visiting places by leaving and boarding your ship I like it. There are lockers and chests to raid for loot via Borderlands but mostly it's to make contacts with people to hire on as crew or to peruse their wares as merchants. I haven't found it something that you have to do often if you'd like to avoid it I imagine it would be possible to minimize station visits enough to not be bothered. My problem so far has been the optimization as space looks great stations barely chug along which is disorienting but otherwise acceptable, but only for now. I expect all this to improve. Another feature and what some would consider a drawback is your limitation to only fly one ship from the cockpit. It's a cool ship though which you can walk around, it's small but the views are amazing and I love the HUD. You can upgrade your ship plenty to keep you feeling more powerful and you can still own and remotely fly and manage your fleets, stations and vessels and I feel it was a good tradeoff.   I don't want to say the game is not worth the $50 tag because it is although it is NOT worth that much as it stands right now. Pick it up if you are a diehard fan but prepared for frustration. I would recommend everyone keep a sharp eye on the updates which have already been streaming out regularly. This certainly was a rough launch but anyone who writes off a game for a bad launch isn't much of a fan as this is certainly nothing new and it will continue as games become more complex and demanding and users machine become more diverse. I'm not defending anyone except my passion for great experiences through games and as a disabled combat Veteran I am more grateful for the developers efforts in bringing this amazing world to life than I am upset with them for a substandard release. Everyone benefits if it gets fixed so let's just hope for that. Egosoft didn't launch and run and that tells me these guys like what they do and are as embarassed about the issues as some people are upset by them.

You don't get the 'I'm playing a mobile game' effect that was had moving to 5 from Beyond the Sword. There's 100% more content than I honestly expected.  That said, there's definite room for improvement and this recommendation is for Civ fanatics only. For everyone else, just wait a couple of years for the DLC and Complete editions to roll out. By then, the major problems like pacing (the tech tree just zooms too quickly bc of the new boost mechanic) should be fixed if only by addition of more content. To anyone else expecting great AI to appear at some point, it's not going to happen until stacks of doom come back.

DISCLAIMER: This is the opinion of someone who recieved the game for free. This is a very cautious recommendation and I would definitely not recommend this at full price.   Qora is not a fun game to play but rather in my humble opinion presents a compelling enough world to keep exploring.  Qora is very much just a 2d walking sim in terms of gameplay (much of the game is just pushing 'space', 'up', and 'right'). Do not get this game if you are out for a game with any deep mechanics. Then what about the audience that enjoys games like the Stanley Parable and The Beginner's Guide? As someone who can accept a sacrifice in mechanical complexity for a casual but engaging world and story, to me Qora was an enjoyable experience. The aesthetic and chiptune music fit my tastes but if they don't fit yours then this game is probably not the game for you.   Qora started off actually pretty boring until about 5-10 minutes in (this is a short game, max 1-2 hours for 1 playthrough) when the happenings within the game actually started to become interesting. The bulk of the game has you exploring extremely varied and nicely put together locations as you try to piece together the past of the land. Depending on your actions there are actually multiple endings for some replaybility.  Overall I can safely say that I did infact enjoy my time with Qora but for the usual asking price of 10 USD I cannot recommend it. On sale however, if you are the person who doesn't mind casual failure state free experiences which are essentially more movie than game, then yes this game is worth checking out.  Lastly it has cards and very easy to obtain achievements :P

My biggest fear going into Dark Souls III was 'too much, too fast'. Since Dark Souls II released in 2014 the Souls train has been running at full throttle (DS2 and its DLCs, Bloodborne and its DLC, and now Dark Souls III and its DLCs). There is certainly something to be said of magic lost when something goes from obscurity to immense mainstream popularity so rapidly and in such quantity. While I do feel this has happened to a degree I am happy to say it didn't impact the end result of Dark Souls III as much as I thought it might.  Dark Souls III is a fantastic entry to the series and a solid continuation of the themes and threads sewn in the original Dark Souls. While Dark Souls II was a wonderful game and definitely Souls it lacked a little of what made Dark Souls such a landmark experience. Dark Souls III has been a return to form in that regard, borrowing from and further elaborating on lore and concepts from the original game. It does just enough of its own thing, though, to have a unique identity.  The visuals are fantastic, the gameplay is intense (borrowing some of the speed and viscerality of Bloodborne), and above all else the soundtrack is stellar. The level design is challenging and varied. Similar to the other games in the series most of the bosses are great with a few exceptions. A few of the bosses are easily the best ever produced in the series. If I had any complaints it would be related to PVP balance. PVP heavily disfavors invaders and some balance tweaking is needed.  With all that said where to rank this within the series? I think it sits either equal to or just behind the original Dark Souls. It is certainly the far better game from a gameplay and technical perspective but it is also five years newer so this should be a given. The only reason it might not be better than Dark Souls is that you can't freshly replicate a lot of the awe and wonderment that the original title created. You can create a different type of awe and wonderment but one that by nature sits atop the pillars already erected by the original.  Where do I rank this to other games not Souls? Well, that's a whole other story. I'm afraid Souls has done so many things so right that I am jaded to anything else. I would take any Souls game over any non-Souls game every time. Dark Souls III has only further cemented this feeling.  If it is truly the last game in the series then what a series it has been! I hope FROM moves in an entirely new direction but at the same time I hope they retain much of the mastercraft they have displayed while working on this series.

But to me, it just isn't anything what it was hyped up to be. At the 130 hour mark I finally got bored and uninstalled.   Now I'm sure other people will disagree. This is just my opinion. That's what a review is, an opinion based on personal experience to help someone decide weather to purchase a product or not.   If you're expecting a 'better' Fallout 3, you will NOT get that experience. But if you just want to play the game and experience it, then I'm sure that this will bring you many hours of enjoyment!  PS: Ignore anything negative that people say about graphics, the graphics are outstanding.

Overall, it's a OK+ game, definitely not bad, but certainly not plausible in terms of the game design. It is evident that the creators put decient effort in storytelling, and if you do read everything on notes, you'll find that this is a delicately written, engating story with twisted ending and siganificant indications for humanistic thoughts on warfare and the future of mankind. However, I find that the tasks and puzzles from the midway through started to be far-fetched and some (for sake of not spoiling the story, I won't mention those puzzles) unnecessary. This is particular true somewhere near the end of the story.  There are some shortcomings of the game design not exclusive to this game, but very common to the similiar kinds. E.g. the repetition of characters' lines without updates after the progress of the story. This is usually found in the indication bubble of an object. Say, I found something for the first time in the game, and by clicking on the object, the indication says xxxxx, but after I solved the puzzle, and return to the same object, it says exactly the same line which is no longer relevant to the story at that point, and sometimes, the un-updatableness of indication bubbles caused some confusions to me as I did not understand if I still need it or not.  I started to lose patience till 2/3 of the game as the puzzles were challenging but not in a logical way, and I couldn't go further with the story and got stuck by the puzzles.  However, having said that, with the price I paid during super sale, I'm satisfied with the game, but not excited about it. I do recommend the game as the story is pretty deep if you really try to analyse it as literature, and I understand the creation of a game is far less as easy as writing a review.

There was always something new to see when you saw those lights flash.  The mood was great, the events were spooky and I only recall maybe two very surprising elements, but just playing through the experiences had me on edge no matter where I looked.  The game does not have immediate replay value; however, if you wait long enough for your memory to fade and come back to it again later, it is still fun and even more fun to foist this on a family member who has not played it or see you play it.  For an inexpensive, yet well done VR experience, it has my recommendation.

This is a great and relatively short game!  I quite enjoy perspective-based puzzle games (although there don't seem to be too many around). This game was no exception.  It had a beautiful and simple aesthetic, and was quite colorful. The sound/music (mostly an ambient, atmospheric humming sort of sound), didn't distract from the puzzles, and sounded excellent. Thankfully, the character's walking pace was quite quick.  The character is controlled simply by clicking where you wish to go.  The puzzles were, on the whole pretty good. Sometimes the amount of things that were moving when rotated things was a bit much, however. Occasionally, the puzzles felt as though I just solved them by trying a bunch of different things out. Also, sometimes the puzzles hold your hand a bit, which can be either good or bad, depending on the difficulty and your prowess at this game.  After completing a majority of the levels, I found myself just grinning at the overall cleverness of the level. Most of the puzzles were satisfying to solve.  Overall, this was a great game, and I'm glad I impulse bought it! :D

It will kill some hours and then you will probably never touch it again.   Meh 3/5, would bang.

It's beautiful, and fun, for a little while, but ultimately has some powerfully bad design decisions that take awak from my enjoyment, or even interest in playing the game.  For games like this, I always like playing a more free form game, (challenge mode for PC) and while it's here, and functional in all it's glory, it feels like it was just added as an after thought, and given no attention. The playfields are just flat, featureless expanses. Not a river, valley, or even so much as a hill to speak of. This makes building a park a boring prospect, and making the landskape interesting requires money that's offered in paultry supply. You can make maps in freedom mode, (basically a creative mode with no money) but these far more interesting maps you yourself construct can never be moved over, leaving them pretty set pieces with no real function or fun.  I hope this gets changed in a future patch, but seeing as the devs ignored the community on this...and nearly everything through their early beta, and the game is now considdered full release, I don't hold out a lot of hope.

I highly recommend Borderlands 2 to any gamer that is a fan of RPG-style shooters, witty dialogue, and psychotic midgets that run around screaming '♥♥♥♥♥' at the top of their lungs. The entire game had me gravitating between laughing myself silly at the dialogue and being scared when a random Badass Pyro Thresher comes out of nowhere.   Unlike the first Borderlands game, Borderlands 2 can be played alone. My experience with Borderlands was that they focused too heavily on making it a 'co-op' game, to the point that early in the game you are fighting enemies that you can't kill. That and the guns were both horribly inaccurate and horribly low-powered. Borderlands 2 on the other hand can be played perfectly fine alone or with friends thanks to the enemies scaling to a balance between all players currently in-game, of course difficulty does make a difference. On Easy for example there's not too much of a challenge even with four players.  I will say that Borderlands 2 has it's flaws, such as the occasional gravity-defying Spiderant or Hyperion loader, or even bright blue and bright red glowing Bullymongs, but none of them impact gameplay at all, and in my opinion just add to the random hilarity of the game.

app_name: Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis
review_text: For starters, I was not born around the time of old point and click adventures like LucasArts', but i would imagine that many people played them. why? Because LucasArts made what are quite possibly the best story driven point and click games. Where to begin? The graphics are as they were 20 years ago, and yet they still feel fresh, so nostalgia will hit hard with older gamers. I have not played the original, so I am not sure if this version is different in any way, but it has the original music, voice acting and graphics, so I assume it's the same. Even though i was born around the time where they started moving on to making more Star Wars games (which were mostly good, and one of them is one of my most favourite games of all time), I still wanted to try these. Last year, I saw a show with a segment about LucasArts' history, and they talked about games like Loom, Indiana Jones And the Fate Of Atlantis, Full Throttle, The Secret Of Monkey Island and many more. And I always like games with a big focus on story lines, and I decided to try them since it would be a break from Call Of Judy or whatever generic shooters are called these days. So I found a bundle with a few of LucasArts' games, and while the controls are a bit fiddly at first, I think Loom, The Dig and Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis will keep me playing 'till the credits roll because of the stories that just make you reflect after the end. It's like reading a book, you say you will read one chapter, and then you read the whole book. It's games like these that make me wish I was born in 1990. Nowadays it's all about pwning n00bs in halozone with masterchef or something. Sorry for the long review about me saying how much i like the stories in these games, But after all, the one thing that sticks around in your mind about a game, is it's story. Thank you for reading.  Edit: Another thing about their games, is that the writing is so good, It's always full of humour.

Gemini Rue is a point and click adventure game with sparse and limited fighting mechanics in various stages of the game that takes place in a dystopian sci-fi future that could be inspired easily from movies like Blade Runner. As it is expected, you explore the environment and interact with people and objects in order to solve puzzles and eventually proceed. There is also an item called the communicator which lets you communicate with various characters throughout the game but also provides you with all the necessary information and mission status.  You play the role of a man named Azriel who was an ex assassin but now has left his past behind him and works as an officer against the organized crime which is run by a mafia-like group called the Boryokudan. Your formal mission is to aid a man who later helps you on your true mission which is to acquire information about your lost brother Daniel. After infiltrating the Boryokudans and reaviling that there is a secret facility where people are sent there to have their memory whipped out and re-trained to become criminals and that your brother was sent there, you gather your crew and set a new mission, rescuing your brother.   The storyline despite being short is quite compelling and there are nice twists at the end as expected. The overall theme of the game is nicely expressed through the art style with retro like graphics and atmospheric music. However, I would really prefer it if instead the 16 bit overlay the visuals were a little more detailed. In comparison, the audio quality is really impressive for being an indie game. The audio logs are rich and clearly there was put a lot of effort into it. So, the audio and visuals are not side by side in my opinion.   All in all, Gemini Rue is a solid adventure game following the steps of the classic ones, yet it feels fresh and modernized. All friends of the adventure genre should definitely try out this game.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Rome 2, ahhhhh Rome 2.....   So much hype, so many expectations, so many promises.... Here is the thing: Overall, now is a solid game, on single player only( at least for me and my buddy).   Almost 2 years after release how does the game fare? Well, the thing is, this game fails because of what sorrounded and still surrounds it, from a design and gameplay perspective.   Allow me to elaborate, if you will: this game was HYPED to no end. Several live action videos and developer diaries, ALL of it to deliver an inevitably weak game. From a design perspective, many strange decisions: unit cards, UI, retainers, generals, agents, faction bonuses, turns per year are just some features that seem like a downgrade from previous Total War games, namely the previous title, Shogun 2. Gameplay wise, the battles suffer a lot, and is in this aspect that most critics arise from. Unit collision is still an issue, cavalry still has absolute no way to it, kill moves are quite franckly a poor way to make units interact with each other ( even tough recently they appropriated the animation of projectile hit to kill other soldiers without using the kill move mechanic, which is just lazy).  Faction wise, there is almost no variety between them, (amazing that even though Shogun 2 has the exact same unit models and soldiers for every clan they still manage to give more variety and personallity than Rome 2's many factions). Seriously, aside from character skins and faction banners, there is no difference in gameplay between Rome and Carthage.  That is not to say that the game is a disaster, it is not. It has some good ideas, like province happiness, and graphics wise it is impressive, it is wonderful to see the ancient world more vivid than ever.  What ultimately makes this game a thumbs down for me is the preposterous practices on the part of the publisher and the developer. Sorry, no ammount of justification will ever make up for a broken game at launch ( don't believe me? watch a certain ' angry'youtuber review of this game); day one DLC also is unnaceptable under any circunstance, for it means it is content that was created along with the main game and cut and sold later to boost their profits ( I'm looking at you greek states DLC ( bought it later for 1 buck during sale) );   Also currently, at the time of this review, we are at PATCH 17! This is absolutely outrageous, I ask you fellow gamer, how can a game need 17 PATCHES to be in a working state? Simple, an uttlerly broken game. Even if the state has improved ( sfter almost 2 years.... yay....), the point is THIS SHOULD NEVER BE ACCEPTABLE. The thing is, the moment they released this game is when they decided it was good enough for sale, and this clearly wasn't the case, so, a Shameful display.  In the end, it is painfull, because this could easily have been the greatest Total War game ever made, a comeback to the glory days of Rome 1, now it is a reminder on how not to launch a total war game.  P.S. The most amazing thing, recently I played with my friend ( fellow Total War fanatic), a Fall of the Samurai coop campaign, over 60 hours of campaign and not a SINGLE issue, NO desyncs and smooth framerate. We tried to play a Rome 2 grand Campaign recently, and it crashed on turn 2...

app_name: Sea Dogs: To Each His Own
review_text: I want so badly to like this game. And I do, on the surface. But out of the many, MANY games I've played (not even just counting pirate games), this has to be one of the most stressful, painfully frustrating games I've ever played. After putting 22 hours into this game, I still felt like I was in a tutorial, and just got sick of it. I'll just put this into pros and cons:  Pros (I'll start off as optimistic as I can): - Cool weapons; also accurate for the time unlike some pirate games - I think this is the best pirate game I own GRAPHICS-WISE, yet also the one I run at highest FPS out of all of them! Sid Meier's Pirates and Blood & Gold: Caribbean have slightly worse graphics tbh, yet I get about 10-20 FPS more on this game, averaging at 70-80, even in large battes and highly populated areas. Hats off to whoever optimized this game, you did the one thing AC4:BF could not! - Acceleration, otherwise the game would be so slow it'd be unplayable. And I don't mean slow as in FPS, I mean the ships crawl like turtles unless you have it as 6x-8x speed. No idea why the devs intentionally had ships sail in real time by default, since every other pirate game I've played has already figured out that it's way too boring. - There are surely other good parts of this game, but none of them are apparent to me after my 22hrs playing, meaning they're too much of a pain to get to in the first place.  Cons: - The English translation is HORRIBLE! Run-on sentences, uncapitalization, misused phrases that don't make sense, etc... literally one of the lines is 'hello I need an information'. It's so weird too, because it's just stuff the player says, most of the NPCs have near-perfect English, as if they're uneducated like they would be in that time. At least 40% of the dialogue looks like it was written by a 9 year old. - The average player, including myself, will spend AT LEAST 4 hours... ON THE FIRST ISLAND! You don't even get a ship until you get a TON of money, and you have no choice but to do a lot of quests (some are okay) and steal from houses! And even if you find anything good, you have to sell it all just to get all of the supplies necessary to leave the island once you get a ship! On top of that, once you've sold all the things you'd rather keep, you ALSO have to sell most of your cargo! For at least 2 hours I was thinking, WHEN CAN I GET OFF THIS DAMNED ROCK?! I thought this was a pirate game, where's my damned ship already! There is also no tutorials, no manual, nothing. I've had to write discussion posts pleading for help at least 15 times before I even bought my ship. It's ridiculous. I only started getting a hang of the game maybe 10 hours in, and I'm usually good at getting into a new game. - Bad combat system. Your crew gets in the way during a boarding, even on a huge ship, and they get killed off by the eliter troops, leaving you surrounded by 20 angry pirates. The worst thing is, if you die, you restart at that boarding, and unless you win, it's impossible to abort: basically, save every time you do something major, or you could get stuck very easily with a ship that's too good for you, and dying over and over will get you nowhere, so you'll have to restart. Over my 22 hour playtime, I've gotten trapped like this two times, totalling to about a 3 hour set back each -- and I do NOT like redoing things. - Little to no gear upgrading. In my 22 hour playtime, not ONCE have I seen another sword or pistol or armor besides the ones I had in a store. All I got once was an arquebus from a special quest, but then I got stuck and had no choice but to go to a previous save, about a 3 hour setback. Damnit was I ♥♥♥♥♥♥, and I never saw the quest again. - Map navigation. You can't go anywhere without 5 pirate ships after you, and they usually outsail me, and you can't avoid fighting with them. Also, most quests give half the days I need to get to where I need to be, and if I'm just 2 days late, the quest not only fails, but my reputation with the quest's nation becomes HOSTILE! How the ♥♥♥♥ does missing a single shipment of cargo make a whole nation think I'm their enemy? That'd be like a kid having his homework in a day late, and then he gets expelled. Then if you wanna build the rep back with that nation, either THEY'LL be trying to kill you, or a governor for that nation will give you another quest -- that's twice as difficult! Speaking of difficult... - The EASIEST DIFFICULTY is what I've been playing on. If this is the easiest difficulty, then whoever plays the hardest difficulty will quite literally throw his computer out their window in rage and proceed to punch their tv multiple times... in the first 2 hours. - No cheats. I'm no cheater in most games, but this game needs it. This game also needs modding, which it can't be yet. All I need is a few extra pesos and maybe I could get somewhere in this game. If I could just have a large ship and crew, some good weapons, and enough cargo, maybe I'd actually continue playing this game. But for now, this game is just such a piece of crap.   TL;DR: There's a part of me that just wants to play this game, thinking 'maybe things will be different today?' but they never are. I want so badly to just have fun with this game, but alas, it's quite impossible. For this reason, this is the first game I am not recommending, which is so sad since this game has so much potential, and I've played many stupider games than this one, but this is the first on steam that I've truly needed to dis-recommend. For these reasons, keep your $20 in your wallet. If you want to ignore this review completely and buy the game anyway, I beg that you at least wait until a sale and spend only a few dollars on it, else you'll very likely be missing that $20 shortly.

A beautiful game on all levels if you're a puzzle game fanatic. This reminded me of Portal. And lately, I've been playing Glitchspace, Teslagrad and The Talos Principle - all games with different, unique, clever puzzle mechanics and interesting stories.  The Swapper is a side-scrolling platformer exploring an abandoned space station. Cloning yourself and swapping with those clones is the simple, but highly versatile method in which you advance through the story. This game is short, but well written, with beautiful graphics. This game is shared by our Steam accounts: all her discoveries would initially show her Steam name, but if she quit and relaunched, they'd suddenly show as if they were discovered by my Steam name. Quake live is a relaunch of Quake 3, and that is not at all a horrible thing. In an era of braindead shooters, and boring movement through flat unistring levels, Quake scoffs your Modern military shooters and rocket jumps it’s way up a wall.  If “realism” is something you want in a game then Quake is not for you (why you want to portray “realism” in a game about killing people is another issue entirely). Quake is instead about creating interesting game dynamics. Physics and all sensibilities be damned, they’re secondary to good game play.  Quake has more than just a few things going for it. The movement is perhaps the most nuanced and complex in all of gaming, The weapons balanced and finetuned over a decade and a half of balancing, the level design is the best in any fps, and the community...well ok the community is mostly elitist jerks like any other internet game, but dammit they’re quake jerks, a special breed close to my heart, spouting out the truths of the PC faith (Always us optical mice, MX Cherry Reds forever, 120+ frames or I’m not even playing).  It’s free. Even if you have no interest in arena shooter I recommend you hop on in and get a quick history lesson with the granddaddy of FPS.

I'm an idiot, and I approve this game. A review from someone who admittedly isn't anywhere near finishing.  Pros: Streamlined trading that actually makes it fun. Streamlined combat that's like Pirates! in space. I can actually broadside and shoot missiles down! Lots of opportunities for pew-pew and mining between destinations/missions. NPC models and voice acting are solid. Price: Cheap, even when not on sale. Responsive devs: I had a problem with a mission marker, and within hours, someone responded to me on the Discussion board.  Neither here nor there: Country rock soundtrack is evocative of Firefly. If you don't like it, there is an option to choose your own tracks. Missions: standard assasination and fetch outside the main storyline. Focus on combat may turn off more laid back space pilots (used to the X games, for example) Simple ship progression - You get enough money, you buy the next best ship and trade in your old one. The more expensive one is probably better. End of story (at least early on). I like the no nonsense approach, but some might find it unsophisticated.  Cons: Few NPC models. No ship appearance customization. Some slightly ambiguous mission marker issues ('Okay, I'm at the marker...nothing here...) No loop de looping or barrel rolls.  Again, as someone who was overwhelmed by the X series, and hasen't quite found my groove with the (admittedly excellent) Starpoint Gemini 2, Rebel Galaxy hits my sweet spot.

Let's address something right off the bat: Does the game have microtransactions? Yes. But they are absolutely not necessary to progress properly and the only advertisement for them is a 'shop' button in the main menu. I didn't even know about those before I bought the game and I played for a good 10 hours before I learned about them at all! Knocking the entire game just because there are ways to power your way through it by paying real money isn't very fair.   If they want to be greedy, so be it, it's not a problem as long as they don't create artificial obstacles for those who don't use the microtransactions... And they absolutely haven't done that, so it's fine. As long as you explore and don't rush through the game (which is what you should be doing in Deus Ex anyway) you will find plenty of everything, including Praxis kits for your augmentations. So let's get that out of the way. It pains me to see so many people giving this game a flat out negative review ONLY because they don't like the microtransactions.  As for the game itself, I think it's very good. I would definitely recommend it to anyone who enjoyed Human Revolution in the slightest. This game improves upon pretty much every aspect of the previous one. The gameplay is absolutely stellar, with more refined mechanics for both stealth and gunplay, new augmentations to try and a decent number of ways to tackle objectives. There is a nice amount of stuff you can find by exploring, which feels pretty rewarding and can really keep you from following your main quest for quite some time since the hub areas are decently sized.  In regards to the main story, it's the most disappointing aspect of this game. The story is decently written and engaging, with some pretty memorable moments and setpieces. However, the ending comes VERY abruptly and the game ends with lots of questions unanswered. It really feels like they have cut this game short on purpose with DLC and/or sequels in mind. It's really anticlimatic and quite a shame when the rest of the package is so well done. The stakes never feel quite so high as in Human Revolution and the finale feels more like the end of an act and not a whole story. I'd say that a big part of the most interesting bits of story in this game is found in side quests and exploration. I still got around 36 hours out of the game after doing everything I could find, and I'm all for quality over quantity so I still think this game is worth its full price... But I can't shake the feeling that they have cut many things that could have been here.  Before I am done, I need to talk about how the game looks. Boy, is it BEAUTIFUL! The graphics technology in this game isn't the greatest ever but it's very solid, and you can really feel the amount of detail and effort that went into the art design and the crafting of setpieces and scenarios. It's like Blade Runner on steroids at some points! I am very impressed with how rich and well crafted the visuals are, the game is clearly a huge leap from the last one graphics wise. There are some ugly things here and there such as grainy interactions between the sharpness filter and some faces, or the fact that body animations (ESPECIALLY lip sync) aren't up to par with the rest of the visuals when in conversations... But those issues are easily outweighed by the beauty of everything else.  I ran into some small performance issues at first, mostly because I was stubborn and tried to run maxed out textures with a single GTX 970. But after adjusting my settings a bit and the few patches that have come out, the game runs and looks very well. Your mileage may vary of course, that's par for the course with demanding PC games, but I believe that anyone who has a rig right around the recommended specs should feel confident buying the game.   Again, get this game if you liked Human Revolution at all. That is the bottom line. Despite having a mediocre story that's on the short side, the gameplay and environments still make it very worth your while.

Please note that the time played (as of writing, 2.7 hours) is not indicative of how much time I've actually spent on this game -- I played on mobile for a few weeks before this launched on Steam.  Shadowverse is a slick and creative card game unhindered by what other games have done. It uses some classic rules, but breaks others to suit its needs. That is to say, instead of being derivative, Shadowverse uses the genre as a foundation to create its own complex and cohesive game.  I started playing with a bit of trepidation, as I'm not a huge fan of the boobs everywhere art style. However, it turns out that there are also a lot of hot guys, so that's swell. The voices are cheesy at times, but in an enjoyable way. Overall, the game is aesthetically and aurally pleasing. The music's okay, but I admit I typically listen to podcasts while playing.  The port is pretty good. It could use some more hotkeys, but it definitely functions well. I greately appreciate being able to play in 60 FPS on my PC instead of my small phone screen. In terms of running on my computer and the ease of linking my account, it's flawless.  I heartily recommend this game to anybody who enjoys the genre, and would lightly recommend this to players new to the genre. Something like Hearthstone or a MtG Duel Deck might be easier to ease your way in, as the generosity of Shadowverse interestingly makes early ranked matches challenging -- everybody has some half-decent decks to make early, and you need to know how to use them. The game does have a story mode to practice in, but the AI is so dull compared to real people that I stand by my stance of 'maybe not your first game, but definitely the one you should stick to later.'

Great sequal in one of the best contemporary survival horror series. Dead Space 2 has improved graphics, slight but noticeable gameplay improvements that make combat that little bit more fun--yet that little bit less scary to beat but it's a trade-off that still works. The game's environments, sounds, rooms with flickering emergency power, etc., all as scary as the original. The enemies are very similar to those in the original but that is fine because it makes sense with the storyline and Dead Space has some of the most unique enemies in any game of its kind. AI is still great, unpredictable, and therefore quite scary.  All in all, this game improves on a lot of what the original already did well. For fans of survival horror, if Dead Space is a must-have, Dead Space 2 is right along there beside it! 9/10.

My friend gifted this game to me on release, and we were wondering if Boris will review it. A day or two later, Boris did review it. While I'll never be as cool as The Shashlik King, I am still a slav, and will therefore post my own review.  THE GOOD The game is a realistic depiction of Slav lifestyle in Russia, Poland and the Balkans. You can squat down, drink vodka/pivo/rakija, cossack-dance to hardbass while avoiding a charging bear, or have a bar fight. You can play as a variety of Slavic characters, from a classic gopnik to your babushka.   THE BAD Only three Slavic countries are represented (Balkans standing for Serbia). Also, you cannot eat semechki or drink kompot. And you cannot play as Boris the Shashlik King. And there is a distinct lack of mayonez.  All in all, a very cheap game completely worth the low price which will not have you on your end of month butterbrot recipe right after payday. If you're slav, buy it. If you're not slav, buy it.

Writing this for the “Love/Hate Relationship” community write-in qualifcation.   I just recently started playing DotA 2 as you can tell. I literally play every game there is with the group of friends I play with on (almost) the daily. Never played DotA 2 with them, however, until recently.   Always heard them talk about it, watched videos with them. Crazy stuff, countless possibilities, crazy combos. All requiring a good game-sense and immense skills when it comes to some heroes.  Never tried to get into it because I've played a lot of MOBAs (have a lot of knowledge on them) and I just didn't feel like getting into a new MOBA (to myself) that required me to learn a ♥♥♥♥-ton of heroes, their abilities, tactics & strategies, techniques, knowledge on how to counter certain heroes, core builds, situational items & just the store's items in general.  But lately, I've been feeling like taking up a challenge like that. I do not like playing a game being very unknowledgeable and possibly ruining the experience of others for the sake of my newbiness. As a new player, I try to play with a full-party being my friends. This means that, yeah, I'll mess up sometimes, but they'll be there to tell me what's right rather than bash me for doing what I did that was wrong. Even when we cannot play as a full-party, however, we happen to come across people that are understanding. Hard to believe, I know, I mean we do come across some toxic players, but hey, that's just inevitable in today's online gaming world.  But what if someone asks me, do I love or hate the game? What would my answer be?  I honestly love it & hate it. It's situational. There are times when I do really good being up against heroes I can instantly learn how to counter and play against, while my friends brief me up on their abilities before I read them real quick in lane. But there are times when ever if I'm told exactly what they do and how to counter them, I just cannot. Some heroes seem like they have really short cooldowns on abilities/ults that should have longer abilities, items that they get to upgrade their ult, etc, all that. It just seems unfair sometimes.   But I know, I'm not one to talk when I just started playing the game, everything is probably there for a reason & things cannot be 100% balanced but everything that I may think or claim is unbalanced or OP might just be normal when all this new knowledge is slapping my face.  I'll get used to it, eventually, I just hope I don't ♥♥♥♥ off a lot of teammates on the way.  I don't feed, I try my best not to, I usually don't end up with a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ K/D ratio (except for the last few games). But this is my love/hate relationship with DotA 2. It's amazing, but it's really difficult. I love it... But I hate it?   Nevertheless, another great approach from Valve.  Zitten

Outstanding adventure game.   I didn't realise that classic adventure games could be so immersive! I enjoyed latest Telltale work, but I would hardly place them in adventure genre. Gemini Rue on the other hand is a real classic point-and-click adventure game, though you could say that the biggest flaws and offenders of the genre were left out. You won't find any illogical obstacles and puzzles, pixel hunting or absurd item combining.   What you get is a great sci-fi noiresque story, where you are a detective looking for his lost brother on some god-forgotten planet. Later the game opens up and lets you switch between the two main characters, the detective and the missing brother. It really does feel like you're detective, getting dirty and doing the groundwork: asking around, getting true identity of people, searching information about them in database. Top this with action sequences where you can cover and shoot at your enemies and you really get an exceptional adventure game. Voice acting and writing is astounding and the story itself is intriguing and suprising. All of that in a indie game made by the handful of people! I did enjoy the visual style of the game and I think it befits the game, but I guess some people could argue that the resolution is too low.   Overall, superb game, highly recommended. A great reminder that the adventure games are a great way to tell a story and don't have to be obscure and plagued with bizzare puzzles.

I wish I could vote neutral, but I really can't thumbs up on this. Main reason: it's too clever for me. I get what it's trying to do and applaud the originality of it, but the execution brings it to a halt to me. Your task is to build certain molecules. Probably awesome if you're a chemist, but it beats others over the head with details. The actual game is layed out in a very, VERY minimal (even abstract) way, and because everything has to fit on a single screen, you either 'see' it or you're messing around. Spacechem certainly isn't the only puzzle game that can get frustrating, but to me, this is one where solving puzzles didn't wow me. Perhaps because the field is so small most of my approaches failed to start (meaning: you better have an elegant solution RIGHT AWAY because otherwise you can't tweak what you build). At first I thought this kind of game just wasn't for me...but after playing infinifactory (which is more or less this game in 3D - by the same makers), I now think it's because this game is just too minimalistic and has a too narrow playing field.

Very fun game that is perfect as the 3rd ghostbusters story. I will say though that the difficulty varies in very strange areas. Many of the fight segments with multiple basic enemies are significantly more challenging than the majority of the boss battles (the spider witch was confusingly easy). Some of the melee areas, most notably in the graveyard, were ♥♥♥-numbingly annoying. Fighting off the ground-spawning headstone crabs and zombies while avoiding a golem and attempting to catch multiple ghosts in the air was not very enjoyable, nor was the onslaught of kamikaze angels that you had to attempt to attach to a gate 3 times whilst bombarded with flaming arrows and yet more crab-stones. It didn't help that ray, who you are with for that whole segment, adamantly refuses to stop dying, especially when seconds away from resuscitating you. So yeah, it's hard, but the game on the whole has very well designed levels, the gameplay is engaging in that the act of trapping ghosts is genuinely fun, and the story is pretty cool (not to mention its great hearing the original cast again), and it is without question a better game to purchase than the wretch that is the new 2016 game. Also, this one's $10 and the other is $60.

After abou 100 hours into the game it does prove to be a decent game at it's core, despite it's shortcomgs their is allot to explore and to experience. perhaps in the long run a bit repatative.  The good: Two Islands to Explore, Ibiza and Hawaii. Ibiza is a nice starting area with plenty roads and off road experiences. Hawaii is were it gets more interesting allot more roads and slopes to experience same goes for offroading  Excellent fun to Trash in certain routes especially with a friend driving around.   Driving experience is very challenging, in a very positive way you can spend hours trial and error certain races/time trials.  Sometimes it does get a bit tedious. And in a negative way you discover a strange performance optimization, where to un-explained reasons you flip/tumble/roll or other. Even when there is no reason for every now and then the car spins at low speeds in certain hard corners (Like a front wheel locks even modern cars which have ABS now a days this shouldn't be happening) It doesn't happen that much. But watch out for gaps/jumps and what not you need to moderate your speeds here.   Haven't noticed it on Ibiza much (So not sure) but there are some mountain roads on Hawaii that haven't been finisched sadly lots of potentiol but very bumpy, instead of smooth driving. Not many just a few roads are like that and you'll have to find them to experience why.   Also to do advanced racing tournaments/cups you have to attain licenses all though a great idea, it leaves no margins for error in the sense you have no experience how the car handles and drive blindly the license objectives which are a bit annoying doable but feels a bit useless. For future iterations these things need to be updated/modernized or something new.   Regardless of all the negative points for me Personally The Driving experience does outweigh the negative aspects of the game. I truly enjoy touring around the islands and trying to find new fun twisties to master.   Online experience besides driving with a couple of friends haven't really tried the Challenges in a big degree yet also there are allot of players that use mods and what not some with good intentions and others with bad.  In the end to do everything is this game is a challenge and also a time sink to get the full experience.  I'm a gamer that usually play's allot of genre's with a few exceptions.

Known to be one of the most, if not arguably, 'The' most controversial game of all time due to its extreme and realistic form of violence. Manhunt's game structure is based straight off a cat and mouse game where running is most of the time your only option to survive. And if the hunters don't murder you, you murder them. Oh and during the whole process you're being recorded and monitored for entertaiment by a deluded director who saves you from your own execution in prison.   The praised concept regarding this game is simple. Kill, run, or die. It's like the SAW movies only with all the traps being replaced with uneducated, disturbed, bad-mouthed criminals bribed to murder you in any way possible. And this game made headlines due to how realistically players dispatched the enemies set to hunt them. The execution style killings you commit with each weapon, which by the way have 3 different stages each, are brutal and very detailed. Manhunt was one of those rare titles where its M rated content was not generated by violent interaction with monsters or creatures but purely between human characters.   If you're a fan of stealth, violent, suspenseful games, Manhunt is a must-play and one controversial gem you shouldn't miss.   10/10

app_name: Max Payne 2: The Fall of Max Payne
review_text: I finished Max Payne 2 directly after finishing the first game. I recommended that and the same apply's here.  In terms of change between the two games, I didn't really feel there was much. Bullet time is less about jumping while trying to shoot as many enemys as possible and more about triggering before entering a room and trying to clear the room before the bullet time's over. I have to admit I didn't really like the change, while fun in itself I always liked the more carefully planned approach of the original. The difficulty in general after how careful you had to be in the original was just a minor annoyance on what is otherwise a fantastic sequal.  This game stands the test of time nicely. You won't find it ugly or out of place with modern day games. The sound comes together just how you'd want it to, the game still contains that wonderful Max Payne theme. It ran well on my fairly modern system (Windows 8 64bit, 1920x1080 resolution etc) You shouldn't have any problems here although there has been known to be issues with other players. (Check the forums first)  I would have to finish by saying both Max Payne and Max Payne 2 are very similar, both are short experiences, both have fun, solid and replayable gameplay, wonderful soundtracks and great storys. I would fully recommend at the cheap price it sells at today.

I was extremely skeptical about this game, being a huge GoTs fan I was periodically checking up on this game when it released and the reviews were pretty heartbreaking. Finishing up the most recent season of GoT I was desperately needing my fix, so I picked up this in hopes that it would sustain me, and my god am I happy I did. The game is not perfect but its really no where near as bad as the majority of critics made it out to be. The story takes place right before the books/show start and it follows two characters of little known houses within the Lannisters Westerlands. Don't expect to see alot of characters popular in the shows (more from the books) Varys, Cersei, Half Hand, and Jeor Mormont are pretty much the only big names you'll run into. (Whether this is canonical or not no idea, but it could easily be put into the accepted lore)  The gameplay is not perfect (reminds me of Kotor) but still enjoyable, you select a sequence of attacks and the said character will then play them out. The one thing that bummed me out is you only have two permanent party members (though they are played separately) and all the others are temporary drop-in characters. All-in-all if you like GoT and want to experiance a little bit more of Westeros, and dont mind a less than perfect (it really isnt that bad, I had a fun time) combat system then definitly check it out. Even if you don't know the series pick the game up, the game is filled with codex's that help explain alot of the elements you see within the game.  +Also if youre debating whether to get this or the Telltale game, get this hands down, the story and game as a whole is much more satisfying.

I was really excited for this game when I first heard about it since I love puzzle games like Myst, The Room series, etc. But 40 dollars is too much for this game, way too much. This game tricks you with all of its pretty colors and mysterious imagery, you think you are going to be interacting with cool things in the environment like you do in The Room and Myst, but no... It is ♥♥♥♥ing mazes. All ♥♥♥♥ing mazes. This whole game is mazes. Sure they do a few creative things with the mazes but after a while they get so ♥♥♥♥ing boring. It ends up being really hard maze puzzle after really hard maze puzzle after really hard maze puzzle... rinse and ♥♥♥♥ing repeat. It really kills the game, the bordom and repetition.   It was so disappointing. When you find yourself looking up solutions to the puzzles, not because they are too hard, but because they are ♥♥♥♥ing boring and time consuming, then your puzzles have ♥♥♥♥ing failed. The best thing about this game is the (Spoiler) environmental puzzles. The first time you find one, it just blows your mind! That is where the game really starts to shine, but the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ thing about it is that once you get all the environmental puzzles it does absolutely nothing. It is so disappointing.  This game borders on the pretentious. It is more a work of art than a game, and I think that is what kills it. There is no satisfying ending or closure of any kind. Sure there is a eureka moment when you realize what The Witness is about, but the problem is that that happens halfway through the game. The rest of the game after that point is just pointless. The game treats it's puzzles like they themselves are the reward for solving them. Which would be fine, if the ♥♥♥♥ing puzzles were interesting instead of just ♥♥♥♥ing mazes! I am an artist, and I went to art school, I understand what this game saying. The problem is is that it is just boring.  This game ended up making me depressed, and not like a good kind of depressed like games like The Last of Us pull off. The Witness just makes you feel depressed, that this mysterious, colorful, and breathtaking world didn't live up to your expectations, that the second half of the game is pointless, the endings offer no closure whatsoever. And there is something else that I just can't quite put my finger on as to why this game made me feel bad. I just can't explain it. I guess it was just the disapointment. I was hyped for this game, and I thought it was going to be something it wasn't.   In the end this game feels more like you are in a contemporary art gallery than a world with a story that wants to be told. It is very subjective in it's interpretations. It is a mystery without an answer and it shows you that what matters is not finding out the answers, what matters is searching for those answers. I personally love these kinds of concepts. They are not normally explored in media like video games, and now I understand why that is. Boredom. The Witness isn't about what happens at the end, it is about how you get there, but like I said prior, that would be fine if how you get there wasn't through hundreds of boring maze puzzles.

McPixel is a point-and-click adventure game. Sort of. The basic premise is that you have 20 seconds to defuse a bomb in each scenario, with various things you can click on to get various outcomes. However, there are some real problems with it:  1) There is no logic to the solution. It's really annoying as it's just trial and error until you get the right solution, which gets really dull really fast.  2) One of points of the game is it's humour. Except it's really not very funny. It's just 'gags' most of which involve McPixel hitting or grabbing something and then dying 'comically'. It's just stupid, and not even in a ridiculous cartoon humour way. I played most of the 1st chapter and in that whole time there was one joke that I found remotely amusing.  Admittedly, I gave up on the game relatively early on, so it may get better later on, but I really don't think that's much of a selling point. I guess the best thing I can say about it is that it's really cheap, but there are far better free games out there, so again, that hardly works in the game's favour. Even at the low price, it's hard to recommend it. I paid about 50p for it in a sale, and even at that price, I feel like it's only just worth it. Overall, give this a miss, and spend your money on something better.

TL;DR – Disappointing endings for fans who want to play the hero, quite buggy, but still a good RPG using Fallout 3's engine and style.  A good followup to Fallout 3, developed by Obsidian (not Bethesda) using the Fallout 3 engine. Feels just like Fallout 3 but with some new weapons/items/characters and in a new environment. The game was quite buggy (good thing I saved independent save states almost constantly), but for the most part I enjoyed the gameplay and the story. That being the said, the ending left me feeling disappointed and unsatisfied. Fallout 3 had a great ending directly influenced by player choice throughout the game. Fallout NV had a zillion different ending options, which is a great concept revived from the original Fallout games, however NONE of the endings are fully satisfying for a player who truly wants to be a hero of the wastes. I did everything I could to have positive karma and to choose the most morally upright option, but every major quest path at the end of the game requires you to commit an act or two which gives you negative karma and leaves you feeling like your hands are dirty. There's a reason folks like stories with happy endings where good triumphs over evil. I'm disappointed that there was no undeniably 'good' quest path option for the character to take. There's merely a variety of greater and lesser evils, different shades of grey from which the player must choose to aid. I chose the least of the evils, and felt slimed at my pathetic ending as the narration reminded me that the least evil option was still evil enough to ruin the fate of a couple of factions. I was merely a pawn thrown into the immovable cog that is the Mojave's depressing fate. Not sure why Obsidian wants to force moral ambiguity on their players. I enjoyed the gameplay and the journey to the end, but was ultimately disappointed by the ending.

This game and the entire Fallout franchise have really set the bar high for all other games, with it's fluidity, optimization, as well as content and storyline.

After playing this game for almost 80 something hours and reading some negative reviews I felt like I needed to weigh in. This game was largely over shadowed by Metal Gear Solid the Phantom Pain, and was dismissed by many reviewers who had bias or little time to actually play and enjoy Mad Max for what it is.  This game is a hybrid of Far Cry 3 with the crafting system and collectables and Batman’s combo combat system, neither of which is a bad thing. Some reviews accuse the combat of being simple and repetitive…. Well that is true but I never got sick of pile driving wasteland scum or curb stomping an opponent. I enjoyed the story enough but where this game was the most fun was the vehicle combat! As many reviews have stated it was fun and intense, the vehicle upgrade and customization systems are really satisfying. My advice, pick this up during a STEAM sale if you missed it. Mad Max was a lot of fun and again if you enjoy the combat in the Batman games and the collectable chase of a Far Cry you won’t be disappointed.

Brilliant, damn amazing game! If you're looking to have a realistic experience of survival stand-offs be it PVP or PVE (player(s) V zombies), then this is your stop. Though, it's not as open world as DayZ for example. The game's maps aren't that big. But they're big enough for an environment, i.e neighbourhood.

I think the game is fun with friends or people that you can actually communicate with in a private server. Public servers are just a mess. -You will be half way through escaoe mode but a new player will join and will ruin the game for everyone. -A player dies and becomes a zombie with infinite lives and can control other zombies with poor hitboxes and latency with the fact that they can strafe makes it almost impossible for you to kill them. -You have that guy who speedruns the game taking as many items as he can and ditching the group only to get killed. -Escape mode(especially in Barlowe Square) turns into nothing but a boring game of pixel hunting for keys or bar cutters until you realize that some troll player probably through them in a unreachable corner or the game just broke. -Escape was meant to be coop but when a player dies it turns into a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ pvp because they turn into a zombie. I don't know why this was a good idea in the first place. -The game is a mess with invisible walls and unusable ladders which I thought there should've been more creative ways to block those areas. -There is no vote to change maps or restart game when it breaks from what I remember but I could be wrong. -What's even the point in having the bow & arrow in the game when you never see a bow or an arrow at the same place? -The hunted gamemode seems to be the only one that's good if you're not playing with friends but a lot of the maps feel like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mazes that if you were playing with a friend you will have trouble finding eachother. Also the fact that it's 'You win if you're the last man' is pretty lame. It would be great if there were some escape route or something and you all had to fight for it or you can band together with a friend but nope. -The game is just lacking with updates and content that it feels like your typical Early Access. There's just a lot of bugs with clunky gunplay and movements from some zombies being invincible to being stuck next to stairs and furniture. -Playerbase is getting close to dying because of the lack of content and boring gameplay -You might as well play No More Room In Hell instead.
