Crea  is a 2D procedurally generated sandbox RPG. It finally left “Early Access” and I was excited to try it out.  The game requires many hours of gameplay to be able to explore it fully. This is my  first impressions review  after playing for a few hours.   Let’s start with the good stuff:    Crea’s visuals can be described as “cute”. Colorful 2D pixel graphics. Everything is a little bit too tiny (I preferred to play with 150% zoom) but overall looks good. Music is nice and relaxing.    There is a lot of content: a huge world to explore, tons of skills to learn, recipes to find and items to craft.   Crafting system is pretty extensive but beginner friendly. There are so many items you can create and build. It is not available right off the bat. You unlock it by leveling up and researching recipes and materials.    Combat is challenging even when playing on “Easy”. Especially in the beginning before you get a chance to upgrade your character and find or craft better weapons and armor.    Unique monsters with different attacks and behavior. Some are very aggressive and will attack the moment they see you, the rest will only attack if provoked.   Developer is very hands-on with regular updates, progress reports and even Twitch streams. I recommend watching those if you want to learn more about the game and the people behind it.   Steam Workshop support (modding is highly encouraged). Steam achievements, cards and badges.     Here is what I didn’t like:    My main problem with the majority of sandbox games (and that includes Crea) is a lack of proper in-depth tutorial. There are a couple of tips at the beginning of the game that only explain very basic mechanics. Everything else you have to learn on your own, usually by trial and error. Some quests or tasks on the early stages of the game would make learning process much more entertaining.    This game needs dedicated servers for multiplayer or more convenient way to play with friends (or strangers). Right now you can only connect to someone else’s server by typing their IP address.    Some minor bugs. I haven’t encountered anything game breaking.     I am giving Crea a thumbs up. It offers fun and addictive gameplay with virtually endless replay value. If you like sandbox games, I don’t think you’ll be disappointed by this one.    PC Specs

I am an old Homeworld fan, so this may influence my review somewhat.  Having awaited for the next HW installment for a long time, I was positively surprised when I saw HW DoK up for pre-order a few days before release, and since I enjoyed the first games I decided to give it a go. Already having the remastered edition I got a discount on the buy. And since I pre-ordered I got the DoK Expedition Guide as well.   HW DoK certainly brings you back into the franchise - there is no Mothership nor space 3D tactics, but HW DoK has the same feel to it as the other games had - same type of mission videos, well told story line, music. The game evolves much like the original games - steady upgrading of your Carrier (Mothership) and units with research, while scaveninging as much resources as you can out of every map. Basicly, for the same reasons I liked the original games so much I also like DoK.  As others have mentioned the multiplayer maps are few - yet for me, this doesn't matter all that much, as I mostly stick to single player.  Otherwise, the main drawback of HW DoK, for me, is like the other HW games - It's so enjoyable it simply isn't enough of it and you keep wishing for more. (Perhaps that is why we like the franchise so much ?)   The Expedition guide adds a bit of history to Kharak and the Kushan people. Enjoyable read which expands a bit on the HW universe.

This is a quick review, summs up my short playtime of this game, and the things you will see if you start playing.  This is a class based voxel FPS with the building style of minecraft and a similar class system to a mixure of LoL and Team Fortress. Each week, three characters are free to play, and during that period, they cost less. Currently there is around 15 characters.  The gameplay is simple.Two teams of five, drafted with the matchmaking system od LoL.Few minutes when the teams are blocked from each other, this is the time to construct your defenses and set up a special offense strategy(like digging a straight tunnel right under the surface, just to place a respawn pad below the enemies base), then the game starts, time to shoot each other, destroy the enemies defense and base, while defending yours.  You need teamwork, much.Most people of the growing community understand that, but there are some kids raised on CoD and BF, who think their defense is supperior and everyone else just blocks them(some kid actually started remowing my turrets and radars from the places i placed them, just to get angry when i rebuilt them.), or think they will win the match by running and gunning(no, that does not work).  The graphics are good, for the style of the game, but it has pretty big requirements.The sounds are good.  I have only encountered one bug, when i, with the sniper class killed 3 enemies in short range combat with my revolver, because i did'nt take any damage until i was in prone.  You get XP and money VERY slow, which might be annoying to some people.That goes for the game in overall, its kinda slow.  The devs are amazing, they give the players every opportunity to express their thoughts, and they actually listen.  There are some problems, like the game is very much based on offense right now, and there are some very OP ways to win(digging a tunnel, building over the map and stuff like that.  So thats it.A good but still developing game of its kind(compared to many abominations), with a developing community, some bugs, and somewhat high requrements.I would say its worth a shot, its free after all.Beause its still in development, this might change in the future, but right now this game is just amazing.  I give this game 9.5/10. TRY IT! I KNOW YOU WANT TO!!!

Another FREE dating sim on steam!  Pros:  -The heroine is a smart girl who can come to inherit her grandfather's company. She doesn't expect anyone to save her but isn't afraid to admit she needs help either. She's also very booksmart, knows Taekwondo and is overall relatable.  -The game isn't soley about romance. It's got a lot of story to it related to the love interests personalities and family struggles, getting the heroine involved in their lives, putting her in danger and BEING LOGICAL ABOUT IT. We also see the heroine deal with her past and the loss of her grandfather, who she was obviously very close to and her possibly inheriting his company and what she wants to do with her future.  -I feel the romance actually ENHANCES the story rather than it being a requirement or an optional goal. It ends up being something you want to pursue to see where the story goes.  -There is too much story to explain in one review...  -The Heroine's friends actually serve a purpose unlike most dating sims where the best friend character is either encouraging or forcing the hero to date or hook up with people. They're also GxG options!  -The 5 demon characters are very nice characters, each of them is distinct and they don't sexually harass or abuse the heroine in a way that's offensive or borderline ♥♥♥♥ (unlike a few other otomes such as Diabolik Lovers and definately unlike 'literature' such as Twighlight or 50 Shades of Grey). They feel like very human characters despite the fact they're demons. They're also very human i that, i can see a real person acting the way some of these characters do. Their love for the heroine also feels genuine. And the fact they treat her with so much respect even when first meeting her is also something i appreciate about this game, especially since so many other books and games about dating a supernatural being can get so creepy when it comes to the romance aspect...  -The voicework is pretty good. I didn't expect a free game like this to be voiced.  -The music tends to loop, but it does set the mood for the scenes where the music is played  -There are straight and lesbian options for the female heroine to choose from (i even made a guide for one of the lesbian options)  - A few months after it's release, THEY ADDED ACHIEVEMENTS TO HELP YOU KEEP TRACK OF YOUR LOVE MAKING!  -IT'S FREE!  Cons:  - No Harem ending (seriously? You have 5 incubi... How could you not have a harem route with INCUBI BEING THE MAIN LOVE INTERESTS?!).  -The screenshots on the store page can be a little misleading. Those screenshots DO appear in-game but that's no the art style you'll always be seeing. The art style in-game can be a bit narmy and goofy to look at first time around, but it could be worse and it's not all that bad once you get used to the style. It's a bit rough around the edges but also very charming as well. And the game is free! It would be worse if they charged money for worse art or no art at all.  -The way you meet the demons and the antagonists are a bit typical at best, but because the 5 main demon characters are so human in their actions and behaviors, i honestly don't mind. The villains are typical but they also have somewhat of a charm too (notably Diana).  -Speaking of Villians and anatagonists though, only Diana is a possible love interest and only she gets some spotlight. The villians the 5 demon boys fight against is a Devil named Malix but his so called 'arc' is extremely short lived. It kind of makes me wonder if it was supposed to be longer and maybe the creator ran out of time, money or other resources? It's a shame as it feels extremely short lived, somewhat random and it could've had a lot more potential and possibly added 2 more love interests to the game and a variety of more endings. As a writer myself, i really get ticked off at wasted potential! I originally thought the Malix 'arc' was going to span a longer period of time, at least enough time where you could get to know each of the brothers, maybe help them train for their next fight with him.    you only get to interact and possibly get intimate with one of the demon brothers though, because Malix comes back almost immediately and depending on your interaction with said demon or even whether you chose to explore the mansion or not, Malix is easily defeated and is never heard from or seen again and may or may not be dead depending on the route you took when dealing with him.    -No trading cards or badges, which is also kind of a shame because i would LOVE backgrounds and emotes of the characters.  -My biggest con aside from the Malix 'arc' is that the game can feel extremely short. Most dating sims give you a good amount of time to get to know each of the love interests and maybe even some free time to interact with other characters who don't have routes of their own depending on the game or if the game is more 'visual novel' than 'dating sim' and vice versa. Sadly you really only get to interact with 1 of the demon boys per playthrough or not interact with them at all... We do get to see their backstories in the short amount of gameplay but because so many other dating sims give us more time to get to know their characters, it's a real shame Seduce Me didn't give us more time to know more than just 1 brother per playthrough! I expected to spend weeks or months with these characters (in a similar fashion to Persona 4 or Hatoful Boyfriend where dates can be skipped or looked over for the sake of keeping the story a good legnth), but instead we only spend about 2 days or so with them, which is FULL OF LOST POTENTIAL!  All in all, similar to my review of Everlasting Summer, there isn't any reason you shouldn't try this game out at least once. It's free so there's no risk in trying it out and it's very different from other dating sims i've played on steam or otherwise. Both of them have intellegent heroes that you control throughout the story and loveable love interests that you can easily get attached to and friendly with if love isn't what you're aiming for. However, this game can be very short and unlike other dating sims i've seen on Steam, you can only get an oppertunity to romance 1 character per playthrough. In addition there is a lot of lost story potential when it comes to the Malix 'arc', lost potential for character development because you can only romance 1 character per playthrough, lost potential for non-romance routes   because to get the demons to stay you're pretty much required to become intimate with one of them  , lost potential in exploring your options because the game play is so short, lost potential in exploring your options because you pretty much have to interact with the demons or not interact with them at all, and wasn't interacting with them kind of the point? There's too much lost potential to explain in one review... Which saddens me as a writer to no end.  Despite the lost potential and short legnth however, I absolutely LOVE Seduce Me because of the choices it made with the characters (like not making the heroine an airhead, not making the supernatural love interests creepy and/or abusive toward her, having the heroine's friends have an actual purpose in the story unlike most dating sims, ect!).  All in all, i give this game 3.8 Spies saying 'Seduce me' out of 5

This is a fun, creepy-as-♥♥♥♥ teen mystery drama. There's very little to dislike about this game and very much to like. The voice acting is really good. The music is suuuper effective (seriously I never want to hear that tape-rewinding theme again. Ever. And I mean that in the most praise-worthy way possible). The art is beautiful. The story is interesting, and in my opinion well told. The relations between the 5 lead characters are the highlight of the game, and that's what the dialogue choices affect (not so much the plot, if that's the kind of game you're looking for). There aren't a ton of cliches here, just well written characters that are all distinct and interesting (except maaaybe the best friend?).   One thing that bothered me was the way some of the dialogue choices were handled. Not the dialogue itself, but the way it played out in the scene. When a choice pops up you have three options of what to say. There's a time limit, though. Nothing pressing, but the dialogue boxes fade over time, and if they disappear, you'll say nothing, and that's a valid choice. The problem arises when you have a dialogue choice while other characters are still chatting. You'll want to get in your choice before it's too late, so you select something while the characters are chattering and when they're done saying what they say, you say your line. Great. Except not, because sometimes (not all the time, and that's why this is a problem) when you select your choice, it just interrupts the conversation, and you'll never get to hear what the other characters had to say. If it was one way or the other (player dialogue waits vs. player dialogue interrupts), it would be fine, but both happen in the game, and there's no indication in any given case which form your dialogue is going to take.  Other than that minor annoyance, there's not much to complain about. Things to note -   -It's a side scroller, but it's not a platformer. There's absolutely no skill-related challenge here, so if you wanted to call it a walking simulator (or talking simulator) you could.  -There's no running. You'll walk throughout the game's world at the same pace the whole time. Most of the time you're walking with another character and there's dialogue to fill the walking space, but it's possible you'll do lots of extra backtracking to get certain collectibles, some of which are story related (but still optional). If you do an extensive amount of that, expect it to be sloooooow. -I have 11 hours in the game, and I played it through twice (there's a New Game+ mode that is definitely worth playing), so a single playthrough is probably about 5 hours give or take an hour depending on how you play.   So, good game, worth the money, two thumbs up!

Sims 3 is a simulation of................ LIFE!!!!!  The game is complex and well designed enough to let the gamers enjoy the possibilities of 'life' as we know it.  It is vast enough for the gamers to see every corners of life first hand but it isn't detailed enough to show every little miniscule details.  Depending on how creative 'you' are, the game can get extremely fun or extremely deadbeat-boring if you arn't.  The game offers some intense customisation Houses, sims, jobs, family, are some aspects that are fully customizable down to every individual faces.  It is a must try and a worthwhile buy(base game) quality wise.  They have their own version of a contents sharing communitites, the gamers are able to download various player made people, buildings, objects from the internet.  Similar to EA's spore, this is one of their trademark franchise that has not failed them, (for a damn good reason.)  The pros are explained above, the cons are:  -The game greeds money, it really WANTS you to make purchases for ingame items, DLCs etc.  -DLCs make the game more fun, THEY ARE INDEED FUN but imagine how much money you'll have to pay for various DLCs you want.(if the prices have dropped to primal dollar values then reconsider the statement above)  -The game can get addictive, it can seriously chew up some valueable time.  -After you play the game for a long bit, it can start to lag horribley due to cummulated cache and such, so consider ventilating a bit.  - Sims4(or versions above?) would be out. It would be more versatile than the previous sims3 and possibly more fun.  -It is reccomended you use Origin :(   If you 'love' and 'somewhat respect' their game, consider buying it... If you wish to be frugal and do not want to spend every penny for their DLCs then please torrent safely :)

app_name: Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Chaos Theory
review_text: The Splinter Cell series has always had a special place in my heart and soul.  Chaos Theory is the most significant fragment within that series - no competitors (but Blacklist does come rather close).  If you are new to the series, or you'd like to try out the games, then Chaos Theory is the place to start.  It is the prime example of a game done right, and I highly recommend you give it a try.  Things you will love about it: - funny accents  - smooth gameplay - great story - great soundtrack - great map design - the multiplayer was great back then, so bring in a friend for CoOp - flexibility in play styles - loadout selections (this was the first time for Splinter Cell games) - even more funny accents - etc.

A bit of missed opportunity.   The moment you start playing Mirror's Edge you immediately feel that it is something special. The view of the city below you, the pure and sterile look, the smooth and seamless acrobats.  The problem is, the more you play the game, the more it feels unfinished.  Don't get me wrong, Mirror's Edge is a good game but it gives you the feeling that had the developers had a few more months to smooth out the edges, it could have been a masterpiece.  The frame rate occasionally stutters horribly. The story barely glues the levels together. The pacing of the game is all over the place for no apparent reason.  As it stands, what you get is an awesome game concept sitting on a very pretty game engine and it still worth exploring for any gamer. No other game will give you the Mirror's Edge experience.  Here's hoping that the eventual sequel can remedy the mistakes of the first game and reach the heights that it was striving for but never got to.

Mass Effect is a shooter / RPG hybrid. This sci-fi game is reknowned for its storyline, dialogue and character customisation. Conversations between you, your team mates, residents of various locations and even enemies are completely controllable, with players having the ability to select essentially hostile, neutral or kind responses. As a result, characters may attack / ally with you, or even live / die based upon your decisions. Furthermore, the camapign can be replayed dozens of times and your choices can change everytime.  The Good Cover based shooter that actually works Dialogue options keep replaying interesting Replayability. virtually unlimited, with multiple endings.  The Bad A.I teammates die too easily / become a burden In-game puzzles to unlock doors is unnecessary Inventory screen can be confusing Graphics. Environment is great, but close-up character detail is average. 1 hit kill enemies (snipers) are frustrating and common Mid-level loading pauses.  Score: 9.5/10

If the Portal series is about bending space to your will through the use of the ASHPD, then Quantum Conundrum is about bending dimentions to your will with the help of the IDS Device. I like how they lead you in gently with each new Dimention, with the skills you picked up in each dimention being useful later on.  True, I did suffer a bit of problems with a few of the buttons you have to press - namely, the ones in 'The Greatest Puzzle In The World... Tribute' and the last one in 'Only a Minor Setback' - although this is possibly due to my laptop. But still, while it might be short, it has a good story - one which made me want to write a fanfic on it - and good puzzles, especially how in 'Only a Minor Setback' you need to deal with losing dimentions as you progress, until the fourth room, where you've only got Fluffy to use.  And the ending... Well, not to spoil anything (unlike the fic) but there is a possible sequal hook in there. Not that the game needs a sequal, though - but I wonder what will happen now that things... well, you'll just have to play the game to find out.

I'm a long-time Hitman player and , in short, It lacks words to express my deep disappointment for this game.  Think about everything that made the series so epic erstwhile: they screwed every little bit of those things.  No more world-wide contracts,no more assassinations and briefing. Instead, we were 'gifted' with an uninspiring and boring story filled with a low quality drama, and with a cartoonish cowboy as an antagonist. Not to mention the brat who 47 has to protect. Such drunken-written story feels so displaced with Hitman's Universe. It seems like a impeccable manual of how to ruin a game. I don't remember playing a game so poorly immersive. ( Blood Money had some flaws, but one can not question its great atmosphere)  Not enough? Wait there! It gets worse : you kill some antagonists through the medium of CUTSCENES. How heinous is that? Were they trying to breake some fans hearts on purpose?  I'm holding myself not to rate this failure in shape of game a ZERO and i won't do so just because of the engine, the well-polished graphics, the killing and shooting system and last but not least, the game's length.   All those factors make the game enjoyable for the newcomers to the series; all of whom, comprehensibly, doesn't give a **** if the devs KILLED the Hitman Series or not.  In summation, that is what i am feeling about this game: so much potential WASTED. The most valid rating for this game would be 5-6/10. Now you can see how laughable paid off was IGN's review. Shame on you IGN

The game's about sneaking, making moral choices and abusing the quickload key.  For me, stealth-based games aren't really a personal favorite, but I still like Dishonored, even though it can be really frustrating at times. If you mess up, it's gonna come back to haunt you later on.

I just finished the game. I couldn't express in words alone how amazing this is. After trying it for the first time I was instantly hooked and found myself unable to stop playing. Real life? I didn't care for it anymore. Problems and worries? Gone from my mind as this reality became very much consuming. I found myself thinking of this game constantly, what I could've done better, what mistakes I could've fixed if I only saved more often to correct them, my noobish start which made things turn for the worse in the end... oh if only. It's been years since I've felt so immersed in something, so taken by another reality. Something I haven't felt since childhood.   This game made me have to think, and act swiftly. It made me think all were attackers, and would punish me when afterwards I'd realize they weren't. If I made a mistake like avoiding someone who'd offer a clue to the next part of the story, it wouldn't screw me over by never allowing me to progress like other games do. Most of all, it allowed you to choose how you would act, and take out targets and allow you to take multiple ways to get to one destination. That quality is what most games miss, and the option to choose peoples fates and your own is what made me fall so hard for Dishonored.   There were times, during the game where I got annoyed with certain things, mainly the guards. They'd repeat the same things over and over like a broken record. It's nice to know where they are, but perhaps a wider variation of what they say would be nicer next time. I don't want to hear harland garland 50 times in a row.... whatever the hell that's supposed to mean. Yes, the guards like to mumble that a lot.  Now the ending.... note I've only played this through once, so this might be biased due to multiple different ones. The ending for me was bittersweet. It honestly brought me to tears. I don't cry for a story unless it's good, and this, and the game itself, managed to do that. So overall, this game, from start to finish was amazing. I'm glad a friend got this for me, I'm thankful I spent the time I did playing it. I have no regrets. Please buy this game at the full price with all the dlcs so the developers can keep making more like this. I'm highly looking forward to Dishonored 2, and any upcoming games made by these people in the future.   TL;DR for ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s: Buy it at full price with dlcs it's amazing.

initially i wasnt a huge fan when it was first released. I found it ok at best and hated the fact that i had to finish all campaigns to access mercenaries mode. Cue news of the remaster i decided to reinstall and give it a go.  On second attempt i actually learnt to appreciate this game for what it is, an unashamed B movie (the sort you find on Syfy channel). The gameplay and combat are fun once you learn its systems and while the story is somewhat lacking the way all campaigns merge at times i thought was clever. Mercenaries is by far my favourite mode especially no mercy (huge L4d fan) and it really comes alive playing online with a partner. So onto the singleplayer  Leon campaign - enjoyable, very similar in the first few chapters to re2 Chris - think Gears of war, enjoyable again, very over the top and good fun Jake - borrows on elements of re3 (ustanak bearing a slight resemblance to the nemesis) good varied gameplay, hated the first chapter though Ada - least favourite of the four, i can see what the developers were trying to capture with the puzzles but ulitmately i found it fell short  Overall, now the hype is well and truly over and it has seen a drop in price I think it is well worth anyones time. Good in singleplayer however should be played co op to gain the most from this title. Mercenaries is well worth the price alone.

I can whole-heartedly recommend Wasteland 2 to anyone that likes 'squad RPGs' (i.e. RPGs where you create a group of characters with individual strengths and weaknesses and develop them and their synergies further during gameplay).  The game's story is well-written and leaves a good sense of progression within the story and the game world. Also, there is some replayability thanks to the main story hubs featuring multiple endings / alternate solutions. The different groups of interest involved are also consistent and well-written.  Character development could feel a little bit more significant, e.g. via perks and special traits unlocked (will be addresses in the upcoming 'Wasteland 2 Director's Cut' version, great!).  Overall, with Wasteland 2 you'll get a full, well-crafted and well-executed RPG package.

Many people have said this is reminiscent of the Sega Genesis era. However, I'd say this game surpasses that era. Honestly, this game is action packed, the gameplay does have a learning curve (meaning you won't just blast through it in a day). And the bosses...are epic, and will punish those who can't keep up with their patterns.  As for the story, I can honestly say it rivals a good JRPG in terms of storyline. And the voice acting is top-notch (These actors actually put real emotion and personality into their roles. It may be one of the few times an anime-like story actually has excellent English voice acting)  Some have compared this game to Sonic the Hedgehog, and that's a fair comparision. But Freedom Planet has managed to capture everything that made Sonic legendary, and even improved on it in many ways. Sonic Team could stand to take a few notes from Freedom Planet, because this game is how you do it.  In my opinion: If you go for this, you'll be getting an excellent game for your money.  Lots of platforming action, surprisingly tricky boss fights, and a story (and voices) worthy of a Square-Enix JRPG masterpiece.

Grow and develop your own company (which you can name, buy trucks for, and buy garages for) to become the ultimate trucking company in the U.S. (California, Nevada, Arizona so far)  WHAT I LIKE -  -The defualt trucks are the 'kings of the road' -Both Steam and exotic mod support -LOTS of customization for the trucks -Default trailers look amazing -Realism includes fines for disobeying the law, speed limits, traffic lights, traffic, other truck traffic, landmarks (e.g. Golden Gate Bridge) (PLUS A LOT MORE) -Free map expansions (Arizona) -Little to no crashes (unless using a corrupt mod) -No 'super-computer' needed (runs smoothly on mostly anyting)   WHAT I DON'T LIKE- -Too many police cars around -Very high citation prices (raised if police are involved or around the incident) -Only 3 default truck designs (unless modded) -Speed limits are high on very twisty roads -Some drop off points are annoying  Overall, American Truck Simulator, or ATS for short, is an amazing game by the SCS Software crew, who were also the creators of ETS and ETS2 (Euro Truck Simulator and Euro Truck Simulator 2)

I think a lot of people had no idea what this game was about before buying it. It's not an intense shooter or space combat game. It's much more sedate. Almost a walking simulator. But definitely a game dripping in theme. It's almost not a game, just a reason to fly around, move in vague directions. A galaxy sized sandbox. So if you don't enjoy games with no direct purpose or direction, then this one isn't for you. If you like something relaxing and enjoy exploring, this game is amazing. Certainly the excitement will wear off over time. Hopefully they will keep expanding the content. But I've had a great 8+ hours so far.  As for the performance problems, I've had zero issues. I have a 4+ year old computer and a Geforce 750 and it works perfectly. So not sure why those with better systems are having so much problems. But this doesn't seem to have any hitching at all.

Rollercoaster Tycoon is one of those unique games that have never been done before and nailed it on the first try, the game completely nailed the architectural part of making rollercoasters/parks in general, there are plenty of scenarios to make the game last several 100's of hours with each scenario taking 3-4 hours to complete with never a boring moment inbetween.  Rollercoaster Tycoon has some downsides however which are attributed to how old this game is, remember this game came out in  1998 . Thats 17 years folks, compare that to the fact that only ONE person made this game in assembly.  I will tell you, that amount of work has been unseen by a single developer company in those 17 years.  Because of its old age the AI has some issues with pathing which you will probably not notice though, also there are some limits of the game because its coded in assembly.  I rate this game a solid 90/100.

Okay, so I'm having a hard time trying to recommend this game, so I made the decision to not do so.  It's a match three game, mixed with a rather basic city building type of game, and by very basic I mean, click this, see if you have the resources, build, then job done! Not complicated in any way, very simple and easy to get the hang of, but it's honestly nothing special. Now on to the actual gameplay!  Like I said, the game is a match three game, so the main gameplay is just swiping so you get a line of three or more, but where it is a little different is the mechanic of the battles. On one side is you, which is a building filled with your gems/runes that you need to match three in order to use as an attack. On the other side is where the enemies will slowly encroach upon your castle, and it's your job to match those gems, be it horizontal or vertical to fire a spell at the enemy. You'll slowly whittle the enemy down with your matching abilities and the battle will be over and you'll be sent back to your village.  The combat isn't overly-complicated, and you will be getting extra abilities that you can unlock to help you along the way, but it's all simply 'okay.' And that's how I'd describe this game in a nutshell. It's merely okay. I'm not the biggest fan of the graphics style, but I can live with that. The combat is fairly simple, but it's nothing spectacular, and the whole village gimmick kind of makes you feel like you're playing a FaceBook game as you have to wait for your buildings to give you the resources you want. If you want to sit and wait and just watch that timer slowly tick down so you can get that extra little bit of ore, then you can, but I can't admit being the biggest fan of having to wait on real time to get something a little extra in a game.   I don't hate the game, but at the same time I can't say I like it either. Which is why I'm not going to be recommending it, because an 'okay' game does not make it necessarily a good one.

This is a very good game, much better than the first part of the trilogy. I really liked the first two chapters, where you are slowly getting into the game by 'living' the main character rather instead of clicking on everything on the screen. You solve puzzles without even noticing they are puzzles. These are just things to do.  As the game progresses and the plot thickens the game changes into a more classic point&click adventures with symbol-searching puzzles, music puzzle, collecting elements puzzles, make your escape puzzles and so on. Thanks to the ability to highlight interactive points the game does not transform into a pixel hunt.  The story is really good, even if the twists are more or less predictable. It's nice to see some locations and characters from the Black Mirror I.  Animation of characters while interacting with environmnet is clumsy. I also did not like the few real-time action situations - they were unnatural, and a bit incoherent (going into action mode in some cases meant you will die after 2 seconds if you don't do anything; in other you could run forever in a room without any progress of the situation). Some of the character's reactions were not credible, as were the dialogue lines (something between implausible rudeness and Asperger's honesty in interactions with other people). Graphics were quite ok, music was standing out too much - in such games/movies you should not really notice it to get more immersed in the atmosphere. Overall a very good adventure game. To be played after the first part of the trilogy, if you want the full experience.

Way of the Samurai 4 is a genre-straddling, blade weilding hose-beast wearing clown shoes and a funny hat.  ...Let me try again. Way of the Samurai 4 is a game of surprising depth and complexity. Much more than just a game about dudes with swords, but certainly has plenty of dudes with swords in it, if that's what you happen to be here for. Similar to a visual novel game, the story in Samurai 4 takes place over a pre-defined time span, and is event driven. Some events are consistant for each play through, while some offer choices which determine what happens later in the story. Samurai 4 is ostensibly set in pre-civil war Japan, but with a number of fun anachronisms to keep things interesting. A diplomatic envoy from Great Britan has opened negotiations with the local Magistrate to bring in new technology and establish trade between the two nations, but a faction of xenophobic traditionalists fear that the integration of the new culture will damage the classical 'Yamato spirit' identity they are proud of. This sets the backdrop for the player's character, a transient swordsman with no default personality, to forge a new future for Japan. The swordplay is overall pretty good, but the real fun is in the ability to get in and customize how your character fights by obtaining different weapons and stances from different characters. Weapons can be broken down into parts and re-assembled, and any combination of parts can be sharpened and reinforced to the statistic cap. Eventually, even custom movelists can be assembled making wholly unique spear and swordfighting styles.  What makes Samurai 4 noteworthy? Our player insertion character is time-locked 'Groundhog's Day' style in this week, replaying it over as many times as we, as the player, want to. And so, it isn't in playing, but in re-playing, that Samurai 4 starts getting interesting. Some things are carried over between stories, such as the main character's swords and whether or not some of the locations are open, but the main events reset each time. With the fore-knowledge granted from having lived through the week once before, the player can manipulate the outcome by chosing who to support and who to confront, and when. A journal is kept, showing which potential events the main character has participated in across all play throughs.  A cautionary note; Way of the Samurai 4 does contain a *lot* of partial nudity and crude humor, on par with the Saint's Row series. The game is only translated to languages other than Japanese in subtitles. If that doesn't put you off, however, Samurai 4 is well, WELL worth your attention. Your Samurai honor demands that you rise to the challenge.

This game, like many of its genre in the late 1990's, does not come with instructions and takes a lot of thinking and exploring to progress, but personally, I'm a sucker for games that make you think a little, (I'm looking at you, Professor Layton!) so finding this gem was quite the discovery! I honestly didn't know what to expect when I bought it, seeing only snippets of it and flashy advertisements for the game all over the web, but it is safe to say that this game was most definitely a $15 well spent. Hats off to you, LucasArts!

'The hardest tracks are ones you most lack confidence and the easiest are ones you are most confident/comfortable with. Because when you lack confidence you tend to go slower but if you're comfortable you go full out because you know how to handle the track. You don't hold back since you're not afraid of crashing and all the adrenaline/excitement rushes in because you can go full out. You dominate the race and are ahead of everyone else. Yet, you get the feeling that you want to 'overtake' every section of the track itself. It's no longer about beating the competition, you're the competition' -Me on Ride gameplay.   I was foreign to the world of motorbikes because I never able appreciated riding one. I played one of the first motogp game in around 2000 and I loved the game very much though. Recently, I tried Milestone's MotoGP14 and despite the lack of support for the game I loved it! I still think riding a motobike is a dangerous and risky business but I can appreciate the thrill and freedom of bike riding.  All this brought me to RIDE. I almost passed on buying until I saw a youtube video clip showing a tuned bike excellently ridden.  Now that I have tried RIDE, I want to share my opinion of the game to help you decide. Overall, the game is good especially if you're a motorbike lover. However, it still lacks tracks to race in in my opinion. The game forces you to start with one of 3 naked bikes and I thought it was rather uncool to force you to start with a specific type of bike. That being said, the progression until you buy a new bike that you like is quick and I now have realized that naked bikes aren't that bad actually.  The game allows you to buy parts for your bikes from aesthetic parts to performance enhancing ones. It also allows you to customize your rider avatar's face and gears (apparel) as well.  The game feels easy to play even with pro physics and no assists especially if you compare it to MotoGP 14. It could be because the bikes are slower than those in MotoGP. But this brought a more arcade feeling to the game than Milestone's MotoGP 14.  Other than the lack of tracks the only other gripe I have is the inability to select the number of laps in any race we participate in (with the exception of online games).  Do note that there's no weather in game neither is day/night setting. I think every track is set to certain pre-programmed time setting. Milestone has a habit of giving you just good enough game without trying to make it really superb.  Online is enjoyable too with stangers or even more with friends.  UPDATE 2 APRIL 2015 Now that I have spent more on the game I have more to share.  The difficulty of the game increases as you upgrade from lower powered bikes to higher ones. Riding a supersport bike may be too easy compared to riding a motoGP bike in Milestone's other game but riding a modern superbike is not so easy. In fact it can feel a bit too difficult. That is until you adapt to the changes.  Wheelie and stoppie happen a lot when you ride a bike with respectable horsepower unless you turn anti wheelie option to full (modern bikes have that tech actually!). Wheelie forces you to go easy on the throttle thus slowing you down despite the bike's increased power rating. Opening up on the throttle aggresively will slow you down as your bike performs unintended wheelies.   Furthermore, the game has its fair share of bugs and performance issues. Custom bike settings are not remembered forcing you to load your bike setting before every race which is annoying.  Stutters can happen if you set the VRAM to auto which is the default setting.  The game crashes to desktop when you try to exit and sometimes when loading it just freezes although the likely occurence of the later is not that high.  Perhaps one of the most annoying bug is that rewind doesn't always work and when it doesn't it costs you the race thus in-game money, prestige and REAL free time  UPDATE 3 April 2015 Just want to add that many people mention the sounds of the bikes are not that good but to me ithey are decent enough. When you use helmet view, sounds are muffled a bit which is very nice and thoughful. You could also hear the whizzing of air past you....which is also muffled if you're in helmet view. Each bike sounds distinct enough although you can't quite immerse yourself in the feeling of raw power when hearing your engine purring and roaring as you race. But the sounds are decent enough.  One of the best things in Ride is the inclusion of older bikes. These are a thrill to ride and race. You get to understand the advancement in motorbike technology as you try bikes from different eras. Most of the older bikes are from the mid 1990s and all throughout the first decade of the 21st century. Bike lovers will understand that they are riding not to show off or to indulge their prestige/ego by riding an expensive sport bike but for the enjoyment and thrill of riding it. Thus, being able to race these older bikes is awesome.  Overall, this is a fun game and that's all that matters. Yes it is easier than Milestone's previous MotoGP 14 game but it's way more fun.  UPDATE MULTIPLAYER The multiplayer part is a blast in this game. It's made in such a way that your own riding is not affected by the ping response to the host. Your fellow players may rubber band if you have bad ping to the host but your experience of the race is flawless. This made it such that your result is almost not affected by the quality of your ping to the host's pc.  It's a lot of fun playing with other people. And way safer too. ;)

This is one of those early access titles that are so much fun that you end up checking for new updates every time you open steam. I simply cannot wait for what the future holds for Beyond Sol!   Pros: Being developed by, Praxia Entertainment, a Canadian team that used to work for Bioware; Beyond sol has all the elements of a great top down space shooter, combined with the hardcore strategy elements of a 4x empire building game. Throw in some massive fleet battles, tower defense and even some economic simulation, you will find this hard to put down.  You begin the game as a lone cruiser pilot in a small region of contested space and you goal is to quickly build a city where people in the region can live, shop, work etc. You will need to expand your territory by building radar and mining installations that will gather minerals tariffs for your growing empire. While your empire is growing you may also explore the universe freely and without restriction, mining, hunting, performing services for other cultures, salvaging, and even outright attacking your neighbors, if you so choose. There is no restriction on what you do.  Your ship can be upgraded and customized in a variety of ways depending on what corporations you have living in your city. You can buy new corporate ships to fly with you or to defend your cities and stations as well. All things considered, Beyond Sol is amazing already, and it's not even finished yet. I can't recommend it enough!  Cons: This is an Early access title, so it, naturally has a few issues. I have found some bugs when starting new games where some of the previous game data will carry over, creating some confusing information, like incorrect research and ship levels, more money and minerals than normal etc. The game is also incomplete and there are simply many ship customizing option that are simply just not available yet. The procedurally generated area you begin in is different every time and always interesting, however, there are no options yet to customize it. None of these issues prevent a player from playing the game and enjoying it thoroughly.

Well, this game certainly shows how far Hidden Object Games have evolved. Even with the remastering, this was painful to go through - the game uses a central hub so you get the same room with different objects numerous times; the sub-sections are rarely more than two screens, and the adventure portions are minimal. The alternative to the HOG sections is a collapse style game, which kind of works and kind of doesn't, and there are find the difference sections that are annoying. And the Hidden Object scenes aren't very good either. All in all, the only reason to play this is to see how much better Artifax Mundi has gotten in their game design over the years.

It is 2am and I just finished my first playthrough. This game got its hooks in so deep that I have put in an average of four hours per day since purchase. This culminated in me beating Tyranny in just under a week and subsequently writing what I believe is my first ever review.   It has been at least four years since a game has done this to me. Incredible lore, meaningful roleplaying, impactful choices, deep game mechanics, and (in my opinion) the best-looking isometric RPG I've played. Tyranny is also up there on the 'power trip' scale -- So if you like to feel powerful, this game should be on a top 10 list somewhere.  Thank you, Tyranny... I think we might need to amicably part ways for a few weeks so I can avoid a divorce.

Pros Great Ship Graphics  Cons  Lack of any real customization Horrible allotment of points for ship deployment Campaign missions and priorities are very linear Constant Micro-Management Battles are boring and lack any true strategy  I saw this game on YouTube and from what I saw I just had to have it. It wasn’t in till I got into the game that I discovered that it was nothing like the video at all. After playing several missions and playing both custom battles and the campaign, I just don’t understand where the developers were doing with the game.   The A.I has a complete knowledge of the battlefield and knows exactly where your ships are. Unlike other deployment type games, the A.I counters all of your ship deployment.   The battels are drawn out and boring, circling around each other in till they fire. This reminds me of 18th century sea combat, not 40k space combat. Space battles in the game are not 3d but 2d, the developers built this game to resemble old fashioned sea warfare and not the 3d all-encompassing battle that it should.   It is a pretty game but lacks any real type of strategy, fun combat, and customization of ships that the 40k universie is known for. They tried to take the board game and make it into a pc game; however they should have known; that you cannot take something as complex as space battles and just use the rules and play from something played on a table.
I don't know how long it's been, but I do know I've gone through 500+ mouses already.

Often we compare Blender's price to Maya, 3ds max, Modo, but it's not fair Blender combine several softwares at once, it is equivalent to a +10k dollars pack he can compete the Autodesk Creative Suite by blending a modeling software, painting, sculpting, video editing, compositing, tracking, printing,and more (some even use it for web and UI design, for example Ubuntu is designed with Blender). And he does some common task better, we can model and do the UV Mapping, get a Base Mesh with the skin modifier + dyntopo insanely fast. I love how consistency and modular the interface is: the shortcuts are the same for modeling, editing nodes so we can quickly use it once we know the technical vocabulary. The software is incredibly stable while evolving at a fast pace a new version is released every two and half months. You can see the list of the most useful shortcuts here:  http://www.giudansky.com/images/downloads/blender/blender3d-shortcuts-infographic.png   Don't forget to support the Blender foundation by donating:  https://www.blender.org/foundation/development-fund/  Or by subscribing on the Blender Cloud (that gives you access to a lot of well made tutorials):  https://cloud.blender.org/

I got behind this game when it was still being worked on. Loved the idea of a planetside like warhammer game as few games actually capture the scale of giant 40k wars. Instead what we got was a battlefield type game. Even then, it's barely that. There are about 4 or 5 maps with 2 objectives capture and hold, or just push forward. Races are seperated for lore reasons. So if you pick an Ork it's everyone else you'll be fighting. Chaos, Eldar, and Space marines. the biggest mode is a 50-60 person match, but for some reason it still feels small.   Another thing that was promised was an npc enemy that everyone has to fight. The Tyranids. Tyranids are perfect for this role as they are too op to be player controlled, and too numerous to stick to 20-30 man teams. BE haviour had plans to make the nids this massive invading force. What was delivered was 1 map that 5 people go through and clear. You just go from point to point holding off waves of tyranids. Very Destiny like. This mode could have been pretty cool. Even if you dont have a big open warzone like planetside, but after playing for a while i've noticed that between trying to avoid shooting your melee teamates whom just go in swinging widely at everything that moves, or other teammates whom feel that small unit tactics just arent their thing so they run waaaaaay ahead to achieve their dreams of becoming a corpse. It's not even close to what the original idea was. Very dissappointing, and kinda boring.  All of these things combined with an overpowered melee system, bugs, poor design decisions such as sticky auto cover, the lack of basic options, as well as the 50 dollar price tag make this out to be one serious let down. 3/10. If spacehulk: Deathwing turns out to be another let down I may just give up on 40k games for good.

There pretty much isn't a bad game in this strategy franchise. The concept is simple: Pick your civ, play through history, win... But the execution is VERY complex. You win in different ways depending on which country you are and which does what. If you try to dominate the world as a civ that specializes in researching technology, Well you're gonna have a bad time. You could be doing amazing for a few hours and a few hundred turns into the game, but make one bad decision or one bad move and you could cripple your empire. This game has hours among hours of replayability and content, and it's relatively cheap. If you get it on sale it's dirt cheap. And I personaly think this Civ is a little easier to pick up and learn then Civ 5, but maybe not everyone will agree with me. But this is a true gem in the strategy genre. Easily a must own for any fan. 9/10.

This is a great short game that doesn't overstay it's welcome. There's four 'themed' wings built around 4 variations of the core 'move around the circle' mechanic, and within each section a bunch of discrete subsections that introduce variations on that wing's take on the core mechanic. What's great is that none of these subsections last too long; right about when you've got a certain mechanic on lockdown, you're on to the next one. None of it is too hard, and you shouldn't get stuck on any section for long at all, which also leads to moving forward at a healthy clip. Plus, I think I said 'oh, that's really clever' out loud more times per hour in this game than anything else in recent memory; the game does some very smart things within the constraints of the aesthetic and mechanics provided.  So the game stays feeling fresh, with basically *zero* padding. It may only last about 2 hours, but it's a great two hours. If you want a challenge, this is not the game for you. Don't expect Super Hexagon or anything like that. But if you want a visually sharp but very relaxing take on a 'radial puzzler,' it's fantastic.

In my opinion, the best platformer ever made besides Super Mario World. Impeccably well-designed, this game presents incredible challenges that will make you feel like a badass for completing, but never frustrates, because lives are infinite, respawns are quick, and you're never more than 10 seconds from the bit that killed you because of the short levels. The controls are smooth, the game's sendup of classic excuse plots and charmingly dark sense of humor in cutscenes is always a great reward to strive for, the levels are varied and test your skills in a variety of ways, and there's a surprising amount of content for an indie game, which are usually rather short. This game is a masterpiece and you should buy it now. Even if you don't think you're up to the challenge, the almost nonexistant penanty for death will make sure that you can take as long as you want to learn, and when you're enough of a badass to conquer the Cotton Alley Dark World, you'll be glad you put in the time.  Score: 10/10

Spooky Heroes is an indie action platformer game developed and published by Gaweb Studios.  (Review Edited on July 30th, 2016)  In Spooky Heroes, you are a knight who has decided for whatever reason, to leave the safety of your home and try to solve the mystery of the hordes of spooky creatures rampaging villages, roads, and attacking anyone in sight. There's really not much more to it then that. You find villages, take quests from strangers, earn money, buy upgrades, unlock heroes.     Pros   -Keyboard controls are customizable to an extent. -Achievements and cards. -Choice of heroes to play as, that you also earn. -Villagers give quests. -Developer seems active, responding to reviews, etc. -Can use a controller to play. -Developer added in a chart to view control options while in game, makes it easier to play. -Developer added in spawn points so if you die on a boss, you no longer need to redo the entire level in easy and normal modes. -Now has quest journal, making it easier to find quests you are working on -Hit times are not AS sluggish as they were. -Rifts no longer appear in areas you cannot see on screen.     Cons   -Choosing to play on a keyboard is still more difficult than playing with a controller, but developer has bridged the gap a little bit. -Managed to find myself “Dead” with x’s as eyes and unable to do anything, to the point of being unable to restart the level, etc, but had health remaining in my health bar. Unsure if bug or if I broke something. -Screen froze up and crashed once on this most recent playthrough, unsure if its on my end or games end.    Neutral   -I'm not particularly impressed by the graphics, and feel that in an age where there are thousands of indie game makers making similar games, it is a must to try to stand out against your competitors, which this game really doesnt to. The graphics can be forgiven though, if the developer continues to polish the gameplay of the game. -For some reason, when I got to the first village, the guard greeted me and told me about 'gobelins' attacking. I'm not sure if it was the developer’s intention to spell goblins that way, or if it was an unintentional spelling mistake. Hopefully the developer can clarify that, as it made me do a double take and not a double take of joy.    Final Thoughts   Before the recent updates, my review was negative, for numerous reasons, the main being that the game was more frustrating to me than anything. Upon taking the time to play it again, I have found most of my issues sufficiently addressed. I did manage to either break the game, or find a bug, but given the developer is very active in continuing to improve on the game, can be overlooked as I’m sure he’ll address it. I changed my review to positive to reflect on the improvement I felt the game has made, as well as my frustration levels shrinking as I explored it again. My only suggestion at this point is to add some more achievements.  Is this game worth it? It is, if you like games like these and want to support an indie gamemaker. If you are on the fence, wait for a sale.

This game to start with is slowly managing to accomplish something that many other racing-based games have not been able to fully pull off, a full in-depth modification system that expands from interior (engine and such) to exterior (spoiler, wheels, exhaust and such) and I love it for that, so I would simply recommend it for that; But the downside to that is, this game is in early access, and many early access games are know for not leaving this status, so only buy this game within reason, I would fully love for you to give the devs support, but I will expand off the previous statement to the fact that due to this game being at it's bare bones for content it may or may not reach the state in which it's a full game, as many early access games don't. -But I will stay optimistic for this game, as I would love to see more done to it, I love the very core idea that this game is following and due to that I'd hate to see something with this much potential go to waste.  I would also like to state that this game is extreamly unstable as of now, with large amounts of crashes occuring within my time playing, making it near impossible to upkeep a constant game without reloading it every 10 mins, and not to mention the lost progress due to this.

An absolutely golden platformer. If you're thinking about getting this game, you probably should. There's a thin story here about some astronauts crash-landing their ship, but it's just an excuse to send you exploring the map and searching for your team mates. The graphics, likewise, aren't going to blow you out of the water. They look nice enough and there are some cool effects in the backgrounds, but for the most part its just blocks and pixels. It does the job though.  What really matters is the gameplay (and the soundtrack, but more on that later). The game itself is a joy to play. And that's because it's simple -- you can move, and you can reverse gravity. That's it. It's a clever subversion of the common platformer. When presented with even the smallest of obstacles, you can't jump over it. You have to flip yourself on to the roof and then back down. This would be easy enough if not for the spikes. Nearly every surface in this game is covered in spikes. And to make matters worse, there are constantly projectiles or enemies bouncing around in patterns. It's a game all about timing and reflexes, and both are pushed to the extreme. It's hard. It's very hard. But you always respawn at the nearest checkpoint, and there's usually a checkpoint in every room, so even the most difficult of platforming segments will never send you back very far if you mess up. But you WILL mess up. It's great to play a game that's simple but very difficult to master.  The map is fun to explore, for a number of reasons. In addition to searching for your crew, you're also on the lookout for 'shiny trinkets.' These trinkets allow you to unlock a few cool end game bonuses, if collected. But the trinkets are often hidden behind the hardest platforming segments, making them quite difficult to collect. It really helps extend the game length, which is good, considering it isn't all that long of a game anyways. Additionally, there are lots of cool things to see just by wandering around, and it feels incredibly satisfying to fill in the entire map at the end of the game. Bouncing around this environment is good fun. But perhaps the best part of this game is the sound. There aren't a lot of sound effects -- there's a noise for jumping and a noise for dying (get used to those both), and a few different sounds for collecting or activating different items. Aside from that, there's nothing but music. But what wonderful music it is! This game has one of the best soundtracks I've ever heard. There isn't a single bad song you'll hear playing this game. And although it's an infuriatingly difficult game, it's hard to stay angry when you're tapping your foot along to the music. It really helps you stay concentrated.  The music and the timing coordinate together to make a game that, when played flawlessly, almost feels like a rhythm game. There's a pulse to running and bouncing around in this world that just feels incredible, and when the music is pounding, your reflexes are sharpening, and your game is going perfectly, it's hard not to fall in love with this game. Highly recommended.

The game is fun and offers some stratagy based money management as well as requiring a bit of luck. In standard new game there are only 2 dungeons, one is basically a tutorial dungeon with not a lot to offer the other is a 3 part dungeon with a 4th area allowing you to fight just the bosses over and over again.  Early in the game dungeon crawling is a must to gain pix, however later on, for me it was after just the 2nd week, it no longer seems really helpful.  Often times townspeople will come in selling an item and then before the time segment is even over will buy it right back. One of the best ways to make money is buying for 30% and reselling for 130%.  One of the best items to stock up I found was weapons, in later weeks they get boosts in value and can be sold for as much as 300% base. For me the last 3 days all weapons were increased in value, not sure if it was luck or game mechanics, but in the last 3 days alone I amassed over 300k pix.  The game does have NG+ as well as a survival mode, but for the most part there doesn't really seem to be a whole lot of replay value after you intitally beat it.  I bought it on sale for 9.99, not 100% I got my moneys worth, but it was definitely a fun game. I have a total of around 7 hours in the game including offline time, and that's about the amount of time it took me to beat it with having to start over once. I'd recommend buying it, but definitely on sale for 9.99 or less simply because of how short the game is, and how little replay value it offers in my opinion.

One of the few Indie games that I can say I enjoyed. Monaco is the perfect mix of Hotline Miami, and Payday. With a simple design, usual heist game plot, this game is easy to grasp, suitable for gamers and non-gamers alike.  However, this game has it's fair share of problems. The new October update, is dissapointing. The Origin campaign, whilst giving more info on the characters, feels like the same mission every time. I feel... more cheated out of a good promise, rather than dissapointed. The 'Zonaco' zombie mode, is, well the same campaign. Only with zombies. So no round based, team oriented survival mode, like I hoped for. Saves us the screamers online, I suppose.   + Story is well written, despite it's repetative missions + Co-Op is tackled well, but the campaign can be played regardless without a partner + Replayability. I have barely taken a chunk out of this game. + Story is split into 2 Acts.  - The October update.  - It's easy to find the quick way around a mission, however the game tackles punishment well.  7/10. A fun, well made Indie game.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Dark Souls is a chincy feeling, semi frustrating, horror action almost RPG that made me chuckle alot. I loved it from day one.   It was made by the same company as Tenchu, and it feels like it. As a Tenchu fan from day one the combat felt right. It gets alot of hype for being a hard game, tho I've played much much harder games. Mostly it gets you out of your comfort zone of dying or messing up. You will die alot and soon you'll realize there's no serious punishment in this. There's no hard save to rely on. The autosave moves at light speed. Everything you do is irreversible and if you mess up or die you'll have to redo some stuff to try again. That's probably the only difficult thing about it. If you've ever played an older game, or more specifically, one that might have been in an arcade and was designed to make you die over and over so you must fork over quarters all afternoon to beat it, technically you've enjoyed the 'difficult' aspect of the game. If that's your thing, buy this game, it will save you quarters. If not, read on...  Now that that's out of the way, let's talk about the real reasons why this game is one of the best ever created.   Dark Souls is the anti-fantasy fantasy.   Dark Souls' world is an experience. The world is dark, elaborate, and utterly serious. It's not a jumpy horror game. Your own mortality is what feeds of your fear in this grim realm. It's not a world you'd ever want to visit. A breath of fresh air in a genre that has been saturated by the pastel suffocation of anime characters and fairies. The bosses have an instant iconic vibe, they demand respect and soon you'll soon know them by name. Most fantasy games have so many bosses you don't even care. Not Dark Souls. Each one is unique. The voice acting that is brought to the table will actually make you stop what your doing and soon you'll be forcing NPC's to repeat themselves just to please your ears. The unseemingly small realm that you will explore will weave within itself. Much like a Zelda game, you'll constanly run into areas and doors previously unaccessible and shout ' THAT'S WHERE THAT GOES!' After a playthough you'll realize almost everything can be accessed from the beginning of the game.   They've trimmed the fat. Almost every single item in the Dark Souls world has a use. It's not overloaded with hordes of meaningless enemies, bosses, or NPCs. Everything has a point. Everything has a value. Every weapon has it's own unique moveset, is balanced, and is actually thought provoking. You won't be snatching up countless mundane weapons and coasting along with them. You'll be experimenting until you find the RIGHT weapon that matches your personality. Your weapon will become part of you. Why? Because the demons of this haunting realm aren't the only enemy you'll face.  Co-operative players and Invaders will plague or bless your experience. You'll be running through the Undead Burg with your currently fancy feeling new battle axe and a player light as a feather, will prance into your world and shiv you mercilessly before you know what's happening. The boss you've been attempting for the last 30 minutes can be obliterated by a friendly fellow in mere seconds, providing that you know how to ask for help. Doesn't appeal to you? Go offline. None of the multiplayer is mandatory.   I'm not going to give credit to Dark Souls for having a new game plus. Although it is a cool feature, it was hardly a new concept when it came out. Enemies have more HP the next time around, Which is kind of cool but, it's an equal balance to your leveling and upgrading providing you didn't derp it up to begin with.   Most of this game, like the Tenchu series, simply sets a goal and lets you figure how you're going to achieve it. Also like the Tenchu series the creators didn't work out all the kinks. Minor random control issues can happen. The targeting system... Is not the best by any means. The slightest movements can swap an opponent 3 feet away with one 30 feet away....yet it's far more consistent than it's sequel. It's up to you to anticipate and deal with this. You do become used to it after awhile.   Overall Dark Souls is one of the best games I've ever played. It provides a unique experience that has sadly been sheltered by it getting a bad rep and being dubbed 'difficult'. Do not let this discourage you. The beginning may seem tough, and require a bit more effort than most games out there, but by the end it's almost a breeze. Once completed there's no quesiton that it was worth it.      Still reading? Just get the game already!

One Does not simply just play Bag-Throwing Simulator 2014, Payday 2 is the only game that combines atmosphere, strategy, and blazing Hot, Adreneline-Pupming Crime rushes flawlessly to create an effect that makes Throwing bags into trucks the most hilarious and fun thing ever, WITH FRIENDS, I am sorry lone wolf-shark-vampire-hunter hybrids of the night, this game is not for you. This game is fun when you and you're friends are working together to hush-down civilians, rob banks, failing at stealthing missions, creating character builds that help eachother (MASTERMIND BABY), and of course, yelling BULL DOZER, ITS A MOTHER ****** BULLDOZER, HEY GUYS BULLDOZER, DID YOU KNOW THERE WAS A BULLDOZER?!?!?!?!?!?!, IMMA KEEP POINTING HIM OUT FOR MY TEAM, BULLDOZER!!!! haha, and nothing is more satisfying then seeing you're friend get rammed by a cloaker, or ninja-ghost-spies as they actually are, and using you're high end perk to yell, PAIN IS MENTAL, and seeing them get right up before your eyes after previously seeing them being beaten in the head with a baton and shot repeatedly. Pain may be mental, but bullets are not, XD. My fondest memories of this game were with friends, and honestly I feel thats how this game was meant to be played. Its up to you Solo-Lone-Ranger-Wolves to decide for yourself, but personally I can't imagine this game being satisfying alone. All in all, the gameplay is simply fun and enjoyable. The Atmosphere never ceases to excite, even after playing watchdogs for hector 40 times. For those who want a real challange at multiplayer FPS, this is what you should be playing.  Pros: + Tactical + Realistic + Challenging + Solid gameplay and mechanics + Well balanced teams, gear, & maps + Practically requires teamwork and communication to acheive victory + PTSD + Suppression effect (Amazingly well done!  Cons: - Over 100 ping and it is unplayable (which is a minor issue considering you can just change servers) - Slow loading times - PTSD  For those who like the run and gun fast play of CoD and Battlefield, this probably won't suit your taste. However, for those who prefer realistic, tactical, and challenging FPS gameplay, I would VERY much recommend this game to you.  [EDIT] I had to edit something in because I forgot to mention it, but it is a core aspect as to why I enjoy this game so much (can't believe I forgot to mention it honestly...). The suppression effect and details are simply incredible. This game excels at both of these aspects, and these are a large part of what makes this game so engaging, fun, and intense. Where some others weren't able to get it right, Insurgency has done it almost perfectly. [endEDIT]

This game captures the tedious monotony and annoyances of truck life perfectly. Take ten minutes to get to top speed; you'll then see a speed camera (but be unable to slow down in time), which will relieve you of $500 and leave you with no recourse through the court system. Cars will speed up when they see you merging. Other vehicles will crash into you, and it's your fault. So real!  There are some disappointing omissions however: you’re not able to take “performance enhancers”; enjoy the charms of “Susie”, the haggard truck-stop hooker; nor collect hitchhikers whose families will never see them again.   Effectively, it's a truckin' simulator without any of the things that make actual truckin' bearable. If that's a deal-breaker, this may not be the sim you're looking for.

I will update after completion but at this time I cannot imagine anything other than 100% positive review. Oh and did I mention the Graphics being Beautiful?  Side note: The first time I played I set everything on Ultra (FX8350 + R9-280) and I noticed a little hiccup (frame stutter) sometimes. But after that I ran AMD Raptr and let it auto-optimize my settings. Still playing with almost all the settings maxed, but since then it has played smooth as butter with not even one single frame stutter.   Would Absolutely recommend 10/10

This game seems to run a great deal smoother than Tropico 4, in fact I'd say it's as smooth as the island rum I make the peasants distill for me which I've given the distinguished name of 'Mein Führerious Alkohol'.  It is indeed a great deal of fun making decisions to see how close to a Caribbean 1984 you can make your island, I can truly see why our real-world leaders are constantly making choices to bring us closer to that reality. The revamped military is also worth mentioning, rather then turning their rifles on the more... vocal Tropicans, there are actual enemy armies to engage from time to time.   The other notable addition is multiplayer. It's a fairly well done implementation, though likely won't be the main focus of most players. Perhaps this game can best be summed up by a saying from the great Sun Tzu, 'Blame it on the goose/ got me feeling loose/ blame it on patron/ got me in the zone/ blame it on the a-a-a-a-a-a-alcohol.'

Tired of open-world exploration? Confused by multiple dialouge options? Baffled by combat with more than two options? Have we got the game for you! Standard Third-Person RPG: Pirate Flavor is right up your alley! Follow along with Gravelvoice McNeverShave and his sister ♥♥♥♥ McNoArmor as they do vaugely pirate-themed things! This game includes such jaw-dropping innovations as:  -Invisible Walls! No more will you have to dread the freedom of exploration! With our patented Invisible Corner system, you won't go anywhere we don't want you to! And sometimes you won't go places we do want you to!  -Combat! With light, heavy AND ranged* attacks you no longer have to devote any thought to strategy! Simply push the attack button until the enemy dies! And with our No-Gain HP system, even trival combat with early-game eneimes will be delightfully treacherous as 3-4 hits will always kill you! Combined with unblockable enemy attacks and unbreakable enemy guard, you will worry about literally every fight! And there's thousands of them!  -Talking! Enjoy choosing from up to TWO dialouge options when talking to characters with the personality of wet cardboard WHILE listening to a character voice so real you'll swear you were talking to a man with a twleve pack-a-day habit!  -Moral Choices! When confronted with a burning orphanage, you can choose to either help the orphans trapped inside OR roast hot dogs over their buring flesh! Sometimes, you won't even know you're making a choice!  -Graphics! With our dynamic light system , you'll be hard-pressed to differentiate rocks from enemy models until they attack! And then it's too late! Plus, NPC's come in up to THREE different models!**  -Useless Minigame! Since doing literally everything other than combat is based off of skills (which involves finding trainers, spending gold, and solving Fermat's last Theorem) you'll be presented with literally pointless lock-picking, arm-wrestling, knife-throwing, and many more!  -Exploration! Never again will you have to worry about trying to find the right hallway! All you have to do is keep going forward! Don't even worry about venturing to outlying areas! If a quest doesn't send you there, we've ensured that it's not worth your time!  -AND SO MUCH MORE! Order now, operators are standing by!  Side effets may include swearing, sweating, rage-quitting, game-breaking bugs, lack of autosave, lack of character growth, lack of character development, and questioning of life decisions. Do not mix with other RPG's. Ask your doctor if Standard Third-Person RPG: Pirate Flavor is right for you.  *Ranged attacks not actually ranged. **Excluding female models. Its a very nice game.  Now that thats out of the way, let me put some glaring problems up:  1: Clear preference to Console players- (bleh) 2: Some bugs, (Such as the monster freezing in solo play) still occure. 3: Lack of content 4: LACKS A STORY MODE- this is a HUGE flaw. I hope this is fixed eventually.   Now that the negitives are out of the way, lets talk about some things it does well:  1: Graphics. Are. Awesome. Looks amazing, good style. 2: Core concept is very very good. 1 player in charge of a super powered monster, 4 others playing little guys. It's like someone playing Baron on summoners rift instead of another team! 3: Three diminsional game play, jetpacks/monster climing (flying in the case of Kraken) make this an awesome game play experience.    All in all, this is an awesome game to sit down with if you have friends that play- the thrill of the hunt, with 4 friends makes this an awesome party game. I would go so far as to say with friends, this game is a must have. If you are playing alone, I would reccomend you pass, as the single player content is fun for a week at best, and the matchmaking system makes you want to group with friends (its bad.)   All in all, I would definitly pay 60$ for this game, so long as a friend has also done so.   Is it perfect? No, far from it.  Is the formula a good idea? I cannot smash the yes button harder. I very much hope to see more content, more game modes and a story some time in the future.
