Honestly, I see huge potential for this game. There is a LOT of work left to do but I appreciate what such a small team has put together. I think Hello Games should have waited a bit longer, maybe another year, before releasing it in order to have a more polished game but I think it could head that direction with hiring a larger team dedicated to weekly content updates.   I did get a refund for this game, for now. I feel $60 may be a little much for this product as is, but I fully feel that Hello Games has the talent to change the direction of the game more towards the vision that Sean Murray has been talking about in interviews. Give me a game where I feel that each planet is unique and, I have to emphasize this, uninhabited by alien lifeforms already. Running into alien life should be a rare and mystical event, not an every planet occurance. Also, please let me build a base that I can warp to at will! haha  Anyways, although I requsted a refund and have recieved it already (thank you), I hope to see great things in this game's future, and the future of Hello Games. I will be keeping up with the updates in hopes that this game changes direction into what it deserves and could be. I've been following this game since it was announced and will purchase it again if it improves, as I'm sure is the opinion of many. I think I can safely say that we (gamers) want to see Hello Games be a success and deliver a unique experience with No Mans Sky in the near future.  Best of luck team!

Short but sweet. Challenging HO segments, great atmosphere, puzzles ramp up gently from idiot-proof to reasonably challenging, and are never truly frustrating. The plot is thin to say the least, but the Dracula-inspired setting and supernatural story are engaging, and the twist, though not unpredictable, is still a nice touch - even if the lack of context drains its impact. The hint system is perhaps too forgiving - though its form is a great idea - and also perhaps too necessary when it comes to finding the next step, but too much is better than too little. Bonus content is sparse, but a bonus nonetheless.

Update - couldn't finish the game because of the well known Rhylfang-Bug. The big spider disappears and as it's the main quest to kill it the whole thing won't trigger. I would love to load a savegame to an earlier point, but Swordcoast Legends just allows you the actual single one. As it saves by itself after the fight - well, I gonna deinstall it. Worst present ever.  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------   What I can tell is, it isn't made for single players. I did play it with some friends until there was this velvet square problem which won't be solved ever. So I decided to play the campaign all by myself.  At first I minded that it wasn't D&D - but what the heck, there are plenty of other games which aren't and they're ok.  What I DO mind: - While loadin the next map I can do the household. And of course I set it all on lowest settings - The only attributes which are used are YOURS. It's ok, if you play in a group, so you can plan & tell each other, which character should do what.  Well, I've got a thief with high charisma to bargain and dexterity of course. So the whole group stands in front of a real strong door. Who is trying to break it? Nooo, strong dwarf, don't mind... Oh! Some Potions - nooo Mr. Intelligent Mage, don't mind, I have got an intelligence of 8, but if it doesn't go *boom* we might drink it! And no, don't try to read the book with important and old runes. Well, I won't manage, but we're out of toilet paper.  It doesn't make ANY sense, not to involve the NPCs - NPCs you don't use don't gain XP. So unless you like to take some lvl-3 thief with you, while rest of the party (and bad guys) are level 9 you do not choose them at all anymore. - Quests and Map are confusing. Sometimes the map points at the quest where to solve it and sometimes where it started. And even though there is a list with the whole quests, the text isn't always clear about what to do. Often it is written, how to solve the quest, although you already did this. So most times I'm just pulling every lever (you know, this thing for what you try to knock out the thief) and hope, that I just trigger anything possible. So don't play intelligent but take everything you can. As there are no consequences for being good or being evil - just do what brings most profit. - Controls suck. Loading? Disabled. Quick-Load works. Most times. If you didn't click on the character picture. Different layers, different controls. - You cant go to the camp and level up or change/compare items of the NPCs. Well, als you don't switch teammates I suppose that you don't need this anyway. - When there is a quest on a local map, it appears as a little purple square. But you have to be near of it. I do actually like the fog, so you don't know whether there are enemies. But as you have already done a dungeon I actually DON'T like to search every corner again just to find the person to solve the quest - and as I do not play daily I sometimes forget, where everyone was. Same is with seaarching every house. They aren't marked. You have to enter every house on the map again, if you did forget where everybody was. That was okay in games like Ultima or Bard's Tale where everyone just drew his map. But nowadays it is a pity. - If you play with others you'll hate shopping. Every time someone talks to a person gameplay is interrupted for everyone. Most times. Sometime someone just did a quest and nobody noticed. - You can't adjust tactics of NPCs. Tactics are okay, but you can't adjust e.g. automatic blessing for the cleric. - What character you built isn't important at all. You're an elf and there is a Drow? Doesn't matter. Selected history of yours? Doesn't matter. - You like edgy 3D-persons? You know, somehow like in NWN2, 2nd expansion? You'll be jolly in that case  So - not worthy to play? Not exactly. If you don't mind all those points, like to figth, to loot it's ok, I suppose. And the mainstory is really interesting. And most times a I'm telling the group what to do in fights they are reacting as I say (at least most times....) We've all been there, trying to get to sleep only to listen to the anti social neighbours blasting out their loud music. Instead of calling noise control our 'hero' takes matters into his own hands and brutally murders everyone associated with the party.  After a very brief opening level to explain the concept its straight into a set of large parties where the objective of the game is to murder everyone. Premise is simple, you can kill people by stabbing them. You must avoid being seen by other party goers or they will call the police who will arrest you and game over. If your seen near a dead body your guilt will be automatically assumed and the police called (even if it wasnt you). In each level you will find a limited number of traps and items like bombs that will take out a group of people at once usually without consequence.  Sounds simple, but it aint. This games takes a lot of patience and thought into successfully completing each level. Unless you get a head start in a chase the cops will always catch you. If you use a trap door or short cut to escape the police a handy man will come along and close it. Other threats could come along like the FBI but also some events that could help you. Prepare to restart over and over. And everytime you restart a level it will be slightly different to what it was in your previous playthrough to keep you on your toes.  Despite it being highly frustrating I recommend this game. Everytime I failed I said to myself one more go, will nail this on the next run. Game is relatively short level wise but potential for new content and I would highly recommend in a sale.

A simpler version of the other total war games when it comes down to the strategic map as well as the combat maps. But more detailed battles and combat.  Cons:  The combat maps lack the variety of modifiers to terrain from what i can tell however the usual height trees and temperature still are in and working well. The strategic map is very pretty but due to the way they have tackled empire management at times it seems a bit to simple.  EXPECT DLC: Some major factions such as Skaven are currently not around so i can guarantee future DLC will add a bigger map or at least different factions replacing place holder ones.   Pros:  Each faction makes the game really unique as they all play drastically differently. Orks and dwarves can ignore terrain and go underground, undead can raise units to life and so on.  Turn timer: god I HATED the turn timer in RTW2 and Atilla, however the turn end at a decent pace not making you wait for 5 mins per turn.  Magic is well done and not ridiculously OP in fact i tend to just stick with the sword and board fellas as they counter other champions and make it look like a duel :P  AI is vastly improved. No more running cav archers at your front line, no more charging you when they really shouldn't. For me so far the AI has taken the best strategy they could in the majority of fights and had me scrambling units around as some trolls or some ♥♥♥♥ has randomly broken through and is messing with my archers.  Optimization: Runs in someways smoother that RTW2 does now let alone when it was released. This will only get better.  END CASE  If you are tired of the same old same old from Total War this is a nice remash of the series and revitalises it quite a bit imo. The game can also only get better from here so may as well grab it and the free chaos

Easily one of the best games in the Tales of series. If you weren't in time to recieve a launch copy for Tales of Symphonia by preordering Tales of Zestiria, and you're a fan of JRPGs, then this game is a must buy.  Now, some people may be asking: 'What's your experience playing Tales of Symphonia?'  This copy (on Steam) is the third copy of the game that I own. I fist played ToS for the Gamecube when it was first released. My first playthrough lasted for 400+ hours and by the end of my 4th playthrough I was able to achieve a 100% finish (after, easily, over 1000 hours of gameplay). Years later, and with my consoles collecting dust in my basement due to lack of use, I decided I wanted to play ToS again and picked up a rom for the game online. I only played through the rom once since the hidden dungeon and New Game+ features weren't working.  Now that this is being released for PC with proper controls I'm looking forward to another amazing playthrough and will be eagerly awaiting the release of the sequal, Dawn of the New World, as well.

I'm still waiting for Galaga, Steam. Where are you?  You would figure that with all the arcade remakes ever to be made in this history of the world wide web, we can find a Galaga $15.00 rip-off with pretty colors remake on steam, but we don't live in that future just yet. We, in the modern era of the computerphone technology, live in arcade remakes of Space Invaders, not Galaga.   We have not progressed this far as a civilization to remake an amazing arcade game and continue to rely on the models of the old, conventional alternative (Space Invaders). This is like how we end institutional racism and deem racism socially unacceptable, but racism still persists in our culture in many different facets and never goes away. Space Invaders reboots are everywhere (persist in our culture) and come in many different aspects.  Void Invaders is not Galaga. It is 15% Galaga and 85% Space Invaders with pretty colors and/or seizures. While it is an 85% Space Invaders remake, Void Invaders has three different modes to the game: endless, wave (true arcade), and 'level', which is almost a storyline mode but with a level up system which doesn't really amount to anything. The level system also relies on power-ups to beat the level as the game gets tougher, but it doesn't detract from the experience (fun). Just because there's a clone-power up doesn't mean this game is Galaga. The ways to get powerups is also much different from Galaga because you don't die to get them, nor are their bonus rounds.  Where is my Galaga remake, Steam/Indie Game Developers? Don't you people remember what fun used to be??  tl;dr: It's cheap and great for all but the epileptics and colorblind folks. It's also not entirely space invaders but that doesn't mean it's Galaga. Where is Galaga, Steam?

app_name: Attack on Titan / A.O.T. Wings of Freedom
review_text: NOTE: I have tried the multiplayer coop and it has disconnect problems. If the game disconnects before the mission ends, you get nothing. This is a serious problem. If multiplayer is a major feature for you, wait for updates or a price drop.  I've only spent a couple hours with AOT so far. Long story short, I think they did the combat the justice it deserves. If you're a fan of Attack On Titan and the combat from the anime entices you, you'll get a lot of satisfaction from this game.  Now the details...  Using the gear is fast, exciting, and surprisingly nuanced. Yeah, you can run into buildings, your lines can be broken if something large like a tree gets in between you and what you're attached to. You can select which body parts to attack. So the movement actually requires some finesse, which I very much appreciate. With some practice you'll be swinging between buildings and Titans with impressive agility.   Besides the story mode, which recreates the events of the first season of the anime so far as I've seen, there's scouting and expedition missions beyond the walls. There's a ton of these missions, many of them with extra things to do like setting mines. Some of the environments have cannons you can use to fire at Titans. So there's a lot of meat to this game.  There's RPG elements such as leveling up, skills, and equipment upgrades. There's a very shallow conversation system and it's not open-world exactly. The game is very action focused and there's very little to do between missions. The levels can be really large though which makes the movement mechanics very fun.  There's typical Japanese action game elements such as mission ranking and bonuses that give you more cash and materials for upgrading.  Negative points. For the most part, you will be using your gear to swoop around the level and take down Titans with your swords. If you feel like this could get old, you may not want to play this game.   Technical stuff. As other reviews have mentioned, yeah AOT wants to run in Win 7 compatibility mode if you're using a newer OS. I haven't seen any performace problems or crashes during the game.  Some may wonder if this is a good game independent of the manga/anime that's worth the money for people who are unfamiliar with Attack On Titan. I'm not sure, perhaps not? You're certainly better off knowing what you're getting into. I think the best thing to do is find a way of watching the anime series to see if the world, characters, and action excite you like it has a lot of other people. This game lives up to the fantasy but whether or not the fantasy appeals to you is a different matter.  Fans of the series that would really like to play a game where you have to master using the omni-directional gear and timing to take down Titans efficiently with mission ratings and plenty of opportunities to improve, you'll really like this game. Good on KOEI Tecmo for doing this right.

app_name: Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Double Agent
review_text: Only buy this game if you're ready to spend a lot of time going around bugs and crashes! Playing on genuine Windows 7 64.  * On JBA HQ level, one of the door is impossible to open (no action panel), which makes it impossible to get one of the secondary objectives without 'cheating' using noclip mode. * On most of the Shangai level, the game crashes when you try to save!! * In game, I can only use the quick load function. Trying to load a specific save doesn't work. Workaround: Kill yourself and then the load menu works. * ALT-Tabbing out of the game makes it crash more often than not. * Sometimes the game crashes randomly (thankfully quite rare). * Loads of graphical glitches. Anti-aliasing doesn't seem to work.

app_name: Heroes of Might & Magic V: Tribes of the East
review_text: In something under 90 hrs, I have my campaign finished on hard difficulty... The game is OK, although i think the computer 'turn thinking' could be faster (hardware doesn't matter really) Sounds make me tired fast so i turned it off. Music was turned off even earlier. Also, I set speed to maximum because it gets reaally boring to watch all these animations and stuff. But all in all the Tribes standalone Heroes V game is a fine sequel to the glorious Heroes 3.  Also, some tips for campaign:  When leveling heroes, go to internet and see the 'skill wheel' so you could reset skills to the most useful combos (usually the skill in the center of the skillwheel is the coolest combo) because SOME OF MAIN HEROES FROM EARLIER MISSIONS ARE GONNA BE TRANSPORTED INTO THE FINAL MISSION AND YOU CAN'T CHANGE THEM IN IT but redo the campaign!!!  The 1st campaign is easy, no tips.  The 2nd campaign is harder, mission when Ornella starts as a necromancer - don't rush the 2nd city near witch's hut, it's hard to get and is not so neccessary to complete the mission in my opinion.  In the last mission, before the 4 spot ritual, make sure to gather all troops and artifacts from other heroes to Arantir , because they're gonna die.  The 3rd campaign with orcs requires you to get good skills and gear for both Gotai and Kujin.  Also, Kujin will come to the final mission (and you cannot change her in it) The hardest mission is the one with Kujin, But here i'd even recommend it on Normal rather than hard because it's kinda impossible if you don't get lucky with skills and are no strategy genius overall ( for example, put 7 units of a tanky kind each in quantity of '1' and let the ballista kill annoying one-target mages, that requires ballista skills )  The 4th campaign is multiple fractioned, here you need to level and gear up Zahir (mage), Wulfenstein (dwarf) and Frida (knight) because ALL three + Kujin the shaman come to fight in the final mission.  The first encounter with Zahir mission, as soon as u take the 1st dark elves city, they will come from the portal right near it and attack u with a reasonable army. Make sure you have some Rakshasas and mages in nice numbers to stand them.  Zahir with the main combo spell would be easy to easy as soon as you get it ( he then casts every kind of magic without even learning them ) (also, the 'mentor' ability of Zahir helps get any new hero a huge lvl boost)

For some reason I really love this game <3 The dialog is terrible, the love story is cheesy, the voice recognition butchers every command I give, but there's something downright charming about Binary Domain.  I played the demo with a keyboard and mouse and hated it (it's a terrible console port), but later after I bought a 360 controller I found the game and sale and gave it a second shot. The first thing that will strike you is the destruction physics of the robot--there's nothing like it in any other game I've played. There is an incredibly visceral feel to every shot you take that either dismembers, decapitates or otherwise cripples a robot. Every weapon feels really good and every baddie feels really good shooting. Boss fights are particularly satisfying as they often evolve and change tactics as you decimate them piece by piece.  Second, the near-future setting and story taking place in a closed-border Japan is really really cool. The environments give an epic feeling making you feel like as small as a fly. And the story, although not terribly deep, keeps things moving at a nice pace alongside the constant action. As mentioned previously, the dialog is really, really, cheesy; but somehow it works. I don't know whether it was done on purpose but you feel like you're in a B-rated sci-fi film except that it's actually quite entertaining. Finally, the game is the perfect length for me--it's always better to want to stay in the game's universe a bit longer than getting bored and having too much. Binary Domain does the former. Overall, A fun sci-fi action-romp that surprisingly became one of my favorite underrated games.

Occasionally, the gameplay feels a bit like 'Where's Waldo?' since many of the enemies hide behind cover or blend in with environment, but there's a huge variety among the 15 levels that keeps the game fresh, challenging, and fun. I personally favored the dual 6-shooters, but found that more powerful weapons (the ranger, rifle, shotgun, dynamite, etc) were necessary to succeed in later levels. The good side:   The art, environment and story are cute. You can walk faster by clicking twice on your destination. The music related puzzles were somewhat original. Death sequences are great (and with almost no consequences). The last puzzle was ok.    The bad side:   It's short with almost no replay value (and mostly just for achievements). Many screens (about 40%, I counted) serve no purpose besides esthetic/ambience (or hidden fireflies, that unlock artworks). And those that do contain something never have more than one purpose. There's a bit too much backtracking (which is made worse by the previous point). One 'item' is quite pointless and the related puzzle relies on an unexplained mechanic that you will use only once in the full game. (  The cat, although a cute companion, will stay available all the time even though it's used only once, it was forcefully crammed in the puzzle you use it for and combining the pets is used only there.  ) Another puzzle uses unexplained and specific mechanics. (  When you have to combine objects to get rid of the crocodile.  ) When trying to figure out the notes you hear, the background sounds will make them hard to discern (turning off sfx/music might help, I haven't tried). The notes will also be played in a loop with a quite short pause in between, which leaves only short intervals for you to try and reproduce them. The girl will sometimes stand in a place that will make it difficult to click on what you actually want. It's also difficult to click on your companions (it will open the inventory most of the time). Parts of the HUD should be displayed all the time without having to wait or click blindly, at least the button to access the music sheet. Also, switching from windowed to fullscreen is only available in the main menu. Some notes are too similar and hard to tell apart. (  H and I, M and N, in particular.  ) Most of the puzzles were not fun or satisfying to solve.   All in all, this game is quite disapointing. I was expecting much more from it. It lacks a lot of polish to make it at least enjoyable, even if short. The dev team congratulates itself in the credits about finishing the project in 4 months. I have to say that, sadly, it shows...

Pixel Boy is a game with a lot of failed potential, the core gameplay is fun but the bland environments, repitive enemies, lackluster bosses and simplistic crafting/progression system make Pixel boy a game not worth playing.  I bought pixel boy because the trailer and screen shots looked exciting, the description promised a game with a lot of replay value and customization and the majority of reviews are positive. My first major disappointment was in learning that the weapon customization system was built around consumable powerups with 250 shots each, this leads to a lot of time spend managing ammo and switching to the basic shot type, rather then getting to use the different weapons. Which is a shame because the variety of weapon types and combinations are interesting and fun, but the limited ammo means you never get to really explore all the different options.  My biggest issues with the game are the length and the difficulty. I beat the game on hardcore mode in about 3 hours, which felt like longer because the game really drags on. I felt little to no challenge after I was about 1/3 of the way through the game, and I only finished it because I felt like I had to. The game is composed of 4 acts of 4 levels each with a boss battle at the end of each act. There is no difference between any level, they are generated from a minimal set of rooms and populated with 4 randomly chosen monsters out of a fairly small set. The boss battles are a joke, the first boss is easy, but takes a long time to beat because of how under-powered you are at that point in the game, while the last 3 bosses can each be beaten in a matter of seconds.  With a little bit of reworking of the progression system, an actual sense of difficulty and some more variety the game could be fun but as it stands it is a fairly dull game with hints of potential for something more. As far as I can tell from the steam page the game is not in early access or anything and the last update by the developers was over 5 months ago, so I don't hold out hope that the game will get better.  I bought pixel boy for about 10$ and I would say it was worth about 25% of that. I would only recommend pixel boy if you can put up with the issues described above and the game is on sale for at least 50% off.

'Rock, Paper, Scissors' The enemy has arranged a number of Rail Guns on a ridge, overlooking the approach as your AAV's barrel up to them, desperately trying to close the range. Your enemy puts his fist in his palm, and lays down 'Paper.' You look down at your own hands...'Rock.' Damn, that's right, AAV's suck vs. Rail Guns. But wait! You quickly order your LAV's to boost up the ridge, and the enemy Rail Guns just can't compete with their speed. 'Scissors,' you smirk, as your enemy curses you. This more or less describes the micromanagement in the tactics of the game. The better you get with the controls, the quicker you will be able to command your forces, which will work in your favor. You'll want to practice this as well, taking advantage of hot keys, as 'Veterancy' is awarded to units that survive brutal encounters, and this can make them significantly more powerful.  But enough about all of that...the real gem in Deserts of Kharak is the Kapisi. You're introduced to this massive Carrier in the tutorial, a sand crawler/aircraft carrier that slowsly rolls across the sand, pounding enemies with salvos of missiles, cannon fire, strike craft, bombers...and all sorts of other goodies. You'll never be able to build anything as big as it, though the Cruisers you'll eventually construct can hold their own against anything but the Carriers. Even the Kapisi (or just an unnamed Carrier in your Skirmish/Multiplayer games) has micromanagement elements. You utilize a finite amount of Power, which can be upgraded through Research. This Power can be prioritized to Armor, Repair, Firepower and Range systems on the fly, presenting you with a dilemma. This ship, if lost, means your defeat. However, this ship is ridiculously powerful after undergoing some upgrades, so more often than not...you'll find yourself broadsiding enemies like it's 'The Black Pearl' and having a blast with it.  One last thing here before I sum this up: I had stated that Rail Guns had been aligned on top of a ridge in the opening lines of this. Elevation plays a role in determing advantages between units. This also affects the map design, which has many levels of terrain, cliffsides, rolling dunes, and many different landscaping elements. While one would think a desert would be rather bland, this one is riddled with downed starships, ready to be demolitioned and then harvested for precious resources, as well as various craters and abandoned ruins.  In Conclusion: 9/10  'What's missing? Where'd that 1 point go?' This game sticks close to the original Homeworld formula. It takes a gamble with the desert terrain and having predominantly land battles, but I would have loved to have seen some kind of 'Home City' element, similar to Age of Empires III's. In this case, I obviously wouldn't be talking about a city...I'd be talking about a starship, which would be able to provide orbital bombardment, radar scans, land reinforcements, etc. as you'd level it up. Similar to the 'Spirit of Fire' from Halo Wars. If not this, then some element of base-building/base mechanics. The planet has been engulfed by sand, yet there's no ruined cities overcome by sand that you see. The lack of structures got to me a bit, and this makes the game play a bit more like a nomadic one, in which it's impossible to 'Turtle' and your base moves across the map, exhausting resources as it goes.

Short Verdict  : As a great fan of the tabletop game, Zooloretto was a big disappointment to me. It's a set-collection, tile-placement board game which is really fun to play. But this port falls short mostly because of its poor AI and the lack of online multiplayer. You should stick to the original game, because this version just isn't fun. If it ever enables online multiplayer support, however, I'll definitely change my review and go back to playing it.    Pros  :    As the original, the game has nice mechanics a decent amount of replayability    Cute graphics, including variations to the zoos around the world    Pleasant and varied BGM    Has a shop that allows you to buy stuff that might improve your experience (no real money involved!)    The challenge level is good in general    Each campaign stage has one or more specific objectives, which might either unlock postcards or net you extra victory points in the match, and that forces you to change your play style a bit     Cons  :    The AI is either too obvious and predictable, or too dumb, to the point of frustration    Porting a game like Zooloretto to PC and not allowing for online multiplayer should be considered a federal offense, really. If it had online multiplayer, I could've avoided the terrible AI and would probably recommend and play this game.    Most of the stuff you buy in the game is useless    Many objectives too luck-dependent (eg.: One stage asks you to have 2 baby flamingos in the baby enclosure. For that, you'll need all 4 out of 4 fertile flamingos, which your opponents may get, and, btw, they may not even be drawn during the match)    The game flow was really slow for me, especially when I was quickly done for the round and had to just watch the computer play    No windowed mode and resolution doesn't go over 1280x800     Bought on / for  : I had it on my wishlist for a while and a friend had a spare and gave it to me. (Thank you, Bio!) Still, it was worth trying! I'd probably end up buying it on sale someday (and being equally disappointed).

SHOWTIME is one of those games that you can easily get into without a lot tutorials. It's simplicity allows a lot replayability. And you get your money's worth. There are better games out there, THE MOVIES, but that is nearly 10 years old, and has lacked support. It also bogs down as it tries to do too many things. SHOWTIME on the other hand let's you run a studio and make films using siple controls. The decisions seem to make some effect on the game, yet I was surprised that one of my sequels made a gazzilion dollars.   I would like to see the following features: Being able to start in 1920 with silent pictures and a smaller studio system, having verticle integration (i.e., buying, running and owning distribution channels AND theater chains), and being able to choose a HOME city and territory.   Some things though seem to make little difference. The city chose to make a film or some of the film or sound quality do not make much difference. It would be great to automatically retain case and director on a re-make/sequel. Also would be great to see how many films a particular actor has been in (one actor I used was a low level actor, after the first film he jumped to A-List status demanding 30+ million dollars, so I assume he also made other films, since mine was a mediocre success).   Other than that I think overall the game is worth it. You will probably burn out after 2-3 hours. But it's a game you can come back to later. Because of this it is within what I would classify a THUMBS UP game.

After having fell in love with Civ V, this title from Sid Meier does not live up to its false hype from fake reviewers such as IGN and so forth. The Art Style is cartoony and marketed for children, it cannot be appreciated by those seeking realism as in previous titles. The AI literally don't talk, only on some interactions but most of the time its gestures and cracking-finger sounds. The superb amount of laziness by the developers is appalling in comparison to its predecessor titles. There is no depth and no immersion, it doesn't feel real anymore. With 497 hours in Civ V as of this review, I can firmly say Civ VI was horribly executed. While the Art is unique, it is not something I can easily look at for hours on end. Save $60 and get Civilization V during a Sale, its a much better game and has the depth for those who intellectually and competitively approach the game and want something appeasing to look at, not Civilization Revolutions and the abomination of a sequel.

If you loved Civ, but are looking for something different enough to be intriguing while retaining its addictiveness, Endless Legend is the game for you.  I expected Endless Legend to be something of a Civilization clone, and that was fine by me. It is far more than that, however. The game mechanics can at first seem a little daunting to get into, and the first time I tried it I uninstalled the game not long after starting because there really isn't much of a tutorial to speak of (at least there wasn't back then). A few hours in, however, and I was completely hooked.  Each faction plays the game in a very different fashion, making replaying the game several times pretty much a necessity. The storylines of each faction are just okay, and the sudden 'jump' to the endgame missions (which are the same across the board) is a bit sudden. Nonetheless, the special abilities and units of the factions in addition to the victories which most suit them make up for the shortcomings.  The combat system is also pretty interesting, allowing you to win difficult battles on skill and positioning, while at the same time not making you suffer through whitewashes if you don't want to. A fantastic game overall, and I can't wait to try out the expansions I've just purchased to explore the new aspects of the game.

*Potential spoilers*  Only If in itself, is a very unique, story-driven game. You follow the story of a man named Anthony Clyde, who, after, shall we say, having a bit too much fun at a party, wakes up in a bed that is seemingly unfamiliar to him. After eventually composing himself, he walks through the only door in the room, only to find himself engulfed in a black smoke upon doing so, he then follows a blue light to, again, end up in a room he is unfamiliar with. It is after this that we are introduced to the game's main 'antagonist' (and I use that term loosely because it depends how the player chooses to see him). This leads me to an interesting point in the games story telling mechanic. The antagonist (who I will not name so as to not ruin the experience any further) communicates with Anthony through a radio, constantly harassing him depending on what choices you make throughout the game, leading to some sometimes humorous, sometimes unsettling dialogue at times. That's right, this game has multiple endings, adding to the replay value of this game, on top of 10 achievements (currently) for any achievement hunters out there.  The gameplay of this game is pretty much a straightforward first person puzzle platformer. You will jump from platform to platform, solve the game's cryptic riddles and puzzles, find some secrets if you explore enough and at the end of the day question whether or not you made the right choice, and that alone makes this game worth checking out or the low price of free.   The game looks excellent for a game running on the unity engine with some sometimes gorgeous views, the visual style of this game is very pleasing to the eye, and if you take the time to take a good look around at some places I'm sure you'll appreciate it just as much as I did.  Only If is one of the most unique indie experiences I've had on Steam (and I haven't had many so don't quote me on that) and I highly recommend you give this game a try if you have the time.

Sword of Asumi  Sword of Asumi is a Visaul Novel [VN] done by Dharker Studio. You're a new warrior for people living in Edo, finding out who the rebel in the samurai academy is. The cosplay bit was cute, but a bit out of place and strange, it didn't lead to any understanding of the character or any development. I recommend based on the story wasn't HORRIBLE, sound track is fantastic, easy achievements and the game gave me a few giggles along the way.

Probably the best game ive played this year. Just the amount of things you can do, things you can see and just the scale of everything, is amazing.   What is Elite Dangerous?   You take a role of a crap sidewinder commander and....its pretty much up to you what you do. From adventuring the stars thousands of lightyears from where no one has ever been, and finding things no one has ever seen. To fighting in extremely intense warfare against other factions, where...if you mess up for a split second, your dead. You can also mine, but be careful, someone is looking to kill you for your cargo...   There is also tons of other things to do, like bounty hunting, trading illegal goods under the goverment, attacking targets/convoys. There is so much to do.  You may think its just another space-shooter where you can customize your ship....let me tell you... its a lot more than just a shooter. I seriously dont know where to start there is so much to do in the game.   Some advice going into the game:   I highly suggest you play this with at least a joystick, makes the experence a lot funner.  play all the tutorials xD  dont expect to be a billionare after the first two hours.  pros:  -great graphics  -soundtrack is amazing  -tons to do  -flight simulator characteristics  -scientificly realistic in what could be made in future tech  -tons more of stuff I cant really think of atm  cons:  -hidden loading screens when leaving/coming out of supercruise ._.  -no getting out of ships yet   -grindy at times   Well now we got the pros and the cons outa da way, let me tell you a story about one of my missions into a conflict area.  Yea yea I know 'another' story time with TheMysterousMustache  BUT WHATEVA  'Ahem'  I started at a space port orbiting a planet, my mission: destroy 6 enemy spacecraft of the opposing faction that was in civil war with the federation. I was going to get paid around 250,00cr for it, so it was pretty good money. So I took off from the spaceport (nearly killing myself with the vertical thrusters as usual trying to get out) and put all pips into engines to get out of mass-lock. Once out of mass-lock I charged up my Frame shift drive and I was in supercruise. After traveling for a little bit I was nearing the conflict zone, so I switched my right planel to 'functions' to get ready to pick a faction when I entered. I exited out of supercruise into the conflict zone with a bang from the FSD. Right after I picked my faction, I was being hit by an enemy cobra mkIII. Now, I was really smart in not upgrading my stock shield generator so my shields went down almost immediately. I was able to out get the cobra off of me but I had lost health on my hull. I fought until I had six enemys destroyed....  At this point I had not realised that I was like the only friendly ship in the area....so I was in a 1 vs 4+ fight. Speedly I tried to figure out ways to distribute power to all my modules... but it was not worth it, I was taking hits from everywhere... I had to put all my pips into engines and get out of the conflict zone!   I put all power to engines, my FSD was charging up! I was going to make it out!  Then.. my shields went down. Then suddenly the front windshield exploded from a direct rail-gun hit and everything went silent... all I could hear was constant faint pounding of enemy fire hitting the ship... I could only see part of my hud... It read that I only have four minutes left of oxygen.....  Then I heard 'FSD charged, throttle up to engage', I couldnt believe it.... my FSD had survived!! I throttled up and I went into supercruise out of the conflict zone, I only had 20% hull left, and three and a half minutes of oxygen left... was I going to make it to the starport before I run out of oxygen?   Once I got in range of the starport, I had one mnute of oxygen left... I didnt know if i was going to make it.I got to the starport then I asked for docking permission. It was granted and I flew in going 200km faster then the speed limit....yeah... Once I got in and landed I had twenty seconds of oxygen left...   To sum up: MISSION SUCCESSFUL!!!  9.5/10

For me, the best iteration of Total War yet. If you like Total War, get it. If you like Warhammer, get it. If you like neither or are unsure, please go watch some videos because you may still like it. It's probably more accessible than previous titles.  I just love it. Lots of vanilla content, lots of mods. There's also both free and paid DLC, with the paid DLC not being a huge rip off for once - it's good value.  Creative Assembly stream, they put up videos, they do mod features, their community team is fun, reasonable and communicative - you can find them on reddit often. (r/totalwar). The level of support and engagement is outstanding.  So the gameplay. I was worried that magic might be overbearing in this game - it isn't. The magic is cool, but it's limited. The game still feels like Total War, it's all about the troops. Following on from that, you have special legendary lords, as well as regular lords (who can be renamed! along with troops too) - they have skill trees, acquirable items and weapons... the list goes on.  There's also great 2 player co-op, or versus campaigns. My only minor complaint is that we can't go up to 3 or 4 player co-op at this time!   It's really good, really really good, and even better with a friend to share in it all. Then once you've conquered the world as all of the races, you slap a bunch of mods on and do it all again fresh!  If you can afford it, if you think you might like it, I can't see you being disappointed.

Dear Older People,  I've almost hit 45 and I have generally stayed away from twitch based games. I am a bit slower and occasionally I need to get up to help the kids or the wife. I never group because I don't want to imposition people if I can't stick around. So, I was really nervous about putting down money on this game.  This game is pretty awesome. As a solo player, I can accumulate a bit more experience if I feel I am stuck on a mission. I don't feel that I am too disadvantaged with the guns; they are not too 'twitchy'. There are skills and abilities that can help me continue my solo, slackardly ways (I use auto turrets as my friend). After you clear a few mobs in a mission, nothing else progresses until you do. You can just leave the game and work on whatever you need to do.  The loot system is satisfying. I really felt like I could mod the weapons to accomodate my poor shooting.   I nearly crapped myself during my first visit to the dark zone. It was a wonderful experience that I look forward to do quick bursts of.  The story and snippets are well done.   Building your own fortress feels important. The additions really matter.  Good times. Don't fear the Division.

The PVE in this game is amazing, however this game is not without its drawbacks.  First off, this is hefty game, and good lord can places kill your FPS sometimes. Make sure you have a good PC for this, I have a pretty good pc myself and I need to maintain lowest graphical settings, and even then there are places that turn 3fps to 3spf (seconds per frame)  One of the biggest draw backs to this game, however, is PvP. First off, there are covenants that do the exact same thing. Two forest hunter covenants, two invasion covenants, and two guardian covenants. You familiar with the Blue Sentinels being summoned to help hosts to guard them against invaders? Well the darkmoon blades are back, and they do the exact same thing. There's no sin, and there are no blue invasions except for forest and guardianship. What's the point of having multiple covenants if half of them do the same job? Does it divide the rewards by giving you different tokens after winning? YES, but you can at least give them different duties to perform.  Furthermore, and this is gonna be the most rage inducing of all: when you're invading, you're prioritized to invade worlds where the host has a summon. That's right, you're more likely than ever before to invade a gank than meet a one on one duel, and this is REALLY common, especially on launch.  However, this game is beautiful in it's PvE sense and has strong lore points. I still heavily reccomend this game (if you can run it) for its lore and PvE aspects, as they are some of the most fun things I've had in a long time in the Souls Series. However, if you're in it just for the PvP, wait untill all the summoning dies down, or avoid this game if you can. You are likely to be ganked 8 times out of 10, and the covenants are unoriginal and unpolished.

This game is 10 years old, and it seriously still holds up. You could release this today and it would still be better than most other ARPGs, which probably explains my unscheduled dropping of the other games I was playing to put 18 hours (so far) into this! In fact, as much as I love Grim Dawn (and acknowledge that it's a 'better game'), I still prefer Titan Quest. Its ancient mythology setting immediate differentiates it from the demons, doom and gloom of most other ARPGs, and it's so refreshing to play through the bright sunlit Greek hills once more. It makes the contrast with the darkness and the musty caves that much more striking.  And has anyone ever done ragdolling better than this game? Smashing creatures in the face and watching them sail over buildings and disappear into canyons is still enormous fun, and later on it's like being Sauron in the prologue to Fellowship of the Ring. Lighting up the screen with spell pyrotechnics also looks fabulous with this edition. I love the combat in Titan Quest (once it gets going), which is probably a good job because it's a long game.  It's not perfect. Titan Quest always had a slow start, NPCs love to blather on about stuff in faux-Greek accents if you keep clicking on them (completionists be warned!), and it's a few hours in before you really start to feel like a warrior. Good job then that they've introduced a speed setting to whisk you past the opening and onto the good stuff. Once you upgrade your movement and attack speed, you'll probably want to turn it back to normal to keep up. There are other little touches, like a new 'pick up all collectibles' button which saves a lot of clicking. Multiplayer also finally works again, and coop in Titan Quest is a hoot.  The biggest improvement for me, though, is that the playfield now scrolls smoothly and without any of the annoying blurry judder of the original. I installed TQ on half a dozen PCs and I could never get rid of it, so having it scroll around silkily at last is a massive difference and improves the feel of the whole game. Whatever they did (triple buffering?), it makes a beautiful game look modern again.  So yeah, thanks THQNORDIC for bringing us these improvements, and giving it to existing owners for free. Hopefully it will allow more people to discover this classic.

Dishonored holds a special place in my heart. It's an interesting pitch to say the least. A stealthy action FPS set in a semi-victorian, semi-steampunk, semi-futuristic psuedo-brtianic world, where whales are the primary source of energy, an omnicient being changes the world from the shadows, and a deadly plauge consumes the city. With tight controls, interesting abilities, and a unique narrative Dishonored is full of surprises, plot twists, and little world building easter eggs.   This game also boasts a moral cause and effect system, leading to increased difficulty for those who kill senselessly or get set off alarms often. Your actions during the missions have a real effect on the world, and the way you solve the challanges put before you changes other characters actions throughout the game. Immediately after beating this game I went back and played through it again- and that is not something many games can say they've done.

You can play as everyone from the movies, from Malfoy, to Ginny, and even the professors. All of Hogwarts is recreated from the big room they all sit in, to the dorms where they live. Lots of fun abilities, like turning back time, make the same world seem new again. There is ALOT of content in this Lego game, very impressive the level of detail. Not being a Harry Potter fan I had a great time playing the game, and oddly like the game better than the movies.   Rating: 10/10 Value: 49.99

We had people shooting at us from up on the rooftops, from the houses, from the sewers or wherever they could take a shot at us from...  You had to be smart, you had to be thinking on your feet the whole time, and at the same time, have control to not pull that trigger on an innocent...  Everything my life has been before and after pales in the light of that awesome moment when my amtrac started in amid a thunderous bombardment toward the flaming, smoke-shrouded beach for the assault on the city… Shells crashed all around. Fragments tore and whirred, slapping on the sand and splashing into the water a few yards behind us…. The world was a nightmare of flashes, violent explosions, snapping bullets. Most of what I saw blurred. My mind was benumbed by the shock of it… Up and down the street and in the alleys a number of tanks and DUKWs were burning, Hajji machine gun bursts made long splashes on the water as though flaying it with some giant whip…. I caught a fleeting glimpse of some Marines leaving a smoking humvee in the street. Some fell as bullets and fragments splashed among them… I turned my face away and wished that I were imagining it all. In the midst of all of this, I thought of my sweetheart back home. Was I going to be sent to a worse Hell that I was already in? I had tasted the bitterest essence of war, the sight of helpless comrades being slaughtered, and it filled me with disgust.   I give this game a 10/10!

Absolutely solid gameplay, a spirit successor to DOD: Source. This is the polar opposite of a run and gun game whilst not being long winded and boring. It has the perfect balance of simulation and pace of play. The way guns handle, sound and look are truly accurate and the loadout selection and mechanics are perfectly balanced. Each weapon has an effective range making the fire rate of a bolt action worth it, and with there being a lot of fire selectable/semi auto firearms (especially with the DOI mod) this gives character and preference to each rifle. Aiming and accuracy is great and realistic, damage is well mapped and balanced (you're gonna get a guaranteed kill with anything point blank to the head, but you're not gonna be sniping with a pistol)  I highly reccommend trying cooperative modes too, the bot AI is as good as human logic, even more difficult than multiplayer on harder settings.  Overall 9/10, An awesome game with top quality immersion (avoid servers with sound mods..). only downside is the source engine graphics (this is a lot better in the Day of Imfamy standalone, and will be much much better when moved to the Unreal engine).

The German boss gasses you.  10/10  Why is everyone complaining about microtransactions? They don't boost any stats! They're purely cosmetic, and you can earn them without buying anything??????  This game is very fun. The objective is simple, kill zeds. You can choose what class you are, and there are many of them such as Support (shotgun lovers), Assault (Assault rifle lovers), Marksman (Sniper lovers), Demolitionist (boom lovers), SWAT (PDW/SMG lovers), and much more. You choose the class that fits your playstyle. Now even though you have a specific class, you can still buy and use guns from different classes. Using them will raise EXP with the class that the gun you're using.   There has been talk about microtransations, and while that used to be an issue, Tripwire helped us out here. When you kill the bosses on the last wave, you're always rewarded with a cosmetic item. You can sell it if you don't want it, or craft it down into building supplies to make a new cosmetic. Cool system, much like TF2.  Gameplay is fun. Gore system is great. I remember listening to a dev say that each zed model has like 40 different bodyparts that can fly off or something. Now thats cool.  Animations are really good. Shooting doesn't feel like poop, and AI aren't bad.  Its very satisfying to pop heads off of the enemies. They wander around for a few seconds and then drop. Bosses are difficult -- as they should be. CO-OP is great, Steam Workshop is also great.

The biggest flaw with EoS is its glaringly lacking help menus. This is a significant flaw because the opportunities offered by the game far outweigh the amount of helpful information about the different tools and installations you can craft. This can lead to extended periods of aimless mining and construction that can feel pointless and frustratingly anti-climactic (I've been surprised quite often at how little I could accomplish during long hours of continuous play because of a lack of clear objectives).  That being said, I've grown to enjoy Edge of Space very much. Here's a bit of context: you are dropped on a foreign planet after a spaceship crash and evacuation. Your codex (the help menus) is obviously incomplete and written very much like the entries in The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy: unfocused and essentially devoid of any useful information beyond vague hints at a distant purpose. As the player, it is your job to extrapolate meaning -and ultimately results- from what little help and guidance you are offered by your interplanetary employer.  Edge of Space plays very much like the old NES and SNES Metroid games, with the added elements of terraforming and crafting. The bottom line is that once you've 'figured out' the basics, the game works. Very well.  So, provided you're willing to tolerate fuzzy objectives and the occasional quirks, you should enjoy Edge of Space for what it is: a wacky, competely over-the-top sandbox with a lot of potential and a ton of fun! And laser sharks!  FREE TIP: Keep digging deeper. And when you think you've gone deep enough, keep going. Edge of Space offers a BIG world!

The previous Thief games (1 and 2) focused on huge expansive sprawling levels, huge systems with rooms interacting with rooms. While this game offers a unique survival simulator, it is terribly hampered with several deep flaws that detract from the overall quality of the game:  1: No actual save option. You only get a single autosave per campaign that will often trigger at awkward moments when you didn't want it to save. What's that, you spent many hours trying to get a character to elite status and you make one mistake and blunder into a feral zombie? Too bad, your character is now dead, FOREVER. You cannot make a mistake, not a single mistake, and this would not be so bad if the poorly programmed AI didn't take control of the characters you are not currently controlling (basically all but one), but it does, so sometimes it's not even your pleasure to make that mistake. 2: Idiotic AI. This includes things like: Not helping out in a melee fight for survival, running straight into zombie hordes when sent on a scavenging pickup mission, or even not defending the base when a zombie night raid summons.  3. Emotionally immature characters. While your out and about kicking zombie ♥♥♥ and gathering precious supplies for your home base, the characters at base will become discontent very quickly and easily, and the only way to prevent them from draining the moral of the base is to take them out on little suicidal raids on zombie infestations. If it was just a quick conversation in home base it would not be an issue, but you have to go out of your way to some randomly selected zombie place and fight side by side with them in order to cheer them up. But of course right after you complete that mission you might have to do it again, for the SAME character, just half a day cycle later. I thought this was a zombie survival game, and not Dr. Phil with a shotgun? 4. Broken hit-boxes and glitched zombies. If there was one resource that was perhaps the most precious in this game it would be ammo, and there just happens to be a horrible glitch that drains this precious resource: Sometimes when new zombies spawn at a location they are out of phase with the game, thus when you shoot rounds at their head the bullets go straight through and do nothing. Sometimes you will have to run around in circles to wait for them to engage you and then you can actually hit them with a weapon.  5. Skewed third-person shooting. While it is perfectly normal for third person shooters to have the camera just above the right shoulder when looking down the sights of a gun, this game has that same effect exaggerated to a unbelievable extent. Instead of it being just over the shoulder, the camera rests several feet away from the shoulder, as if it were the perspective of a drill instructor. Unfortunately, the invisible drill instructor cannot help you aim better as the mouse sensitivity and aim axis is wobbly and frustrating to master. I can understand the aiming to get wobbly if the character was fatigued, but to have it this inaccurate as a default just screams troll intent from the game developers. 6. Your outposts are only somewhat effective. While it is advertised in game that establishing an outpost at a location will stop zombies from spawning in the area, this is a lie. Even after having several overlapping outposts in a given area, you might still have zombies spawn on the main thoroughfares. While outposts do indeed reduce the amount of zombie spawns, the effectiveness of outposts is one of degree and not definition.   Overall I would say this game is too plagued with glitches and suffers from poor execution of brilliant game concepts. While the auto-save only function can be argued to be an effort by the developer to make the game seem more hardcore, the fact that you are FORCED into the auto-save-only system takes away the freedom of the gamer and thus the ability of the gamer to fully enjoy the game. What they should have done is offer a choice between a regular mode (that has normal save and load functions) and then a hardcore mode that would be autosave only (for those with the patience to wade through trolled frustration).   This game has crippled my appetite for zombie survival games, and I now regret even purchasing this game on sale for ten dollars.

This game is strangely soothing.    You take over a collection of villagers, and the difficulty range is based on what condition you take them over in. You generally speaking play completely from macro management, but this means the town you develop becomes organic rather than dated or clunky. You can get plenty of fun out of struggling through starvation and bad choices you made maybe 10 game years back. At times I genuinely had to go back to a save from an in game lifetime ago to prevent disaster.   That said, past a certain point the game becomes limited in scope. You build a town to a point from which it can't possibly fail; and here is where the game grows slightly tedious. You repeat some of the building projects you've already struggled through but on a much larger scale to accomodate a growing population. But the lack of any grand projects or developments to take your villagers through slowly finds you losing interest. Even pointless aesthetic buildings would alleviate this; a castle or walls for my town.   It also becomes strange when considering the modern standard for video games to be multiplayer. I quite enjoyed that my village was a haven from my friends turning up and wrecking the place - at first. But after some time you feel the need to share it with someone, and the lack of even rival in game towns or potential bandits reinforces the loneliness that will have you booting up an MMO of some sort in ways that other solitary games never do.  The game designers themselves deserve a pat on the back. The game is the most polished game I've played in a very long time, and this is part of the appeal that makes you feel like you're truly working within an artistic environment. I was extremely grateful to play a game that felt like it was properly released for once, and this alone made it worth purchasing.   Would strongly reccomend at the cheap price and quality of the game, and know I'll be coming back to it sporadically for many years to come.

The quick time events are action packed, and discovering each fable's powers or abilities keeps the game exciting and new. As with all Telltale games, you can feel the weight that your decisions carry and you may be moved to rewind to a certain chapter in order to fix a problem you created for yourself, whatever you decide to do will affect you all the way into the end of the game. After finishing this game and hunting down all the book entries on the world that this game lives in, I am now going into the real world to pick up the Fables comics, and I encourage you to do the same.

Years ago there was a browser based Flash game that was similar to this. It was really bare-bones. No sunlight component, no power-ups, no daily level builder. It was just a randomly generated set of cones to dodge, and the game would get faster at higher thresholds. Still I remember my entire Comp Lit class in middle-school playing it every chance we could get. It was always one of my favorite arcade style scoreboard games. So when this released I bought it on both PSN and Steam and didn't regret it.  It has just the right amount of basic, fun gameplay elements that make a game really hit the mark. The features that have been added to that old browser based version I remember really turn it into something great, and it is very refreshing to return to. You can play a couple rounds and then put it down, take turns with friends and family, or just get sucked in for hours hunting the high-level achievements.  I nominated this game for the 2016 Steam Awards in the open category as '5 Minutes to Kill' because it's definitely my default when ... I have 5 minutes to kill.

I've been on and off about buying this game. It's always drawn my attention, but then I'd watch the gameplay on YouTube, and I'd decide against buying it because it struck me as all fighting, and the occasional open world exploration. I was wrong. Yes, there's a ton of hand to hand fighting, but there's so much more than that. There's a lot of collectibles, tons of randomly generated people you can help out (that isnt't repetitive either). There's also a lot of different rewards systems too, such as the 'clean driving streak' (not driving like a hopped up meth head in a police chase on his third strike).  The game mechanics are good. The hand to hand combat takes some getting used to, but over all, the controls are smooth. I only have about three hours of game play so far, so all I'm able to comment on are my first impressions (which is the most important part in my opinion). The story is good, the graphics are really good AND well optimized. I,m about three hours into the game, and I,m impressed. If a game hasn't 'hooked' me by three hours, it's probably not going to. I consider this a good buy.   In case anyone is wondering, I am playing this at maxed out settings using an MSI GTX 970 4GB (in regular gaming mode) and it's not even breaking a sweat. I've experienced absolutely no screen tearing and no texture popping. It stays at an even 60 FPS (my monitor is older hha' so it's on a 60 Hz). I'm using an Intell i7 950 3.1 GHz processor, and an ols gigabyte Z87 mother board. My system is pretty old save for the graphics card, and it's playing the game just fine,  Just to show you how powerful the MSI GTX 970 is, all my hardware (minus my GPU) is about 7-8 years old. In the factory OC mode (using the Gaming App, I'm playing Far Cry 4 at max settings with smooth 60 FPS (I am not using the MFAA though, only SMAA).   So, if you're like me and you have relatively old, but powerful hardware, Sleeping Dogs should run very well as long as you have a good graphics card. Feel free to ask me any questions if you have any.

I rarely write reviews for games, but I figured I would address some issues for someone who is on the fence about getting this title. Being an avid player of all the Baldurs Gates, and Icewind Dale series, I was excited when Sword Coast Legends was released. But to my dismay, after the release, all the horrible reviews started flowing in, so I held off on buying it, thinking it would be a huge disappointment. Wow was I wrong! I should have got this game a long time ago! After 19 hrs of play, I can't get enough. But don't get me wrong, Sword Coast Legends is no Baldurs Gate. If you are looking for the depth of Baldurs Gate, this game is not it. But don't let that stop you from playing this game, because it is awesome! If it's the bad reviews that's holding you back, forget them! Or you will be missing out on a wonderful adventure.   A few things I'd also like to point out are:  -This is not a Diablo clone like some reviews say. It's nothing like Diablo at all. -This is not an arcade game like some reviews say. Played on a normal difficulty it requires tactical pausing to survive. -If you like RPGs like Baldurs Gate, Icewind Dale, Pillars of Eternity, or something similar, GET THIS GAME!!!!!  One other thing I'd like to mention, that can only be described as the only negative aspect of this game that I've encountered, is the Vsync doesn't work. When i first installed this game the screen tears were so bad I almost stopped playing. No matter what options I chose when customizing my video options, Vsync was unresponsive. But fear not! I enabled Vsync through my graphics card settings and Voila! Vsync works! And screen tears are almost completely gone!  Get this game. You won't regret it!

Early Access Review

If you played the original, you already know what this game is about. If you haven't, then let me tell you that this is likely one of the finest Tower Defense games of all time.  As in the original, the whole game is about defending your ship against hostiles while ferrying cargo from point A to point B. Enemies can come from 6 different angles, and you have to respond with proper turrets as necessary. All constructions serve an important purpose, there are no 'useless' things to build. Everything is useful in the proper circumstances, and a large part of the game is figuring out what works best where and against what enemies in order to get through each run in one piece. The difficulty curve is well paced, gradually making it harder as you go along, and I only stumbed a few times on a few particular runs that frustrated me to no end, but in the end it turned out I was simply lacking either the correct tower or ship layout, and once remedied success came not long after.  In the previous game you unlocked towers pemanently, and once unlocked they were available to build as many copies of as you wished. In SRG, however, you are instead tasked to craft each buildable copy of a building at the mission hubs. This does not only require money, but also crafting materials which are awarded after each run. These materials are physical, and must be moved while onboard your ship like any other cargo if you want to relocate them to another planet or station, and therein lies the main difference between this game and its predecessor. During each run you can only build as many copies of a turret or building as you've crafted beforehand. The quota is restored after each run, so they're never permanently consumed however you will find that you have to increase the quota for many of the more essential towers quite a abit, and it can become a bit of a chore to haul all this stuff around the universe just to be able to craft more stuff.  This is where the multiplayer portion comes into play. You can design multiplayer contracts that will appear as selectable missions for others players, allowing them to haul your stuff around for you. A player is always rewarded by the game for completing a multiplayer contract with additional crafting materials or bonus money, but in order to entice them to chose YOUR contract over others you can assign a premium to be paid out as well. This will be taken out of your own pocket, but if you don't want to you don't have to. Most contracts out there have no premium (the cheap ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s...) but as you still get material rewards you might as well do them if they're convenient to you. Even if the materials are of no use to you, you can still sell them to the merchant vendor for actual money.   All in all, I loved the original and thoroughly enjoyed this one as well. They had some server issues the first week or two of the launch, but it was still very playable. Nowadays, it doesn't crash or freeze for me at all, so it seems to me that all that has been taken care of. As mentioned, the mid-game can seem to be bit of a grind but that's mainly because you're trying to haul all your crap around yourself. Use the multiplayer contracts to both make it easier for you and create content for your fellow players in the process, and don't try to play through the whole game in one sitting. It is best enjoyed doing a few runs every now and then to avoid repetition.  If you like tower defense, I highly recommend you check out this game.

The new free update 1.4 patch that just dropped has fixed almost all the issues I had with the game's endgame from launch. Sure the playerbase has dropped substantially so there are longer quese if you are looking for co-op by yourself but so much has improved.   - Time to kill has been reduced to make you feel powerful again rather than wasting all your ammo on a single enemy only to get one shot by it in the end.  - Loot drops, quanitity and quality has been reworked to feel rewarding, so that the difficulty you undertake only affects quatity and the level of enemies now effects quality, meaning you can gear up at your own pace easily and comfortably.  - Some of the more overpowered gear sets have been balanced making PvP slightly more viable an option without the fear of getting destroyed by someone running skill power tank builds.  I'm sure there are many more great changes in the patch notes that I haven't mentioned here but these are the big ones that stuck out to me coming back to the game after 3 or so months, it's the game that I always wanted it to be now almost.  Haven't come across hackers yet since the player base has dropped so not sure if they've actually fixed that situation. Also if you are willing to fork out money for the DLC they are top notch too. With survival just being released that has really good rewards and is a nice new twist, and underground being an interesting dungeon like experience, they are probably my second favourite DLC to Diablo 3 ROS.  But yeeee solid 8/10 now in my opinion.

London looks incredible, as does fighting on a moving train or carriage. The new driving system is pretty decent as well.  And it actually runs well and isn't a buggy mess.

I really can't recommend this game at all unless you already know how to play this game or have no problem researching on forums how to properly play the game.   The first thing I noticed when I first started the game was that there is no tutorial on how to begin playing the game. It literally gives you a message saying visit the forums for more information on how to play....really? Very poor and lazy on the developers part. There needs to be some sort of introduction into the game instead of just tossing you into it with no real directions oncesoever. Also, if you have ever used Windows 3.1 then these boxes will look very familiar. It looks a bit sharper in the screenshots provided but when you start playing, you will notice the ugly edges on all the charts/messages/etc and it feels incredibly cheap. I expected quite a bit more polish out of an officially licensed game. I saw the screenshots but it looks much poorer once you actually start it up and feels like it was meant to be played on an old box monitor instead of on crisp hd monitors. Some chart boxes looked so poor they almost seemed like they overlapped. It's like if you took nearly a dozen Windows 3.1 boxes and put them all together and added in some pictures/icons/colored font/etc.  The two things I liked about the game was how in depth it is and the player portraits. The portraits were all in some kind of weird but somewhat realistic looking shots. David Ortiz looked almost like a claymation version of himself which I thought was neat and looked closed enough. This is great for a simulation game since once you progress to a point where you need fake players created as rookies then they can just use any random faces and it won't look weird next to the other real players since there are no real player photos in the game to begin with.  All in all, I honestly can't recommend this game. I'm a huge fan of sports, sports simulations and tycoon games but this feels much more appropriate for gaming in the early 1990's than an officially licensed game in 2016. I do like the direction they are going in and I may revisit this series if they release a game in the future that adds a proper introduction to the game, better looking boxes/charts and possibly some larger bolder font.

This game should become a part of a 'Digital citizenship' curriculum for sure - there is no privacy on the internet and sometimes, there is no truth either.   Orwell is, for sure, one of the few games concerning very recent and 'hot' topics and does so perfectly.  Although I suspected it would be anti-surveillance (it's called Orwell, after all), it positively surprised me. It did not push me at all not to cooperate with the government, it just let a few hints slip - like 'We had to change the facts a bit to prevent panic' - sure, that's not a big deal, it's just the beginning, the first little white lie that ends in a dogma.  The best job Orwell does is making you feel helpless and powerfull all at once - only you know the whole story, you choose what Orwell knows and what it does not, you can set someone up or you can set them free but ultimately, you never know what the Agent is going to do...will he misinterpret the data and send someone to prison? Will he understand the reason you upload certain information? He never sees the 'bigger picture', or the part of it that you have access to. Are the people getting hurt really not on your conscience; did you JUST do your job? Are you really as impartial as you fancy yourself to be? What if it was your life being dissected?

When you think of Survival Horror, games like Resident Evil and Silent Hill probably come to mind; but these games have a fatal flaw: the player gets to be a big hero, fighting and defeating the monsters. Amnesia takes that away from you, leaving the player in a position of being alone in a scary and mysterious place, trying to piece together the mystery surrounding his being there. The puzzles are legitimately intriguing and the monsters actually quite scary; and since the game doesn't really have any combat to speak of, all you can do is try and avoid the monsters while you uncover the story. This is the best survival horror game ever, and if you follow the suggestions - play alone, in the dark, with headphones - it will scare the hell out of you.

Hello I'm Cosmic and welcome to the latest addition to the Wolfenstein franchise, Wolfenstein The New Order. This new sequel to the much beloved FPS franchise has been developed by Machine games who are a Swedish developer formed in 2009 and hold experienced developers who worked on both Riddick and the Darkness video games.  So finally we are back in the shoes of the Nazi killing machine that is William 'B.J' Blazkoviz. Those familiar with the previous Wolfenstien titles will know that Blazkoviz is the reoccurring main protagonist. Once again your pitted against General William Strasse more commonly known as Deathshead. The New order takes place in an alternate reality of 1960 where the Nazi's somehow found advanced technology that allowed them to win world war 2 and thus take over the world. Blazkoviz was injured in 1946 at an assualt on deathshead compound and spends 14 years in a polish mental hospital. Once he awakens he sets of to find the resistance and take the fight back to the Nazi world regime.   Full Video Review -  http://youtu.be/8tChROv54Ic

This game is something worth killing some time with. I'll try something new in this review by listing pluses and minuses.  Positives: + It's free + Game modes are fun + Reminds me of Team Fortress 2 in some ways + Easy to find co-op vs bot matches + Able to run the game at max settings on a GTX 750 TI with well over 60 fps. (note: didn't use VSync when testing)  Negatives: - Matchmaking is very slow when searching for PvP (wasn't able to find a match) - Requires you to basically play with your own steam friends in order to play a match that isn't co-op  Overall: I recommend this game because it is free and you'll find maybe at least a bit of enjoyment out of it. In Witcher I really felt like a fleshed out character, that prior to the game has had real relationships, adventures and experience. I cannot say how they relate to the books as I have not read them though.  Don't play this game if all you want is flash (play the next Witcher for that, I'm in the middle of it now). And if you do play, stay for the long haul. The first chapter originally put me off when I first played years ago, but I started from scratch again this time and thoroughly enjoyed.
