app_name: Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon Future Soldier
review_text: The latest installment of the Ghost Recon franchise is a good game its not great though.  It is no longer a squad based tactical shooter game like the first few outings - you cannot pick members of your squad (they are all the same), you spend the first half of the mission sneaking around and silently killing people then the second half involves doing a call of duty run and gun everything in your way.  The weapon customisation and the ability to pick your wargear prior to each mission was good, but it doesnt make up for the fact that it is very similar to the latest COD outing... Look its still worth playing and the graphics and squad interface and sync kills are a great feature.

app_name: South Park™: The Stick of Truth™
review_text: South Park: The Stick of Truth is a great comedy RPG from Obsidian. It remains true to the show and allows the player to intergrate themselves into it.  Great mechanics, full of comedy and crass humour. It is recommended for anyone who enjoys the show, RPG's or both.   The problems it has is that the game is a little too easy and it is short for an RPG.  There is one recurring abortion gag with some ridiculously tedious mechanics that you will need either guess work or a guide to get past and it is used a couple of times in the game making it irritating.  And there is little replayability except to make the opposite choices of your first play through and if you are a completionist.   In the end it is quite a funny and well made South Park RPG that takes the ♥♥♥♥ out of the gaming industry and everything in general as Trey Parker and Matt Stone are well known for that will leave you satisfied you played as if you had watched the show.

I wish there was an 'uncertain' option for reccomendations, instead of just 'yes' or 'no', as that's how I feel.  When you start out, you have a freebie load with junk gear. You can buy any new items you want with wallet points that aren't that hard to earn (You get 1 for participation, 2 more for winning a round, 1 for capping 5 points in a round. items cost around 3-10 points, and the rounds are about 15 minutes). If you want enough items for more than 2 loads, you'll have to pay. No problem with the wallet system, it's most certainly not pay to win.  Now onto the tanks. I expected a bit more variety... Here's a list of the major parts categories: 1. Weapons. These attach to turrets or to the hull (On the 'hammer' hull. The hammer hull is a huge joke as the weapon cannot really be aimed, fires independently of the turret, and the hull itself is weak.) You can get flamethrowers, machine guns, sniper rifles, lasers (That have a stupid velocity and travel about the same speed as machine gun bullets, which are also commicaly slow.), cannons, plasma guns, and several others. Note, you can only fire 1 weapon at any time, even if you have twin turrets, a weapon on your hull, or a turret with dual weapon slots. 2. Hulls. Your propulsion system and your turret(s) both attach to the hull of your tank. The hull provides armor, a larger hitbox, and (for some hulls) a place to attach a useless weapon or two supports. 3. Turrets. There are many turrets, with many different goals. All provide varying hitpoints, rotation speeds, weapon slots (Up to two, but they cannot be fired together), and support slots. Some do not have any supports. 4. Supports. Since you've been wondering what they heck I keeo saying 'supports' for, I'll explain. Supports are guns, armor units, healers, weight reducers, etcetera. They do less damage or are less effective than any other items designed for their task, but can be fired with your weapon. Hence, you will always want a turret with a support on it, as they give you a significantly better DPS. 5. Propulsion. Tanks can be moved by treads, legs, fans, or even a... slime ball. Treads are generally better as all-rounders but cannot strafe. Fans can strafe and move across water, but are less powerful. Legs let you climb onto walls but are unpredictable, awkward, and generally slower. The slime ball is a strafing tread.  So how do the developers get their cash if this can all be earned, and quite easily, in a few days of playing? As a F2P you have limited item storage space, although you can store more parts as you level up. (Last I leveled, I could store 11 parts, which was enough for me anyhow.)  Now onto the technical details, where the tasting gets unpleasant. This game, put frankly, has the optimization of a marathon runner carrying dumbells and pushing a wheelbarrow. My PC does better handling Planetside 2, an extremely cpu intensive game, with 200 players in a battle, than it does this little low-poly tank game with 8 vs 8. Kinda ridiculous. Controls are tanky, mouse sensitivity can be changed by a sliderbar with arbitrary numbers on it. There is no raw input option.  To wrap up, how's the balance? Are there ridiculously overpowered weapons that only jerks use? Yes and no. Sniper rifles do insane damage, but have stupidly powerful scopes (probably 6x zoom on small maps), bullets slower than real life arrows, and the tank itself jitters in a ridiculous manner. Flamethrowers will pawn anything within their grasp faster than you can say 'flammenwerfer', and don't have any true downsides on a fast tank.
Games works smoothly though it would be nice if the game had achievements.

McPixel is not exactly a puzzle game. It's more of a humourus take on the genre. You are McPixel, a guy who finds himself in the strangest scenarios (a train about to explode, a pyramid about to explode, an iceberg about to explode, a tower about to explode... you get the idea) and needs to save the day with the few objects he can use. Initially you may think that you can use logic for that, but... nope! Everything is absurd. First, the fun lies in trying to save the day, then you replay trying to see all the possible endings (mostly: explosions), or, as the game says, 'find all the gags'.  I played the four chapter of the basic game and had enough fun, then got bored and didn't even try the numerous DLCs freely available. It's so cheap that it is hard not to recommend it, you will laugh several times playing it!   My favourite levels were those based on famous movies and videogames. And you can also kick Batman and Darth Vader in the nuts!

Pros  - Not Sim City 2013 - Looks very good for a game from 2004 - Gritty, Realistic, Approach to City Design  - Large Selection of Terrains - Easily Adjustable Settings - Amazing Soundtrack - Modding Kit makes for a longer game  Cons  - Terrible System Optimization for Newer PCs - Borderline Broken Demands on Easy and Medium Difficulties without Mods  I have been playing this game for years, picked it up on Steam last Summer. I have to say, the biggest issue I have with it now is Optimization on my PC. You cannot run the game past 1600x1080 without massive screen tearing and Region View Issues and Terrain Glitches (Purely Cosmetic.) Overall I would say the only other noticable issue, after 10 years (10 year old game better than a game made in 2013, who the ♥♥♥♥ knew?!) is the broken system demands on Easy and Medium. Honestly I see no reason not to buy this game.  9/10 - Great game if you like City Planning and Building games.

The pros:  The game is quite fun and very addictive. It's a strategy RPG with a unique concept that is pulled off extremely well. It's the perfect application of crafting mechanics, too. The graphics are beautiful, and the music is impressively good. The concept of going from small-time to big time is a brilliant idea for a game of this manner.  There is a lot of attention to detail in the game, with secret things to find both graphically and plot wise. It's easily worth the MSRP, and is one of those things you don't know you want until you have played it for yourself.  Protip: Don't try hard mode on your first playthrough. It's really unbalanced hard.  The cons:  The programming is so glitchy the game feels like a beta version. The textboxes overflow when people are talking and transitions are often jarringly abrupt, just cutting out and flickering when a level ends instead of having a proper 'you win' screen. The controller controls need major work as they just operate the mouse cursor instead of having true controller support.  The writing is mediocre, with very poor grammar that could have used major proofreading. Some of the scenarios are sort of nonsensical due to the writing. The appearances of numerous Kickstarter backers break the immersion of the game, it would have been better if they didn't have to awkwardly say their full names every time someone appeared. The backer characters all look like the same nerdy white guys, and dont make sense in the setting.  With the lack of characterization you'd think there'd be some kind of character creation for your team, but instead there is very little in the way of choice if you want a decent team, I wish you could choose from more diverse appearances and just assign certain personalities to them, instead there are kind of obvious 'best characters', so if you didn't want a cartoon beaver on your team youre flat out of luck.

Great combat system, art, and style;mediocre everything else. The narrator has WAY too little variety basically making it a necessity to mute him, while the lore and story are better left ignored. That doesn’t matter too much in a game like this, since the gameplay is what’s important.   Unfortunately, the exploration mechanics are so obtuse and set against the player with cheap RNG to the extent that you can't even capitalize off of what’s supposed to be a positive result. For example being surprised basically dooms your party in certain circumstances, and almost always results in a hero dying or being brought down to 0 hp based on the order they’re put in, while surprising the enemy just results in the enemies going twice in a row, which means you're still taking more unmanageable and frankly unreasonable damage considering that you have to survive consecutive fights and losing heroes has lasting effects on what you have to do next. Each time you lose a hero or they become inflicted with something you need to heal, it is not money you primarily lose, it is time. Not a resource in the game, but your actual free time. Whenever you lose a hero, you more or less have to play a couple more hours just to grind out new heroes to replace dead ones, which can easily result in you losing more heroes due to bad rolls, making it so you need to grind even more. The game is just a perpetual meat grinder where you’re going to spend 10 hours just trying to reach where you were before a bad roll killed your party. This reliance on RNG for the main difficulty ruins the fun of the combat, since whenever you do beat a difficult run it feels less like something you earned, and more like you just got lucky.    In the end for a game that expects you to put 50 hours into it to beat, it doesn't nearly make itself feel worthwhile enough to bother finishing. While I enjoy a lot of the time I put into it and I will probably put more time into it in the future, considering how frustrating it can be to play, how much of a time investment it is, and the repetitive nature of the game, I cannot recommend Darkest Dungeon, as I cannot recommend a game that doesn’t feel worthwhile to beat.

The Expendabros is a clever little piece of promo material for The Expendables 3 movie and the original Broforce game. I haven't payed Broforce yet to know how different the gameplay is, but you essentially massacre goons in a playstyle much like Metal Slug, though without the vehicles and end of level boss fights. There are occasional miniboses and a couple true bosses, but they aren't a focus of the gameplay, and more a focus of the end plot. You start off with one character that shoots dual pistols that have limited range, but moderate fire rate. Don't worry about damage, pratically all standard goons die in one hit. Throughout the levels are cages that have other Bros which automatically change your character to the new bro, lets you play with the bro's different abilities, and gives you a free life. Lives are precious, as while it is extremely easy to kill most enemies, it's extremely easy to die. All Bros have a melee attack and a standard attack which is usually ranged, though one Bro has medium ranged melee attack, and a special that requires ammo, but usually has high damage or area of effect.  It's very fun and doesn't take itself too seriously. It can be a bit fustrating to die from an unfortunate environmental explosion or hazard. And the 3rd or 4th last level killed me way much more than I'd like to admit, but I still had fun despite the constant deaths. The pixel art is charming and the sound design is intentially over the top with hard rocking soundtracks and a 'roid raging announcer. There's not alot to this game, but it's a fun little experiment that doesn't really do anything wrong (besides a UI issue on the Configure Keypad screen) and it can be finish in around an hour. Oh, and it's free.

Summary:    A point and click adventure game with heart, Anna's Quest does something that I haven't seen the genre do for a while: tug directly on the heartstrings in terms of empathy. While it's gameplay is what you'd want out of the genre and does a great job in really being in that sweet spot of not being too easy, but not being too painfully frustrating, it's the story telling and characters that carry it in a way that hasn't been done in the genre for a while. There's not a lot of humor and the visuals lack compared to some of their prior work in Deponia, but its clear that they wanted to make a heart-warming story more then anything else, and great voice acting and writing makes you want to see what happens next. It's a game that I'll be completing for the story alone, and that's what you need out of a game in this genre. Definitely worth a pick up for narrative focused fans or Point and Click lovers. Really enjoyed the previous two games in this series, but the following points ruin this one:  * Boring combat/reduced gameplay - the removal of several of the features from 3089 (such as slow-mo and melee weapons) means that now you and the enemies are just two entities who see each other and instantly start firing until one falls over. If you're lucky you will be able to position yourself so that your gun is barely poking around an object and essentially use poor clipping to win.  * No open world - instead we have a claustrophobic dungeon where the procedural generation can often leave you disorientated and searching for a tiny air duct in a long series of rooms. The rooms themselves are pretty boring, a far cry from the massive landscapes of previous games.  * Terrible, TERRIBLE, hacking mini-game.  The dev has been impressive with updates in the past, perhaps the same will be true here... but for now avoid this half-game and get 3089 instead.

I have over 600 games in my Steam account, and the vast majority of them are still unplayed. So now I'm dedicating an hour to each of these games and writing a series of One Hour Reviews. An hour isn't very long to judge a game on, so think of this as more of a first impression, and take this review in the spirit in which it's given. Be sure to read other reviews from players who have spent more time in the game.   One Hour Reviews #48 - Disciples III: Renaissance  I'll start with a disclaimer that I've never played any of the other Disciples games, so I didn't have any particular expectations coming into this game. But, having played some turn based fantasy strategy games before, I kind of had an idea what to expect.  I can't say my immediate first impression was that great, as the game opens up with a narrated cutscene that sounded like they just asked someone in the office to record rather than spending the money on an actual voice actor.  The game offered a choice of game modes when I started a new game. For my review, I stuck to the Campaign game mode, and started with the tutorial, which acts as an introductory mission to the overall campaign.   Gameplay seems pretty similar to the other turn based games I've played, with my party moving around on an overland map gathering resources. When running into encounters, the game will then switch to a tactical hex based map where turn based combat takes place. It didn't seem like there were a lot of options in the actual fight. Each of the party members has a basic 'attack' which varies based on the type of character it is. For instance my main character did a melee attack. But other character types can shoot arrows, cast an offensive spell, or heal other party members. Characters can also go into a defensive stance rather than attacking.  Another hallmark of these types of games are the towns that you can upgrade to add other building types, which in turn unlock other types of characters that can be recruited into your party.  There is some customization you can do with your main character. As you progress and get experience, you will end up getting skill points which you can spend to buy skills. You also get attribute points which you can use to up your main attributes. Treasure can be found on the overland map and certain items will be equippable by your main character, which generally serve to upgrade stats.  I got through the tutorial and played about 30 minutes into the first mission. It never seemed difficult to me at all, but I was playing on easy, so that is a reasonable expectation. I'm just not sure if it's supposed to ramp up in difficulty in later missions.  The UI is servicable but felt a little clunky at times. I didn't like the fact that I couldn't hit Escape to bring up the options menu. Instead you have to click the gear icon at the top right. In fact pretty much all of the menus in the game are accessed by clicking a particular icon, but it wasn't always obvious from looking what icon did what. It took some getting used to.  I did like how right clicking on anything of interest would give you a popup with details on whatever you clicked on. At a minimum the popup will tell you what the item is and what it does. I also liked how if the item was 'recharging' (such as a well that you can drink from every 4 turns), it would tell you how many turns left until it could be used again.  Overall I found the game to be a bit simplistic for a strategy game, but reasonably easy to pick up. I do think there are much better turn based strategy games out there that I would recommend instead of this. Still, I had fun in the time I played so I can give this a lukewarm recommendation.

I'm probably an older gamer and I have very much enjoyed previous incarnations of Elite - In particular I spent many hundreds of hours on Frontier over several years, whenever I was bored it was a game I could always go back to and find something new to do or set some new challenge for myself. I was really excited about Elite: Dangerous and it is a game I have really tried to love. Technically it is very impressive with a huge game universe and nice graphics and decent combat. But at this stage I have to say that out of the last decade or so this is the gaming purchase that I most regret.  In practice this has taken the worst elements of the older games (grinding, repetitive generic missions, predictable NPC AI, etc) and combined them with an innovative range of modern annoyances (crackpot developers, troll players, sometimes toxic community, shared online universe even in 'solo' mode, etc). I'm not saying the game is terrible - but overall I found it disappointing, lacking both features as an MMO and content for it's (sort of) single player experience.  Now, however, with the release of Elite Dangerous: Horizons I believe it is time to throw my toys out of the pram. Horizons being a season of DLC for Elite: Dangerous in the format of a new AAA priced game. I can't see how driving around empty planets is going to adress the games more basic flaws, and I don't have much interest in the games other allegedly upcoming features. I won't be purchasing Horizons - but I'll still be sharing a universe with Horizons players with an economy that I imagine will become geared towards players who can loot and craft and so on. I can't even be happily obsolete in peace in the non-existent single player campaign.  Apparently this is part of a ten-year development program. So as far as I can tell it's an early access game for the next ten years and early adopters get the reward of buying the game full price ten times? In the circumstances I am even offended by the 25% 'loyalty discount' I have available for the purchase of Horizons, given that I'd still be paying £30 for a season pass of DLC with much more to follow while I still don't have anything that feels like a complete game for the expensive title I already bought.  In conclusion, big jobs to Elite: Dangerous and then some. This now obsolete version of the game isn't great as a standalone product, and anyone happily signing up to the ten year schedule of payments for a space MMO needs to be far more optimistic than myself that someday a lot of depth will be added to the game rather than just it's vast empty breadth.

NOTE: Comments in this review will focus on the quality of the port. Story, characters, gameplay and music are virtually unaltered (from the GBA version, with the exception of the music which is borrowed from the SNES version). Personally, this is my all time favorite game, and I've 100% each version, so it is with great hesitation that I do recommend this port.  While the most convenient method of obtaining this classic, it is hard to recommend this version of Final Fantasy VI. By far, the best version is the GBA version and this version carries a lot of the positives that the GBA port had, but it is nearly destroyed by poor support and a misguided art direction (the art itself is not bad, but the filtering of the sprite work creates a shinier and overly smooth overlay that makes the sprite work and background contrast with one another).  However, I feel it should be said that many convenience features were added in this version and the game is fully playable and not incredibly buggy. You WILL get what you pay for; I just wish more effort was put into this port. However, I do feel that SqueEnix has learned a great deal from the previous PC ports, as the one major positive I can say about this port is that it has not yet crashed on me.  On a final note: This is NOT the worst port of Final Fantasy VI. Personally, due to the worse translation and load times on the playstation version, this version just barely wins out. Still, if you truly want the ultimate FF6 experience, please find a GBA and the GBA port.  Thank you for reading my review.

A combination of almost-broken controls and unfair game mechanics kills this game's fun.  I thought the game's premise was intriguing, and not at all crappy. I got this game (DRM free) in a Groupees Bundle earlier this year (2015). It's a turn-based game where you compete against the CPU to try to own and equip the most profitable toilets.   It has two major problems. One, your CPU opponent can send the sanitation board after you, and even if you cleaned your toilets 100% in your prior turn, they can cite you for having a terrible mess and divest you of that toilet and all its equipment. Not fun, and doesn't feel fair.   Two, the icons to control the various functions of the game are so, so tiny, it's an exercise in tedium just to click the buttons. And you have to click a lot of buttons to equip a new toilet, so the combination of these two flaws (losing your toilet and all its equipment, and the tedium of equipping a new one) kills this game. Either alone might be tolerable. Together they are infuriating, adding insult to injury.  I guess I played about 90 minutes before giving up on it. It's definitely a turd.  I loaded this up and let it sit (no 'sit' or 'load' jokes, please) in order to accumulate playtime, just so I could warn you all away from this. I see that the existing negative reviews aren't particularly informative. I hope this one is more helpful.  Finally, I just want to say that it is obnoxious for Valve to require playtime (and not just ownership) of a game before reviewing. It should be obvious by now that many people play games before they get released on Steam, and those people have legitimate opinions, too. It's also obnoxious that they have yet to fix their review-posting bug whose only workaround is to post a one-word review and then edit it.

Overall, this is a fine remaster and I'd recommend it to fans of the series.

Proof that FMV works if you have the atmosphere, the setting, and actual actors who don't make you cringe. I'm glad to see FMV making a comeback (mainly from the Brits strangely enough) and this grips you from start to finish. It's not very long but the price is fairly warranted when you consider just how much they probably had to pay for. Using a real cold war bunker for the setting is incredible and the static photos of the long-derelict building adds an incredible atmosphere.  I love exploring in these kinds of games and reading every document, computer memo etc to piece together what went wrong. In Fallout 4 it's one of the best parts. People are dead, and all we know is what they've written down. The only problem with The Bunker is the twist is pretty much given away as soon as it starts. If they make more of these kinds of games (and I hope they do), I'd recommend having less FMV scenes and more exploring to find out what happened. The flashback FMV scenes work great and if you got one every time you found a document that links something, I think the impact would be greater.  Still impressed with this, especially as its gone to consoles too. More of this please :)

This game reminds me of how I felt about Starfox 64's multiplayer. It's an absolute blast to play with your friends for a night or two, but there's not enough to the experience to keep you playing longer than that.   The strongest aspect of the game is the perfect controls. It works brilliantly as a proof of concept that you can do intense space dogfighting with just the mouse and keyboard, executing tight hairpin turns and avoiding bogeys on your tail by closely maneuvering around structures. It's a riot for the first couple of nights.  The problem comes when you start to realize there's not much point to progression. You don't really unlock anything that spices up the gameplay, you just get stronger. Add that to the durability loss you receive for anything you equip, and you're in for a very long grind. You eventually realize that every match will be the same experience.  The devs are in an interesting position. They have a very solid foundation to build a great, addictive deathmatch game upon. The only flaw of the game is its stark lack of complexity and gameplay diversity. It needs more.  This game is like a perfectly seasoned rice dish. It tastes good and fills you up. But after eating the same thing for awhile, you're going to wish it had some meat and vegetables in it.

Wow, how utterly disappointing. Was a huge fan of Heroes 1,2 and to some degree Heroes 3. Somewhere after version 3, this game series lost it's way. This game has pretty bad physics since it's a HL2 mod, but the realism makes it 10x better. If you run, it doesn't show you a bar detailing how much 'Sprint' you have left, You have to figure out how much 'Sprint' you have by how many breathes your character is taking. E.G If he's literally hyperventilating then that means he can't run, but if he's taking small breathes then that means he's perfectly OK. There is no health bar, however instead of finding out how many breathes your character is taking, you have to see how red your screen is.   In NMRiH you have to take on certain tasks such as putting gas into a car, or getting hold of a radio so you can call in for some help. You can play by yourself in this game, but it sucks, so you really need to play with other people to get the full expierence. With a group of friends this game is the most fun it can really be. Other then that the guns are pretty good, however most people would reccomend you take melee. I tried going melee only in NMRiH and it suprisingly worked. All items weigh you down, which is +1 to realism.   7/10: L4D2's little brother.

review_text: I'm so glad to have this game again on my PC, the graphics looks very smooth than the PS3. I love this game so much! I definitley will play it again in the future. I liked this game enough to play it 70+ hrs and get every achievement. Having paid full price on pre-order, I still feel that I got most of my money's worth out of it in enjoyment. That being said, if you get the game on discount you will probably feel better about the purchase afterwards.   The themes of post-apocaliptia, scavenging for limited resources and exploring the ruins of a bygone era all things that appeal to me as a fan of the Mad Max world. The added bonus of vehicular mayhem with a somewhat customizeable car certainly adds alot of potential fun factor.  What this game does right:  - Stylistic atmosphere and graphics (lighting, smoke effects, wind and dust, explosions... good stuff)  - Crazy car vs. car combat with limited adherence to real world physics makes for a fun arcade feel  - Varied, but not overly difficult ground combat, with multiple ways (with skill upgrades) to eliminate enemies in gloriously brutal fashion  - Colorful and interesting NPC character models  - Varied enemy types and their respective combat techniques and day/night appearance of alternate enemies keeps fights from feeling overly repetitive  - Making each location to explore unique enough that the numerous 'fetch-quests' give you reason to really explore the open world provided without it growing too stale  - Opportunity to race your car in 'Death Races'  - Mostly good in-game engine cutscenes  - 60fps highly optimized PC Port (with minor menu flubs)  Where this game falls short:  - This game is crazy easy for any mildly experienced gamer  - No pressure for survival as all resources are abundant and/or need is completely nullified through game progress  - Limited story provides no real opportunity to become attached to main character or any NPC  - Apart from achievement, no reason at all to do 'Death Races' since rewards are nil/laughable  - Mixing and matching car upgrades is pointless... best is best, period  - In-game currency of 'scrap' is so abundant and easy to acquire that intermediate character and car upgrades could easily be bypassed for top quality gear too early in gameplay providing no feel of progression  - Limited or no tutorialistic explanation of simple game elements (how to quickly abort a failed race run or progress in the story) leads to avoidable web-searching frustration  - Major bosses feel extremely 'cookie-cutter' in a boring way  - One really stupid and nonsensical cutscene (if you play, you'll see it)  Overall, the game had a fair balance of pros & cons that neither made nor ruined the game for me. Play this game for the atmosphere and the arcade style simplicity. Don't play this game for the story or the survival aspect. This is a casual game with ZERO replayability. Purchase with hefty discount is reccomended.

Don't know if I'd say it's worth $50 for just the game. Sure, I've been having a blast playing the DLC campaigns, short as they are, but at the time of this review the game has yet to meet my 1 hour/dollar mark that I believe is how games should be priced bare minimum.  They might as well call this Arkham City: Shadow of Mordor, as not only are the Assassin's Creed and Arkham game mechanics replicated to plagiarizing detail, but the story is, too. '_______ is just an average ______ until one day his family is murdered before his very eyes which compels him to seek justice on those responsible'. ______'s Wraith friend is a bit more interesting, in that we slowly learn more about him as the game goes on and even get a prequel DLC where he makes a doomed attempt to usurp Sauron.  And, even though it'll probably garner a lot of hate, I don't find the nemesis system that compelling. The individual Captains really only get in the way when I'm either actively hunting them or travelling around. They never inspire the rage that my fellow gamers seem to feel when they kill me, just a weary resignation that I tried and failed. Plus, they never seem to be around during the main missions, the one time where I genuinely WOULD be ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off and would then have a compelling reason to seek them out: because having them out of the way means I can complete the story mission easier. Plus, while the Black Captains look really cool, the later boss fights against them are pretty lame, the last one committing the ultimate sin of becoming a series of Quick-Time events. Lame.  I'll admit it was a lot of fun to brand Uruks and set them loose on their unsuspecting comrades (and thankfully the game saves that card until about halfway through), but there comes a point at which the entire Uruk army's upper echelons are my slaves and I'm left with nothing to do but stop myself from branding the few remaining non-loyal Captains, lest I have no more missions to watch them from. I think a welcome change would be to have the Captains you control occassionally come to blows with one another like they do normally, forcing one to pick a favorite or try to find some non-lethal way of ending the duel. It would've made for a much more interesting experience.  But, despite all the salt I just poured on Middle-Earth: Shadow of Assassin's Creed, it really is a great game, adding something new to the Tolkien world that WB's been milking while also providing great gameplay and at least SOME incentive to seek out the collectibles. It has issues, yes, but if a sequel could be made that corrected what few flaws the game has, we'd probably get a game that would make gamers cream their drawers and put Ubisoft's corporate heads on suicide watch.

This is definitely a FPS that is for those with an acquired taste for this kind of game.  There are a lot of good concepts in the game. A randomy generated world, an interesting survival-esque style of gameplay, and an odd setting in general to keep you on your toes.  However, the gameplay feels sluggish. Also, limited inventory space is something that is seldom fun in any game: but it's even less so when your main objective markers (that are literally scattered around the world) have to be carried back to the center of the world so often due to small inventory space. It's backtracking that artifically increases the length and difficulty of the game.  It's not a bad game. And depending on your tastes, you may end up liking it a lot. But it's certinly not a game that can appeal to the masses.  5/10 'Mediocre'

In the days of WW2 the combined allied forces faced off against a ruthless foe. A force which sent dozen of allied units to the gates of Hades with little effort. Battalions of tanks, squadrons of aircraft, sqauds of infantry and fleets of ships were all reduced to rubble. An enemy created by the gods to leave nothing but chaos, madness and destruction behind. A force which knew no mercy, showed no mercy and expected none in return. What is this force you ask? Game Balance...  On the surface, Tank Operations: European Campaign may seem like a decent turn base RTS game set in WW2. It is not until you actually start playing the game that you find that things are wrong.. very wrong.  Plot is cliche and nothing you haven't seen before. You play as the American forces during World War 2, sent to the European front to defeat the Nazi occupied Europe.  Gameplay is unbalanced. In the campaign, you start of with a few starting units. You can build new units such as infantry, tanks, aircraft which will become useable next turn. As a turn base game, your units have limited 'moves' each turn. You can create new units like infantry, tanks, aircraft etc via buildings or ships. Some units also have special abilities (which need recharging afterwards) such as improving sight or damge. One of the my biggest gripes (as mentioned previously) is balance, which is shown by the fact that your units do pittiful damage, compared to enemy forces. To demonstrate this, in the first mission I had a two fleets of ships. I sent my landing force composed of tanks and infantry into the beach head. I immediately targeted the coastal guns to help defend my navy. My units did abissmal damage to the gun, but I thought it would be ok after I shot at it with my ships. After doing the later and ending my turn, the coastal gun (which somehow managed to survive 3 tank shots, 1 infantry attack and shots from 3 of my battleships) regained most of it's health (WHY!@!) and it along with a handful of tanks and infantry managed to decimate my force. By mid way I had lost most my ground forces, my aircraft badly needed repairs and my navy were being blown up to smitherines. Yet, I still was unable to eliminate a SINGLE coastal gun. This was honestly quite frustrating and made playing a chore rather than being fun.  Sound is decent, if mediocre. Weapons (ie. tank, ships, aircraft fire) sound decent, but nothing special. Music is forgettable. Voice acting is quite poor.  Graphics arent that great. I personally don't mind the artstyle in these kind of games. However, the lack of animations (while moving) is quite cheap.  If you are looking for a fun, challenging and FAIR game, look elsewhere.

Trying to figure out where to start on this review, well lets just say that i would never have found and played this game on my own, it was chosen by a friend on a random cheap game challenge.  The challenge was to play the said chosen game for 10 hours, we all moaned and groaned at the idea of these random games possibly being fun for 10 hours, little did i know i was actually to have a goddam good time. Now to the game, ill drop it in sections to make it easier.   Gameplay:  I was very surprised, im normally not a fan of these kinds of games but the combat was very precise and quick, a easy to do hard to master kind of deal. The RPG side of it i was confronted was oddly simple yet deep enough for me to quickly realise the difference in how i could build a character, it allowed me from the start to know what i wanted to build and how to play with next to no hand holding, i was impressed. Just to squeeze in something i also enjoyed, the boss battles, they actually had mechanics to them that wasnt just walking around and dodging, it added a little more difficulty to the battles and made them more interesting.   Story:  The story was quite intriguing, you play the opposite side of the way these storys would normally go, from the evil perspective and how overwhelming that evil is, i actually had alot of fun playing from this side. The story itself is nothing to special but interesting enough to carry you along the path of becoming stronger and beating bosses.   Sound:  Its sound was not terrible but i admit i kinda got sick of it half way through the game, but it is fitting with the game and i enjoyed it up to that point, its kinda a mix of dark upbeat, very much suits the mood of the game.   Conclusion:  Im very happy with this purchase, even at this price point, it delivered on everything i expected and didnt expect, this is a gem for the price that it is set for, and if your into these kinds of games and looking to kill a little bit of time, definantly dont skip over this.

Spacecom: A Beta Review  Playing this game for at least 8 of my 9 hours, I thoroughly enjoyed it. The idea behind the game is to simply build and conquer, either destroying the enemy or invading them with the three different units in the game. Gameplay can be slow at times, going through the motions if you would, but at times of getting rushed by 3 AI on expert it gets pretty fast (and upsetting.) The artwork for this game is very simplistic. In general, most or all of the art is 2D but since the game is played through an eagle's eye view, moving around gives the game dimension.  While playing, bots/AI were either very easy after the first few times to extremely obnoxious. It took me a while to figure out the strategy of the bots in each difficulty, but after around 3-4 matches you could understand how to beat them fast.  Mulitplay was the trickier bit for me. As of now, I've played 7 matches online, but dropping out for a total of 3 of them. The 4 games that were actually played were fun considering I knew I was against another person. The only recommendation I would ask for multiplayer is some kind of reconnect timer (such as DotA 2's 5 minute until it's safe to leave or StarCraft 2's minute timer) since beta still has issues in servers.  Overall, fun game, pretty simple to grasp, PLENTY of strategies to find out.

This isn't so much a horror game as much as it is a story that leads (rather than forces) the player to ask questions about their own existence, perception, and understanding of what it means to be who we are.   Soma is the greek word for 'body' and that certainly plays a large role in this story as you try to answer the age-old question of Monism vs Dualism. Are we a body with a soul/spirit/consciousness? Or are we completely physical and there is nothing beyond that to who we are?  It's a fun and engaging story, well made and thought-provoking. The puzzles are entertaining and feel relevant rather than minigames. The character development is believable and doesn't seem forced. Absolutely worth every penny.

Dead Effect is one of those 'it is what it is' games. In Dead Effect you get guns, ammo and bullet time. In return you use guns, ammo and bullet time to knock the stuffin' out of zombies in ridiculous, cartoon-y splatters of blood, guts and brains. You will do this over and over again as you collect money to upgrade equipment, find tablets to flesh out the backstory and enjoy some of the cheesiest, most poorly acted voice-overs since the beginning of time. Don't let my smart-alecky sentences chase you away. I had alot of fun playing this and am already at work on a new game plus with the intention of enjoying the other modes shortly after. The game basically is a B movie splatter-fest combining Doom, Dead Space and Resident Evil. It's not nearly as good as any of those games but it doesn't need to be. It does what it does well enough and is plenty entertaining, especially for the low price tag.   The game is deliciously simple and after stomping through Volgarr, Betrayer, Deadlight and several other games that required some thought, strategy and timing, it was fun to turn off my brain entirely for this one. As a hard as nails space marine, you're way too tough to jump, think crouching is for fools and can't ran worth a crap due to your heavy space suit. None of that really matters as you blunder from one point to the next pressing a few buttons (barely disguised as mission objects) while blasting every single zombie in your path with shotguns, assault rifles, magnums, pistols and more. It's not very difficult, although the grenade zombie is a pest but as long as you have cash you can pick up right where you fell in a heap by paying to revive. Dying's not much of a problem anyways because the levels are fairly short and really once you've got a few guns at your disposal, you'll shred everything like paper.   Having a new game plus to carry over your gear and earn more cash to buy those elusive, extra expensive guns was a great idea and the weapons themselves are satisfying to shoot. By no means is this blaster-o-rama without its flaws. The graphics for the zombies, guns and splatter FX are all pretty solid but the levels are mostly a bland set of corridors, walkways and other similar areas. It doesn't detract too much from the score (and as an old school gamer I don't give a hoot about graphics really) but don't expect to get any twisted scenery to match the dire situation your'e fighting your way through. Since zombies are the opponent du jour, it was cool to see some varied types; chainsaw wielders, spitters, grenade tossers, armored zombies, etc., yet none of them really pose much of a challenge. You might die a few times at first while figuring out how to manage their numbers, but again, once you have the tiniest modicum of strategy and beefed up guns, they go down like paper planes in a tornado.   While not a major problem, the controls will sometimes get you in a bit of trouble as they can be touchy and have you staring at the nearest vacant wall instead of the revenants you need unleash hot fury upon. Another slightly irritating aspect is that other than the chainsaw, there is no melee combat. In close quarters you've got the electric arm, shotgun or saw...but that's it. It would have been sweet if you could carry more of your levelled up guns around, or swap out a few of the things they make you carry for other guns. The grenades weren't overly useful at all.   There's really only two bosses in the game (three total if you count the second encounter with the first one), but they are so easy I'd practically bet you 50 bucks that you won't die while fighting them. Aside from very early on you're never in that much trouble, so those looking for a white-knuckle, bullets to bedlam challenge, you're barking up the wrong corpse. This is seriously exactly what you need if you're just looking to shoot thousands of bullets, drop mobs of the undead and really only tax the trigger finger. Nothing more/nothing less... It was honestly alot of fun, if not something you have to have (depending on your tastes). I will certainly play Dead Effect 2 and it'd be a hoot to see this with some multi-player options. I'll make a list of pros and cons for you, and then please excuse me, I have more zombies needin' taught some manners!  Pros:  -Fun arsenal of weapons that are a blast to shoot and upgrade -Satisfying, old school combat  -Enough secrets, modes and extras to encourage additional playthroughs -Mobs of zombies are a hoot to pummel -Easy to pick and up play  Cons -After a few skirmishes there's virtually no challenge -Upgrades are awesome, but also render you darn near invincible  -Putrid boss fights that are far too easy -Controls can be a bit touchy and oversensitive  -Few more levels wouldn't hurt -Other than the chainsaw, there's no melee combat or close quarters hand defense -It would have been nice to be able to carry more than 2 weapons at a time or at least swap out the electric arm, or grenades for another gun  This is a budget game that does things right. Unlike the semi-horrid Land of the Dead, this is a zombie smashing romp that's worth taking. It's far, far, far from perfect but if you know what you're getting and that's what you wanted, you'll have some fun!  Rating: 7/10

To begin, here's my syestem specs:  i7-4710HQ  NVIDIA Gforce GTX 860M 4GB Memory  16GB DDR3 RAM  So, clearly this game has gotten quite a bit of negative press from fans and professional reviewers. I for one, have played for over 30 hours at the time of this review, and I honestly haven't experienced a single one of the reported issues. The city looks beautiful and the game mechanics have been (generally) improved from previous iterations of the series.  This being said, the game is very hardware intensive. It takes a pretty powerful system to be able to rock this thing on 'ultra' graphics. I personally believe, (but cannot confirm) that many of the issues that people are experiencing are for this exact reason. If you are unsure as to whether your machine can run the game without any issues, then it probably can't. I guess Ubi capped the FPS at 30, which caused a sort of ♥♥♥♥ storm amongst the community, but I haven't really noticed or cared. I haven't had any frame rate dips on my computer, and the visuals look great to me, so /shrug. Oh! Also, the game download is a staggering 55GB, so if you have limited hard drive space, keep that in mind.  The story is engaging as ever. It does seem a bit rinse and repeat to an extent, but I've enjoyed it thus far. They said that the player character would be less 'godly' but the combat is very much the same from the previous games, and once the best gear is unlocked, it is quite difficult to die. Speaking of, I enjoyed the ability to customize your character. It adds a bit of personality to how you prefer to play the game.  The ability to free-run down a building is a welcome addition. The NPC's feel much more alive, and they are frequently seen doing any number of activities as you're walking through the streets. The buildings are well designed, and you can travel through many of them, so there are naturally more ways to get from point A to point B. The map is very, very large, and as far as mechanics go, I would say that this is the best AC game to date.   I haven't played any co-op missions with anyone yet, but I have solo'd a few in private and they seem to be pretty good.  All in all, is it a must have in your collection? No. Is it the best AC game that I've played? No, comprehensive consideration causes me to give that award to Black Flag. Is the game woth picking up if you are a major fan and you have a rig that can run it? In my honest opinion, absolutely! It's been quite a bit of fun and I'm glad that I purchased it.

This is a great game. At first you may be a bit restrictive in downloading this game as it is free. HOWEVER I can tell you from my experiences of this game that it is a must have. Yes there can be bugs/issues at certain points but this game really shines throughout, graphics can be VERY GOOD (if you have a decent PC) and there is so much you can do in the game there really isn't any argument you shouldn't download this game. Yes, there is a P2W element in it - but it is small - from my experiences within the game this element isn't a big issue, don't like a gun? Pick up another! All modes are very well done and can be really fun at times with examples such as the 'BEAST' gamemode in Versus and 'Survival 2.0' in CO-OP being my favourite gamemodes so far. I will probably have other favourite gamemodes later on as even though I have put over 4 hours into this game, I haven't experienced half of what it has to offer.  It does have issues however that MUST be addressed. For one, the bugs in this game can at times ruin the gameplay, if your playing a mission in which you must kill all enemies and one enemy glitches out/gets stuck, you can't continue unless you quit out and start a new game. Another thing that can be game ruining is the server problems. Sometimes it takes a while for the game to start and sometimes the game only starts when it feels like starting, sometimes the connection is unbearable in getting 1 bar connection in game which glitches about frantically due to the bad connection HOWEVER the connection is actually really good most of the time and runs really smoothly except when the server problems start and really worsens the connection.  Overall this is a solid, well done, fun and addicting game with lots to do. Deffinetely worth downloading!

In my restless dreams; I see that place.  QbQbQb What kind of title is that.   This game makes me happy in the way that Loco Roco(or as I will now call it, LR) for the PSP did. Happy, innocent, colorful mANIC PUZZLING ON A 2D SPHERICAL PLANE.  Its like...ok. Tetris and Snood had a kid. This kid was given a very weird name and is the king of bouncy, happy music.  Here, you just match blocks. In sets of 3. This set of 3 can come in any order you like. Stacked, or in other stacks, there's naturally a limit as to how big your stack can get before you lose a life, but its forgiving in that way cuz the loss of life kills that entire stack.  As you gain points, Different added effects also very gradually come into play Just rarely enough that you can't count on them, and just commonly enough, that they're a lovely sight to see indeed.   The music starts out very bare-bones and as you play, the music picks up, fireworks eminate off your planet, butterflies go every which way, and the very planet itself pulses to the beat.  And for the price, you cannot beat this experience. You could try, but in all likelyhood it would only add to the whimsical soundtrack.  This is one of my few 'feel good' games.   10/10 extremely well done.

Ok first things first, I LOVE THIS GAME BUT IT HAS FLAWS! This games is not for everyone, but it is for anyone (Note the difference). Anyone who is thinking of buying this game probably should. What you see is what you get, and in this case that is the best space-pilot sim ever created. This game tries so friggin hard to make everything as realistic as possible, and because of that there are some things in the game that are amazingly fun to do (Like flying around and blowing up other spaceships, trading slaves and making dat money, smuggling, exploring the entire friggin galaxy and so on) and some that become extremly tedious and boring after a while like flying between different solar systems, doing repetetive missions, grinding reputation for different factions and so on). This game really puts emphasis on the SIMULATION part of the genre, and that is both a good and bad thing. Elite is extremley gorgeous and has amazing graphics and sound-design through and through. There is an unparallelled level of polish in this game, which makes it an absolute joy to fly through the HUGE galaxy that you are placed in. TLDR: Its a very realistic simulation, very fun, has a lot of grinding, can get very repetitive.

This is another worthy CRPG from Obsidian in a long line of meaty games. If you are not looking for a little difficulty and a lot (seriously) of reading in your games, then steer clear Tyranny is not for you. If you remember Baldur's Gate & Icewind Dale with quite a bit of nostalgia, and enjoyed Pillars of Eternity more recently. Then play this game. The visual style is quite different than Pillars in subtle ways and the voice acting is solid, sometimes great, sometimes not. Writing was very good and the characters are not the standard black and white that is too often seen. There is a very interesting dynamic happening in how you play and the decisions you make as a representative of the distant 'evil' overlord.  Combat is not especially challenging at the normal level, but does require some thinking and management. However the addition of combination attacks between party members was fun and fairly powerful, and brought something that CRPG combat could use more of...drama. Visuals on those attacks were impressive and spiced up the combat mechanics. Class design, spell creation, the intro sequence, the ability trees, character relationships, and Bethesda-esque skill leveling were all great things in Tyranny.   I do have some small criticisms to add, though they do not stop me from wholly recommending Tyranny. Some systems felt a little light in that more could have been done to flesh them out to fit the story, but I'd rather not spoil too much on what was light. The load times was longer than I'd like, but I had the same issue with Pillars.  Responding to some other reviews/comments regarding this being a short game I strongly disagree. I read nearly everything as some claimed to do and put nearly 40 hours into a Tyranny playthrough - even missing a couple of things by mistake. Granted I personally like to get every detail and explore the lore. It might not be the depth of Pillars, but it is not far off. I will likely be replaying with a different class to get a different general party makeup to get a different ending as well...at some point in the future.  Tyranny is a very good CRPG with a very cool world, exciting combat (for a CRPG), a solid story, and hopefully more Tyranny is coming...ending is not technically a cliffhanger, but I think the writers have some ideas if more Tyranny happens. I sure hope more is coming.

Admittedly, I started playing this game as a joke. Had a bit of money, was bored out of my mind. Wanted to do something that was a change to my normal pace. Came across this game by accident and thought it would be amusing. Upon first impression, it looked like some typical anime love story. What could possibly entertain me more than that?  The first part of the game is a warmup. The decisions you make will determine who you end up 'with' in the second half of the game. Once that's said and done, you start spending time with the person the game selected for you (no real choice, it's whoever you paid the most attention to in the game). It's adorable, it's funny, it's awkward. A bunch of teenagers just going through high school. I graduated high school two years ago, so I easily relate to the situations.  But low and behold, this game had more in mind for me than anticipated. As the second half of the game progressed, it became more fundamentally obvious that this game was about to take a dark turn. Though with the girl I 'picked' (I basically just went with the flow and ended up with Megumi), I had no idea it was gonna turn in the direction it did. Sure there are 'happy, good' endings to this game, but it's dark side, the side that gripped me most, was a helluva lot more than I bargained for. After finishing all scenarios (Shiro's getting to me the most cuz all endings for her are depressing regardless), I will have to take a couple of weeks/months before I have the energy or will to do this again.  This game was riveting. From beginning to end I found myself wanting more. The sheer anticipation of learning about these characters kept me playing. I don't obsess over stuff like this at all, but it's even more rare for me to get emotionally attached to something. This was no exception. I legitimately felt like I was in the main character's shoes from start to finish. I felt everything he felt. More importantly, I experienced intense drama that I haven't seen in a long time. This is a novel. If you want a more interactive game, I do not recommend it. But if you are like me and look for plot, development, and/or emotions, this game I would recommend first for years to come.  Thank you developers for the experience. I hope to be able to delve into your next creation with as much excitement as I leave this game with. I give this game a 9/10.

Even now in 2014 I enjoy this game just as much as I did the first time I played it on the original xbox. Quriky characters, over the top storyline that edges up to the entertaining side of ridiculous, and classic gameplay that has fallen out of vogue in recent years because too many games tried to cram into that genre. Every few years I dust this game off and give it another run through while wishing the sequel will finally get made. Well, at least an HD remake of Grim Fandango is coming.  I'd like to address a few of the complaints I keep seeing though in case anyone is contemplating this game:  -It's unstable- I'm sorry about your computer and your inability to use a search engine, but that's hardly the game's fault. Keep in mind, this is an older game so it is recommended to play it in compatibility mode just like every other older game. I had exactly 1 crash, when a cutscene loaded, and looked up how to fix it on google instead of ranting about it being worthless. Runs just fine.  -The controls are bad- This comes most often from people trying to play using a keyboard and mouse. The game was developed for the original xbox, what do you expect? If you have any experience with this kind of game, I can't understand why you'd even want to play it with anything other than a gamepad. Although, if you are using an xbox 360 controller to play, I recommend using the PsychoPad mod to change the on screen key icons back into gamepad buttons. This was ported a while back, so the whole thing of detecting which control scheme you were using and adjusting icons accordingly hadn't caught on yet. It's not required, but it makes the experience a little more seamless.  -The story is childish- The characters are children, at a summer camp. What did you expect? Shakespeare? Of course the children are going to have a childish understanding of events and react to them in a childish manner. They're children!  -The circus is impossible- Not really. Difficult? Yes. Quite a sharp difficulty curve from the rest of the game? Yes. Impossible? No.  -It's ugly- Not a fan of art, eh? Sorry, not every game is Generic Greyish Brown Shooter With Lens Flare 7.  -Random camera complaints that differ between people- Well the fact that no one can agree on what these camera issues are makes me think this is a highly personal thing rather than an issue with the game. In later parts there are some weird camera things done for effect, such as seeing yourself from the point of view of the thing attacking you, and that can be a little disorienting for a few moments but that's not even remotely what people claim. It's a bit like someone who hates turkey ranting about the quality of a traditional thanksgiving dinner. Of course they're not going to like it, even if it's the best turkey you've ever eaten. The only other thing I can think of is maybe this is a quirk of trying to use a keyboard/mouse to play. This was a game designed for the xbox, so just use a gamepad and you'll be fine.

Have you ever wanted to play a game in which you can be a cackling, all-out, theatrical villain? Or perhaps the more tech/gadget-focused, stone-cold killer villain? And will you save the world, destroy it or do something ridiculously over-the-top with it?    The Good

Our favorite Undisputed God of the Universe, Chou-Chou, returns with her crew for a brand-new story revolving around the zodiac. Taking place immediately after the first game, you take control of the goddess Syrma, who meets Chou-Chou before the end of the first game’s events. Syrma is out to stop an ancient threat to her world that has recently awakened. 9999 levels of maniacal mishaps and ethereal adventures await Syrma, her crew, and hordes of Shampurus!  Starting the game is something of a chore. It follows on directly from Mugen Souls, and the ruler of the universe, Lady Chou-Chou, is tired of the little piece of space that she spent her last adventure obtaining. She decides to take over twelve more planets, but her plan quickly unravels when she’s accidentally transformed into a powerless chibi version of herself. Yes, this is the actual plot. More surprising, it takes up to two hours to get to the basic gameplay. This Metal Gear-busting figure includes countless cutscenes, a few tutorials, and a music video, but it’s by no means the end of your learning experience. You’ll keep being introduced to new ideas for a long time.  This is funny, because the mechanics themselves are actually fairly simple to grasp. The battle system is standard, although still enjoyable, and most of it is controlled via self-explanatory menus. The vast tutorials are presented almost as a parody of other JRPGs, but as is so often with titles of this type, the developer falls into the trap of doing exactly what it's satirising. “Look at us,” the company screams, “forcing you to read this 15 page how-to on changing underwear – isn’t that so JRPG?” Yes, changing underwear. Don't look my Steamìs hours, I played this game so much on Xbox 360.  Videogames definitely aren't just for kids any more. If that wasn't obvious after the barrage of 18-rated releases over the last few years, Condemned takes a gas pipe and beats you around the head until it's all too painfully obvious. Imagine the crazed villagers from Resident Evil 4 but more demented, visceral melee combat with the most brutal weapons ever seen in a videogame, dark and gloomy environments that could have been ripped from the most grim of horror movies, a heavy dose of the surreal, and then experience it all from an immersive first-person perspective. Condemned has already freaked out Xbox 360 owners, but PC gamers aren't safe any longer.  As agent Ethan Thomas you're called to investigate a strange murder scene where the victim has been set up with a mannequin to create a dinner scene. After collecting evidence, noises are heard in the apartment above you, and a short fight later two cops have been shot dead with your gun, the killer has escaped and you're the prime suspect. Back at your apartment you start to see things and a mysterious man appears with stories about your special powers. With the cops on your back and the mysterious Vanhorn advising you to flee through your window, you set out to find the killer and clear your name.  The game then uses the premise of brutally beating up violent mind-altered tramps, drug addicts and other members of society that the world has left behind, and sticks with it for its duration. You don't get much explanation for this, other than information on a disease that's killing birds and seems to be infecting humans too (albeit only those living on the streets). The actual core storyline revolving around the murders moves along nicely as you fight enraged tramps through a tube station, a shop, a school and more. It's all pretty grim stuff and over the ten or so hours it'll take you to get through it, you'll predominantly be smashing enemies in the face with blunt weapons and occasionally getting hold of a gun of some sort.  It's certainly not going to win any awards for its depth of gameplay, but when this basic concept is so entertaining, it's easy to see why Monolith (the studio behind last year's F.E.A.R.) didn't try to add any more complexity. One button swings your weapon or fires your gun, another raises your weapon to block an enemy attack, and you have access to a taser (momentarily halting an enemy) that comes in very handy, particularly against enemies carrying guns. If you're brave enough you can kick enemies, knocking them to the ground and enabling you to brutally kill them with one of four finishing moves. These aren't essential to your progression, but they do add some variety to proceedings.  The only diversion from the relentless beating is the odd bit of crime scene investigation. This is handled very well, but it's more or less given to you on plate, with the game prompting you whenever you can whip out some high-tech gadgets. Responding to the on-screen cue will make Ethan take out the tool he needs and then it's simply a matter of looking for something that shows up under the light, following the smell of rotting flesh using your detector, taking a sample or something similar. Your findings will be instantly sent over to Rosa, your friend in forensics, and she'll give you her findings with calls to your cell phone, giving you little titbits of info on the killer you're tracking. Given that the focus of Condemned is clearly the frenzied combat, the simple nature of the actual detective work is understandable, but some more depth would have added an extra dimension to the gameplay.  There's not much light in Condemned, but the little there is, is used incredibly well. In trademark survival horror fashion every environment is dimly lit, with each object casting a menacing shadow. When you do see some brightness it's often blinding, offering little comfort over your previous adventuring in the dark. Fabulous lighting aside, Condemned does all it can to unsettle you: the psychotic enemies all look a little worse for wear and move with striking realism, blood and human remains litter the environment, everything looks trashed and a creepy black and white picture effect crops up from time to time, if only to mess with your head some more.  You could dissect the game's presentation and pick out some frame rate problems and the odd dodgy texture, but when you're playing you really don't care. When a tramp smashes through a wall and charges at you holding a nail ridden plank of wood, the last thing on your mind is how next-gen it all looks. It's all made more unnerving by some truly deranged sound effects and horrible, meaty sounding combat. Round this off with some impressive voice acting from the main characters, and you've got one impressive package.  As with Monolith's previous PC game F.E.A.R., you're going to need a high-end PC to play this as it was intended. A steady frame rate can be achieved by cutting back some of the more advanced features, but you end up losing some of the atmosphere that makes the game so great to begin with. Condemned moves at a slower pace than a traditional FPS, so you can get away with a less than spectacular frame rate. Be aware that the game forces you to play in widescreen, and unless you're using a widescreen display, the game will feature black bars at the top and bottom of the screen.  Condemned delivers an experience that's worlds apart from what is usually on offer for PC gamers. At its core it's about as simple as a game could be, but it's presented in such a deranged and immersive way that it's hard not to get caught up in it all. Smacking someone around the face with a spade and watching blood spray from their mouth is a sight that will give many people the wrong view of videogames in general, but if you're old enough to play it and can stomach it, there's plenty of perverted pleasure to be had from Condemned.

This game is simply amazing, easily onme of the best games I have played in my entire life!  Why is the game so good? - It has been developed by an amazing team of developers with lots of experience. The love they have put into the game can be seen in the amazing level of detail and the soul of the game itself. - The atention to detail is simply amazing. From screws and bolts to the created Guest language called Planko, this game is full of small details that make the experience incredible. - The sound of the game is amazing too. All ghame was recorded on a live theme park and individual pieces on a ride produce each its own sound that together make the attraction come alive. Also the sountrack is incredible relaxing and perfect for creativity. - The graphics are outstanding! From beautiful sunsets to detailed building textures, this game is simply beautiful. - The game has a soul! This is possible thanks to the original content created by the developers. For instance, they created original brands like Chief Beef that make the world more believable. Also the mascots are full of detail! - The freedom for creating stuff is the best thing this game has to offer. There are no limits to your imagination thanks to the building and the terrain tools; hese are the best I have ever seen!!!  The only things that can improve are some optimizations, better management and improved simulation. For instance, Guest complain all the time that they won't do a small queue and you cannot decide what kind of items to sell (e.g. theme of hats, sizes of burgers, etc.). I am sure these thing will get fixed soon!  I definetively recommend this game and thank you Frontier for all your hard work!

Decent corrider shooter for the new generation of games. Very reminscent of recent ID and Bethesda offerings, like Rage and Dishonored. Solid game, about 20 hours playing it on Kill Me Plenty difficulty. Story driven, with minor branching story path, but overall you're playing it for the chance to blast techno-Nazi's using space-Jew technology. Story is silly, characters are cool, action is oddly paced, offering you 'stealth options' to get through most areas. Game could have used a few more 'shoot your way through everything' experiences like it did in the last few levels. Wolfenstein 3D fans will feel at home, though I'm not sure many fans who were around to play W3D will be as drawn to it as a new generation of gamer will. One who won't appreciate the nostalgia. In that case, they'll be amused just to duel-wield laser AR Marksman rifles and blast Nazi's to bits. Probably shouldn't play this game if you're German.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops II
review_text: Over all I had some fun with that game but the lag on lag in mp is just to cruel. When you fire a whole magazin into an enemy only getting hitmarkers while he kills you with 2-3 shots it feels just bad. The spawn system feels just off. You get spawn killed half of the time on maps like nuketown especially. Some people may do enjoy the fact that its more spray and pray and you can even hit while hip firing a sniper rifle but I don´t. And the word 'Team' in Tdm was never as wrong as it is in Bo2. It is pretty fun while playing with bots when you don´t have that ping and fps problems though. They put much affort into the sp with a story that takes you back to old times but also lets you play in the near future. The fact that you can also have a loadout like you have in mp is very nice.  Pros:  -Nice Single player  -Great costum system for emblems and Co -nice weapon skins -variety of mp maps  Cons: -Very Bad Spawn Points  -Spawn Camping in nearly every map -Very unbalanced Dmg system and Lag on Lag competition -Steering feels very unprecise  -Long waiting times to find a lobby (mw3 takes half the time to find a lobby-didn´t find   a button to deactivate the dlcs to speed up the search time like in mw3) -Boring reward system for Camos which requires head shots most of the time instead of  completing different challenges)

Dishonored is an excellent game. I really didn't want it to end. So much so that I bought the sequel 'The Brigmore Witches'. The game begins with a cut scene showing who you are and what is expected then you find yourself in prison. There is a mild tutorial that will tell you how to carry out actions and then you'll have to get on with it. A bit of trial and error will be needed at the beginning but after you've mastered the techniques the game roars into life. You'll need judgement and planning however you decide to play. Kill everything or leave some alive? Maybe you'll et them all live (with a few bumps and bruises of course. You don't want them to have as much fun as you. ☺)? One thing you'll need to know is that how you play the game will change the ending.   There are minor elements of RPG, deciding what skills and weapons you want to use but the gameplay is entirely up to you. There are ways to complete each mission whatever path you choose. At the end of each mission it gives you a rundown on how you fared and gives you the opportunity to restart the mission if you feel you could have done better. This isn't a run down the road and blast every thing that moves type of game although you can if you feel the need. You don't need pin-point reflexes or a fast trigger finger. It can be as mild and mellow as you feel like making it.  I got a great sense of achievement from even the most basic of tasks. You want to get to the top of that building but there are two guards below, two sentries on the other roof and who knows what else is hidden. You make a plan and it's a great feeling when you get to the roof ... and you haven't even completed the mission. I set a goal of working through the entire game without killing anyone at all. I had to replay a few missions because I got a bit careless but if you accidentally kill someone you can go on a killing spree, finish the mission and then restart it again. Some missions I did two or three times to see if I could do them differently. Explore everything and you'll have a blast.  When I think of the fun and sense of achievement I get from this game I really am absolutely amazed that it's selling for such a low cost. I think most people who like this type of game will love this. For less than a tenner you really can't find a better deal.

Galactic Civilizations 3 feels like its in beta, because it haves so many game changing glitches. They introduced some new mechanics, removed others and held others from Galactic Civilizations 2.   Here is some problems I found that makes the gameplay loses its immersion: - A.I. in war with each other just stand next to each other making some attacks 50 by 50 turns. I had Drengin and Marauders fighting each other in my territory for over 100 turns and each one of them had 4 fleets staring at each other but they just don't attacked each other. - Alliances doesn't matter anymore. That's right! One of the funniest things in GalCiv 2 doesn't work anymore. If you are allied with someone and other faction declares war with you, too bad, your allies don't care at all. In GalCiv 2 when someone declares war at one of your allies you have only 2 options: Declare war on the attacker or break alliance. - Battle view is broken, if you choose to watch a battle of your fleet, you watch it with 1 result then it justs do a quick battle after and with a completely different result and that counts, the previous one don't. - The A.I. declares a war or ends a war randomly. The events dont have any connection at all when its between A.I and A.I. - The United Planets... God its annoying. They propose the same thing over and over and over again. With 20 united planets meetings in my playthrough 11 of them was to propose the same festival (10% morale bonus all races), 6 to elect the chair race (and my race was not mentioned ever), 1 to end all wars, and 2 to other random events.    Here is some new mechanics in GalCiv 3: - You now have a territory, not just that old influence territory, now other races haves to negotiate an Open Borders treat with you to legally stay in your territory. And if you refuse? They just stay anyway and you receive a spam message in your notifications inbox EVERY SINGLE TURN, and you don't have a way to disable it. And if you enter another race territory without permission they starts to dislike you. - Your planets now have a 'standby' mode. You can choose to increase economic, research or influence by 5%. If you don't know this the game just don't let you pass the turn, you have to google it to learn and continue your playthrough. - Now when you have an Idle colony, ship, shipyard or just finished a research, your 'pass turn' button turno into idle colony, ship etc. The weird part is that you have to set the ship to explore, survey or guard to ignore it.  Here is some improvements from GalCiv 2: - Graphics. - Sounds. - The grid(map). It's the most beautiful grid I've ever seen in a turn based game, and you can rotate, zoom, move however you like. Awesome! - Instead of building ships in a planet now you have to build shipyards that stands in space. You have the option to unanchor it and move it 1 square each turn but the farthest from the sponsors planet the slowest you build. And you can have more than one planet sponsoring each shipyard, which is very nice. - Custom races: You can now choose more details of your custom race, it helps when you decide to play against them. You can even change the images of the race. - The ideology tree and ideology points are much better now. There are powerful ideology skills to choose from.  I don't recommend the game. I bought it with 50% discount and I regret it. Just buy Galactic Civilizations 2 Complete instead, you will have much more fun. Maybe I will play this game next year to see if some glitches have been patched.  Edit: Added my opinion on ideology.
