After getting this game on a recent Humble Bundle, I just finished the story, and gee, do I really want to get my hands on episode 2 now!  This is a visual novel with no choices at all (it's pretty much an eBook with pictures, music and save/load) about a runaway teenager who decides to tackle the stock market in a future Moon colony. Now that's an unique setting :-).  As expected, he has to mature by leaps and bounds, especially when he runs against people with very different views and backgrounds than his. (Yes, he is a bit of an insufferable prick at first, but hey, he was a teenager boy with issues!)  The story was quite well written, and makes up for the strange occasional hiccups in the adaptation of the original version to the Ren'Py engine and the odd sequence with no matching background (tsk, tsk). You also get to learn a bit about some of the complexities of the stock market, where it is entirely possible to buy things with money you don't have, and sell things you don't have as well!  I'd consider this game as the first episode of Umineko: it starts slow, but it ends on a high note and it really makes you want to start on ep2. Keeping my eyes peeled on its English release!

Game of the year here gentlemen. From refined systems, to unstacked cities to beautiful animations, world map and my personal favorite the OST.  Civ. 6 builds up on its predecessors and takes the best parts of them and further polishing them.   Gameplay: My favourite change for the gameplay is the specialized districts which adds another layer of strategy as well as making it easier to manage your cities once your civilization gets huge. Next is the tech tree and Eureka moments which help you plan out which way you want to progress in terms of tech. THe new Great Person points and overview is a nice touch and can help you pick best Person which fit your playstyle.  Stacking units is back but only to a limit of 3 and thats a good thing unless u were a fan of civ. 4 deck wars.  My only dissapointment is lack of Civs. I wish there were more civs but i understand that 2K and Firaxis focus on getting the systems right and game working. I hope more civs will be added in future *looking at you Poland and winged Hussars* -Lack of scenerios  Graphics: First reveal, many people had complaints the graphics were too cartoon-ish. Personally I find it quite asthetically pleasing and units/cities show minor differences depending on civs you are playing for example Greek Swordsman have little Spartan helmets which other civs dont. Animations for combat are beautiful such as the last kill would be a unique animation for example plaing as legion the last unit that gets killed has an animation of legion knocking them to the ground with a shield then proceed to jump over them while stabbing them. The way fog of war works is superb and you can even tell which tiles in your city are being worked on as well as buildings/wonders/districts being constructed turn by turn.  Sound. Sean Bean in my opinion is a good replacement for Leonard Nimroy to be the narrator. Firaxis game even got him to record a line for which his meme is famous for (that made me chuckle when I first heard it). Christopher Tin is back and has composed an amazing opening theme that rivals to Baba Yetu. Each civilization has its own theme that changes as game progresses through. For example if you are playing Russian Civ. or meet them you can hear snippets of Kalinka which is a well known Russian song and so forth.  Final Thoughts. The game is on good track I am sure people who have put in over 20 hours or so can point out finer things that I didn't pick up yet. Let's hope the devs keep a pusle on the game and interact with community and hopefully improve the game as well as giving us more content to fiddle with

I don't even think this deserves to be on Steam.  First, it's not even F2P. It's a bit like Club Penguin, in CP you pay to get upgrades and items to wear and different coloured pets and stuff like that. Personally don't see too much of a problem as long as children aren't stealing credit cards to pay, but in CP, you can play for however long as you like for free with the only limitations being you can't wear certain items/obtain them. Plus both Amazing World and Club Penguin are aimed at children. After Level 10 of Amazing World, you can't earn xp or do much because most of the quests require Level 13 or above. You CANNOT level up without PAYING A SUBSCRIPTION TO BE A MEMBER. That is NOT F2P.  The missions are just the same things over and over, run to speak to this person, pick up things in that corner of the game, a small platformer type of game then TP back to the quest-giver. It's just that over and over, there's no creativity in the missions.  The description does not mention that it is capped at level 10. It's a misleading pile of bull. It shouldn't be on Steam and deserves to stay on it's own website. I would not recommend parents get this for their children, I wouldn't recommend a child getting it for their parents, I wouldn't recommend it to ANYONE... or $30 for 12 months access to level up, wear clothing and do quests if you really want to repeat gameplay and missions.

Total War: Attila is the latest installment in Creative Assembly's long line of historical Total War games, and it is a true gem. With all new mechanics, a larger campaign map, and a new time period, Attila picks up right where Rome 2 left off. If there was one thing to complain about, it's that Rome 2 should have been this all along, or that Attila really feels more like a very large standalone expansion to Rome 2. Anyway, on to the good!  The game picks up at 395 A.D. Rome has been divided into the Eastern Roman Empire and the Western Roman Empire, respectively, and the Huns have begun their slow migration west. Throughout the entire campaign there is this shadow of the impending apocalypse, with events popping up tying themselves to Attila's birth and adulthood. The mood is fantastic. It really works to set a tone that the people of this time period really thought the world was beginning to end, and it does so incredibly well. New mechanics even tie into this, like settlement destruction. When besieging or being beseiged, buildings can take damage, even catch on fire that will spread throughout the city. The effect is awesome, it makes sieges play so much better, and it looks incredible. Provinces now have fertility ratings that effect how much food is produced, with settlements that have previously been razed offering lower levels of fertility, making it that much more important for you to protect what's your's.  Speaking of which, razing is another new mechanic brought into the fold. Settlements can now be burnt to the ground, either by the controller or by the attacker. This mechanic is great. Horde armies will typically burn your cities to the ground, depriving you of their resources and weakening your burgeoning empire. They can be resettled at exhorbant costs, though they will not be nearly as fertile as they once were. Hordes are yet another new mechanic. Many factions have the ability to just pick up their armies and turn them into mobile cities, allowing for them to move around the campaign map to where they are best suited. This is another well done mechanic, allowing for factions like the Huns to really hit you by surprise when they want to because you can't always accurately know where they are concentrated.  Religious choice is something else that Attila brings to the table. Now your faction has a spot in the UI detailing what percentage of your people are what religion. Should a religion grow enough within your faction, you can actually change it to become your faction's new state religion, with each religion offering their own set of positives and negatives (watch out Christians, you will take serious negative modifiers when facing the Huns). The family tree is also back, and it is surely welcome. Tied in with it are new governmental offices of which you can place members of your family and statesmen into for different bonuses, but you have to watch out for the balance of power within your faction. While complicated, it is much easier to grasp than Rome 2's political system and actually has effects on what's going on, rather than seeming like nothing more than a waste of time.  Speaking of Rome 2, many of that game's mechanics have been refined in Attila. Stances now do as advertised, units actually follow orders that are given to them, and shield walls now actually protect the unit from missile damage taken from the front and left where the shield is held. Units still blob up when attacking, but it isn't as bad as it was in Rome 2. However, units still break formation whenever ordered to attack, requiring the player to do silly things such as tell the unit to walk through the enemy.  The factions also offer a wide variety of diversity not just in their units, but also how they play. Play as the Saxons or the Franks and you're in for your typical Total War experience, start with a settlement and expand. Play as the Visigoths or the Huns and you're now a horde army, settlements mean little to you when each army is it's own contained settlement. Play as either Roman empire and right at the get go almost half of the map is your's, but watch as it falls from a poor economy, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off and starving citizens, and multiple attacks from almost every other faction and you're just trying to survive. Not to mention that the Hunnic hordes are gathering in the east...With their sights set on Rome.  Now I know that I've given this game plenty of praise compared to my Rome 2 review, but that is because this is a well polished jewel of a title...However, there's always bad. The biggest complaint that I have about this game is that it is everything Rome 2 should have been. Had CA put the time and attention to detail into Rome 2 that they did Attila then there would have been no need for this game to come out. And really when playing it it just feels like a really big expansion to Rome 2.  Now, it would be unfair to judge Attila based on what Rome 2 didn't do right, so my biggest complaint is the freaking pay wall that CA put up on half(!) of the factions. Within weeks of this game releasing there were numerous DLC faction packs that came out, prompting one to ask why CA had to release them as paid-for DLC when they were most likely finished along with the main title? Now, I'm not against DLC, I understand that it is a necessary evil in today's gaming world, but to have half of your available factions as DLC is more than a little extreme. At least it's not as bad as Rome 2, which had just as many faction packs as well as additional unit packs for sale.  When all is said and done, Attila stands alone as a fantastic example of what a Total War game could be if given the proper amount of love, devotion, and attention that CA has seen fit to provide it with. Though it is a lesser known time period (the dark ages, afterall) than their other games, mechanically it is by far the best Total War to date. I highly recommend this game to anyone who is even slightly interested in the Total War series, and I believe that if you can get past the dark ages time period, you will fall in love with this game as I have.  8/10

The campaigners, the multiplayers, those-who-are-so-unskilled-they-have-to-fight-an-actual-battle-against-the-AI-on-easy (It's a bit of a mouthful to say that category.) and a category that don't really have a name but enjoy pitting, for instance, 7000 Samurai against 2000 Royal Marines (That example was from FoTS.) I personally am a combination of a multiplayer and that last category. The multiplayer is *%?$ and they seem to have adopted a more of an avatar system which means that the player must work long hours to do anything other than a Yari Ki spam army comp. The modding for campaign seems to be in abundance with all sorts of modifications available from Steam Workshop. But I disliked vanilla Shogun 2! Just didn't suit my needs. But when on a last hope I would get at least some of my money's worth I got FoTS and JESUS that turned the game into the best game I've ever played for that last category-thingy! So, I recommend this game only if you get FoTS (Fall of the Samurai DLC) and don't just play Vanilla.  P.S The More blood DLC is crazy but quite funny to play with and it's relatively cheap, I think!

Eternal Senia is a free ARPG (action rpg, meaning the combat is not turn based) made in RPG maker. The game was simple but quite fun while it lasted. For a game that's 100% free, there's really no reason not to try it.  Pros: +Great soundtrack. Admittedly it's small and loops a lot, but the game is free, so I can't complain. +Gameplay is simple but sucks you in. Nothing too complicated here, this is a relaxation game. (Except for the final boss, which is unexpectedly difficult.) Just touch enemies to auto-attack. There's also a set of special skills that consume a magic meter. +Touching story +Character art is scant but what's there is excellent. It looks much better than most doujinsoft on Steam. Almost professional level. +I got eight hours of gameplay out of it  Cons: -Final boss is MUCH harder than the rest of the bosses; it feels slightly unbalanced with the rest of the game.  This game was better than a lot of games you have to pay for. I think the creator could have easily gotten away with charging $2.99 for it; people would still have loved it.

At the time of writing this I have 130 hours. So time to add my opinion of this game for what its worth.  I originally went into Elite on the viewpoint that despite what the 400+ hour people said it must have kept them at least entertained for 400 hours for that opinion. Nothing really could be farther from the truth anything further than 100 hours and you will find that sitting in the park watching the ducks is far more entertaining.  True the game starts of really promising it gives you a lot of eyecandy (that hud is just beautiful in the extremes), some missions at the base go earn some credits your way approach. And at the start this goes all very nicely. Within a few hours you have probably dotted your way up a few ships. Then the game halts very abruptly.  For once you are in the midgame. The amount of credits needed to go up to the next level of combat ship or trader ship (I will attack explorer in a bit) is so much that you have to pretty much do rinse and repeat for what seems like an eternity to get anywhere else new. There are many people including myself who have done trade routes while watching a movie or something if that is a necessity then the game has failed to be engaging on that premise alone. Combat provides entertainment for a few minutes or so then the game just feels like any $5 arcade shooter space game.   On to exploration I kept this one separate because as soon as you get an Asp (mid-game) or a kitted down Hauler (early game) you are pretty much good to go... absolutely nowhere. This game boasts an exploration system, that does absolutely nothing. The best part of the system I must admit is getting your pilots name forever locked in the universe map... I guess thats cool... perhaps... but lets face it nothing is new in this game at all. By the time you leave occupied territory you have seen most star types and planet types to be seen. The only goal really of exploration is to spend all your time trying to get to the black hole Sagittarius B. Nothing is going to wow you and give you a 'no man has gone before' feeling. You are not going to encounter new lifeforms and civilisations as they simply do not exist, not even humans. Explorers don't need weapons as there are not even pirates past a certain point. You go to a planet hold you scanner button planet scanned move on. It is as dry as it sounds that duck pond is even more inviting.  Even 'exploring' the explored space yields little in interest. All the base types are of identical design moreorless. To the point one of my most amazing discoveries was finding a base that was an orangey colour instead of the usual metal grey. And for all I know that may have just been my GPU getting distracted from the boredom of rendering the same thing over and over again.  Do I regret my purchase no not really. Its got a lot of potential and I know the devs may throw new things out. But Steam asks whether I would recommend it to others like in a purchase sense. No I could not thats why it gets a thumbs down.  The final point really aswell is while Dangerous could go somewhere, it feels very beta like... it has a core and wanted layers but got none before release. I have seen this far to many times with these big projects. Both from indies and big devlopers. Which the Elite devs have already decided on going for this Horizons project at the end of the year. Which in past experiences very rarely does a dev company acheive perfect synergy updating its older products while developing its new ones. While they promise new features for Dangerous I doubt there will be anything that will throw this downvote to an up.   Update: While I am not reviewing Horizons as I do not own it. My lastpoint made in this review about Dangerous going somewhere but probably being moreorless left in the state it is at. Have not played recently but I am getting a lot of comments confirming this is still true, this Horizons release seems to have made Dangerous updates less frequent as stated would happen. Also seems pretty much from what I have heard (but I would read ther reviews on that page as I have no first hand of it) Horizons is a similar hollow shell state as Dangerous was released in. Now that game is early access still so it could be fleshed out but I ain't based on this experience going to hold my breath that this is not another $60 chocolate surprise egg with disappointing chocolate on the outside disappointing toy on the inside.      Due to a lot of quite non productive comments I have gotten recently I will have to add this. I will delete comments based on but not necessarily exclusive to these criteria.  Obvious fanboyism with no mertiable argument - 'You shouldn't have bought a space sim then' REASON: I love space sims I am the freakin fanboy of space sims. The original Elite, Wing Commander, Freelancer, X3 played them all to death still do. I have gone back to my witrhout hyberbole 5000th hour of Freelancer recently. So don't tell me I don't enjoy space sims and should nto have bought one.  The Played too many hours to have a negative opinion - 'You have played 130 hours and wrote a negative review you idiot.'  REASON: I never understand these types of comment. They are either you havn't played enough or you have played too much. Again its general fanboyism I criticised your game and in reality no matter at which point I gave a negative review you would have used this argument. Its not even constructive especially when most people who post this don't give me the reason I should play more or should have realised sooner. In my opinion 100+ hours is a really great time to review a space sim on its merits or lack of. If it was a first person shooter with 10 hours of gameplay yeah maybe 100 hours may be a bit of an odd playtime for a negative review.  Don't get me wrong I ain't just deleting posts that disagree with the review. There are comments here that disagree with me and they stay because while I do not agree with many of them they at least bothered to give a constructive counterpoint. But unless you have a counterpoint. Then thumbs down the review. I ain't dealing with fanboyism that is constructed in the same ettiquette I would expect at a kindergarten hurling insults and the such. We are all here for our love of games and our opinions may differ. I ain't going to fuel flamewars even if you have struck the match.

Comfy med-low pop mmo with cute aesthetics and lots of grind.   The main drive in this game is the class variety and how classes work with each other. There are currently 4 main classes being your typical mmo swordsman/cleric/archer/mage that makes up the first 'Class Rank'. Each 'Class Rank' allows the players to further involve themselves into the same class up to 3 'Circles' or pick from two new classes avaliable per rank. At the moment, 7 Ranks are in the game with an 8th on its way, 15 classes per class tree for a total of 60 classes and most of them have 3 Circles worth of abilities.  Players get permanent xp bonus from the combined levels of the player's characters, including deleted ones, making it easy to reroll and start new characters on a whim. Biggest downside is that character limit is at 3 unless you pay a small cash shop fee that averages at 3$.   PvE is solid and bosses is where your build really shines or fall altho dont expect anything serious before lvl 100.  PvP is broken as of now mages with heavy CC just rekt people up and the melee class is basically useless in that context, outside of a few gimmicky skills. PvP without mages is fair enough but could use some tweak here and there.  The F2P system is supported by the purchase of Cash Shop currency to buy costumes, hair dyes and some quality of life utlity items such as tp scrolls. Tokens can also be bought with bonuses like extra xp from killing monsters, increased dungeon instance avaliability and trading benefits on the market, mostly less tax and more selling slots. These Tokens can also be bought with ingame currency from other players on the market worth a day or two of grind for mid-high level players given said players know where and how to grind efficiently. Token benefits last for a month and once its ran out players can usualy buy another one right away from the market if they saved a bit.  Content is being released somewhat steadily and on the 26th of July there was a considerable update bringing balance to some classes and some new game modes and events.   I would recommend Tree of Savior to anyone looking for a comfy low effort mmo with a lot of class variety and cute animu grills with somewhat fair F2P at the moment.

Welding Simulator 2015  This game has a LOT of potential yet sadly it fails to act on it.  When I loaded this up I assumed I would be building a moonbase or something along those lines.  Instead, I got to run around with a repair tool and take part in small minigames while repairing things.  Essentially, you have to restore oxygen to a base on the moon, yet there really isn't any challenge to it. This game was alright at first, I felt like there would be a challenge, yet the closest thing you have to one is wobbling over to the a power coupler that is about to break. Simply, you start by repairing solar panels and connecting power to the oxygen production system and just repairing that to let you beat the game a little faster.  You start out in a Rover near a toolshed and you are told to grab a welder and go over the panels, which takes about 3 minutes on it's own because you are extremely slow. Repair them, which takes a few minutes even if you complete the minigames that pop up which are a lot like the 'Scary Maze Game' where you have to stay inside some lines.  Then you have to repair the couplers, if one breaks you have to replace it with a new one from the toolshed. Then you have to use a robot to repair the stuff on the life support thing since it is leaking something that I guess can kill you through a spacesuit.   TL;DR: Lots of potential, fails to act on it at all. Basically it is Welding Simulator 2015. If they added more content or let you build your OWN moonbase and repair it when it goes to ♥♥♥♥ then I would recommend this. But until then it just a way to kill a half hour or 2.

Ok, so here goes. Just to clarify, there is a 60FPS OPTION as well as a resolution option that goes up to 1900x1200 (can be set to fullscreen). Everything can be found under the settings option (edit: settings can be found under the 'more' tab in-game or just by pressing the ESC button). The link option works great. I had no problems linking the info from my mobile app to this (big props to the devs).   On a side note, I played this on the mobile app for quite some time, so the time displayed here is of course not relevant.  From a gameplay point of view, I find this game personally more appealing that HS (hearthstone).  Mostly because of the evolution mechanic which allows for comebacks, even if you don't hit the mana curve perfectly.  I just know that someone is going to comment: git gud etc. Well, to that I say 'chacun a son gout'.   If you are still reading (don’t really know why, but thanks) here is a list of a few other pros: - esthetically pleasing (debatable) - sound and music (ok-ish) - the story missions are a nice touch to give the chars a personality (even if a tad corny at times)-there will be new story missions, which means it's going to progress - you can get gold MUCH easier than in HS, which of course makes it viable for someone to not spend a penny and still have fun and be decently ranked - there are in-game achievements as well as missions (nets you quite some gold) - the first expansion is already out - 7 different playable chars/races, which gives a nice diversity of playstyles - evolve mechanic, which honestly makes it stand out of the other CCGs (mentioned already above) - the devs are also have a nice policy of handing out a few card packs every time they hit a milestone (like 5 mill players etc.). I find this really nice of them. - approved by Kripp (HS player and twitch streamer)  The biggest turnoff for people, that I can think of, might just be the anime aesthetic (you'll see a lot of reviews covering waifus etc.). Well, I can't blame you for that but trying it once won't hurt you nor your wallet, since it's free. I hope this helped someone. Thanks for reading and enjoy your day.

Auro is the only turn-based game I still play, and the only recent game where I don't feel awful when i'm in a tough spot and trying to find a solution by experimenting. I also appreciate how Auro uses an 'ELO' system to encourage the player to keep playing and improving, instead of just a high score list. The art style and music are very nostalgic; it feels like i'm playing a game on a sega genesis or super nintendo. All in all, Auro is a very good game that feels worthwhile to play.

At first glance, Quantum Break is a cover-based shooter ported over from some Microsoft-based system that apparently also does games... sort of. At second glance.. oooo, I recognise those very nicely modelled and animated characters... those are some familiar faces...  From there, the distractions keep coming up one after another; This plot is actually interesting and feels like I might be able to affect what happens in it. None of the simple puzzles throughout the game are pushed into your face so hard you can smell the logic like some games, so you can usually feel like you have figured them out mostly on your own. There is limited exploration, which actually keeps the levels nice and tight, focusing the story forward... and that is the main distraction.. the story is rather good.  Split into a gameplay segment, then a live-action mini-series, then back to a game segment, the story is told in two really interesting ways. The player can affect the live action part of the story by making a few select different choices during the gameplay side of things. I won't lie and say this is better than ANY moral choice system/multiple ending plot any game has ever done. IT is not as far as I can tell so far. What it DOES do... is it knows how far to go and then polishes that aspect to fit in really nicely with the rest of the game, rather than get crowbar'd in last minute. The actors do a fantastic job in both sides of the game and I think that dual-media style actually makes this a hard-to-put-down title.  I keep saying there are distractions in this game.. and I say that purely because the combat is almost generic, but with a few unique aspects. Without spoiling much, the character abilities are nicely thought out, well done and fairly original. Everything ties so neatly around this though, that it is a very slight smudge on an otherwise shining title.  I'm trying not to spoil things at all here, so sorry if this lacks detail or consistency, but I would recommend this game for fans of narrative driven stories with action sprinkles.

I'm sure other reviews here will say roughly the same as I'll say. This is a rather poorly made port of DMC3 for the PS2. It's not entirely unplayable as some may lead you to believe, but it's bad enough to make you want to not play the game despite how fun it is. Most console controllers will refuse to work for the game and what DOES work will leave you wanting more, such as not being able to use one of your joysticks. You CAN download a program to fix this and it's really not the main issue with the port because it can be fixed. The real problem comes from the fact that you will have massive, and I mean MASSIVE fps drops in multiple locations resulting in it being neigh unplayable. But in those non-large areas where fps is stable and you fix the controller up, it works rather well for a port.  I wouldn't recommend it because it's far too much work to get everything in working condition. (At this point there might even be a fix for the FPS drops but I didn't care to look for it)  THAT ALL BEING SAID If you don't have a problem with everything I said, feel free to pick up this game.

Wolfenstein: The New Order surprised me. It surprised me with its high production values, interesting storyline (!), wonderfully realized characters, and excellent voice acting. There are enough references to events in 'Return to Castle Wolfestein' to support this game being a 'proper sequel', but it stands alone if you haven't played the previous title. It also gives many nods to the original 'Wolfenstein 3D'. The alternate history 1960's where Nazi Germany won WWII is very interesting, very dark, very sci-fi/high tech. It's over the top, but it holds up. There were many, many really thoughtful design decisions in the look and feel of the environments, the weapons and vehicles, etc. The developers obviously took the time to ask what a Nazi dominated world post-WWII would look like, and the results are excellent...and often disturbing. Again: it holds up. For all that, though, the crowning achievement in W:TNO is the story and the characters. Invest some time in this game. You won't be disappointed.

At first I fricken loved this game, I still love the building and exploration aspect of it.  I don't like the quest immersion side of things, don't get me wrong, I love questing it's something that made me spend hours on WoW. Thing is though.... if you're building or exploring in your single player universe and a friend joins you and decides to start working on the main quest. They gather an artifact or get some rare items or such... They unlock the artifact in your universe not theirs, thus they ennd up having the artifact no longer be obtainable by you, and you possibly lose the chance at getting the reward bag from the quest giver because your friend did the quest already. Sure you can go and battle the boss and area again but no more reward bag for you. Same goes for unlocking some of the personal shops in the outpost, such as the teddy bear for the ore selling bears, someone else makes the bear and hands in the quest, they get the bag while you sit in the corner getting the ore you need.  An example if I may, I made a new character, got them set up and to the outpost. I head to the ark to ttalk to the Esther.... when I get there I notice one of the orbs on the ruins are glowing green. I didn't remember going and unlocking that at all, but I pay it no mind at all; I figured I could just start a new character and the progression would be gone.... boi was i wrong, i get the new character to the outpost to see that nothing had changed, stuff was still unlocked that i didn't wish to unlock myself. I ended up deleting my whole universe folder, losing all progress in order to just be able to work on the main quest line myself, but I've been spending this whole day building a base with a decent farm and trying to make some pixels and get my character's minipulater ranked up. Than they come into the universe again and ended up unlocking another artifact {the other one was no longer there in the wall because i've reset the universe} so I now had both an artifact and a weird vender I didn't unlock both end up being unlocked.  So in all honesty I wouldn't recommend this game to friends at all, because with the current situation, I wouldn't even be wishing to play the game along side others unless they made sure they didn't do the main quest. Now if there was any form of commands set up to reset the old ark this review would end up back to a recommended.

Have you ever dreamed of snooping around someone's house while they are away to learn about the nity gritty problems of their life? Then this is the game for you. Do you have more than a vouyer's interest in your video games? Then look elsewhere. This may be your family's home, but you are little more than an intruder rifling through their stuff. If you are into that sort of snooping, there is a bit of a family drama going on, but...  ****SPOILER**** *** ** * All your searching accomplishes nothing. I feel like the only reason there is a game here is because your sister is just needlessly cryptic in her letter she leaves on your front door. * ** *** ****END SPOILER****  Truth be told, I thought this was going to be a detective/escape the room style game. I guess I just read the description wrong. I mean there is some 'searching' for a couple of combinations and keys, but this is fairly trivial and almost handed to you. And detective work? See my spoiler. Ultimatley, I would have prefered to read the story as a bug than slog through it as a game. State of Decay is a fairly unique case. On one hand, it is somewhat unstable and rather buggy, while on the other it is a fantastic open world zombie survival game! Although it tends to recycle voiceovers or character models, there is sitll something rather fun about these particular survivors as you level up their skills and grow personally attached to them. Very much like in XCOM: Enemy Unknown! The dark zombie infested world that these survivors live in is rendered fairly lovely, despite a few lackluster low res textures on the characters, and the ridiculous amount of bright color filters will put strain on the eyes of some players. As for the sound and music, it has a few standout tracks especially during combat with hordes of zombies! The gorey sound effects are also quite satisfying and fun for an appropiately gorey game.  State of Decay is good, and made even better by the DLC it has. The Breakdown DLC is by far the most hardcore and difficult zombie survival game mode of all time, and you WILL get wrecked if you aren't staying on your toes scavenging for resources or killing rampant zombie hordes! It, in particular, is an 'endless mode' version of the normal campaign. I played Lifeline pretty extensively, and it has a military perspective with an entirely new map! It's pretty good, but it's very difficult and quite rough with wave based zombie sieges that occur between helicopter extractions (you extract high value people and rescued civilians).  That's the thing about State of Decay. It feels very much like an early access game with the numerous bugs and stability issues, but yet I keep coming back for more! I'd say that that this game is the Grand Theft Auto 5 of zombie games, and you will hardly run out of things to do in this robust open world game! Just make sure you bring your patience, because your allies and other survivors are constantly asking for your help. Grab that crowbar, reload that pistol, and get your zombie survival on!

The world in The Vanishing of Ethan Carter first appears as a beautiful yet sleepy backwoods town, but it quickly becomes apparent that not all is well. A more desolate, abandoned feel grows on Red Creek Valley, like it's been neglected by the outside world for decades, its prime time having long passed. This eerie view of our mundane world is also visible through the documents and newspaper clippings found throughout the valley, portraying an outside view of life in this rural neck of the woods. These external reports are starkly contrasted by Ethan Carter's own imagination, channeling the same goings-on into those of the supernatural, seen in stories scattered across the valley.  The Vanishing of Ethan Carter shows an interesting and deep outlook on life—the mundane, sleepy world, against the fantastic imagination of a child. This powerful story combines with a beautiful, polished world together with an immersive music score and great voice acting yielding a top-notch game. But it is what you make of it, as you can beat it in a couple hours if you run from start to finish and skip most of the mysteries, but you will miss out on its deep story. On the other hand, if you explore the whole world and look for all its secrets, puzzles, and mysteries, the game will really shine its brightest.  Another unique and enjoyable part of The Vanishing of Ethan Carter is its detective gameplay that reveals pieces of the story with each mystery solved. The Vanishing of Ethan Carter is as close as you can get to making a detective novel into a game, and the result is something excellent. The game's succinct yet powerful, thought-provoking dialogue and story tied with its eerie, dark, supernatural premise makes it well worth the time of anyone that enjoys well-crafted mysteries and games with deeper meanings that will stay with players long after finishing.

After playing this game several hours, I can say for a discounted price it's quite worth it. Don't expect too much from it though. The centre of the gameplay is crafting. You find tons of parts to craft your spells. You first start off with just 1 spell-'element' and gain another with each level you finish. By using them you can level them up and choose between 3 things to upgrade which are damage, critical chance and critical effect. The whole crafting thing is pretty much too complex to explain in detail here. With wrong crafted spells, bosses can become hard in a dark souls kind of way. With well crafted spells you might be able to kill them in under 2 minutes. The fun really lies in discovering what spells you can create for your special needs. Basically there are 3 type of spells you can cast. One of which is a offensive ball or ray. there are different types of those too. The other one is an AOE which can have many different effects and a shield (which I barely use ever so it's pretty useless to me). The story doesn't really get rolling and isn't that exciting. The levels - although not open world but very linear - are beautifully crafted thanks to the Cryengine 3. Enemies spawn every few meters and you gotta defeat them. New enemies are introduced only about every level and there is much re-use in enemies.  For less than 10€ I'd still recommend it.

While this game may follow the classic, linear 'Call of Duty'-esc shooter formula where most of the time you're just along for the ride, it still brought a lot to my attention design-wise. The game takes place mostly underground, and where any other game would have made this feel like the most evil, horrifying place in the entire map, this game actually made you feel reletively safe in your cobweb-infested den. When the game forces you to leave and book it across a wide, open landscape, you always feel watched and it really builds tention. Finding supplies is a nightmare and those last few moments running towards an air filter when empty really made you cringe. I only ran into a handful of bugs; Here and there a few guys will see you through a wall or a cutscene won't load (my personal fault, I really don't have a gamer Graphics Card), but it's definately good for something to keep you mildly addicted to for a solid week or so. Overall, this deserves a hearty 7.5/10 if you ignore the bad placement of ammo, because I'm pretty sure I'm just bad with stockpiling.

Real talk. I bought this game for a few dollars during a Humble Store sale. I thought why not? It'll be good for a few laughs. I mean, it's a dating sim about birds right haha.  So, I sat down to play it with a friend because clearly this would be a brief and probably entertaining ride. And it is. We named ourself (the main character) something funny, and began our story of avian seduction. We did different voices for each of the characters and laughed at the absurdity of it all.  Then... something happened. Something strange. Hours passed, as we'd finsh route after route. Reaching every ending, unlocking the special character and BBL (which is probably the best part of the game). We were so invested in the story we lost track of time. We were actually falling in love with these ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ virtual pidgeons.  Long story short, I spent another $40 on the Kickstarter to get plushies of my favorite feathered boyfriends. I love this freaking game. Buy it. Don't even listen to reason. Just buy it.  If it's on sale, buy it. If you're considering buying it, buy it. If you love birds, buy it. If you love dating sims, buy it. If you want your heart ripped out and stomped on with tiny bird feet, BUY IT. You get the picture.

The first thing i did before launch was look at the setup, 3 monitor resolutions, no option for V sync, no graphics options. so that was worrying. Alpha Protocol is a hard game to recommend: it carries many of the hallmarks of a really good game but is ultimately let down by a series of hugely frustrating bugs and gameplay decisions.  Alpha Protocol featuring detailed skills progression, branching dialogue, and choices during the progression of the story that ultimately shape the outcome to your style of play. All great stuff, but the barrier to enjoying all that is running the gauntlet of psychic enemy AI, baffling checkpoint placement, and wonky keyboard and mouse controls (especially during the hacking minigame).  By far the most annoying of these issues is the AI. Various enemies randomly vanish between loads, characters will spot you from across the map and immediately raise the alarm, patrol points will be arranged in a way that makes it almost impossible to ghost your way through most areas, and guard behaviour is often wildly inconsistent: you'll shoot a guard in the head and everyone will suddenly be alerted; reload and do the exact same thing and suddenly nobody notices. Even cover hides you from view one minute and makes you visible to every enemy in the room the next.  It's a real shame: if the AI issues alone were fixed this game would be an instant recommendation. As it stands, it's only worth getting if it's cheap and you love Obsidian.

As a walking simulator, this was a decent experience. The narrative was well done and the graphics were also above par for this type of genre. The content of the experience was meaningful and I even comtemplated on the narrative once the story was complete. However, the experience and places you may wander to is short and limited respectively.  My personal opinion would have been that if you are going to limit the space in which you can wander around in, then give logical and reasonable limitations, not just an invisible wall. I would have also appreciated a sense of time to see the sun starting in the early morning and maybe moving towards a sunset to give the player a sense that they needed to accomplish the 'unknown' goals by sunset.  As a walking simulator, for the first time, I will recommend this game. However, do not be led to believe that this is anything other than a narrative and exploration experience. There are no NPCs, no action (not even jump or run are available). If you find this game on sale and you enjoy this type of genre, then go ahead and have the experience, it's one you won't regret.

This game is an amazing mix of so many genres. - 4X turn-based strategy on a hex field - RPG (30+ attributes for every character, leveling up) - CCG (made by Gwent designer) - Roguelike - Survival - this is a post-apocalypse world and it's tough - Economy: managing food and fuel supplies, not letting your high-level characters die of hunger  Features: - Technology tree and rare resources - Amazing deep and detailed crafting system - you can craft hundreds of different swords, shields, maces, bows out of collected resources and monster remains - You have to make choices in equipment - your medic just can't wear armor, sword and shield at the same time because his Strength is low. A shirt and a club instead? - Freedom in style - you can brawl through, or you can persuade your enemies to drop their weapons, or you can sneak on them, or you can hex them with magic - Interesting and deep quest lines and random encounters with different and risky choices - do you want to try to talk sense into a dragon? Oh your social skills were not enough and he devoured your party. - Growing team - from ~5 characters in the beginning to ~25 characters in the late-game, every which of them is as deep, detailed, dear and different as your Baldur's Gate party - Multiple quite different classes: gatherer, craftsman, warrior, bandit, medic, witch, dwarf, elf, goblin, demon - Mythology, gods, quests and monsters are all from old Slavic mythology, and character names are all Slavic. It should be so familiar to Witcher players - Balance is great and I always feel that the game is tough but manageable - You can 'autocomplete' battles to avoid tediousness of simple encounters and focus on development and exploration - Procedural random-generated world guarantees replayability Civilization-style  Oh my. You've got to try it. It's one of the most non-boring and deeply engulfing games I've played.

Stasis is a point and click sci-fi adventure game with hades of Dead Space and Event Horizon. Taking place on a research ship called the Groomlake, a man named John wakes up from Stasis in a mysterious and dangerous situation and must find his family and escape from the horrors aboard.   To get the bad out of the way first, this is definitely a flawed game. The animations have really jerky transitions which are jarring, there's a lot of 'adventure game' logic where you do unintuitive things to progress, and the pacing at times is gods awful slow. When you're walking across a room at a crawl, possibly literally, it's easy to get bored or frustrated. The isometric viewpoint occasionally makes it difficult to tell where you can go or what you can interact with.   However, it does a lot of things right. It's creepy. It's disturbing. It makes you want to know more about the setting and the events. There are a lot of interesting setpieces. Some of the puzzles actually are really clever or interesting when they're not running on vague logic. The voice acting, music, and sound effects are pretty good as well. The graphics and visual design is fantastic, top to bottom, and you definitely feel like you're in a derelict spacecraft where something unspeakable has happened.   The game runs about 7-8 hours, but aside from achievements, there's not a ton of reason to replay it. There are no story branches. There are no secrets really to miss (again, outside of a handful of achievements). But many adventure games share this same problem, so it's more of an issue with the genre as a whole than Stasis in specific. I did enjoy my time with the game and would recommend it to people who like their horror mixed with sci-fi or like their adventure games with a darker shade.

Zombie Night Terror   is a fresh take on the zombie genre. I love zombie games, but picking through the veritable horde of zombie slop will leave you with very few worthy candidates. So, when something like this comes along that tries to escape the cookie cutter zombie survival archetype, I perk up.  Zombie Night Terror  definitely fits the bill.  The comparison to  Lemmings  is apt, as in both games you are tasked with herding a group of mindless fools towards the other end of the map.  Zombie Night Terror  diverges, however, in how it approaches this, and the end result is two very different experiences.  You start out with a base zombie type but are soon treated to a host of different mutations that you get to apply at your discretion. You can pause and unpause the game and issue a series of commands, or play completely without it for an extra added challenge.  The world and story is rife with references to various movies, including but not limited to  Night of the Living Dead, Return of the Living Dead, Terminator , and so on.  Without spoiling too much, the story involves an experimental drug, called Romero (eh, eh?), and the whole apocalypse is basically one enormous drug trial run horribly amok.  The missions vary a great deal in both length and complexity. But whether there are enough missions to justify the price point is entirely up to you—your mileage may vary.  The game is fun, no doubt about that, although you will run into a few irritating frustrations. At times, the game explains perfectly what you need to know, and at other times you don't have nearly enough information. I may just be an idiot, but it would have been nice to have a few more things explained.  More importantly, the game leaves very little room for failure. You can easily end up in a state where you've killed every single human on the map, but because you lack a zombie in one specific location, you simply cannot finish the level. It is  incredibly  aggravating.  Beyond that and a few minor bugs, I didn't run into any major problems.  PS. Did you know zombies can fly?  TL;DR: If you're okay with the price tag, this is a great game. If you're not, it's still a great game.

Team Fortress 2 is an incredible gem. It definitly has to be my most favorite game. Throughout the six years I've played so far, it has done nothing but provide the combination of fast-paced action and great movement, the hard work of developers and the community, and the joy of virtual headwear. The Meet Your Match update has to be one of the most tremendous things that has happened to this game. Newer and casual players can utilize community servers or casual play, and more cutthroat pros can have fun in the competitive mode. As Team Fortress 2 has grown, like so many other games, decisions had to be made. Like so many other games, these decisions are met with hate from the loudest players. I feel as though Team Fortress 2 has recived tons of hate for an update that is just a week old (as of writing this review). These players complain about the game coordinator (which puts you into servers) being broken, or matchmaking being full of bad players, or some of the restrictions the TF2 team has placed, and even configs which can make your game unstable. These players generally make very vague posts or reviews, or say that valve will never fix these problems. Already, the TF2 team has made countless updates and blog posts stating that they are listening. They have fixed bugs in the game coordinator or added new options for casuals. They have personally said sorry for the rough launch. They have been quick to release patches, yet players just seem to want to hate. As you read the reviews, take each and every one with a grain of salt. There will be server errors, weird bugs, and ragers. The queue time may be long (2 minutes tops), the items server may go down, or your game might crash a few times. To me, this is just regular valve game antics. No game has ever had a launch or patch where players are happy. People got mad when Overwatch came out and the servers were down and games like CS:GO has had over-powered weapons that were patched after a week of complaints. TF2 is enjoyable. It has been for a while now, but people always find little excuses to start a riot. The game is free, so why not try it out yourself? Constant updates, huge amounts of content, a thriving community, and a team of developers that will listen to your every word. Play a couple rounds, and make your own judgement. Do not let reviews of petty issues or fixable bugs keep you from playing.

A thinking man's game.  The gameplay is usual Mahjong Solitaire, where player has to pair up similar mahjong blocks, while avoiding locking himself out of victory. However, in this game it's spiced up by the combo system. It rewards players for taking quick decisions, which can in turn be deadly and lose them the game. Balancing speed is crucial to success in this game.  The soundtrack is on point. I am amazed at the quality of royalty free music, that developers masterfully picked. I spent about an hour on just main title listening to music on first launch.  The storytelling in this game is revolutionary. There is no VN dialog moments like you'd expect. Instead, the music sets the mood, and the female characters comment on current activities. It makes the game much more immersive.  The only two flaws I can think of, are lack of Steam Workshop to add more characters/boards to the game, and for some people the Japanese art direction might be off-putting.   In conclusion, for only one dollar, this game is simply too good to pass up.

This has bad design all over it.  Your character always runs right and you have to jump at the right time to avoid obstacles. This is a simple game, but it works against you in a way that is just dependent on your abilities as a gamer.  I won't say that the game sucks because you don't know what's coming ahead of you and it's all about memorization. Some people won't like that, I'm not the biggest fan, but I play retro games made that way and manage to find them fun if they feature good design otherwise (good controls, level design, enemy behavior, etc.).  The game sucks because while the camera is often placed fine, there are very important moments when the action gets fast and you have to make a sudden fall to avoid an obstacle at the top, at those moments the camera stays in place and doesn't give you the focus necessary on the bottom part. (One might not know what I'm talking about if they didn't play later levels.)  And you'll notice a ton of moments when the game is slightly buggy or the controls suddenly don't respond in an optimal way. I wonder how I got through the game at all.  I heard Runner2 is an improvement in many ways, in fact a great game. The first game though, it's frustrating for all the wrong reasons and that's coming for a retro gaming fan like me.

A true classic, 'Red Orchestra: Ostfront 41-45' (released in 2006) still manages to place the player in an immersive, believable and variable environment of the bitterly fought Eastern Front battlefields.  It has a limited single player mode but the game shines in it's multiplayer aspect as live players take on the various infantry roles/associated weapons and on some battlefields, crew tanks and other infantry support vehicles.  The weapon modeling and textures are visually exquisite, have sounds that are probably recorded from the actual weapons, and have an uncanny sense of realism for 1940 era weapons.  'Tripwire' Interactive, has, during the active life of the game, updated it with a large database of maps and skins that make each historical battlefield a distinctive experience.  Mods included are 'Mare Nostrum'; based on the Desert War and 'Darkest Hour'; based on the Allied invasion of Europe (D-Day) as an integrated part of the steam purchase.

app_name: Grand Theft Auto IV: The Complete Edition
review_text: The funnest game i ever got to play on PC. The story is about Niko Bellic, A man that escaped from the bitter war. He's on a mission to find 2 people he escaped with in the army, And to have a new life in Liberty City!  OKay let's go with the Multiplayer Shall we? In multiplayer there's many stuff to do there. There's Free Mode (My Favorite), Cop's And Crooks (2nd Favorite) And many more. I recommend you buy the GTA IV Complete Pack for more game modes and such.   Let's go with gameplay now. The gameplay is cool and smooth, The shooting is good with and mouse but driving is better with a game pad. You can make a choice whether you want to drive better or shoot better. Speaking of driving, The driving is horrible but is still okay, There's one mission you have to do where you have to race, And it fails. And if you try too drift, your car is gonna make 360 and crash into a car. The game is still good. Now there's another, NO CHECKPOINTS!!?!?!?? Really? There's no checkpoints when you die. So after you die you spawn at a hospital and get a taxi to go the gun store and ammo and body armor, And maybe you got hit by a car on the way out so you have to go get a hotdog or go to burger shot or go to cluckin' bell THEN Go back to the mission. That just's horrible sometimes. It can get on your nerves pretty fast.  Last but not least. The installation. The installation for this game is absurd. Sometimes they gave you the wrong actviation key for it, or you can't see the full key. Then you have to go yahoo answers and rockstar support sites and youtube tutorials. Other than doing that, Im a nice guy. Here's the link:   http://support.rockstargames.com/hc/en-us/articles/200145226--Updated-1-3-14-Problems-logging-into-Social-Club-to-launch-GTA-IV-for-PC  (CLICK ON IT :D)  That's all folks. I'll be giving GTA IV A 7/10 (Not too bad but slips on minor stuff. - Knick Knack) I recommend it, but there's two ways into getting GTA IV On PC.   1.The illegal pirate (You can just get the cracked version free.) 2 The normal way (Just buy it on steam like a normal person)

Sigh... this is another instance of one of Square Enix's games gone sour. Don't get me wrong I love Square Enix and most of their games/series, (Except for FF13) but this isn't one of them.  Pros: Fantastic visuals all around, you could play on low and the game still looks pretty decent  Atmospherically epic and keeps you on the edge of your seat (imo)  Weapons are all particularly useful and fun to use  Now for the cons... oh boy Cons:- Short, like stupid short. You could probably beat it in the 7 hours I played the game  - Skill tree is absolutely worthless. Unlike Payday 2 or Skyrim or any other games with actually amazing skill trees, you could beat this game easily without any of these skill. It makes the entire system pointless and makes me wonder why they brought it into the game. Oh yeah and upgrades for weapons are also kinda worthless.  - Repetitive!!! Dear Lord, I don't think I have ever seen anything in any game that I've played that is as repetitive as the combat in this game. There are some enemies, you kill them and then you probably go to a puzzle or two beat that and then there are more freaking enemies to kill. It's the same process for the entire game and it makes up I'd say about 80% of the game and it isn't fun and is an incredibly monotonous way of extending game time and it is boring as hell.  -Now for my biggest complaint and the one that really stuck out to me... the 'tombs'. Tombs are a huge focal point for the Tomb Raider series, and they went and simplified them to hell and back where (I believe) there are no enemies and you just walk into a cave that the game alerts you about when you get close to a it and you do a puzzle, get the relic, walk out of the 'tomb', and continue your journey. That is the most ridiculous thing ever. WHY?! Why take away the thing I loved about this series and screw it up?!   Anyways now that you know my opinion let me sum this up. This I recommend ONCE and never touching it again. I don't recommend 100% because it is entirely worthless. I will give this game a 5.5/10. This is a game that I will never cross paths with again.

This can be rather frustrating but if you can deal with that, it's a fun game. I should point out that i only got this game as a result of it being free when pre-ordering F1 2013... I'm not so sure i would rate it so highly if i had paid the rather high asking price for it. The DLC (and there is plenty of that) is also rather over-priced in my opinion...

Payday: The Heist was one of the most fun and frustrating games that I've played in a long time, I'd keep going back to it no matter how much I'd yell at the screen that there was no way that guard saw me through the wall. So it comes as no surprise that I Pre-ordered Payday 2, hoping that they'd keep up that level of gameplay and management of the fun/frustration meter.  Payday 2 is a fun game, still frustrating, but at this point the frustration mainly comes from the end of the Developer rather than the actual game. Promises made during the Pre-order have gone unfulfilled as well as some dispelled by semantics. Safehouse customizations still don't exist, and the 30 heists we were promised at launch got turned into 'each day of a heist is a separate heist so they count to the 30', not to mention that there still isn't a single 5, 6 or 7 day heist in the game.  Promises is the crux of the issue lately however. Crimefest is an event that Overkill started to give back to the community that helped make their game the success that it is today. Last years Crimefest was a success with a few hitches, but this years is by far the worst thing that could have happened to a game that you not only have to purchase, but one that has 25 DLC and counting. They added microtransactions as a reward for completing a series of in-game milestones. The main issue is that we were promised on multiple avenues from multiple Overkill employees that microtransactions were not going to be a part of the game. This is one backtrack that I cannot in good faith support.

Payday 2. Where do i begin...  I remember the days of good old 2014... The DLC was decent, it was controlled and the crimefest was actually good. Heck, i know all of us secretly love Hoxton Breakout. But then, somewhere end 2014/ start of 2015, things started going wrong. Overkill started releasing all these useless DLC, think Western Pack and Ninja Pack. The Hype Train angered us, after all it was the first event where we had to actively fork out money for something that was so 'hyped'.  We, the community, forgave them. But then it got worse.  I, when i first joined in 2014 crimefest, loved the game. I spent so much money buying DLC because i genuinely enjoyed it, and i understood thatit would also help Overkill. Heck, Some times i even blew half my pocket money just to get some of the DLC at FULL PRICE.  Then, DLC quality went to hell. Chilvary Pack was just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t... Reskins... those damn reskins....  The last straw. Crimefest 2015. So much changed. Our weapons were all changed beyond recognition, i.e now we dont even have to bother modding for damage. Patchett was nerfed to hell.  The modding community also got a nice kick in the face. PD2  Stats is now gone. Apparently, OVK promised that FBI files would not obsolete PD2 Stats. Guess what? Go to  pd2stats.com  and take a look. goonmod and canary got broken.  Now... the worst one. They abused our trust. The new skin system is ♥♥♥♥♥♥ like hell. Making people pay for skins that actually have an in game effect? Now thats just crap. In 2013, Almir stated that microtransactions would not be a thing. Here:  . Hell, they even banned peeople who pointed out that fact. OP of that post was banned until it was removed.  We are angry. So many people are leaving. The disgusted veterans, the people generally sick of paying money continously. PD2 wiki mods are leaving. KarateF22, the guy who made this great guide to mod guns, god bless that man's soul, is leaving, because, and i quote, 'I cannot in good conscience write a guide which, by definition, will need to mention those cancerous pay to slotmachine skins with stats'  I loved this game. I really did. Now, we have nothing to say. To a developer that has repeatedly broken our trust and broke the game, i am severely disappointed. I am now writing this review to make people think twice before they buy this game, because i do not wish them to get a disatisfactory experience in this game. Like many others disgusted with this update, i will be uninstalling this game from my computer. To the other members of the community who read this review, i hope you can follow me in doing so in order to get some sort of response from OVK. To those who do not own the game, i recommend you do not buy it. Sincerely, A dissappointed PD2 player.

Great throwback to the past. We finally get a proper sequel to the original Wasteland. Fallout 1 and 2 played a decent homage to it but they focused more on the vault dweller than the group. Combat is challenging at first but once you get the mechanics down it can get a bit easy. The game has excellent voice overs and writing. Some of your game decisions have serious weight behind them. I'd recommend reading the various bits and pieces of lore as it adds to the game as a whole.   The only knock is the FedEx style quests. This is a problem in just about every RPG. Go here, collect this and bring it back. There are a ton of sidequests available and I tend to be a packrat so it can be distracting at times when you have to return to a questgiver just to unload. I suppose there is no way around it but after nearly 30 years or so of playing CRPG's it can be a bit tedious.

So far it's a great game. The biggest thing I don't like is that it constantly uses the internet, so if you do not have internet, or do not have a stable, constant connection, DO NOT BUY IT. Otherwise, if you have a bandwidth limit, beware when playing this game for a long period of time. This game also will send you back to the main menu if you lose your internet connection. The story in the game so far is good. I won't give any spoilers. There are plenty of cars in game, but you must earn a lot of in-game cash to be able to buy them. Car customization could be better, but this game isn't a simulation, so I didn't expect engine swaps, turbo/supercharger add-ons, etc. What's really cool is how you can modify cards for street, track, dirt, etc. One thing that bugs me is that the car sounds don't change when you switch said modification classes. For example, the Dodge Charger still sounds like the stock engine in a dirt/rally config. This is bittersweet. It's homeworld in HD, and its beautiful. I bought this and I enjoy it. But serious issues exist. Pitting the HW1 factions against the HW2 factions is a great idea but broken upon execution. Formations for strike craft do not work, so the grouped HW2 strikecraft make quick work of their HW1 counterparts. Paying for research has been imposed on the HW1 factions which helps slow them down further. The game is still in beta so hopefully things will be tweaked. The biggest thing I would implement is to have the ability to hold shift to build large quenes of strike craft and corvettes for the HW1 factions. If it seems I am harping on the strike craft and corvettes a lot is because they represent a good chunk of your fleet which you will lose a game without, especially going toe to toe agains the HW2 Vagyr. Finally, documentation between HW1/HW2 and Remastered match differences would be great. Im not sad I bought this game, in fact I quite enjoy it, but its like a off paint stroke on the mona lisa, outsiders might not notice but to those who grew up with the originals it will be like a splinter in the mind.

Easily the best series Telltale has released. Wolf Among Us is a more mature and complete story than it's competitor, the Walking Dead. Unlike TWD, Wolf Among Us is complete, and has many twists and turns that will have you on the edge of your seat. The story puts you in the shoes of Bigby Wolf (The Big Bad Wolf) in Fabletown, a district in the Bronx where creatures and characters in fairytales and fables have moved to. As acting sheriff, it is your job to keep these mystical beings from messing up the place, and when a single murder catapults you into the dark underground of Fabletown, it is your job to clean up the mess that is corrupting the people, by any means necessary. The decisions you make will carry on with you, and with a dark past, it is up to you to decide if you will bring order to the streets through respect or fear. Bigby's entire journey through the worst Fabletown has to offer comes down to a single question to ponder; When Justice meets the Law, which side will you be on?

This game is rather hit and miss. The graphics are fun in their own retro style, and the theme works very well with the nostalgia look, but there are some definite rough patches. First and foremost is the cramped level design; I understand urban encounters are the goal, but most of the designs don't allow creative solutions or flanking for strategy. Put a tank in front, give him/her a big gun, and seal the exits. This is not to say the game isn't challenging at times, it's just that most of the challenge comes from conquering high health or high damage monsters.   On the positive side, the gameplay works well and doesn't try to be an epic adventure. It's a point and click combat mechanic based around a goofy B-movie universe. On that metric, GhostControl is a huge success. There is a charming and easily immersive core underneath a very bland exterior. I would recommend it, but be prepared to play for 60-90 minutes at a time. Anything more than that and the game starts to feel a bit tedious.

This is a great, streamlined strategy game with a heavy focus on -- surprise, surprise -- building a fleet of starships and improving their stats. You improve the ships by spending resources and you get the resources by exerting 'influence' over planets (basically, you conquer them). You can buy influence or gain influence by either completing a mission at the planet or by giving your crew shore leave there. Shore leave basically serves as an 'end turn' button on the main strategy map and allows your crew to rest and recover, so you can move again next turn from planet to planet, improving them (by building cities, improvements, or wonders) and gaining influence.   Aside from the main strategy map, you'll also be conducting an awful lot of combat missions on a more detailed battle map. You'll fire torpedoes, which you can detonate at will after the first turn when an enemy is nearby (or you think it's nearby), or perform scans, or simply shoot an enemy vessel with your ships' weapons. Asteroids little the 2D battle arena that provide some cover for your vessels as you manoeuvre into an advantageous position. The best thing? These battles, while numerous, move very quickly, so you'll never get bored.   You can win the game through a variety of means, though the most common one is the 'population' victory, where you control 51% of the galaxy. The end isn't particularly exciting: you'll get a dialogue box that essentially tells you, 'You win!', followed by a short and rather nondescript video. But the game is fun! Yes, it's nowhere near as complex and involved like any of the Civilization games, but it isn't supposed to be. This is Sid Meier's Starships, not Sid Meier's Galactic Empires. It's streamlined and simple without being simplistic. You'll be doing all the standard 4X things: exploring, expanding, exploiting, and exterminating, but at such a brisk pace you can easily finish a full game in a couple of hours or less.   If you expect this to be the next big Sid Meier game that will devour your life for months if not years on end, you'll be disappointed. (And incidentally, congratulations on having so much spare time on hand!) If instead you're looking for a quick, fun turn-based strategy game with lots of fairly interesting options and brisk combat, then Sid Meier's Starships is worth your time and money. I thoroughly recommend this.

Now that I'm a few hours in I thought I'd give it a go. I'll update as I get further. First of all, I really do like this game a lot. It's just that most reasons for liking it have already been beaten to death by the other reviews, so I won't go into them much. This also counts for some of the negative comments. For example, I personally did not find it a big problem to be stuck on the 2-D plane because it made everything just a bit easier to play, but I can understand that it would frustrate some people. Has also been mentioned a million times so I won't go into it further. With that out of the way I'll start with the actual review. The first thing I noticed was that for an unexperienced gamer like me, the controls took some getting used to. Most reviewers and trailers made it look really easy and I can imagine that it is when you are used to this type of gameplay, but if you are not it feels somewhat strange at first. I personally htink the game could use a slightly better tutorial concerning the difference between portsides and turrets and when to use which. Right now they explain what is what and then just leave you to find out what to do with it. Once again, I'm sure this comes natural to most people, but if this game is your introduction to the genre it takes some getting used to. Secondly, I've seen songs of praise about the voice acting in this game, and given some of its predecessors I can understand why, but it really isn't anything special. It's definitely not bad, but but it's not some sort of masterpiece either. Continuing on that I also think that the amount of in-game text actually sopen by someone is very limited. For example, if I'm on a mission to protect some trader and I choose to hail the his ship, I get the standard options of any other trader in game. When I offer my help he/she says what they all say, they're doing just fine. Of course they are. Because I am helping them. Which brings me to the thing that really bothered me. Especially in the second trailer they make it look like there are conversation options that influence how characters behave. I have not really seen any of it. The storyline as far as I've seen it is incredibly linear and has nothing to do with the choices you make in the rest of the game. There are no '{accept} {decline} {really decline}' options at all in the first portion of the game, with the possible exception of a pirate that offers to spare you if you drop your cargo. Something that I'by the way, have not yet been able to convince any trader to do. As soon as you threaten people, it does not matter how much bigger your ship is or how much of their ♥♥♥ you've already kicked, especially with the pirates you'll just get the same smug remark about the cold of space, even if they just admitted that I'm 'tearing them apart'. Maybe this changes later in game, but so far I find it rather frustrating. So the 'Negotiate' part of the game feels a bit thin, even if they already put it in quotation marks for me.  A very big plus to the game that I do not think is mentioned enough is the ability to create a custom soundtrack. Most people just mention it passing by, but it is quite possibly the biggest reason I bought this game.Yes, the soundtrack is good in and of itself, but the ability to cruise on your own tunes really makes a difference to me. I'm a big fan of soundtracks and the reason this game appealed to me was because of the sci-fi/western mix that I like so much in movies and tv. So when I play and get to hear the soundtrack from those shows it greatly adds to the emmersion. I think the feature is really nicely implemented in this game, with the small sidenote that in between different states (Idle, Station, Combat or Ambience) the specific song plays on loop. Usually this is not a problem but if you like to add the conversation bits of Radio Free Mars like me, They get really ennoying after hearing them 5 times over between stations. There is also no reason why you should not include the Final Countdown in your Combat folder. There are a few things other reviewers have said that I would just like to agree with. Cosmetic customization would really help give the game a higher level of personalization. The story is not very engaging and can make the game start to feel a bit grindy. Risk levels are not always fair, but I think that's part of the charm of the game. A simple delivery to a station that is not under siege should be smooth sailing, but who knows what pirate gang might just have decided to get in your way? There are no guaranties in this Final Frontier. Personally though, I would not mind a a multiple save system.   All that said, I really enjoy this game and think that it does its job well.

This is one of the greatest games I've ever played. When I first heard about it, I read some reviews and watched some gameplay videos. I'm not a big magic fan, and the videos showing Geralt casting Igni was pushing me toward the 'no' column. But then it went on sale for $30 during the winter last year, and I decided to pick it up. I can honestly say that I'm disappointed I didn't spend full price on this game. I wish I had given more money to support the people who created this experience.  It's fantastic. Everything about it is extremely high-quality. I can't think of another game where I felt so immersed in a world that felt so alive. Everywhere you go, there's something going on or something to do.   The combat is fantastic. I wish it felt a little heavier, as Geralt feels very light on his feet for someone who's so big, but I guess that's one of the perks of being a Witcher. It has a Dark Souls feel to it, where there's a lot of blocking and dodging, but the parry and block abilities feel much more familiar than they do in Dark Souls. Both swordplay and the different casting signs are both extremely well-done and upgradeable through the points system.   Speaking of the points system, the RPG elements of the game are fantastic as well. You can add points to gain different skills, and many of the skills have multiple tiers that allow you to further enhance your abilities. This, coupled with the fact that you can only have a certain number of abilities active at any time, forces you to focus your upgrading in certain categories. If you want to be a deadly combat expert, a powerful sign-casting mage, or a potion-heavy alchemist, you're going to have to put your levels and points into these categories. As for armor and weapons, there are many with multiple upgrades available. I stick with Witcher gear, but there are plenty of other options.  NPC characters and voice acting are top-notch, and the character models are extremely good. In fact, the monsters in the game are probably the best monsters in any game ever made. Each monster is extremely varied and there are a ton of different ones. The scaling of them is great as well, and the game gives you a fair amount of time to hone your skills and strategies before throwing you into your first monster battle. But in order to perfect your tactics, you're going to need to level up.  It's an open world game, so there are obviously glitches. I would venture to say, however, that the glitches are nothing compared to other open-world games. Sometimes monsters are unable to come out of the forests because they get stuck running into trees. Sometimes your horse winds up standing somewhere funny. I've seen pop-ins and pop-outs while playing occasionally. But I've personally never encountered a game-breaking bug while playing.  Bottom line: buy this game. If you like combat, you'll love this game. If you like adventure, you'll love this game. If you like really good graphics and an engaging story, you'll really love this game.   Oh, and there are just so many topless women in this game. So there's that.
