Let me tell you about Sunless Sea:  When you are able to look back and honestly say that you were able to overcome the gods and monsters that lurk around the underzee with only your wits and cunning, accumulated at the cost of several ships and many dead zailors, then you will understand what a great game this is.  Im going to be honest with you, when I got this game through early acess, I hated it. There was no point to the game. Nothing was finished and their was no point in travelling around the map, and your only options were to wander pointlessly until you died. So I walked away for a time. But I have recently gone back and played the finished product and I can honestly say that I fell in love with the game. Sure the story needs some polish and I would appreciate more options when it came to some of the locations, but the joy of the game is discovery. This is not a game for those who want to sick close to London and play it safe. As you travel farther and farther out you discover more lucritive oppertunites, and more interesting storylines( fun fact: you can sail to the edges of the map, witch turned out to be some of the best/worst decisions I ever made). Also: Don't be afraid to die. Death is only a temporary burden, and lets you start off with some of your old goods allowing you a slightly easier time, each time you restart. So don't worry about dying! Thats half the fun! Now get out and start sailing!  Overall Opinion: 9/10  -Needs more story. Not too much but a few of the locations could use more fleshing out.

The only gripe I have with this game is the cutscenes. The animation isn't too good. Characters body parts stutter and overlap somtimes during movement. Castle Crahers is a great game to play with friends on a shared screen. It is based in a silly medieval fantasy world where your goal is to save princesses from an evil magician. All that was done way before Aventure Time became popular.  It has an original style, with the vibe of older games made by the same developers, and a good sense of humour.  The fighting system is quite complex for a game of this genre and presents the players with a good variety of combos which increase in numbers with level progression. Friends of mine who were not used to games of this type usualy feel overwhelmed after a few levels.  The level progression adds an element that enhances the experience as an element of competion between the players. It allows players to upgrade different stats which unlock combos and abilities which gives options 'builds' for different playthroughs, although magic and melee weapon attack are always the best options to choose from. It is also fun to have different players focusing on a certain stat more than the others.  There is a choice of characters to play with which except for the visual style differ only in the types of spells they cast which can be very different from each other in terms of damage, area of effect and the effect itself. I found this more than enough to make want to play the game again as other characters.  A variety of weapons and consumables are also present, but they do not change the gameplay to a great extent. The absence of different bows other than the starting one is also disappointing,  There are a lot of different enemy types, some of which can be unlocked as player-controled characters which adds to the game's replayability.  The levels are numerous and with a different feel to most of them. One of the most pleasant surprises was how long the game was.  Definetely the best co-op side-scroller I have played and I have played a decent number of them.

An incredible game, with loads of charm, but sadly a charm that wears thin quickly.  The game's past plans of being an expansion show, as the enemies are incredibly simple and non-varied, as well as obvious reskins of prior units with slightly reworked AI.  There is a large emphasis on grinding through the activities, harkening back to a Just Cause 2 'please explore our map?' feeling from the missions.  The game map is a modification of The Third's map, and the acitivities are all the same as Saints Row: The Third. (Even the powers are much the same as the DLC from Saints Row: The Third: Trouble with Clones) The powers and storyline are fantastic, though, and well worth the playthrough. Just don't expect many differences from the third. If you get it, make sure to do a certain hacker's side missions though so you can get access to the dubstep gun.  Best. Gun. Ever.

I've read reviews, I knew it was going to be good. But boy, I never expected it to be  this  good! Best 'small' game I've played since Valiant Hearts. You don't really need to know anything more obout this game that it is a wonderfully written journey though a man's lfe and memories, which touches upon the topics of life, death, friendship, loss, unconditional love and dreams. But if you really need more and you like bullet points, I've broken down the game into categories:    Storyline  - fantastic: moving, heart-warming and with some nice plot twists, the story built its pace throughout the game very nicely.    Music  - excellent music, nicely fitting given moments. Not much to say about it but it was near perfect (for this game, of course).    Sound effects  - gameboy level of sophistication but decent.    Gameplay  - nothing special - clicking through dialogues, clicking objects, some kindergarten-level puzzles and two 'arcade' moments. Truth be told, it's a good thing it wasn't more engaging - this way the player can focus full attention on the story. The game mechanics are just there to justfy it bein a 'game', rather than an animation.    Graphics  - very simple, yet aesthetic (unless you were expecting AAA level of photorealistic graphics, in which case the graphics in this game is terrible)    Game time  - To the Moon is very short, realisticly speaking, you need about 4 - 4,5 hours to finish it. And there is very little value in terms of replayability. However, the length seemed adequate, at no point did I have the feeling that it was dragged-out.    Offensiveness  - Only offensive to platypus lovers and Dragon Ball fans (well, sort of...)    10/10  - Would cry again

But no bug of great importance will affect the pleasure of playing it.

This game is garbage. Why do you ask why it is so bad?   Game Content,The Batmobile:  FIFTY-TO-SIXTY PERCENT OF THIS GAME INVOLVES THE ♥♥♥♥ING BATMOBILE. The Batmobile's controls are very clunky and you're going to smash into a lot of things when you're driving around. The only time I have absolute control over the Batmobile is when I am in its battle mode. For the portion of the game involving the Batmobile, almost of all it, you will fight countless waves of unmanned drones, which makes up a majority of the Batmobile experience. The miltia commander for Scarecrow's forces will constantly blurt out annoying and cliche dialogue while you're fighting in the Batmobile which will quickly make you roll your eyes and turn off the audio for the game. Aside from the drones you'll need the Batmobile to solve puzzles so simplistic you'll imagine they were made for fourth graders, and if you even think you want to do Riddler challenges be prepared for an endless amount of suffering from the Riddler's Batmobile race tracks.   Game Content: Replayability stripped away, challenge maps do not exist anymore:  There are no challenge maps in this game. Yep, that is right.  There is a new mode that attempts to emulate challenge maps, but it is incredibly shallow and is essentially a neutered version of challenge maps system in previous Arkham Games. There's no extreme maps or none of the power ups or handicaps you can slap on your character during challenge maps in Arkham City. You can't play as anyone other than Batman for an example of lack of content. There are Batmobile challenges..*groan*.. fyi.  Game Content: Sidequests are IMMENSELY repetitive and boss fights are non-existent:  Yea...  TLDR sidequest description blow:  1) Disable nukes in exactly same way and fight waves of drones. 2) Beat up thugs to shut down gates or use Batmobile; you do this I swear 100 times in a row. 3) Beat up thugs or use stealth to shut down watchtowers; you do this 1000 times I swear. 4) Fight Hush for 5 minutes and defeat him with a single counter. 5) Chase Manbat through Gotham and stab him twice with a needle. 6) Chase 5 Penguin trucks and beat up thugs 5 times, and beat Penguin via one hit on last mission. 7) Stop Harvey Dent robberies 5 times in a row, and beat Harvey in the final robbery. 8) Beat Deathstroke via Batmobile and beat him with a single bunch. 9) Find a guy dressed like a pig after spending 2 hours analyzing corpses and beat him a quick 10 min fight. 10) Chase Firefly with the Batmobile over 3 times. 11) Beat up thugs and search for firefighters, and endure a cutscene each time; 17 firefighters in total. 12) Beat up old black man's thugs  Boss Battles:  Batmobile..vs Arkham Knight  Batmobile.. vs More Arkham Knight  Batmobile vs tanks  Batmobile vs tanks  89% of boss battles Batmobile  5% Robin  5% Catwoman  1% Joker  Game Content: Gotham City IS DEAD, WORSE THAN ORIGIN's CITY.  There is no real reason to fight the thugs in the city. At least in city there were political prisoners to save, but here..  no not really..   Technical/Performance:  This game is riddled with bugs, some of which are gamebreaking. A good example of a potentially gamebreaking bug is a problem in the game where Batman falls through the map and dies. Glitches infect this game like a plague. Sometimes when I glide around with Batman he will kick and flail around like a dying fish or he may even teleport across an area.   This game is extremely unstable. You will need a monster of a PC to get this to even be somewhat playable, and by that I mean if you so desire above 40 FPS consistently. Total Biscuit can''t keep this game running at 60 FPS consistently with his Titan Xs and I can't with my 970, Intel I7 4790 and 16 GB of 2133 Ddr3 Ram.  The fact that this game is unstable is a fact, you can test it out for yourself. I recommend frap's fps counter. This game is also capped at 30 FPS because Iron Galaxy, the port team knew it was a very unstable game and so did Warner Brothers, the publisher of AK. You can confirm the fps cap in the game ini files and WB's knowledge of this game being broken before release on gamer new's articles like  Kotaku(http://kotaku.com/sources-warner-bros-knew-that-arkham-knight-pc-was-a-1714915219) .  DLCS:  If you are thinking of buying a season pass please do not. You will not DLC on time like console gamers. All DLC are timed exclusives, meaning no dlc for you until i.e gamestop's contract for the Red Hood story pack with WB and Rocksteady is expired. This game is too broken as of now to have DLC as a consideration anyways. All you will get if you buy a season pass is skins atm.   My review of the DLCs:  Harley Quinn Edition: Play as Harley Quinn for 10 mins and unrealistically beat up Nightwing in hand-to-hand combat.  Red Hood Edition: Play for 10 minutes killing Black Mask's hoes, which is cool, but annoying because of how little content there is.  Scarecrow Nightmare Missions: MORE BATMOBILE, MORE, MORE, EVERYWHERE IN YOUR FACE.   *Random skin DLCs that you'll not really care about after 10 mins*  That's all that is out right now.   Anyways, this has been a long enough review.  I can safely say this game deserves a 1/10.

The Witness is one of the best artistic achievements created in the past decade.  i realize people will probably disagree with me somewhat, because at face value, The Witness is already an amazing game. it takes place on a beautiful, perfectly designed world with addicting line puzzles that teach you it's mechanics without a single instruction, and has the most amazing gimmick that involves using your perspective to find puzzles within the world itself.  but beyond that, there's more. on the back of the paper i used to solve this game, the front being riddled with chicken scratches of various puzzles and grids, i've written down what i think The Witness represents, and what the narrative, or at least my interpretation of it, is trying to present.   now if you haven't played The Witness yet, stop reading here. just know that it's a 10/10 game, and it's absolutely worth your money. play it, beat it, read up a little bit on the stuff you missed, then read this review if you still think you haven't justified your purchase.     The Witness is a game. that's my theory.  and i mean this in the least patronizing way, not in the 'story doesn't matter' way. The Witness is, on the surface, the quintessential 'video game', but as you go deeper into the mountain, it starts deconstructing it's own medium.  The Witness condenses aspects of modern video games into one college campus sized area. how it teaches you, what direction you ought to be going in, and just what the hell is going on occurs through the player exclusively. all the areas are 'dungeons' with 'bosses'. there's about 20-50 puzzles per area, and they gradually introduce new mechanics or flip older ones on their heads a lot of these puzzles' purpose is to set up a pathway to get to the final boss, which is whatever lets you get to the laser box. The Witness follows a base mechanic that is a stereotypical structure for video games.  but as you get deeper into the mountain, you start seeing puzzles that rotate within the screen, puzzles stuffed inside glass cubes, puzzles that are broken in parts, or puzzles that realize as you draw them. that is when The Witness goes outside of itself; it intensely deters from reality. puzzles don't just add up to a collective effort, they start formulating in the world and create impossible scenarios. The Witness is taking the similarity to Myst that you see in it, the hope of a concise, linear narrative and snapping it like a twig. it is just a game, and that's the story.  i could go on about the relevance of the audio logs and windmill theater (which i will on my game journalism site  ubgamingnews.tumblr.com  and  twitter.com/ubgamingnews  pls share) but i digress. chances are, you brushed off my warning and read this before having played The Witness. while it's best to go completely blind, i hope this review encourages you to play what is easily the best game of the year, and one of my personal favorites of all time.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: As someone who loves story-driven game, I had always had my eye on Brothers, just wanted to wait for it to go on sale. Now I've finally played it and I have to say it was everything I had hoped and more.  As you probably know, you play as two brothers, each being controlled with a seperate hand on eithert he controller or (as I played it) a keyboard. Either brother has different tasks they can preform, the older being able to pull heavy levers while the younger can get into small spaces or between bars. All this leads to a very unique experience as your brain tries to tell either hand to do different tasks for ither brother or maybe the same task but in reverse, etc. The game never gets terribly difficult, and in all I beat it 100% (for achievements) in about 3 hours, which includes re-playing a few sections I had missed an achievement in. It's not a game for those who want to grind, or have hours of questing adventures. It's an experience, you play through a story, a tale of two sons, if you will. So in the end, it all comes down to, do you wnat to have a fun and heart-wrenching experiene with a game? Then I say definately play this game. If you're not really into that, then don't bother, this games not for you.  So lets get down to the story - oh wait... spoilers incoming, I recommend playing before reading more, or if you don't intend to play it but wnat to know some critical points about the story, read on.    The story is amazing!  It starts light, then gets very dark pretty quickly, to the point where I think I yelled 'WTF!' at my screen. I felt like the game took me for a ride through a series of emotions from a sense of adventure, fun and mayhem to a somber mournful reality. All these emotions did not happen at individual times, they are interwoven throughout the story, character or people off to the side will die or live or love will blossom. Not all lives are caught up in the boys story, and thus have their own endings while you're still playing. Even a simple silly thing of two bird they you set free finnding love is tucked away in a corner of the game you would easily miss, and less them 10 minutes later a new beloved creature will pass away from starvation and exhaustion as you finally gave it it's last flight from it's brutal cage. You'll have to side-step giants corpses or cut off their limbs to make it to the next path, bathing yourself in the behemoths blood and feeling nothing but pity and sadness for your actions. Or be thrilled and delightled only a short time later as silly sea-turtles coo at their mother. But nothing stung me as the ending, the final series of events you play as only one brother. Feeling as if one of my hands had been cut off, I pressed on with only the right pressing buttons. It was so strange how to game made me feel wrong about a way I would play most games. The younger brother is afraid of water, and would alwyas hold onto the older brother shoulders to help him swim. But now, without his brother, he wouuld have to swim to the final area to hel his father. So I spend like 2-3 minutes just trying to get the kid into the water, but he wouldn't do it until I just instinctivly began pressing buttons with my other hand again and felt a huge gut-wrenching bundle of emotions when he began swimming using the keys which in my mind where reserved for his brother. A game has never made me felt os many things, with it's controls alone. The younger brother has to do a few more tasks with his brothers 'help' or just the left hands keys, however you wnat to think about it. Which is so odd and painful and moving at once. This game is not perfect, I wouldn't even say I liked the overall ending. I felt hollow and just sad when it was done. Yet, it made me feel all these things, and more, throughout the game. So do I think it's good, heck yeah, like I said it's an experience. But not if you just to just play something light. I try to tell people to think of some games like movies. You don't want to watch A clockwork orange or 2001: A Space Odyssey when you're not in the right mood for it. Games like this are very similar in that regard. You need ot be in the right kind of mood. Sometimes I just want to turn off my brian and watch a dumb action moive, or play minecraft and mine for a hour of non-brain requiring fun. This is not a game to play when you're in that kind of mood.    Okay I've rambled enough, I hope thaat helped some. Love the story, very neat controls, very effective ending.

app_name: Deus Ex: Human Revolution - Director's Cut
review_text: The only Deus Ex sequel which is worthy of bearing the title (so far). Having played through the vanilla DX:HR four times before playing this and loving every second, I still wish I could have played the DC first time around.  The differences between the base game and the DC are small but important, adding to its open-ended nature of several key ways. First off, this has all pre-order bonus content, including the excellent Tracer Tong mission, giving you more toys to play with and, as a result, more options for overcoming any given situation. Secondly, the Missing Link DLC has been properly incorporated into the storyline, allowing you to reset your augmentations mid-game incase you've invested points in stuff you don't use.   And finally, and most importantly, the layouts for boss battles have been cleverly reworked and expanded upon, accommodating a broader variety of play styles. Ever wanted to play as a stealthy hacker but couldn't because the bosses would turn you into pate in 3/4 of a second? Now you can!  Even if I weren't a rabid Deus Ex fanboy, I'd still regard this game as one of the best of the past decade. Absolutely essential.

Verdun! First looking at the trailers and such like half a year before writing this, i didn't think much of it, but i decided to give it a shot when it was on sale on christmas. And what a christmas it became....  TL;DR  Pros  +Epic atmosphere +Great core gameplay +Authenticity (To some extent) +MUSTARD GAS +Squad based gameplay  Cons  -Arcady BS elements (Such as hitmarkers, crosshairs, score/kill feed) (Disabling above mentioned BS (hitmarkers, crosshairs) will handicap you.) -No dedicated servers  And for those who want a little reading..!  -------------------------------------------------------------  Verdun has a truly epic atmosphere and it gives the player a true trench warfare experience. The game starts you off with some classic music from the era, and right from the start puts you in the right mood to crawl in mud attacking... Or to bitterly fight for every last inch of your trench.   So, instead of vague flags or areas, you're fighting for actual trenches and not just some 10x10 meter area on the map. The core gameplay is very simple and it WORKS. If you start off as the attacking team, your team will (hopefully) give its best shot at attacking the enemy trench and try to capture it. If the attack fails, you have to fall back to your trench and its the opposing teams time to attack. However, if you manage to capture the enemy trench, a counterattack will be ordered and you have to go from offensive to defensive really fast to keep it. In theory the whole match CAN be just one attack phase, if the attacking team is fighting fanatically enough, and the defenders holding ground steadfast.  All those times you've been THIS close to capturing the enemy trench, but you run out of momentum and have to fall back, is just what trench warfare is all about. I cannot put in words how satisfying it feels when capturing the enemy trench after a long stalemate of neither side gaining any victories, but then... You finally manage to do it and prepare for the enemy counterattack. And it feels really really good to take the enemy trench and If all goes well and you repulse the counterattack, then its time to put your skills to the test on the next trench.  However, the arcady crosshairs and kill feeds and all that scoregarbage eat this atmosphere away, but not enough to ruin the experience, atleast for me. Sure, you can disable the crosshair, or bind the whole hud under a button to show it, but this puts you at a disadvantage, so it would be just handicapping yourself. And for the love of god.... HIT MARKERS WHY???????? But overall, the gameplay is nice, it could be alot better if they had decided to nurture this atmosphere the game has AND REALISM, instead of the crap i mentioned just now.  It has leveling and whatnot too, but you can't get a dramatic boost over a noob with just the unlocks.... unless it's the squad unlocks. Squad level can only be raised by playing with your friends, over and over again, which is nice. The squad level can basicly turn your ♥♥♥♥♥♥ little mortars into a real artillery storm. Oh and you can unlock mustard gas, damn is that deadly.  Overall i'd give it a 7/10. It's very fun, Best enjoyed with friends!  See you in the trenches, comrades. And remember, Keep calm & STELLUNG HALTEN!  I Might Write more to this review when i have the time :D

DISCLAIMER:Verdun is not responsible if you get symptoms of:Trench Foot,burned lungs from gas,Shellshock,or any other forms of sickness you may get from this experience. Verdun is one of the funnest and hardest FPS's I've playd in quite some time,worth every penny in my opinion.It has authentic weaponry and great attention to detail when it comes to the mapping.It also gives a frequent feeling of dread when running into no-mans land not knowing when you'll be killed.Beside all this there are also some cons aswell.First,being one of my most experienced,is frequent lag and disconnection.Like.........every other game.......Then again that could be just on my end but after reading some other reviews it seems common in other people's experiences aswell.Then there's also a sometimes toxic community you may run into but that is very rare,atleast in my experience.As far as the cons go,i can't think of many bad things to say about this game.Now for pros,there's many.Authentic weaponry,time periods,backstory and actual history to the maps during loading screens,and just the vibe you get while playing.All in all,this game is a solid 8\\10 on my list.I recommend this game heavily.

I really wanted to like this game. I really truly did, as it was a game I had on my wishlist for months if not years. So naturally, when the Winter sale came around, and I got some money, I went ahead and bought it, ignoring the reviews that discuss performance issues in regards to lag and stuttering in the game.  Once it was done downloading I started it up and was actually excited to start driving trains around. That was until it had to update and download a few hundered thousand assets. 'Okay, it's a new game so I can understand it having to download some stuff.' It didn't take too long to do (5 minutes roughly) and then I was all set to go.  First time starting it ran just fine however, the second time that I started it, I decided to check out the content manager to see what there was to download. So the content manager opens, and it works for about 5 minutes or so before it locks up. Naturally, I bring up task manager and close it down and wait for it to stop running on Steam to try again.Except it doesn't. I let it go for 15 minutes before force closing steam itself and restarting it.  Needless to say, the 7 hours I have logged into this game are mostly issues with the content creator freezing up or the game having to delete and update assets. Very little of the time has been spent actually in game, which, once it's loaded in and works, I have no issues with at all, even in regards to performance issues.  Overall, no I would not recommend this game as it is rather buggy in the curret version and it appears that it will stay this way as N3V has T:ANE to focus on.  TL;DR - Spend money elsewhere because this game is full of bugs that don't even allow you to get to the game itself.

If you buy this game, immediately look into mods. Without them, it's just not worth the purchase. Previous iterations of the XCOM franchise prided themselves on torturous levels of difficulty and tragedy. It was balanced by a keen sense of style and humor, rooted in 50's/60's-era alien invasion paranoia. With XCOM2, the developers seem to have bought into their own 'difficulty' mystique and made a game that borders on being more frustrating than fun. To do this, they added gameplay elements (e.g. timers, terrain-ignorant fields of vision, and plot-wedging 'Advent Project') to ensure the game is challenging for experienced players, but often defy logic. On paper, some of the new elements sounded great; previous iterations frustratingly relied too heavily on RNG to increase difficulty. The result, however, puts XCOM2 'on rails', meaning that despite the awesome amount of thought they put into class customization, maps and new 'concealment' element (which tantalizes an option for stealth gameplay), missions can only really be accomplished one way. Players are forced to brute-force their way through most (if not all) objectives, which quickly becomes rote and frustrating, given the difficulty level. In other words, missions can't be accomplished with much creativity on the player's part. This vastly diminishes the reward for employing strategy - and this is a strategy game after all. Thankfully, there's a community of modders who felt the same way. I recommend saving yourself from feeling like you wasted money by looking into the highest rated gameplay mods on Steam and download (i.e. 'subscribe' to) them before even starting your first campaign. Sadly, XCOM2 has lost much of the whimsical approach to alien invasion, in favor of scarier, or more intimidating aliens (unsuccessfully, IMO). There are story, voice, and character design mods to be explored too but dive in at your own risk.

Don't play this 'game', nor should you view it as an accurate depiction of depression. You don't make the choices in the game, instead, the developer has already crossed off certain options in order to force you down the same bad ending. Depression Quest only tries to show the worst case scenario for depression, not at all acknowledging that it is treatable by the choices you make, instead, it portrays the only way to treat depression is with pills and a therapist. Notice how I said 'tries', because even then everyone in the 'game' acts like a complete idiot around the player.  Accuracy aside, this is just a poor excuse for a 'game'. From the static overlay constantly on the screen in a pathetic attempt to make the mediocre story seem 'deep' to the lack of multiple endings with your choices basically doing nothing, there's a reason this game's top reviews are mostly negative.

I really wanted to like this game, but I can no longer recommend anyone spent 60 bucks for a laggy screensaver/tech demo.  I was so excited for this game. When I first started, I didn't care much about the low-res textures and 20-40 FPS I was getting, I was just excited to get off the planet and start exploring. Alas, the more I explored, the more I saw the similarities between each planet. Each planet has the exact same textures, just recolored. The flora is of a limited variety, and you'll find a lot of planets with the same exact mushroom sprite, just recolored to seem different. There may be 18 quintillion planets, but they all pretty much share the same basic resources and population. I got bored after about my 10th system. I tried using mods to spice things up (BigThings, LowFlight), but I got bored of those even quicker.  In short, this game is just an overhyped broken promise from Hello Games. If they ever improve the game to be more of what it was shown as, then I may repurchase it, but for now I'll go take my money and spend it on a game I'll actually enjoy.  P.S. If you want to play a game with much better orbital mechanics and exploration, Kerbal Space Program is 50% off this weekend.

Really cool RPG with a touch screen-like type hitting/dodging mechanic (using your mouse, however) that feels at home on a mobile device. Not really a deep story (small twist: you fight alongside the princess you're trying to save), but generally fun over all. Music is very good, often with a good beat that works well for combat. Voice acting is excellent- characters are all fun loving or light hearted, without being too over the top. Art is the real draw here- it reminds me of the style seen in 'The Yellow Submarine', with maybe a hint of Alice in Wonderland. The Blue Meany would fit right in there! Worth noting that the hit detection is fairly good, although there's some occasional bugginess in some attacks (one boss monster had an attack where their dodge arrows disappear prematurely at a certain step).   Only downside that stuck out to me is how long the fights can go on for. There's a point where, while you won't really lose to the enemy, they take forever and a day to kill, dragging otherwise fun fights into monotony. There's also a significant difficult ramp up from Casual to Normal mode, but you don't really need to play the higher difficulties unless you're showing off. I will say you probably shouldn't be sleepy when playing this game- your reflexes define just about everything.   I feel there's a lot more good than serious detractions to Fearless Fantasy and I heartily recommend it.

So this game is basically an adventure comedy game that is a parody of the real life console war, it’s full of jokes, puns and a whole lot and I mean A LOT of references of things ranging from games, movies, comics/manga and popular cultures (mostly Japanese culture though). Personally the thing that kinda bothered me with this game is the amount of anime fanservice (boobs) they shove into your face, if they did a little less of it I might recommend this to non-anime peoples unless you tolerate those things like I do. While the story isn’t exactly great but the comedy aspect of the game is what the game is best at. Pros:   Hilarious jokes and they actually keep creating new ones instead of recycling jokes which is one of the things I enjoy the most in this game   Really good turn based combat system, at first it’s started as a simple system but it gets tougher and you have a lot of strategizing to do as you progress in the game   The lily system adds more depth to the combat system as it can help boost up your characters depending on whom they are partnered with   The plan system is really useful and can help you create things that you previously can’t buy in a shop and make them available for purchase in the shop. You can even make the monster easier or harder depending on your preferences with it   The soundtrack in this game is awesome     Cons:   The port feels kinda bad imo, the prompts are in controller mode so I was really confused as what button to press and I have to ditch trying to play with the keyboard and mouse completely but it’s not exactly impossible to do if you try hard enough. (Although I got to say it ran really good though, no hitching or whatsoever)   Eng dub is severely lacking, basically it’s fully voice acted from the start until the first boss then after that it’s complete silence. There are parts after that that are still voice acted but it’s so little, maybe this won’t bother most of you but some people like me prefer to hear English VA   There’s a lot of backtracking involved because you need to gather things only some dungeons have and those things you need can really help you in a battle from creating a plan, not to mention some dungeons are connected in a chain kind of way where you can only get an item from a certain dungeon 1 and that item can help unlock dungeon 2 where you might get plans/items to unlock dungeon 3 from dungeon 2 (Dungeonception in short).   Somehow money in this game is very little and you have to constantly work on side quests in order to get them   A lot of the areas are recycled, a few of them are just extended but they are basically identical. To people who want to see new environment and not the same looking area over and over again you will not like it    BE WARNED the game is currently buggy to some people, I’m just one of the few lucky people who didn’t encounter a single bug in the game. If you don’t want to risk buying a broken game I suggest waiting a few more months until they patched up the bugs  Edit: Seems like they patched out all the major bugs already, good job IF ;)  Side note: This game also taught me that pudding is one helluva of a drug

Awesome!  Awesome!  And did I say   Awesome!   yet?  This game harkens back to the glory age of PC gaming, when the idea that this game could be put on anything other than a PC Powerhouse was actually laughed at. And it goes back even further!  This is the game that Eve tried to copy. Eve ended up more like Fly For Free or Allods Online than this game. About the only thing it has in common with Elite is it's setting: In spaaaaaaace!  If you're looking for a game with real combat mechanics; instead of 'push this button to cast that spell,' and 'use this button to autoattack,' and 'your success rate is dependent on a roll of the dice,' then this game is for you!  I played Elite Frontier: First Encounters. While this game has a permanent speed limit on all the ships all the time, it makes up for it with a new 'Phase Shift Drive.' With this new drive, the high speeds often seen in First Encounters literally becomes a relic. Even if it was permitted, you'd hardly need it and barely use it. While I consider the speed limit a (exceedingly minor) flaw, the game more than makes up for it by being pure Elite in all other regards.  Want to be a bounty hunter? Yes you can!  Want to be a pirate? Sure thing!  I'm not sure if they've included the military option, but even if they haven't, I'm sure it will be there soon enough!  And they're working to restore some of the things that made First Encounters stand out, such as planetary landings and atmospheric flight.  Finally, they want to add features such as walking around space stations and star ships, and having your friends help crew a single vessel.  But even without these features, this game is just heavenly!  The distances are vast. You get a deep sense of the isolation and scale of space.  There are no artificial bottle necks where pirates can attack you with impunity. If you get attacked by a pirate, you can activate the phase shift drive and (hopefully) escape. With luck, they won't be able to follow. If just going really fast doesn't help, or if they drag you out of 'super cruise,' you can always go for hyperspace, which gets you to an entirely different star system ... without having to find a jump gate, first.  That was one of several things that broke Eve Online so hard that that became unplayable for me. Just to travel between systems, you risked running in to individual(s) waiting to shoot the hell out of your starship. I ended up broker than broke early in the game because of that crappolla. To make things worse, I had to travel between systems because all the 'non-inter-system methods to earn money' options were being hogged by other players, too often violently so.  Add to that, in this game, everyone who can, you know, shoot, as in 'Call of Duty' shoot, actually has a fighting chance. Knowing a bit about aerial combat helps, as well. But if, when you pull the trigger, you stand a decent chance of, you know, hitting the target, then you've got a fighting chance.  In Eve Online, people didn't need to line up the target to blast it to kingdom come. Instead, they just sat around and used a 'targeting' skill that turned their lasers in to, well, laser guided lasers! And if you were just starting the game, they loved blasting you. Why? Because your 'targeting' skill was so 'bad,' you couldn't hit the broadside of a barn! Literally!  It was so disgusting, I set that game down and walked away from it.  At least in this game, I know I have a fighting chance, and I'm not artificially forced through bottlenecks to give griefers a chance to blow me to kingdom come!  And that is a bit of piece of mind you, too, can enjoy!  But if you like hunting down other players, this game gives you that, too! And it would be a real sport, if you're in to that sort of thing. Especially if the prey can, you know, shoot back and -  hit  you - and they prove to be  better  than you were expecting. In other words, having great gear is, well, great, but knowing how to use it is at least as important as having it.  It can be as relaxing or as exciting as you want it to be. I, personally, call myself a 'space trucker,' as I tend to focus on moving freight. Dull, peaceful, but just the way I like it! ☺  Give it a try, do different things (you're bound to find something you like about it) but don't expect it to hold your hand. Even with all my Elite experience, it still took me three days to learn many of the finer points of this game. There are even horrid comment threats (my fault) to prove this on the Steam forums!

Late review, but, just gonna say: Awesome game.  Great Soundtrack, Colorful UI, Amazing Mechanics and somewhat Touching Story.  I would say something about each part, but really, words can't exactly describe the neat feeling of Hacking to a beat from the likes of Remi Gallego, Carpenter Brut, Tonspender and much more.  Being a computer geek personally, I can verify the whole 'Based on real UNIX commands' thing is pretty spot on. Granted, it removes a few commands, but I am sure that can be passed on given the audience this is meant for (All.)  I highly recommend this game if you're a little tired of something like 'Uplink' for instance. Or if you just generally want to feel like a Hacker. It's really addicting.  9.8/10  Now, honestly, this sort of lacks a few things though, and they might be commonly shared things. Like... Upgrades, as one thing. It's understandable to not add Upgrades, since it would probably make the player OP. But really, it would be nice to get additional RAM throughout the game. I don't exactly care how, or why, it just seems like something that would be a good addition. Another thing could also be more Paths. This is a bit of a spoiler, so read off from this next bit if you haven't unlocked all the achievements or done everything possible:  So far, I only found 2 paths to go on. The first path being the goody-two-shoes, going with Entropy, and going through with the CSEC tests. The second path being tracking Naix, deleting his files, and then going on from his E-Mail to vandalize Nortron's servers, to get into the /el community (A really funny spoof to the likes of ♥♥♥♥♥♥, ♥♥♥♥♥, or any particular Image Board that stays in the theme of Free-Speech.) and going on from there. Personally, I would love to see something like a 'Independant' path or a 'Brutal' path. Something like those I wish could be in this.  But, asides from all this though, again, really awesome game.  Side Note: Red Line. Have fun with that ;)

This is another VR game where you shoot enemies as they fly towards you. However, this one does not benefit from room scale play areas, and furthermore, there's an annoying feeling that you're going to lose your balance as the turret/ship you're in moves. I found myself having to turn sideways and brace myself so that I didn't accidentally lean over/back. Better to sit down and enjoy it since you don't need to move anything but your head anyway.  In this game, you play the role of a turret gunner for an Eve Mining corporation fighting over a mining claim with other ships. Aiming is as simple as looking at the enemy to move the crosshair, you pull the trigger on one of your vive controllers to shoot, you press the touchpad down to reload (which takes a few seconds). If you pick up a powerup, you can use it by pressing the trigger on the left hand controller, but you can only hold one powerup at a time so use it or lose it.  After completing a wave, you're rated on how many enemies you killed, and some other stuff that went by pretty fast, and overall you're rated 1-3 stars for your performance. Between each level you go back to a hub to choose the next mission (or to replay an older one if you didn't get the best score)  Things I liked about this game:     controls are simple     the aiming responds as quick as you can turn your head     there is some humorous dialog      Each level is satisfying without being too short or too long     You can look down and see arms and legs, which adds to the realism of the scene   Things I didn't like:     it's best if you're seated, but then again, it's a turret shooter so how much do you need to get up and move when you're seated in a turret?      Standing during the opening scene made me feel as if I was being dragged backwards (I subconsciously started leaning forward to counter it)     Once the game started with me standing, I didn't see an obvious way to reset the scene so I could sit down. After that, if I sat down my face would have been inside the body of the player model.     The reload takes a long time. I'm not sure if it was shorter if I had bullets left or not, but I had to get in the habit of reloading every chance I got or I'd be reloading while missiles came flying at me.   This is another solid VR game...I didn't play the whole way through so it's mostly a first impression of about 10-15 minutes worth. I look forward to going back and playing it some more though.

While the game brings its own ideas to the table and there really are pretty good ones, at the moment it's not enough for me. I feel like the devs should try more innovative stuff, which they clearly can, but might not dare. Spectator modes are really cool, business model was just slightly cheaper than Hearthstone IIRC, the long promised mobile version (at least Android) is still missing. Last time I played the playerbase was extremely small. By now I imagine it's even smaller or about the same or just completely dead.

Warning: There are no 'feels' in this review.  On aspects of this game:  Art: It's okay, but it lacks depth. Most of the images are limited to a single color, making everything feel bland.  Animation: All of the animations are less than 10 frames and loop infinitely, even though they appear to have not been made for that. For example, The leaf animation consists of a few leaves which jump down about a foot every second and then magically teleport back to the starting position. The character's walk cycle is a simple right foot up, left foot up cycle. The worst one is the crows, which (for some reason) gradually vanish, only to suddenly reappear in the next frame.  Sound: Stock spooky sounds and footsteps.  Gameplay: You hold right, then you hold left. I kid you not.  Story: There are a few signs around which describe someone being lost. Most of them have very basic and vague lines that don't really contribute anything. At the end there's someone implaed on what looks like a flower, and then the game cuts to some lines of text about someone who was murdered.  The entirety of this game is meant to envoke emotion in the viewer, but the lackluster presentation doesn't warrant such a response. There are better games online for free.

The dexterity required was beyond my capabilities even when I tried with k/m which is difficult for me. To be clear, I can get by in Batman games and suchlike so it is plausible for devs to tame this down on easy mode.  On 'easy' mode [I test all games on that] the platforming puzzles do not scale down in relation to the difficulty, or scale down enough in difficulty. The combat does--the precision required to do those puzzles does not. I tried this specific puzzle more than 200 times and could not do it.  I recommend this game IF you are not cognitively or physically struggling. It's lots of fun, for what it is.    I cannot however, recommend it for anyone with challenges due to the precision nature of some of the jumping sections unless you want to frustrate the crap out of yourself. I'm still stuck at that broken down church and the rest of the game is now unplayable for me.

Lume is a puzzle game with a very cool art style. The game's structure is simple: solve puzzles to find the components you need to power the house. The game's art is beautiful, and the sets were clearly filmed with an actual camera. The music also fits well with the game.  The compliments end there, however. The puzzles are arcane, with no help. I solved the easier puzzles, but others give you frustratingly little info. One required interaction with an object I thought I'd interacted with already. Another assumes that turning around changes which direction 'northwest' is, which is simply wrong. I got frustrated with those puzzles before finishing them.  Lume is incredibly short. There are about 6 puzzles, then the game ends. If you look up the answers, or even hints, expect to finish the game in under an hour.  If you like the art style, then maybe you can find more value in Lume than I did. If not, even if you like puzzle games, you should skip this one.

Below is my 'Black Hawk Down' encounter in Arma 3 you can skip beyond it and find my list of pros and cons.  So I was playing on a King of The Hill sever with my freind Vlad. My friend and I needed a transport to get from our HQ to the AO. (it's about 3 km or so out) This nice guy by the name of 'Josh' decides to lend us ride in his Ghost Hawk. He welcomes us with a good ol' 'Welcome Aboard!' and then blasts ACDC through his com. This guy then flys through the sky like a pro doing spin flips and never going above 200 meters elevation. Since Josh is doing all this while going 300+ km/h we arive at the AO in seconds. Me and my friend thank him as he returns to base and both unanimously agree that we must fly with Josh from now on. We start taking Josh's rides as are main from of transportation. It all goes well until the seventh or so trip... Josh was circling around the AO looking for a landing zone when out of nowhere an rpg hits the Ghost Hawk. Luckily Josh is a badass who plays ACDC so he's able to crashland his chopper with minimal damage, the only problem is the Heli has crashlanded in the middle of the AO... We all jump out of the crashed chopper but josh has been knocked unconsious. My friend rushes over and starts reviving him while I spot for enemies. Once he revives he tells us that he needs to be protected as he repairs the chopper so he can escape. We are happy to oblige. Things heat up very quickly as enemies start to swarm attracted to the huge bellowing column of smoke coming from the Ghost Hawk. We defended Josh like champs but alas we were soon overwelmed and the ghost hawk was obliterated via explosive sachel of the enemy. Never before had My friend and I met a guy like Josh.  He will live in our hearts forever. Josh's sacrafices will never be forgoten.  Pros   + Exellent graphics and a sold game engine (for the computer that can handle it)  + a very fleshed out weapon and veichle arsenal (though to be fair if compared to arma 2 it's actually smaller)  + HUGE caps-lock letter sized (270+ km) maps that put battlefield, GTA, and expecially COD to shame.  + very realistic combat with bullet drop and realistic health.  + lots of strategy implimented into the game and well practiced by the community. (by well practiced im noting that alot of these players are vet or former military, in other words they know there ♥♥♥♥ about combat)  + extensive single player campain  + exellent multiplayer scene. Notable features:   ~ having a good ol' combined arms conflict on King of The Hill severs  ~ salvaging, stealing and blowing up other peoples ♥♥♥♥ on Wasteland and Dayz severs. (Wasteland has NO zombie, Dayz has ALL zombies)  ~ getting high on weed and trolling cops on Altis Life severs (these severs are were all the noobs and trolls go so don't expect to see many vets here.)  ~ No 11 year old COD ♥♥♥♥-for-brains.  ~ Clan severs, Wasteland, and KTH are full of Badasses like Josh, may he never be forgotten!  Cons   - Compared to the core game the DLC dosen't have that much bang for its buck so i'd suggest combo-packaging it to save money.  - A very unforgiving learning curve. Like I mentioned before there are alot of pro players here. in addition to that the controls are very complicated and numerous. At first you will find strategy-minded enemy players outthinking you. (the computer AI is pretty dumb though but still dangerous) Also a thing I noticed about this game compared to other FPS's is the guns have much more kick and are much less easier to weild then in COD. This is not a game were you just 'Gun and Go', there is, and i can't stress this enough, ALOT of strategy in Arma 3. Skill in this game is measured by combat aptitude, not how quickly you can twitch your thumbs.  Josh and community alone is worth the purchase 10/10

Max Payne's signature jump...things are always satisfying, and slaughtering the infinite hordes of sleazy New Yorkers is as satisfying as any modern TPS, with a dark, comic book story to boot. Note, this game is DARK. Like, really, really dark. As far as I could see, there wasn't a single human (apart from Max's wife, who is dead) who wasn't either severely psyschologically disturbed, depressed, psychotic, self-centered, or just an all-around slimeball. Avoid this pile of crap!  Arkham Origins attempts to be what Arkham City was, only more of it. It has twice the city area to cover, twice the crime scenes, lots of 'crimes in progress' to rescue people from, and lots of cut scenes and scripted events. It also has twice as many continuity errors and is twice as shallow.  It also has lots of bugs, many of them game wrecking. Sometimes Batman randomly flickers, usually while flying. Control is terrible, movement is very slow, and the game is very confused. Usually, diving / crouching will fix the issue. Usually. You will find yourself unable to complete certain encounters and events, because you randomly can not interrogate foes. This is especially problematic post-credits. (And yes, the credits are 32 minutes long, and cannot be skipped.) You will find the game randomly not working. Freeze followed by crash to desktop; often at a check point, leaving your game stuck at that spot. Really. Search for Copperhead crash, you'll find many instances of it.  The main story is 10 to 16 hours of gameplay. The sidequests are another 10 to 20 hours (including all of those dang riddler items). Plus however long you take to play the challenge maps, and if you do any of the multi-player stuff.  Overall: Good concept, rushed out the door to make money; inadequately tested, and only partially patched. Not worth the time or money.

La-Mulana is a terrific game. When I read reviews for it, the one word that stuck in my mind was 'hard', but having played it now for at least 20 hours, I think that word doesn't quite do it justice. The word, for me, that best represents La-Mulana is the word 'fun'. Yes, it is very, very fun. The controls are tight, the enemies are interesting and varied, the boss fights are awesome, the world is huge and consistently fascinating, and the puzzles are actually stimulating (which is practically unheard of in a metroidvania).  If you have ever enjoyed a metroidvania, I wholeheartedly recommend this game to you. This game absolutely nails every aspect there is to the genre, with its own distinctive flavour. It *may* even pip Super Metroid as my favourite ever metroidvania...

The original GRID seemed to nail the balance between a simulation and an arcade experience. GRID: Autosport has a more sim-like feel, which may appeal to some but not to me. The cars feel big and like you have to lug them through turns, yet oddly there's a tipping point while braking where suddenly the car will basically do a 180 if you so much as tap the stick. The braking feels all over the place, probably because the game doesn't convey an intuitive sense of speed: 75 mph feels the same as 150 mph. Regardless, this is a game where proper braking is absolutely crucial, but the game does you no favors. The distance markers are too small, for example, and the announcer is useless. It all adds up to an unintuitive sense of control, and in racing games, if the control isn't there it's a good bet the overall gaming experience is in serious trouble.  I've only played a couple drift events but so far it's a hot mess. This game seems to think spinning your tires = drifting. The sweet spot of control through a turn is almost non-existent. You never get that 'let it all hang out' feeling through a turn because going open throttle on even a straight will send you out of control! What were they thinking? But worse, there is no sense of what makes a good drift or a bad drift. There's some vague connection to markers on the sides of the turns but I've done absolutely sh**ty drifts of just tire-spinning and fish-tailing and they seem to score as well if not better than a controlled slide (on the extremely rare occasion the game lets me perform one).  Menus and skill progression, similar to Dirt 3, are terrible and totally uninspiring. I don't know why it's so hard for them. In the case of G:AS it's an extremely minimalist design that could easily have come from an 80s or 90s game, and gives you very little sense of progress, or why progress even matters. The tiers in the original GRID were not a standout but they did the job. Every game Codemasters has produced in the last few years has this sort of wannabe minimalist avant-garde look that also happens to be just passable at conveying information. It's better than Dirt 3's triangles, is about the best I can say.  And Codemasters, it's just a videogame. You don't need to confirm every freakin' decision I make. Do I really want to exit Practice Mode? YES, FREAKING YES!! If I accidentally pick the wrong thing and lose my race, it's not like I just deleted my dissertation or formatted my hard drive. So enough with the stupid confirming. Please Please fire the menu team. They have no idea how to design menus that compliment the rest of the game and they're annoying as hell.  Bottom line: all this adds up to a game that is just not terribly fun to play. It's not a horrible game but in both real cars and car racing games, the crucial make or break element is that intangible feeling of driving excitement. Racing is supposed to be edge of your seat type stuff, and this doesn't feel like it at all. It just feels mediocre. Glad I got this on sale but unless future Codemaster game reviews are overwhelmingly positive, this is likely my last purchase.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Having played TellTale games before and usually going for the nice guy choices, I played the first 4 episodes fooling myself and being careful with the 'decisions' I was taking, with really no major consequences -as expected. The story kept me all in all interested, and once you get used to watch a movie and from time to time take 'very important' decisions, you can actually have a nice experience with the game, although of course, it depends on what you were expecting in the first place -and how much you've paid for it (you should probably wait for the 75% sale).   Then suddenly and out of nowhere    the Game of Thrones factor kicks in, all the main characters start dying    and that's when you just start laughing thinking how unimportant your decisions were and say   '♥♥♥♥ it! let's kill everyone!'    out of curiosity.   Once you reach that point where you realize    there's no happy ending for you   , you start taking all the '♥♥♥♥ being a polite lord' choices and risking everyone's lives just because you can, and you start having such a great time with    everyone actually dying (as expected!)    that you don't care anymore about that moment when you thought your past decisions meant something.  TL;DR:    Dying was never this fun!

First I'd like to say I'm not saying Downwell is a bad game that no one should bother playing. Since Steam doesn't allow a 'neutral' or 'in the middle' or even a 'has things that can ruin the game for some but not others' option I'm chosing to not recommend this game at the current moment. Updates may change this. Steam should really add middle option though, because I'm more just saying things that I really dislike, and that are fair things to warn about.   Downwell has a lot of things I like about it. It has art direction I like, music I like, and gameplay I like. But I still have a big problem with the game. The graphcis are too clustered to tell what if going on, especially when there's little color variation between you, the background/enviornment, and enemies. There's also lots of explosion effects, gems to collect, and other such effects that get in your way of seeing where and what enemies are. I found that I would get hit or die because there were enemies that I just either couldn't see, or popped up too fast to react to when they're hard to see.  There's also no control rebinding, and little sound and display options (only a master volume setting, an option to turn BGM off, and to play in a window instead of fullscreen.). If the developer updated the game to give an option that would maybe give enemies an outline, or remove effects that get in the way of seeing what's happening on the screen, or maybe some other remedy that would make it easier to see what is happening, I would recommend this game.  Still a game worth considering, and if you think you'll be OK with the issue I stated, or don't find it a problem, go ahead and buy the game. It's refreshing and has some fun to be had.

review_text: How you guys probably see, i have played  2 Hours 2!!!!^^

The amount of problems that this game has despite being at full launch now is atrocious. Good fun with friends, but otherwise be prepared to be ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥tted out of victories as a survivor. If you're the killer, then have fun running around and killing people, you have the long end of the Dead by Daylight stick!  Pros: -Fun to be chased -Fun to chase -Unique and interesting perks -A story(?)(items have flavor text not unlike Dark Souls) -Items aren't totally useless -Each character at level 1 is the same except aesthetic, but each has 3 unique perks that you can unlock by leveling up -The level up system (Bloodweb) is randomly generated each time you level up to give you items, item modifications, perks, and offerings (which alter the map somewhat) -The level up system is actually pretty cool since you don't have to escape as a survivor or kill everyone as the killer in order to get experience points (Bloodpoints) -Actually fun with friends, especially when you're a party of 5 and one gets to be the killer -3/4 of the killers are fun to play and fun to play against. I'll get to that 1/4 later.  Cons: (and they're pretty heavy) -The game's balanced in favor of the killer, and not just gameplay-wise (even though that's a serious issue when the killer has 4 perk slots with ultra-rare perks). The killer is the host, so if he's lagging, you're lagging -What's matchmaking? -You'll be trying to READY UP and get in a game for at least 5-10 minutes before finding a killer (as a party of 4 survivors, in my experience) -The perks are ♥♥♥♥ until you start getting to the higher levels, and you don't unlock another perk slot until level 15 -The FOV on the killer's garbage unless you get a perk to increase it -The perks on your Bloodweb (the level up screen) are randomly generated, so you're not guaranteed to get a decent perk -Graphical errors -UI errors -The READY UP lobby system, when with friends, has you READYING UP every time you fail to match to a game, which is very, very often. -Escaping from the hook, bear trap, etc. is completely luck-based. So sometimes you ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t the killer by escaping immediately, other times you're screwed, despite attempting to escape 20 times from that bear trap while the killer's off in the distance somewhere. -The ranked system is pointless -Matchmaking with regards to levels is pointless. It seems that level and rank has no influence whatsoever on who you match with, judging by the amount of times my level 1-10 rank 20-18 party of survivors matched with level 40-50 rank 15-10 killers. -Killers are damn toxic, since they're entirely allowed to be. There's nothing stopping them from standing over your bleeding body to keep other players from reviving you for the entire game. Granted, if they single out an individual player then they'll probably lose the other 3 since they'd just be free to repair generators and leave. But of course you wouldn't be surprised to hear that people do it anyway, just to be a ♥♥♥♥ to the one guy that escaped them once. -The Nurse is ridiculous and isn't fun to play against. Part of the game's counterplay for the survivors is to drop barriers or vault through windows that the killers can't pass through. The Nurse, however, has a teleport ability to just pass through these objects and the walls themselves, with a comparatively very short time to recover. Not to mention one of her unique perks, which reveals any survivors that attempt to heal themselves or another -When bleeding out on the ground, you have the options to either crawl away slowly or 'Recover'. The 'Recover' option moves agonizingly slowly, but decreases the amount of time it takes for another survivor to pick you up. That's great and all, but with how long it takes to fill up that bar, it should just let you pick yourself back up right then and there  That's all I could think of after several hours of gameplay. Now, does this game have redeemable qualities? Absolutely. This game is a ton of fun when you have a full group to go at it with. But that's the only way I could have fun without being an invisible dickbag while solo-queueing as the killer. Would I recommend this game to others? No. Not right now at least. Maybe in a few more patches the matchmaking system will actually exist, it won't ask you to READY UP 20 times before matching you to a game, and the killers won't be so heavily favored by the game's mechanics and systems. I would really like to know is how the current state of the game (8/21/16) counts as being a full release, given the amount of bugs and errors I encountered in the few hours that I played.  tl;dr: the game's alright, I guess, but wait to get it for a month or two. Let it get fixed first, and grab some friends to play it with in the meantime, unless you like being a sneaky dirtbag solo-queue killer. I wrote this whole thing out while hiding in a closet in the killer's basement. gg. Also I don't know why it says I reviewed this product for free, since I definitely payed money for it.  [Edit: 10/25/16: Hey look it's Michael Myers wow! My initial criticisms of the game still stand, aside from the recent patches that changed this game from Ready Up Simulator to Waiting for 1 More Victim Simulator. Something that I still have to complain about is the shoddy matchmaking, if you could even call it that. They said they fixed it in Patch 1.1.2e. 'Adjusted Matchmaking for Survive With Friends to be based on the average rank of the group (instead of the host’s rank)' Then please explain why my group of ranks 20, 20, 20, and 13 just went up against a rank 1 killer. And, as usual, the new character is OP until they sucker a bunch of people into buying him, then hit him with the nerf hammer afterward. It happened to the Nurse, it'll happen to Michael as well. And by the way, remember those Bloodpoints I mentioned earlier as being the XP/Currency system in the game? Well, good ol' Mikey is the king of generating those. The first game I played against him had him generating 20,000 Bloodpoints after killing only 1 survivor. That's I-N-S-A-N-E. Some good things happened too though. Nice update for menu UI to make it easier to navigate, new music that's pretty 10/10, updated some animations, some solid bugfixes, and the introduction of a new currency system for the Shrine of Secrets (it gives you new perks). It's made the game easier to play all-around, and that's a pretty good thing for a (mostly) free update. Oh hang on, I'm calling it right now, this new currency for the Shrine of Secrets? They're totally gonna take those and sell them in bundles for money. It's too juicy of a deal for them not to.]

Did you ever play the original Sim Tower? If so, that might make you a bit of a fossil like me, but you can rejoice in that Project Highrise is a VERY worthy successor - perhaps THE successor we've been waiting for. The premise is the same: remain profitable while building bigger and better rooms in a tower-shaped space (both above and below ground). This is not a game about designing nice architecture, but a strategy game of balancing the best number of rooms with services and accessibility to keep your tenants paying rent, the building maintained, and the elevators fast! The primary objective is simple: make money. In Sim Tower, this same goal limited the scope and scale of the game (you basically built until you'd built everything and made money) and Project Highrise is no different in sandbox mode. There are also scenarios you can play which increases the variety of gameplay. Basically, the developers have taken the concept and expanded it in every way possible to give the game more longevity than its predecessor, but in essence, this is one of those games where you will likely have most fun building, then scrapping your game to start over (rinse and repeat  ad infinitum ...).  New to this neglected genre of building management are more granular levels to micro-manage: business types to accommodate (not just 'offices') and who won't thank you for being placed among too many competitors, the need to incorporate often-expensive building and business services that each occupant requires - as well as all the primary utilities, there are money-winning goals to aim for (in the guise of contracts) and a decent level of micro-management for anyone who likes to tweak individual tenant rents, sift through statistical reports and such. The developers have made a great job in recreating the Sim Tower experience in 21st century clothing with the kind of management and strategy we expect in games today.  While this game sits largely in the 'casual' game genre, expect to lose quite a few hours to it. Recommended for all tycoon fans! A must-buy to fellow aging Sim Tower fans!

7.5/10 Fairly enjoyable casual game. Pick this one up if you like wolves, wildlife, or playing relaxing sims.   Pros  - Nice graphics - Wide customization - Relaxing gameplay - Kid-friendly and educational - Recent updates! - Optional online multiplayer   Cons  - Short gameplay - Some parts can be frustrating, like crossing rivers with pups - May or may not be replayable to you  This game was easy enough for my six year old brother to pick up and play without trouble, and several years later it's not much more difficult. There's not much to it if you're looking for long-term gameplay unless you want to get all of the achievements, but that should not sway you from picking this up if it interests you.  There is a multi-player functionality, but as I have not played it I will not comment on it.  The game starts out easy enough. You play a wolf on the search for a mate. Earn points by taking down prey and predators, but beware bull elk and bears! You must find three packs in the map and play a 'minigame' of sorts to interact with them. Successfully running off pack wolves will earn you points, but they can take your health down to near-death if they're stronger than you and you don't run away when they tell you too!  Once you find the three packs, you can start seeing 'dispersal wolves'. You can make these your mate. Once you have a mate, hunting becomes much easier, and you can continue playing in the first map or you can move on to raising pups. This game is very kid-friendly (with the exception of possibly prey corpses when you feed), so don't worry about any actual mating being depicted. You move straight from choosing a den in the next map to guarding your active pups.  One great thing since this game has come to Steam is that a lot of the bugs with the pup stage have been worked out. Before I found the game very difficult, as my mate would stand around doing nothing while predators attacked my pups literally every two seconds, leaving me no time to hunt to feed them.  This is no longer the case!  Predators attack much less frequently, and your mate will run to carcasses to bring food to your pups. Playing on the easiest setting was easy but not brainless when it came to this stage, which was nice.  From here, you take your pups on a journey to the summer hunting ground, which means leading them across the map. I found this to be the most difficult stage, particularly carrying pups across the river without them drowning. The easiest thing I found was to take one pup across and then let my mate carry them all across the two parts of the river crossing. The hardest part of this stage is keeping all your pups fed throughout the journey, as you move far from any elk herds. Keep your belly full of 'food for pups'!  If I remember correctly, the game ends here. You can, however, play as one of your surviving pups, or a new wolf altogether! You can play through the previous stages endlessly. Beyond this, there's not too much gameplay to be had unless you want to partake in the online multiplayer.  All in all, I recommend this game if you enjoy an occasional play here and there to relax. Don't expect to get hundreds and hundreds of hours out of this one without getting bored at some point! Otherwise, it's a great stress-relieving simulation game.

Company of Heroes is a very interesting twist on the status quo of stratagy. It's fast pace, yet very tactical. Instead of massing units like most ofther stratagies it often requires deploying the right troops, and sometimes knowing when to withdraw instead of dualing it out. It's single player campaign attempts to be emotional, and it does have a realistic side. Unlike Men of War's mechanic of cover however, the troops sometimes like wandering else where when fighting, proving to be annoying if you want them to stay put. The diffrences of three factions, yet being the same is also very interesting.  Bases in this game are less of an influence then in others, and in some missions you have no base. The sit coms are nice, and some twists in the campaign is also fun. One problem I have is that there is no way to kill a unit you don't need, or need to remove to get more population, forcing you to suiside it instead...which...ehh... The second problem is that some missions carry over items, buildings, and units from your last encounter on that map. Although interesting, it makes it very difficult to transition from what was once a defense to now an assault. All your MG enplacments are now not as useful, and you end up with rather odd (but funny) situations. For example in the brittish campaign against the german airfield. The second part had me constructing defenses and had my command vehicle on one of the trencthes I had built in the prior mission...it was funny but somewhat troublsome.  I got this game on sale and felt like it was a good buy. It might be worth full price, so pick it up if you need something long to acomplish.

[9/10] Bastion is an action RPG with an interesting way of story telling. Almost the entirety of the game is narrated by a man named Rucks. While the combat may be a bit repetitive, it is still an extremely fun experience. The look and gameplay in general somewhat remind me of Diablo, which is a positive. The story is really what makes this game shine against others in its genre. You, 'The Kid,' are stuck in this strange world that builds itself as it goes, and your quest is to rebuild it, restore it, back to what it used to be. This game is fantastic, mysterious, atmospheric, and even immersive at some points. It is all around a work of art. Supergiant Games did a great job with this game, and I can not wait for their newest title, Transistor.

You might sometimes experience something that seems a tiny bit off, but most the timethats cuz of people playing on servers where they have a ping of 600 -_- gg.  Is it easy? Let me awnser that question with a question: Is building a pocket sized nuclear reactor easy? The game requires a lot of skill and practise to even be somewhat good at it. When you start out expect to die.. a LOT. Luckely for the first i believe 15-20 ranks there are beginners servers, so you wont have mega OP level 60's on your game. But then when you thought you were getting good... BAM promoted to lvl 21... now your gonna have to play with the big boys aaaaaand it starts all over.. prepare to die a LOT. But once you get somewhat used to the more advanced playstyle with all the dragging and ♥♥♥♥, you will start to do okay and the game will get a lot more fun again, like back when you where the higher level player on the beginner server, only you're nowhere near the highest rank and you stll get killed quite a lot more, but who cares right!  Watching youtube tutorials on basic moves and tactics is highly reccomended because a lot of the good moves are combinations of swings, moving your mouse and moving your character in VERY specific ways.  All in all i think is a fun game which i love to play, but you definately have to be willing to get through a rough start (i think i didnt get to what id consider a reasonable level untill like 40-ish hours in... and still i die to most level 45+ or even a considerabel ammount of my own level.) so you are warned, it fun, but you will get owned a lot when starting out, well i did at least :P

I recommend this game, but beware that matchmaking is poor in certain regions. I live in Asia and can never find games. If you are going to get this game, bring friends or do not bother at all.  That said, the game does have surprising depth for the price and genre. At least in the beginning, there is a 'correct' way to play each character that synergizes with the characters strengths. Of course you can play each character however you want, but with starting gear each character should be played according to their own roles. The dwarf, for example, is best played with a shield to block enemies and protect the team. You can definitely play him with a good two-hander as well. The playstyle becomes altogether different and team composition needs to be tweaked accordingly.  Aside from this, there are two things to note. The game is definitely a gear grind. If you dont like that, avoid it at all costs. Also, sometimes with latency the hit detection becomes a little questionable - you will swear you hit things but it just wont register.  Otherwise, the art direction, level design and all other aspects of the game are great and I highly recommend it. Just wish the multiplayer were more accessible.

Similar to the original deus ex in a lot of ways but unlike deus ex where i find certain mechanics like stealth for example a bit dated now, i always found nothing dated about system shock 2 and it still holds up very well today. It's still an amazing game i just think that most gamers are spoiled with the hand holding in modern games and they will never be able to get into this kind of game and experience it which is sad. You have to figure out what you have to do and where you have to go by yourself. There is no magic waypoint all the time telling you where you need to go. You have to read logs to find codes for doors to progress and research specific items needed to progress using different chemicals that you have to find in the levels. You have to carefully manage your inventory and supplies throughout the whole game or you won't be able to progress. The game forces you to use your brain and you get a great sense of accomplishment when you complete a stage you have been on for hours (engineering).  This game also rewards you very nicely for exploring every area of the game with items which encourages taking your time and exploring every area of the ship. This game has some of the best sound design of all time with the voice of shodan and the sounds of the ship and the music and sound effects etc. It's still honestly one of the best games ever made i would put it somewhere in my top 20 i can't really think of anything i dislike about it.  10/10 (Masterpiece)
