I do not drive trucks for a living, nor have I ever.  In fact, I don't even drive a car - I live in a major US city, and some medical issues prevent me from driving safely. When I could drive a car, I didn't enjoy it.  So why am I in love with this game?  I bought this on a whim. Hey, it's $20. If I hated it, I could always get a refund. I played for about half an hour the first time, barely making it out of the tutorial. I had chosen the third 'level' of controls (shifting and mouse steering) out of four, which made the game unintelligible to me. I considered, and discarded, the idea of getting a refund. It was a neat game, but definitely not for me since I never knew how to drive a standard anyway.  Then I started it up again later that evening... and I've been playing for hours ever since.  Something about this game is so soothing, so freeing - it's unbelievable. The graphical fidelity reminds me of GTAV, although (obviously) less detailed and not bothering with animating humans. Neon blinks uselessly in open starry nights, proclaiming doughnuts here / left turn only / ACME goods. A year ago, I took a ride with a friend out into the wild empty desert around Barstow (and yes we did have Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas audiobook playing while we did so). This game does a wonderful job at capturing the grit and loneliness of that open, alien world.  I finally figured out how to not roll backwards when I get to the top of an onramp. That was a good thing. I feel clever when I do things like this - I understand now why people drive, I think. Downshifting to slow down when I see brakelights flash briefly in the distance... and then, sure enough, there's a traffic light I didn't anticipate but I'm already slowed down!... these things make me feel that private sort of joy you get in any video game when you make a particularly difficult play.  I don't know why I like this game. I'll never be great at it, and that's okay. I have no interest in trucking, or driving, in the real world. But I wanted to write this review to let people know that this game might grab them in the way it did me.  Give this game a try.    One quick edit while I'm thinking about it: Most of the negative reviews here are due to two main factors: 1. Actual, real, live, human truckers disappointed that the game does not perfectly capture the depth or distance of driving for 40+ hours at a stretch (I am okay with this; I have no desire to sit for that long). I understand, but I disagree with, that as a criticism. Games must remove some of the tedium from real life. 2. People giving temporary thumbs-down because the existing world is rather small. True. There are only two states out of the forty-eight that comprise the continental US. The company has stated they will add more.

This is a very depressing game and makes you think 'If that were me in reality...' Definitely recommended.  Graphic : it's gorgeous. When you zoom in, there are so many details that would've been missed or taken for granted  BGM : love it.. love the music! Adds to the depressing atmosphere, and yet soothing enough to keep a little hope  Plot : the character profile/bio is interesting and makes it more personal when we're playing as someone  Difficulty level : seriously, it's not about that at all. It's about how far you're willing to go to survive and how 'smart' you are. I died in the 28th day to suicide, where my other friends died long before due to starvation. Now I know, tools tools TOOLS!  Price : Very reasonable. This is an endless game and we just want to play it again and again despite the heart-breaking nature.   Personal opinion : I'm not an avid fan of survival genres, but I LOVE this game. I only wish we could make someone read or smoke a cigarette when they start feeling depressed, especially after their other comrades died.   Recommend : A definite yes. I died just a few minutes ago, but gained new survival knowledge and hope to do better in the next try. At least not to die by suicide.

I dont understand this 'game' really. First off, it literally is a slide show where you pick one of three photos, rinse and repeat until you've gone through the 'story', which best I can tell is a cyclist who lost his memory in a crash? Pretty sure the cyclist in question is the developer (maybe I'm wrong though). Basically, this is a random photo album, which, if you take it as that alone, is not bad. I dont mind flipping through the odd photo album here and there. As a game with an alleged story however, it is absolutely a failure, since it really isnt a game, and really doesnt have a story, even though it tries to trick you into thinking there might be one. There isnt even any continuity in the photos. maybe it is a set of three pics of a pretty girl or a kitty cat or an old lady or some camera lenses (lol at that set of pics btw)...but there is no real timeline, no real way they tie together in any seeming fashion, and nothing to suggest they are really even connected except for the product description that claims they are.  Now, this does sell for less than a buck (although my own key came from a bundle I bought at some point), and for a buck or less, there are some nice photos to look at and some decent music to listen to, and so it probably carries value from that alone, but pretending this is a game and selling it as such is pretty, i dont know, pretentious at best, and flat out deceptive at worst.  Also, there isnt a full screen mode and the developer apparently insists that you fix the game from your end rather than bothering to optimize it himself.   There are trading cards, so I suppose when all is said and done, you will end up having invested around 50 cents for a decent photo album with some tunes, lol.  If you are expecting a game of any sort however, I would steer clear of this.

Set in the Mongolian steppes about 60 years in the future, Cradle is a first-person adventure game with a rather unique premise. You wake up in a wonderfully detailed yurt that looks convincingly lived-in. Most of the worldbuilding in the game is concentrated in this tiny space, with dozens of newspaper clippings and foreign objects outlining a fascinating transhumanist future which has partially, but not completely, also arrived to this tent in the middle of nowhere.  Outside on the vast plains, a hovertram line stretches out to the horizon with precious few other landmarks besides a huge, abandoned dome of some sorts. It's up to you to puzzle out the story and the big picture by reading and observing things, but mostly by gathering parts to an old android in your possession.  Getting that thing to talk for the first time is a revelation in a game which had been very silent and tranquil up to that point. Unfortunately, at that time you will already have passed the best portions of the game. The first few hours of Cradle are as good as anything I've played, with a truly engaging world and lots of fresh ideas. Sure, you do run up against a rather jarring piece of videogame logic right in the first puzzle, but I was willing to forgive the game for that. However, it got much worse.  It's like the writers and designers eventually had no idea what to do with their creation. The story gets progressively less interesting when more and more details about it are revealed. The puzzles wither down to simple 'find the switch' challenges that can get truly frustrating if you get stuck. The game isn't really willing to give you much pointers at all, which I can appreciate, but it is a bummer to run in circles for an hour just because you've missed a switch.  Eventually, the game settles into you just hiking between two points on continuous errands and forcing you to repeatedly play a ridiculous minigame that feels unbelieveably out of place with everything else and is absolutely not fun in the slightest. The minigame feels like an attempt to add more gameplay to decidedly a pretty walking-simulator-ish experience, but it just doesn't work at all. I feel like the entire sections could be stripped completely and the game would only be better for it.  Better or, well, as good it could be with the rest of the story continuing the sabotage the experience the futher you get. The story ends up being such a letdown, a mishmash of different scifi cliches slapped together to form a jumbled mess of disappointment.  After the first few hours, I really thought Cradle would be one of my games of the year. After completing it, it's nowhere even close. It has an extremely engaging atmosphere and impressive graphics, but the story just twists upon itself way too many times and you end up feeling like chasing your own tail for half of the game. Such a shame! Huge wasted potential.

It's a quick little game that is fun to play. It accomplishes a mix between shmup and puzzle game. The first part of each stage is spent calculating a path through enemies which you can actually close off (i.e. make impassible) if you are not careful. The second part of each stage (a boss) is more like a traditional shmup.   There are six stages total, plus a boss run mode, where you fight the six bosses with a single shield (though weapons and speed are maxed), using the ship of your choice. I found that most bosses took no longer than 30 seconds to beat, fewer in boss mode because of the maxed weapon damage.  The game has music that fits with the fast pace style, and the puzzle aspect is interesting enough for the time the game lasts.  To beat the mission mode and boss rush mode, it took a total of 1.5 hours on my first play through, so if you're concerned about length of a game in relationship to the price, I would venture the game would take 1-3 hours depending on your level of familiarity with shmups.

Oxenfree is very much a story based game. You will not solve any 'puzzles' or have to use any logic to complete anything in the game. (There is a small secondary collection mechanic that does use a 'bit' of sluthing) It is a game of exploring an island, and dealing with the weird stuff that happens.   Story: So what you are left with is a mysterious island, and a story of a bunch of kids who have to deal with the mystery. Unfortunately the authors don't take long to explain the exact nature of the mystery so you are left to spend most of the game just trying to figure out how to make it stop. As I mentioned before, there is no puzzles, so figuring it out just means continuing to have conversations with the people around you until you click on the right glowing dot on the screen. The other 4 kids you are on the island with are all fairly honest real characters, unfortunately they are almost all pouting teens, or whining, or trying to hard to be funny. And much like the rest of the game, you cannot choose your time to interact with them. You can only respond to the various complaints or thoughts they have by being rude and interrupting with one of the 3 choices of dialog the game offers. But if you don't act fast the choices fade away and they continue to just talk at you. The most genuine and beautiful moments of conversation are when you are in flashbacks where you are seperated from the game you are playing, and use that same system to talk with your older brother. In those moments it feels like getting to talk to him is meaningful and a special gift. Sadly that feeling is only there for brief vignettes. Ultimately the game implies that your choices are meaningful, but the results don't hold up. And as this is the games core mechanic/experience the game really falls short.   Art & Enviroment: While the island is beautifully hand drawn painted artwork, and the character design is nicely stylized, and the visual FX are top notch, none of it really covers for the plodding slow way you move around the island to get to the next triggered event point. If you are lucky you will be in a section where your character uses a run animation to get somewhere, but you have no power over when that happens. Also due to the art style there is lots of ramps/stairs, and the character slows to half the already slow walk speed to move up or down them. This takes what is a beautiful interesting world, and makes you just want to have a fast travel option. The slowness seems deliberate as all conversations are real time, and thus you need lots of wandering to be able to listen to all the dialog the characters speak at you.  Mechanics & Game Design: As mentioned above this game has a lot of focus on the conversations, and the mechanical implementation of the system seems interesting, but its use in the game starts to wear out as nothing in the games progression is linked to those conversations. Ultimately you could play the entire game without ever responding to a single character and the vast majority of the experience would remain unchanged. The other major mechanic of the game is a handheld radio that you carry around. At first it feels special and seems to be a new tool not used in games before. But it quickly becomes a button to progress the story forward.   Radio Mechanic:  1. There is nothing to click on, but the scene seems really important. 2. Enable the radio, is it red? If yes go to next step, if no close radio and wander around more. 3. Spin radio dial until you feel the controller vibrate.  4. Adjust till controller vibrates a lot. 5. Watch doors unlock, machines turn on, weird stuff happen.   If you are intersted in this game please consider playing some of the other great games that are in this genre instead or as well as. These games are all much better versions of this type of atmospheric storytelling: Life is Strange Gone Home Firewatch Undertale

Took the first Watch Dogs, fixed (just about) every issue the game had, added some extra touches, changed the location, characters, and storyline. I genuinley had a great time playing through this game and I feel as if the few flaws that the game has are massivley outweighted by the pros.  A fun, engaging, interesting storyline (I should also mention that this game does not take itself seriously and the humor is genuinley funny.) in which you play as a newly recruited member of DedSec, A hacker group whos intention is to fight for the privacy and right of the people by taking down organizations which think otherwise. In fact, the whole game revolves around hacking and it opens up a lot of freedom and interesting options that don't go unnoticed. I personally LOVED the hacking and it truly showed the whole 'the world is connected' vibe that the devs were going for.  The characters you work along with are interesting, have unique traits about them, and are fun to play alongside with. I also want to add that who you play as yourself is one of the few main characters in video games that I actually WANTED to play as.  You play in San Fransisco, which the developers have recreated in a way that makes the world truley feel alive and dynamic. While there are a few minor flaws in the citizens and certain areas, it doesn't detract from the world you play in. (I may add that this game is GORGEOUS.) The hacking in the game is tied into the world perfectly and allows for you to truly feel how alive the game is.  The few issues lay in the combat and the bugs. The combat hasn't changed that much since the first Watch Dogs, which isn't a totally bad thing. The AI is great and exhillerating to fight against, the stealth, while being fairly linear, is great, but the gunplay is a little lacking and feels like it needs to be polished. I've also noticed a few bugs, which while not being game breaking, can be annoying/confusing. The biggest ones I've found is that restricted areas aren't always restricted (sometimes I show up to an area to steal money or research points and it's completely abandoned when othertimes it's swarming with enemies.) and random times someone will call 911 on me even though I haven't done a thing.  Despite the set backs, the game is still phenominal and I would totally recommend it for anyone looking for a new open world game.  I don't know,

app_name: STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight: Jedi Academy™
review_text: Pros: + For using a very dated engine, it looks pretty good + Interesting Story + Uses the original movie soundtrack + Decent amount of character customization + Missions vary in length, so they don't feel like you're doing the same mission again and again  Cons: - Being able to choose your missions is great on paper, but because only one of the 5 missions you get every section actually affects the story, they feel like a massive waste of time - Ridiculously dumb enemy AI - Force powers seem disappointingly weak - Usage of original movie soundtrack is iffy at best, some times it fits perfectly, other times, not so much - Shooter gameplay is rather bland - While the lightsaber customization is good, most of the bosses can be beaten by either swinging your saber around like a mad man or simply shooting them with the concussion rifle, which can render many of the special moves pointless - Being forced to upgrade powers in order to enter a mission is annoying  TL;DR: Despite getting some things right, it's decent at best. If you've got money to burn, then buy it. Otherwise, your not missing out on much.

I played Assassin's Creed II, III, and Black Flag before playing this and to some extent, I shouldn't have. Assassin's Creed is, I think, a worthy predecessor to the series, but there are some flaws. Combat and movement feels very clumsy at first, after playing for a bit it still is, but you kinda get used to it. The story is also very repetitive. After completing the first three assassinations, you get the idea of what to do. It just gets too repetitive. The visuals, however, are amazing. It really is a feast for the eyes. For a game released in 2007 (I think), the graphics are stunning. A bit low compared to modern games, but still impressive. If you can put up with a repetitive storyline and clunky controls, you'll probably enjoy this game. Despite these cons, I can honestly say that I enjoyed this game.

Visually, the game is absolutely gorgeous. The animations are smooth and elegant and it's obvious that a lot of time and effort was put into designing the game. The sounds the player character makes are very important in terms of gameplay, so it's a good thing the sound effects are excellent, as is the sound design overall. As I said before, the stealth mechanic is excellent, with multiple pathways that cater to every play style to get through each level. The player can use line of sight, distraction etc to great effect to either sneak past or silently dispatch enemies. As the player progresses through the story line, you begin to realise that you will be faced with a decision that must be made without knowing what to believe. The one qualm I have with this game is the puzzles and challenges are little too easy but this doesn't detract from the overall experience. 9/10, if you enjoy 2D platformers and/or stealth games you'll love Mark of the Ninja

review_text: Absolutely love the game. Not only does it offer an amazing story but the side quests are even more entertaining. When it comes to Fallout you dont play for an aspect of the game, you play for the whole masterpiece that it is. Whether it's just exploring for hours, going on quests, or just killing random things you'll be having a great time. I really can't say enough about it. I've never heard of someone who plays it and dislikes it. I first played and beat the game 3 years ago and still play to this day, there's always more to enjoy! I give it a 9/10 (I say 9 and not 10 for the constant bugs, but I promise you grow to love them). And don't even get me started on the hundreds of mods. Although it should be noted you'll have to do a lot of fooling around to get it working on Windows 7, otherwise you'll experience constant freezeing and crashing making it unplayable. It took me about an hour and 2 youtube videos to get it working personally. Still recommend to everyone I know in a heartbeart. Its second only to Fallout New Vegas Ultimate Edition.

My gosh did I have a good time with this game! The platforming in this game I will admit is quite simple, but enjoyable nonetheless. The game is presented in a 2-D format, and the gimmick is that you can flip the view of the world you inhabit 90 degrees at a time, but we'll talk about that later. The atmosphere alone got me spending quite a few hours on this game. Quite a bit more than I should have. You go through sweet grassy plains, to a graveyard, a village with another race of people that have really large heads, and sewers. Though, I never even got to the sewers, because you needed a key to acess it, and I was too lazy to backtrack to find a key.  The plot itself is bland, but the idea of it is that you are Gomez, a little pale boy from a village bias about the non-existence of cubes. That is until the town elder tells you in his incredibly broken English that you are about to embark on an adventure. Then, a floating yellow cube appears, and goes by the name of the Hexahedron. It starts talking in a weird language that is very hard to understand, but we'll go over that in a little while.   Once it is finished talking, he'll give you a magical fez to wear, which gives you the ability to flip the 3-D dimensions to view another side of your 2-D world. After you begin toying with flipping dimensions, the Hexahedron will start to shake in a jerky spasm. Flip the world enough times, and he'll shatter into a bunch of tiny shards. THe game will reboot as if your computer crashed, and your adventure begins to collect the 32 cubes that make up the Hexahedron, to stop Gomez's world from falling apart.  I have covered the majority of this games' positives, but really, you are probably reading this to see the faults. The first thing you'll notice after looking at your world map, is that there's a lot of space to cover on your map. By the time you are done the game, your map is going to look like a 3-D model of your computer's motherboard. And if navigating the map was too hard for you, you're not going to have a good time back-tracking. I know I didn't need to backtrack really, but if you neglect getting a lot of the cube pieces in a given area and moving onto another portion of the game, you are really going to regret it.  While were on the subject of backtracking, do you remember how I stated that Gomez's world is falling apart? I probably should have also mentioned that space is starting to rip. As you venture through the game, you'll notice these floating black holes that kill you if you touch them. Going forward, they're not a big issue. But backtracking? You might as well be climbing a mountain made of wet bar soap. I know that if you're lucky, they'll disappear if you re-enter the room, but my gosh, they are like trying to get away from that mean, annoying kid on the playground.  I know that even with the black holes, you can still progress with some challenge, but their existence is not a pleasant taste in your mouth.  The worst thing though, is the language. In order to complete some puzzles, you need to read a button order presented in tetronimos, but in order to understand the button order, you have to turn your head 90 Degrees to the left, which is not enjoyable in any aspect. Phil Fish, you're a great guy, but if yopu do change your mind and do end up creating Fez 2, (Which would be awesome.) make the language somewhat understandable! Some of the character dialogue is impossible to read, even with some kind of chart! Even though I had a good go with this game, you are not going to walk away from your computer with a smile on your face if are not patient. But if you can come out on the other side of the backtracking and black holes, I do believe you will eventually forget the agony, and stop to look at the beutiful scenery this game has in stor for you, or it's truly moving sounds. That's why, my final verdict for this game... is an 8 out of 10. I really do hope you check out this game and see how you do. and try to re-create the Hexahedron completely out of Anti-Cubes if you can plow through the flaws!  I recommend this game to players in-between casual and intermediate. Have a good playthrough!

Only recommended if you're a fan of the original game and want to see the it redone in a modern engine with more modern gameplay.  Impact isn't a bad game but there isn't a lot to truly recommend it. The gameplay is similar to the old Alien Breed with the addition of new weapons, enemies, and a 3d camera. Despite some of the enemies having ranged attacks or special abilities, the AI will charge you the majority of the time or amble about a short distance away not using their abilities. I'm not sure if this is any different on hard but that's how it is on normal. This also means the weapons, of which there is a decent variety, are ultimately just another way to dispatch the rushing bugs as nothing requires a change in tactics.   Similar to the original Dead Space, your character, the chief engineer, will shuffle fom waypoint to waypoint fixing everything that needs fixing and believe me a whole lot of ♥♥♥♥ is broken. This can get pretty tedious as the story is not advanced much in this first installment of the three episodes.  If you are planning on playing through all three episodes, be aware that your progress does not carry over from each game. Each episode is a separate executable and there is no way to import a save file to carry over your progress. This means at the start of episode 2, you have lost all your weapons, upgrades, and items acquired in episode 1. This feels very strange considering how quickly each episode picks up from the end of the last one.

If you are ready to get sucked into a classic isometric rpg then this game is for you.  It is very enjoyable recruiting the NPCs to join your party with their own personalities and goals instead of creating a whole party at the beginning. Character customization is pretty good and allows you to make your hero whatever you wish  Story: Pretty basic become a hero by slaying the bad guy main storyline but it is very entertaining and the extra info you learn about why what is happening is pretty exciting. You are in no way forced to continue the main story though and can explore all the surrounding lands to your hearts content and get tons of hours of gameplay without even progressing through the story.  Combat: I love tactical realtime with pause combat. You can play any battle without pausing but it is highly unlikely and you will not do well without pausing to queau your parties actions. Tougher battles must be thought through and you will cannot advance with just putting your head down and trying to beat down combatants  Customization: Already touched on this a bit but you are pretty free in making your hero what class and race with proficienies in wherever you want them. Just note that you should read into the classes and realize what you want to do with your character instead of creating what sounds good at the time and then realizing it is not right for you.  Overall: This is a must buy if you love classic isometric rpgs with a real time pause battle feature. Great open world that you can explore and get many hours without ever touching the main quest line, and when you do decide to pursue the main story you will not be dissappointed.

Wayforward's attempt to capture the Ducktales magic is a total success, bless me bagpipes!  Being born in the 80s, The Disney afternoon and shows like Ducktales were a big part of my childhood. I played a lot of the capcom Disney games back then, back during a time when a show/movie tie in game had an actual chance of being something good instead of the most cheaply made thing possible to sap money from kids who didn't know better. I played them all; Darkwing Duck, Chip n' Dale, and of course both Ducktales for the NES. These games manged to deliver great fun even when they didn't have a lot to work with. So when a remake was finally announced almost 25 years after the original, I was tense and wondering just how well it would be handled, thankfully my fears were for the most part, needless.  Remastered takes the settings of the original NES game, which its levels were all loosely based on actual Ducktales episodes, and fills in all that was missing from the NES age of limitations. The art is cartoonish and very beautiful. While the 3d on cell shading seems jarring at first, it ends up working very well to make you feel like you are playing in an actual episode. To add to this effect they have added an overall plot. It was hinted at there was an overarching plot in the original but its limitations really didn't let it say much, its now fully backed by dialogue and the insertion of a intro and final stage, the final stage being unique now instead of just revisiting Transylvania. The original 5 stages have been fleshed out to feel like you are playing 5 episodes of the show that are loosely connected into a mini arch like was a common thing to do in the show. Many of the cast makes their various appearances depending on the stage like the nephews, Gyro, Launchpad, Fentin and others, all voiced very well to deliver a great experience. The music in the game is faithful remasters of the original, the only one that is a tad weak is the Transylvania because it gets a bit dub-steppy but in all the music is excellent, and as an added bonus the original 8bit themes are unlockables, including 8bit versions of the new stages.  The only complaint with the game is not one I share but I will note it for those that it may bug. The voices are not by their original actors from the show. It is worth noting that while this bugs purists its not exactly fair to hold it against them as many of the original actors are actually dead or too old to call upon to get the voices. Don't let this minor issue hold an entire game away from you that's great, give this a go. And in a personal opinion, I hope to see more well done remakes like this, Darkwing Duck being at the top of my list with possibly the Ducktales 2 gameplay being next in line.

You could argue the original game set the standard for CRPGs back when pen & paper was the norm and most RPGs were text, static image based or just looking like Lego on a screen. Then sequal came and did what the first did, but so much bigger and so much better, over 5 glorious CD's of awesomeness.  The story continues where the first left off (mostly) and after a brief stint in jail, had you delving deeper into your heritage, catapulting your charater into the 'uncertain' future with an epic climax to rival most games today. Then when you thought it all was over... the expansion came and just added more epic to a final conclusion.  This Remastered edition leaves much of the original features with 2 new 'questlines' to explore, all with some much needed engine and UI updates (with some not so much needed overly highlighted avatars) and well worth the cash to play the pretty vast original story game (if you want to do *everything*, expect a good few weeks of play). BUT if you do find a cheap 2nd hand copy of this game with it's expansion, or even the 4 disc of both 1 and 2 and thier expansions (which would probably play much better on the current crop of OS's), you'd be a fool to pass them up.   In my not so unbiased opinion...

I've been playing this game in its various incarnations since it was PernAngband in the 1990s, then Troubles of MIddle Earth, and now finally Tales of Maj'Eyal - free from copyright infringement and better than ever!  It's truly exceptional, and these days, comes with so many lovely enhancements that Roguelikes have traditionally lacked. Mouse control, skill hotkeys, unlockables and stat tracking, and global chat with other playters, MMO-style, in the game window.  Underneath all this is a game system that is elegant, varied, and will keep you occupied for hours upon hours upon hours. Each character plays differently and has a host of interesting skill trees to work through. The dungeons are randomly generated, of course, but within some boundaries that ensure that each has its own flavour and unique style.  Death is permanent, as with all roguelikes (although there is another little helping hand in 'Adventure' mode which gives you a few extra lives to play with) but whenever I lose a character it's never a crushing blow - because now I get to enjoy starting over with a brand new one.  You can download this game for free from  te4.org  but it's well worth a fiver to support DarkGod and help him keep updating this brilliant title.

Okay so, in short, this game is bloody beautiful.  Following on from the first season, I immediately felt like Clementine had properly 'Grown up' from the second I had control of her. Telltale managed to create the perfect balance between having Clementine mature, while keeping the nature of her youth. While gameplay is very simple, the story is the main part of the game. Each episode runs for a perfect length and has you biting your nails in anticipation for the next. I must issue a warning that while the actual game itself is very easy, the choices you make throughout the game are not. literally every choice you make in this game is difficult. Wihtout giving anything away, some choices in this game truly are dreadfuly painful, leaving you with a sunken heart regardless of your decision. Everything in this game from the length, the characters, the art style and the music combine perfectly to orchestrate a dark, morally impactful masterpiece of a story. if you're looking for a game that will grip you from start to finish and captivate every emotion inside you, look no further than Telltale's 'The Walking Dead'

I never write reviews but the team at Turtle Rock has earned one.  Short Review:  Evolve has gone free to play and it feels like it should have been this way from the start. The game felt like a money grab back when it cost $60 plus all the DLC you had to get to truly experience what the game had to offer. Now it doesn't feel like such a money grab because if you're taking awhile to unlock content you know that you haven't spent any money on a few hours of enjoyable gameplay. The true determining factor of if bringing Evolve back to life and keeping it alive will be down to two things. The cost of purchasing silver keys with real money, and if they continue to add more game modes to change up the feel of the game from 'Find monster, kill monster.'.  Long Review:  When I played the beta back in 2014 I enjoyed the game but when it came out it seemed like the true price tag of the game was hundreds of dollars in order to get all the characters and content that was in DLC. DLC is fine but not when it vastly surpasses the cost of the original game and it feels like you get very little for the $60 you spent at the start. Now all that content can be earned, albeit pretty slowly. At the moment the more expensive characters take about 20 games to earn enough silver keys to buy. Silver keys can not be bought at the moment but when they do if they come at a reasonable cost ($1= 1,000-1,500 silver keys) then it's an enjoyable game without too much being behind a paywall.  The game market is a competetive one and offering only a few versions of one game mode is just not worth very much. If we were to compare this to a game like COD (purely based on content offered not your view of the franchise) then that offers a single player campaign, a multiplayer suite of game modes, and a horde mode/special ops missions. Evolve only offers the 4v1 game mode and at the moment that is only Hunt (though it previously had and will likley add, spinoffs to this hunt mode). In short a game that offers 4 sub sets of games and one that offers just one game type. Which would you pay $60 for?  I believe that's what Evolve faced last year. Throw on top of that the ridiculous DLC paywall, the hunter players requiring everyone to work well as a team, and you have the low player base Evolve ended up with. In the end what you have is a game either worth about $20 or as the team went a free to play game. Hopefully that decision will lead to people slowly spending a little money at a time for the content they want. Primal Carnage is a game which promises so much, yet fails to deliver. There are many issues with the game itself, I will list some here:  -Game would plain not run at all on my computer, seems to have something to do with AMD systems. Has been better now though. -Controls are very clunky/laggy, feels like im running around in circles when trying to hit humans with dinosaurs, espically raptors. -All dinosaurs, but mainly big ones suffer (for me atleast) from a terrible point of view, its very hard to eat humans as tyrants, this combined with control lag its a bad experience. -Servers are fairly empty, with ones only in easten USA, so trying to play down here in New Zealand with ping below 250 is impossible. This could explain control lag, but its still a issue. -Dinosaurs move around at Mach 1 it seems with in close combat, which again, combined with high ping is not fun at all. -Dinos have huge amounts of health, it takes fowever to kill things that are large.  -Humans feel generally very underpowered, this is ssen with the comunity as people wont join the human team, its not uncommon to find a server with 20 people on, but with 10 in spectator waiting for a spot in the dino team. This leads to more dinos than humans making balence problem worse. -Skins are contined to be added, even though major issue are yet to be fixed.  All of this, combined with the relitivly high price of $24 NZD its simply not worth purchasing. If you want dinosaur combat, go to ARK, its a better game a few weeks after release than this is after this whole remake and release.  'nough said

Yeah, it's goofy and good.  To elaborate: Victor Vran pretends it's an APRG in the vein of Diablo. In reality, it's more of an action game with a bunch of RPG elements. There's a lot of buffs and debuffs and damages and armors but most of it can be ignored during the slaughter. The key gameplay mechanics are dodging - something you'll be doing a lot of in the action game way, by rolling from projectiles, jumping over shockwaves and whatnot - and mastering weapons.  There's under twenty weapon types, but each handles differently (by having three attacks associated with it) and each has different mechanics associated with it - so with a shotgun you'll be timing shots and executing enemies, with a scythe you'll harvest souls of and use them to empower your moves and with a rapier you'll be counting your hits to get the maximum out of its combo-empowering abilities.  You can switch between two weapons on the fly and you aren't locked out of any by stat requirements - simply because there aren't any stats. You get passive boosts by equipping a set of cards with values associated with them and figuring out the most effective hand of cards you can use with your limited capacity is actually quite fun.  The emphasis on action gameplay is also further reflected in level design - the maps aren't randomly generated and there is a set of challenges on each one to complete for extra loot and experience; those range from 'accidently fulfilled' to 'good luck figuring out a build that can do this' in difficulty - and if that isn't enough, another set of (really rather ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ly) challenges for each map opens up on beating the final boss.   Graphics are nice enough, could use less spiders but that's a pretty common issue with RPG devs running out of ideas. I think this game actually suffered a bit in the story/presentation department by coming out at around the same time as Incredible Adventures of Van Helsing, with which it apparently shares a lot of style - hell, even logo/title screen with a steampunky hatted hunter looking in your general direction. I wouldn't call Victor Vran derivative, though; for one, his hat gets mercilessly mocked throughout the game.  Which brings me to the story and writing and what's actually tying all that murder together. The story is minimal - there's this cursed city and you're there to figure out what happened and destroy the ancient evils, blah blah skeletons vampires ghosts demons. The game is thankfully aware of how shallow this is and pokes fun at it quite a lot. Victor Vran doesn't take itself seriously - at all. Your constant companion is a snarky voice in your head that isn't ever afraid to deliver a low blow to your confidence - from the abovementioned hat mockery through pondering the meaning of mass murder for loot and experience to videogame references. The jokes are mostly rather memetic in substance and a lot of them fall flat but they're usually delivered fast enough to make at least some of them stick - my favorite is probably the Stanley Parable dungeon.   I don't think this game will ever become a beloved classic, but it's definitely a step above the typical point-and-kill ARPG in gameplay and gets a lot of points in my book for its admitted and embraced silliness. Definitely worth getting on a sale.

If you've not yet played this classic turn-based RPG, you've been missing out. Fallout and its sequel, Fallout 2 are outstandingly atmospheric games whose settings are highly developed. Made during a time when all RPGs had to have magic and swords, the developer bucked convention and gave us giant irradiated mutant insects, hulking supersoldiers poised to either conquer what's left of the world or preserve its past with future and ancient technology coming together to form the seeds of civilization in a desert wasteland ravaged by a nuclear war long since lost.  I love this game, it's awesome.  Those of you not used to older graphics and a sometimes slow-paced combat system and are unwilling to adapt: this may not be for you, but I hope that it is.

My little brother decided to buy me this game. Twenty-five cents was all it costed, and he bought it because he has no impulse control.  I decided 'what the hell' and decided to take a look at the game. My very first words were 'what do i do' followed by 'PURPLE SPACE STRIPES WHAT THE HELL'. And thus began my descent into madness. It began simple; fly to the portal and avoid the majestic purple space stripes. It was a simpler time. But things began to pick up, as I realized three things.  1. You can shoot purple space asterisks out of your face. 2. You shoot white space asterisks out of your butt for propulsion.  I failed to achieve thought number three, as I was dumbfounded by exactly what the hell my ship was supposed to be; an enigmatic green mountain with snow against the fluorescent stripes directly from the depths of hell. Eventually, the endlessly looping music gave me a new question; why do I have no hitbox? The purple space stripes are fickle mistresses; sometimes, you could fly deep into their embrace; other times, they'd simply tear my tiny little space mountain into tiny space mountain shreds.  I then proceeded to punch my little brother in the face, and continued my adventure. All I could see now was planes of darkness, travelling across the purple space stripes. Travellers against the legions of hell. And as I flew, my little brother called the 'space portal' a purple space hexagon, causing my final drop. I fell so far, that I pierced straight through hell, and found myself in the abyss.  The abyss of purple space stripes. That's about the time when, for the safety of my sanity and the entire universe at large, pulled the plug on my computer and lobbed it out a window.  perfect 5 / 7, watch out for space asterisks and you'll be okay

To put it simply... This game is AWESOME! And I usually don't tell things that bluntly in my reviews, but I can't help thinking like that. I wasn't into the base concept of the first game at the beginning, though I liked how the events did unfold. But this sequel - is so much fun and emotions. It's a lot less silly than the first one, since the situation is... well, pretty bad actually. The game is intense and full of choices and endings, the music is as cool as in the first one, and the art is a lot better (it's actually really nice!). And well, we still have (even more) choices that impact events unrelated with the romance and ending, that will shape other aspects of the story, wich is always cool in a game like this one - you're not ONLY focusing in the love story, you also have other important decisions to do.  I've already told it in my review for the first game, but I did only one character's route, and the route was awesome enough for preventing me from trying the others. I'm no completionnist, and the route I did was satisfying and perfect enough to be my only 'canon'. The question is, should you get TW Rome alexander? I say yes.  What you get is a fast paced sort of narrated campaign as alexander vs the persians and india. You will have few but elite troops vs ever growing large armies and a short time span to conquer everything.  There is also a few challenging historical battles to win and some mods that require alexander. These mods expand upon the somewhat lacking unit roster of the vanilla game. I really reccomend diadochi TW ( successor wars) mod. IF you dont know if you should get it or not, if you are a fan of alexander then get it, if not then get it for the mods.  So here are pros/cons  Pros + play as alexander/macedonians vs large persian armies. + great music. + good modding community. + nice historical battles. + new units are cool.  cons - few factions - only 1 campaign - campaign map is a bit small. - you cant play as philip II of macedon. - few skirmish battle factions.

So far I've only played episode 1, and the optimization is just as bad as everyone says it is. I had to play at 720p because the game was crashing at 1080. Even then, the games performance is pretty terrible, plus there are plenty of graphical bugs that need to get sorted out. The poor optimization could have been worked around if there was a proper options menu, but the only options are texture quality, AA on or off, and VSync on or off; that's it.  Besides that, Tell Tale delivers the quality of story we've all come to expect: nothing short of riveting. At the games current state, I recommend waiting till all five episodes get released. Maybe they'll sort out the bugs and performance issues promptly afterwards. However, the game is ultimately a must own.

As somone who has played their fair share of other MOBAs I feel comfortable saying that DOTA is fantastic. Although I am mostly a League of Legends player and will probably remain that way (for the time being) Dota is a breath of fresh air. Dota 2 currently is unrivaled in it's pure depth and challenge in the MOBA genre, for me at least that is where the game shines. On top of all of this EVERY HERO IS FREE!!! How awesome is that?! While I will probably never play Dota as my main game I will still whole heartedly enjoy a refreshing match every once in a while. Arguments could be made to call Portal 2 the best game ever. The original Portal was a great game. It combined the feel of a first-person shooter with puzzles. The concept is so simple it's genius. You shoot one portal here, shoot another there, and you can walk between the two. Use that to solve the puzzles presented to you. I don't want to spoil much, but Portal 2 provides a few more features than just being able to create Portals. It provides gooey gels that allow you to run faster, jump higher, and shoot portals where they otherwise could not be shot. It also has some anti-gravity elements.   My goal at playing Portal 2 was to beat it without having to check a walkthrough once. While I did get stuck a time or two. Some heavy thought got me out of it. Sometimes you make the puzzles much harder than the should be, when in reality, the answer is literally right in front of you.  This game has story, graphics, gameplay, and it will make you laugh your balls off. Definitely play the original Portal first.  10/10

Fun game. Enemy Within is better. EU is pretty difficult, but not for good reasons. RNG will frustrate you. Imagine in The Matrix, when Trinity put her gun to the Agent's head and said 'dodge this', and then he DOES dodge it, then shoots Morpheus from half a mile away, then Neo panics and jumps off the roof. It's tough going if you don't save often (sorry, I do save a lot, and I do reload when my favorite guys get killed due to cheap nonsense). Some people just play the game straight through and don't care if all of their troops get slaughtered, but that's not me. Anyhow, the RNG and other cheapness does die down as you level up and get better gear. It does feel like your armor/weapons/upgrade paths all pretty much lead to the same place though. It's not very varied, and you'll pretty much just end up with the same guys on each mission, although you might swap out an extra sniper for a heavy or something. If this game had 10x the weapon selections and tech paths to allow more customization, it would be much more interesting.  Enemy Within fixes many problems with this game, and improves on it.

TL;DR Version: Great game, great AI, great story and great gunplay. A must buy for FPS fans and even tactical shooter fans. 10/10  One of the best FPS games I know of. Great AI, interesting story and fantastic John Woo-esque and tactical gunplay. The game has a unique combat system which I wish today's developers would take a note or two of, the game puts you up against squads of 5 soldiers which communicate with each other and co-ordinate to try to take you down, which in higher difficulties can make the game genuinely difficult because the AI are extremely intelligent. As an example of such, during my playthrough I had to resort to taking cover in a room with open windows in which my enemies could see me through, instead of charging me head first like most modern FPS games AI would do, they actually had 3 of the 5 soldiers laying down suppressing fire and trying to flush me out with grenades, while the other 2 were trying to flank me, at the end of the gun fight I was flanked and killed due to the fact that I had no idea that the enemy were flanking my position. As a result of that gun fight I laughed my ♥♥♥ off because I hadn't been outsmarted in sooo long by AI and it was such a enjoyable experience. In my books F.E.A.R has the best AI I know of and still holds it's own even after 11 years, F.E.A.R is truly a masterpiece and I wish there were more games like it. The story of F.E.A.R is quite interesting but it is true that at times it can feel very drawn out but the visceral gun fights and atmospheric horror keeps you drawn in. It's a game that doesn't rely on jumpscares and forced perspective views to scare the player and I will admit that in my first playthrough I had some heart pumping moments which I won't spoil. All in all F.E.A.R is a great game to grab and for $9.99 USD it is a great deal, even for people that aren't fans of horror, I believe this is a game that every gamer should experience and it will forever be one of my top 5 FPS games and I also inspire to take what F.E.A.R has done right with it's combat and atmosphere and down the line create a homage of what F.E.A.R was in a game I hope to create in the far future. 10/10

After 645 hours on record, all jobs completed on the highest difficulty (on loud), and thorough experience with both this game and its predecessor I would absolutely love to recommend this game; however, after 2 years I no longer can. The company in charge of this game: Overkill Software, has been on a declining slope showing nothing but contempt and money-grubbing for the playerbase that has gotten them to such largescale sales in the first place. With continual lackluster updates and DLC being made, lack of decent communication with the community, and complete boldface lying to the community with promises of no microtransactions year earlier only to be added 2 years later it is clear that the only thing that matter anymore with this game is how much Overkill can profit off of it. Quality control has been completely lost, the heists have become lazier, uninteresting, and flat out boring as time went on after the first Crimefest, and nothing has been done about it. Infamy 2.0 was the last real update that many veteran players were looking forward to, but the rewards ended up being completely useless cosmetic masks that no one asked for. Not only that, but with 'a year of community input' going into that update, it was pathetic. Huge community backlash followed, and Overkill said they would address it, 6 months later there is still nothing. And now here we are in Crimefest, where all the rewards from banding together as a community and completing arbritrary goals to gain FREE content gets us microtransaction pay-to-win CS:GO skinned rifles Day 1. I cannot think of a bigger middle finger slap to the community face than that, it even surpasses Hotline Miami 2's ending. It's clear this game is going down the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, and anyone who has a lot of time put into it will tell you that. Overkill does not care about their older players, they don't care about their players at all, the only thing they want is money and they're going to use money-hungry schemes to sucker in new players who will inevitably get bored with the game and leave. So should you buy it? Do you want a game that you'll get bored of in about a week? Don't spend any money on this game. Supporting it will only send the video game industry into more of a decline its been in for a while now.

Wow.  If you have a system that normally runs good with the more recent games (I get usually anywhere from 30-60 with even the best games), this game is really worth it, imho. I don't have it on the highest settings, but fairly high (I have 2G video, so need to put textures to medium, but everything else high or ultra) and I get 40-60 fps, which is good to me (30+ is more than enough to me). But even if the graphics are on a lower setting, I gotta say this game is worth it.  I'm usually picky about games, and have a hard time getting into them or finishing them :P Although I only started this one, the gameplay (simliar to Assassin's Creed titles in style of play, but still different in many respects) is fun. The story and setting, to me, is really well done. I think it helps that I like this style of game more (I've read all the Tolkien books at least twice), but wow, I'm really liking it so far.  I was lucky to get it on sale, and appreciate it cause I can't normally spend full price on games these days, but even at a higher price, if money doesn't concern you as much, it really is worth it.  idk, obviously not everyone will agree with me, but I find this game really a gem.  Only an hour played so far, but at this time, I give it a solid 9.9 / 10.  Blessings  EDIT: Well, I'm well into the game now (25 hours or so), and I had no idea there was a new area, I was grinding the first area... well, cause it's fun :P It's cool that I'm getting to see all new scenery.  When I bought the game, I was really uncertain about it. But now that I've gotten fairly far in the story (about half way), I'm really impressed. The graphics are amazing, the story is interesting, and the voice acting is really well done.   Yes, the game can be difficult and takes practice, but as you upgrade your abilities and such, and really learn to use them, the game actually becomes somewhat easy. But even so, I get rough moments, and there are so many moves, you can switch it up here and there which makes it fun.   The bosses (captains and warchiefs) each have a 'personality' that you see very briefly which makes it story-like (they introduce themselves before a battle), and their strengths / weaknesses makes it a fun challenge for the battle... not counting other mobs that get in the way. It takes patience, but wow, it's worth it. This game is a gem.  I'm a pretty big Lord of the Rings fan, especially the books. I loved the movies and all, but the game impressed me more. Of course, not everyone will agree with my review, but that's ok. :P  I only write reviews for games I really like... well, this is a definate 10/10 for me.

Today I found myself sitting at my computer, bored senseless, wondering what to do. Then I see this game's banner appear on my steam store page. It intrigued me, so I decided to watch the trailer. Needless to say the game looked interesting, and I decided to proceed to purchase it.  From the minute I opened it up and from the minute I finished my playthrough, I was completely immersed and hooked into this interesting story that developed around your decisions. I have not played many games like this, but to me it seemed like an original idea, and not some kind of sims clone.  The art in the game is a nice touch as well. Its sort of Borderlands-y which really attracted my attention since that cell shaded art is a kind I really enjoy. ------Spoilers may lurk below.------ The story mainly consists of acts in which there are around 9. These acts each have 3 destinctive endings which you as the main character find out infomation about them and then decide wether to go with the Father's, Mother's or Son's choice.  However, it doesn't just stop there, these acts intertwine with each other and change the story as they go. For instance, during one playthrough I decided that I wanted the son's outcome and told the Father to fix his toy car, since I had been slightly neglecting him throughout the recent acts. After this had happened, the car remained fixed through the duration of the rest of the game. However, during another playthrough, I found myself in the same dilema, and decided against fixing the car. Throughout the rest of that game, the car remained unfixed. I thought this was a really nice portrayal of how these decisions affect things in the long run, even if it is only for small things such as a background prop. It gives the story a little bit more dpeths and the idea that your decisions do actually matter in the long run.  Another interesting idea that was given throughout the game was the idea of compromise. This was when you find out another family member's wants, and decide to compromise it with another family want. E.G The father wants to work on his novel some more, but his wife wants to spend time with him. The compromise you can select is where the father works on this novel and the next night they share a small but important drink together to help them stay connected. I thought this was a really good idea since it seemed to reflect real life a bit more, giving that idea of realism. However, you can only compromise with two characters, leaving a third disappointed.  During my first playthrough and more memorable playtrhough of this game, Dan got the novel finished, accepted the job in the Uni and won an award for his book. Linda developed a stronger bond with Dan, and although she didn't go to Arts of all, she didn't give up on painting and lived a generally happy life together. Tommy was my downfall, where his decisions did not seem that important to me as he was still quite young, he became isolated and was bullied. He then found himself bouncing from dead-end jobs constantly around his 20s.  Saying this game is difficult would be an overstatement. It's not. But the choices are where the difficulty meter kicks in. At the end of the day your decisions help affect this families future, and by doing so the consequences are also yours. It's whether you get so inbedded with the game that you begin to feel sympathy with them. This isn't the same for all people though. The game does also have its downsides, such as the 'space bar does everything' rule. It doesn't, of course, but a majority of the buttons require this space bar, and sometimes things can become jumbled. For instance, during one scene a character was so close to a light that it was very difficult to click on her rather than the light, which was annoying since I was playing on Stealth. Another thing is that sometimes when you click on a light, your game will begin to have some sort of mini-seizure, cause your screen to go black and then re-open itself. It doesn't close but it does get annoying to deal with if it does happen. The bottom line to this review is if you like this type of game. If you are into more story driven, stealth based games then this is down your ally indefinitely, so more RPG based. However, if you arn't into that kind of thing, and prefer FPS and action based games, I wouldn't buy this, you would get easily bored.

The original Mass Effect isn't very fun. The lore and universe it sets up are amazing, but your inventory fills up so fast that micromanaging it becomes necessary at annoying times, sometimes it feels like you pick up more junk items here than in Diablo, the romance options were pretty lame, the combat was just lame, vehicular segments were the worst (climbing up tall hills/mountains at -just- the right angle to try to get over them, copy/pasted buildings on various planets with very slight differences to their arrangement of furniture and such, side quest overload at the Citadel with almost no side quests anywhere else in the galaxy, and...yeah, it just wasn't very good. I'm glad it happened, though, because it meant Mass Effect 2 happened, and boy did I have a good time with it. Oh, and the ending song on Mass Effect 1 is totally great in my book. <3  Mass Effect 2 doesn't do much for the lore, in all honesty. You get a little more info on mercenary groups and an organization called Cerberus, and that's about it. Mass Effect 2 is all about gameplay, and it fixes everything I complained about above. Those things are either gone or made better so that you have a good time. Far more romance options with more interesting characters, all fresh maps, the vehicular stuff in DLC is easy to maneuver around in, side quests are all over the place instead of conglomerating 75% or so of them into one map you pick them up at, the combat lets you use special abilities more often while still balancing it out and making it so much more fun with shields, barriers, and armor on the enemies, and instead of mountains of useless items, you pick up a gun, and it's added to your loadout you can choose from on a mission. That's it. No tiny incremental stat gear like in Mass Effect 1 where you waste your time dealing with paltry differences in gear.  Mass Effect 2 is head and shoulders above Mass Effect 1, and it is the best game in the trilogy for me. Mass Effect 3's ending was a travesty, and though it had some solid gameplay, I still enjoy 2's better somehow.

I think it's about time for a fair review of this game. Let me start by saying that I almost agree with the way it is marketed as a F2P. I would recommend that the game should be put out as a FREE DEMO. That would at least stop all these childish 'Reviews' done by kids who don't have any money to support the developers. Within the first 10 minutes of exploring this game you can tell that a lot of people put a lot of hard work into the amazing details of the enviornment. Even the details in the audio are spectacular. The gunshots sound LOUD and realistic.  You start off with a handheld device that maps your hike and allows you to identify various animal trackings such as hoof/paw prints and droppings as well as audio callings. You can track one animal at a time by finding these track domes throughout the wilderness. Once you started tracking one of these animals you will follow a brighter traking dome trail with the freedom to switch trails whenever you wish. A lot of times, while you're tracking a mule deer, you will hear a different deer calling as you get closer to them and you have the option to switch tracks and start tracking this closer deer. That is a nice feature to the hunt, because I have spent hours tracking a single deer, determined to get this specific deer only to find that I've made a mile long circle through the woods. That can happen in real life though, so I'm not too frustrated with it.   My advice is, check it out and track a couple mule deer. You may really like the layout of the game. If you do, great, find an upgrade package that works for you or just explore the vast wilderness and keep tracking mule deer for free, or track other animals and take pictures of them instead of killing them. I wouldn't mind paying $50 to play this massive game for a single year. If you try the game and don't like it, what did it cost you besides some time and hard drive space? Chances are you will enjoy this game if you're really looking for a good hunting game. Just look at it as a DEMO, not a F2P.

A truly worthy successor to the SimCity crown. Fully engrossing city building on par with SimCity 4: Rush Hour. Plan out zones, city districts, bus routes, taxes etc. Everything you could ever want in a city sim. There are no mayor's advisors like SC4 though (some may find them charming, I had a little less sunny disposition to them). Instead you'll have little helpful popup balloons if something requires your direct attention. You can otherwise tell what your citizens need by little speech bubbles above their houses. Laying down zones is functionally different from SimCity also. You need to manually lay down the road instead of clicking and dragging with the zoning tool and it creating road for you. Not a big difference to me, I kind of like the complete control it gives you. First time I started it up I was so engrossed in the experience that the first time I looked at the clock 3 hours had passed without me noticing.

Borderlands the Pre-Sequel: a great addition to the Borderlands series. After beating the game, I discovered how much fun I had exploring, fighting, and looting my way through Elpis, the moon that the story takes place on. As usual in the Borderlands series, there were halarious characters and exciting easter eggs to be discovered. The main plot is about 4 mercanaries named Athena the Gladiator, Wilhelm the Enforcer, Nisha the Lawbringer, and Claptrap as the Fragtrap. All with unique personalites, each character that you can play as took a job by the contractor of Handsome Jack, a Hyperion worker who is significant in the storyline of Borderlands. Although Borderlands the Pre-Sequel's graphics were about the same as Borderlands 2, I did not find it bothersome, and the colors in the worlds I traveled in were rich and pleasing. New weapons, including cryo weapons (ice-like element) and laser weapons, were fun to use. Adding the 'zero-G' factor into the game really added a twist into the combat of original Borderland titles. One unique element of zero-G is the butt slam, a jump-crouch-slam technique that can throw off enemies allowing time to heal or recover from something. Multiple vehicles can be driven in the Pre-Sequel, including the standard buggy, now know as the moon buggy, and the Stingray which is a floating vehicle that also can butt slam in its own way. The story line was filled with infamous boss fights and daring challenges that kept me, the player, really wanting to keep going and strive for the best loot and inevitably completing the game. Here is the summary described as pros and cons:  Pros: 1. fun loot 2. great sories 3. new characters that add a funny yet satisfying addition into the game 4. epic boss fights 5. Zero-G (duh) and the butt-slam 6. rich color schemes 7. The Grinder, a new machine to combine weapons to make new ones!  Cons:  1. Anyone who hasn't played a Borderlands game in the might feel lost in the story line 2. side missions led you to the same places, and I wish they added a few more places to explore 3. graphics were about the same, but it did not bother me 4. a little more heads/skins for characters, but that can easily be added in  Overall, I did NOT feel that the game was rushed, like some people may say. A great 3rd game into the Borderlands series. I think they did a great job with the story and missions to experience and explore. Rating: 9.6/10 People New to Borderlands: 7.9/10

Basic idea of this 2D Arcade Top-Down Shooter, is, you run from baddies & shoot at them for as long as you can, then, they eat you alive.  You have good arsenal of weaponry at you disposal. Different mods of firearms, flamethrowers, plasma-ion-gauss toys, rocket launchers and some other weirdness. Most of them are quite playable and offer unique experience, but, some just really bad and get you killed very quickly :)  Enemies are not too diverse in form and even less then that in essence, but hey, they come in different colors !!! Nevertheless, while number of enemies will usually be your biggest concern, towards some of them you'll develop quite special feelings ...  And also there are powerups, popping-up all over the place. Not too important at first, but later, picking them up in right order (or just being lucky) is pretty much the only way to finish a campaign level.  Oh yeah, different play modes, campaign, survival, co-op and some other joyful ways to experience inevitable death ... Survival is arguably most interesting one, though, honestly, I've enjoyed it more in original game. Now, 6 to 7 perks per game is not too much to go by.  There isn't much to say about graphics or sounds (thanks god days when those were important are gone), but overall gameplay is awesome, game is addictive as hell !!!  Enjoy, don't forget to order a T-shirt ;)

In my opinion it is one of the best games releases in the last five years, at the very least. Certainly the best game of this years so far, and easily one of the best RPGs I have ever played.   It has a HUGE, living world. Great combat system. Deep and rich story not only during the main campaign, but also the secondary missions and side quests.   Graphics are astonishing, the quality of the characters expressions is exquisite and unique.  The world is pretty interactive when it matters and it also bring the franchise trademark, which is an adult, mature storytelling, filled with morally dubious and ethics intriguing decisions to make, which also have an impact over the story and how the world responds to the players decisions.  The only con I would highlight is the game 'concession' to consoles, which makes it inventory and interface managing a little bit outdated and arkward on the pc. ProjektRed could habe built a more pc-friendly interface to take advantage of all the accessibility a keyboard and mouse can provide.  Apart from that, a flawless experience.

After seeing trailers and previews for this title, I knew I had to have it. It satisfied my need of nostalgia, and made me want to play a galactic dictator (or monetary star baron) all the time. The initial rush was great, and I enjoyed playing this title.  Until the release of the Antarans.  Sure, give us races that we played with in Master of Orion 2, that was great. Sure, give us the Antarans, they could be annoying but controllable. But with this? I have one planet, with a star base and a missile battery. And what happens?  Defeated. Repeatedly. Doesn't matter if I try to rush with colony ships, they get blasted to bits by this overpowered race of roaches. And they always pick on me, even with three other races out there to butt heads with. And it doesn't help that I only have one colony while everyone else cranks out colonies like there's no tomorrow. Even on Very Easy, I get stomped with no relief. Best case scenario is that I ally with a race like the Humans; instead, I end up with races like the Silicoid or Bulrathi, both expansionist and warlike, and not keen on sharing space.  Oh, and let's not forget random events that seem to severely hamper me when least expected. Even though the previous Master of Orion titles had those annoying mishaps occur, I could reload a save game before all that happened, and I'd manage to breeze through that turn with no harm done. Here, it seems like the events are already fixed, and WILL happen to you no matter what you do. Even choosing the Lucky trait didn't give me breathing room from, you guessed it, the ANTARANS!  Frankly, I'm disappointed with how this game has turned out in the long run. Sure, I can turn off the random events and Antarans, but what fun is that if I did? I enjoy the game, but these new updates are killing off my joy for it.  So until then, I'm just going to let it gather dust until they either reduce how OP'd the Antarans are, or at least beef up my defense.

If you're craving a God game that is a spiritual successor to the 1980s hit 'Populas', then Reprisal Universe is what you're looking for. First, and most importantly, the programmer is actively monitoring for feedback and tweaking the game to improve the overall experience. Secondly, it provides interesting innovations that warrant grabbing this instead of just going back and playing the original Populas game. Third, and most important, it's not by Peter Molyneux. (That may seem like the least important element, but go give Godus a look over and really dig into the commitment and how many of those commitments have been broken-- then come back and compare against this game where the developer is actively engaged with his community. The difference is night and day.)  The game ramps up steadily in difficulty. (plus) The game is at its core a God game. (plus) The AI is not overwhemling in any map, yet offers a decent challenge in the latter maps. (plus) This was not always the case (in the initial release, the AI over utilized one of its free powers resulting in an unplayable game-- but the dev fixed this in the first week of player feedback.) It's inexpensive (plus) It has a lot of campaign content (30 maps, plus.) It has an even larger number of 'Universe' maps (not sure about the total, plus)  Some of the negatives: This is a retro pixel game (negative for some, not for me) You have very limtied control over your populants/peeps (negative for some, not for me) No music (but new sounds that go with the game play, and you can always use the Steam Beta Music Player to play the original Reprisal Soundtrack, if you like. Negative for some, not for me.) Scrolling is rough (keyboard tapping required, or a newly introduce mouse scroll which still needs some work. This is a negative but the dev is working to improve the overall experience so a plus for me.) Map visibility is limited (this is a negative, but it is an homage to the retro Populas UI. There are some differences. Some find this frustrating. I can let it go.)  The only item that is a real negative for me, the author has committed to working on a fix. (The most likely is center-mouse-button-click-and-drag to move the camera around.)  So I don't really have any problems with the game. I'd give it a 8.5/10 personally. And, I expect further development will be done to address remaining issues.  If you're looking for the spiritual successor to Populas, or just looking for a God game to scratch that itch... Buy this, it'll be a number of hours of retro fun. (If retro is not your thing, give it a pass... but I'm telling you, you're missing out.)

To be honest the story in this game didn't impress me at first. But the more I played the more amazed I was. Developers did well to take a lot from various fairy tales (which you probably heard in your childhood) and put it together to create a story of a girl who just wanted to help her grandfather. So after some time I just wanted to keep playing to know how the story ends. And it ended as fairy tale should end - with a pretty good moral. This is a kind of story I would like to tell my children to teach them something important. For me playing this game was like reading a great book that you wouldn't stop reading until you finish it and when you finally close it you just hold it in your hands with your eyes closed because you need a moment to rejoice.

I can't help but be reminded by the fantasy film 'The red balloon' by Albert Lamorisse again with a boy being the protagonist however following around his red ballon with a life of itself.   I think there may be some complaints about the amount of input involved in this game and therefore may be targeted for the 'this is not a game' arguments. Maybe it is better titled an interactive story, either way this is an unecessary debate, you simply need to experience this work of art because it is exceptional.

Maybe the best way to describe Shutter is to ask you to imagine a chimpanzee relating the plot of The Exorcist to you via frantic ape sign language. You vaguely understand that it's probably a great movie with a tense atmosphere but the chimp's medium of storytelling and your lack of total comprehension hinders the experience. Shutter has a very decent central concept and the beginnings of a classic ghost tale running through it but it is hindered by being an hour long, uglier than a squashed frog and possessing no good reason at all for having fixed cameras as a primary view. The gameplay is pedantically dull, mainly consisting of you rolling around taking pictures of floating objects and uploading them to your technical support, Barry, who constantly accuses your photos of being clever fakes or software glitches.  There are a couple of nice ideas here and, if you're a horror fan, I found it worth the playthrough for the surprisingly competent epilogue chapter but wait until the price crashes down. You can check out the fun I had with it below.  https://youtu.be/JlYbKXNTzRk

Do NOT let any of the glowing reviews fool you. This game is STRICTLY P2W. Oh, they try to lure you in, lul your suspicions by having an intersting class system and the first 15 or so battles be easy. But then you learn by battle 10 there is no strategy. Combos only exist as a side note to the main point. Rush.   Agro rush. There is no other point to this game. Chuck your strategy book out the window, this is a game of raw math. Do I have bigger numbers than my opponet.   And when I say P2W I am not kidding. Once you start seeing how ungodly powerful rare and above cards are compared to what you can normally get? Yea. If you dont have rare or better cards you may as well just ram your head into a wall for a good 20 minutes, it will be more rewarding. At least youll have a concussion and wont feel insulted for wasting your time.   TL:DR : Game claims to be a MtG clone + extra. Is really P2W worse than MtG, almost 3x over. Do not waste your time.
