I went in thinking 'This is exactly what I was looking for in a stealth game, even though I never did thought about that'. A Rogue-like stealth game, it just makes so much sense and it's great!... on paper.  The problem is, this game falls short. A great idea that is, in my opinion, just not polished enough.  You start with a throwing knife and a gun loaded with 5 bullets. The aiming and hit-scan is wonky, sometime I aim properly and miss my target where sometimes I just run and gun to get headshot after headshot. And the throwing knife... the problem with it is that it's a THROWING knife. I have a hard time thinking that a stealthy person on a mission would throw away its only knife and then be weaponless.  Also the guards will sometime see you from afar and yet, sometime you could be near enough to whisper insults in their ears and be unseen. Oh, and when they see you, they will shoot. Either you get the 1 shot-kill treatment or the '80's movie vilain' treatment; 6 shoots and still be alive.  The 2 powers that you have, see thru wall and slow time, are nice but the X-ray vision is hard to get used to. There is no cooldown on the screen to tell you when to use it again and it shows you a cone to display the enemy sighting range... or so I think? Sometime the cone go thru the wall and it gets confusing fast.  And this is just a pet peeve of mine but the menus and options are not easy to navigate. Pressing ESC to quit is not enabled, the screen resolution options makes the UI moves left or right if you transition it. I'm fetching far, I know, but everything adds up in the end.  Dev, you have an excellent premise for the game, just need a bit more polishing   I love it and will keep on playing but... the game is hard mostly because of problems here and there. I personally love a game that is hard by implementation and design (Dark Soul series) and not because of 'bad' design for lack of better term.   TL;DR: Great idea but bugs and rough edges makes it hard.

Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas (GTA:SA) is the third and final (unless you count Liberty City Stories and Vice City Stories) in the GTA III era. The game is the prefect endcap to an excellent series.  In the game it's the early 90s and you play as ex-gang banger CJ who, in order to get out of the crime ridden life of his youth, left Los Santos (Los Angeles) and moved to Liberty City (New York) and has been gone for a number of years. When he finds out his mother has been killed he reluctantly comes back to Los Santos for her funeral. It is here where he is dragged back into the old gang banger life style. It is also here where you follow his story.  Of all the GTA III games I had a hard time in choosing my favorite. Especially between GTA:SA and Vice City. In Vice City I loved (LOVED) the 80s theme and music and the 80s gangster, movie and pop culture references. That being said I totally digged the early 90s stuff too. But, more importantly, I loved how big the play area was. You didn't get one city you got three. And one of the three was a version of Las Vegas (Las Venturas) which, in real life, is one of my favorite cities to visit. So much to do. Also the controller mechanics in this game was a lot better than GTA Vice City. It is hard to go back and enjoy that game after playing San Andreas for a while.  Late game I found myself concentrating less on missions and more on stealing cars, planes or whatever and just 'driving' around the game world. This isn't bad but it sure made me wish the game world could even had been a bit larger and had more to do. Looking at things today it looks as if GTA V will have the more things to do thing down pat.  This isn't to say the game was perfect. There were some flaws for me but it was mainly due to limitations of the game and hardware that it ran on. For me the game would have been improved if you could go inside more buildings. There were a handful of casinos to go and gamble in but I wanted more. Also, throw in a club or two just too hang out in or dance. Make the properties you earned a little more detailed so you could do more things in them. Also, more things to do in the world that aren't mission related. Mostly stuff like that.  All in all I really enjoyed this game and recommend it to anybody who enjoys the sandbox style game and a big world to explore.

I love puzzle games and I've played a huge number. I consider myself pretty good at them but by no means an egg-headed expert boffin. I'd read lots of reviews for ECT, all talking about extreme difficulty, so with a bead of nervous sweat trickling down my neck I gulped, reached out a trembling hand and installed...  Each level is a 3D structure which you can rotate to view from all angles like a CAD model. You control a flat square which flips over and over to move around the grid of the puzzle. The early part of the game is mostly Sokoban based puzzles where you push balls and cubes towards exit squares or off the edge of the grid but before long you enter new worlds where the rules fly off at a tangent in totally unexpected ways.   Variety
Very satisfying and beautiful experience.

Probably the best 2D stealth platformer there is up to this date. There has been no games that masterfully implements gameplay aesthetics and stealth mechanics like Mark of the Ninja does. Sneak your way to victory with the help of multiple distraction devices (noise maker, smoke bomb, distraction flare, etc) to go right through fortified compounds. Use lethal ninja techniques and weaponry such as the hisomu terror dart, spike mine, ravenous insects, and more to take down specialized foes. Different foes requires different take down approaches so be creative. I had plenty of pleasure trying out the different methods to brutally assassinate my target! I could drop a noise maker to distract my victim and place down a spike mine where they are not looking, then grin wickedly as I watch them walk to their death!! I may also terrorize my victim with the hisomu terror dart and shoot a distraction flare at another guard to make the terrorized guard blindly shoot at his own comrade!! You can also be different ninjas who have different abilities, adding replayability. Like I mentioned before, there is no better game that simulates a stealthy ninja experience than Mark of the Ninja, so consider this a purchase.

I highly recommend anybody thinking of purchasing this game save your money. The community is tiny, most of the servers always empty, and there is good reason for it. Every server with a population is overrun by toxic players and enabling 'admins', if you can call some of the kids they put in charge administrators. Admins enable their friends griefing, some of them grief themselves. The vote kick/ban system has no memory, so the toxic players just come back the next day even if they are removed one day for bad actions. No global ban list, no meaningful moderation by adults. The classes that were once reasonably well balanced are now unbalanced so badly a single player can hero-mode through entire castles/teams. It's really not worth any amount of money at this point, far better similar games out there - I would suggest one of those. A gaming experience like no other.  A laconic description would go something like: A first person puzzle game with space-bending mechanics. While technically accurate, this description hardly does the game justice.  Antichamber is a game that will stretch your brain in multiple ways: it requires lateral thinking, attention to detail and pushes you to question things that you take for granted in videogames. I have not played a puzzle game before or since with such thought and sheer imagination put into the puzzles. Despite the strange, alien nature of it all, with persistence one can divine a certain kind of logic behind the puzzles and make progress, even in the face of seemingly insurmountable challenges.  A masterpiece that I would reccomend everybody with any interest in puzzle games try at least once.

A superb 2D platformer that oozes humour and references pretty much every single action movie ever made.   Graphically it looks beautiful for what it attempts to emulate (the 8/16-bit era) and the controls and movement are silky smooth and work wonderfully. It also features local co-op and a built in level editor which is also an amazing amount of fun.  The gameplay consists of running through various zones rescuing your comrades and shooting the bad-guys. 'Get to the choppa!' It has destructible terrain, tons of playable characters (each with their own distinct abilities and weapons), bonkers bosses and 'MERICA to the extreme.  Truly a joy to play and I would recommend to anyone interested in it.  Jimbo Rating: 8/10

I've been using AGK for a few months now, and I love it. I have 1 AGK game on Steam, 1 past greenlight, and 1 just starting greenlight ('Curse Of Yendor'). It is so easy to release my games on Android & Amazon too. Just follow the 'export' options and it works great.  I also do contract work, making educational games, and AGK allows me to rapidly prototype ideas and show them off.  Be warned: this is a 'language', not an 'editor' or 'engine'. There is no 'drag and drop'. You use words and symbols to tell the computer what to do, and you have to actually use your brain a bit.  But there is tons of help out there, here on steam, and on the TGC forums.  I highly recommend AGK!

Yeah, my playtime is pretty low, but let me tell you my story, the story of a Deprived trying to fight some monsters: I heard so many good things about Dark Souls 1-3, that I just had to buy it in the shop today (10€ cheaper than Steam, so I recommend you checking shops too, if you can). I got home, installed the game, started it and everything worked just fine. Until I got my first crash in the character selection. 'whatever' I thought, tried again, finished it and started playing the game. Crash after crash... Checking the internet gave some clue about how to fix this. Took me 1 hour though. ----------- No lighting quality, low shaders, no motion blur and shadows off. Game ran just fine from then on. It ran perfectly fluent before, but now it didn't crash and I could make my way to the first boss. ----------- After MANY tries, I killed the su**er and I was ready for more! Amazing feeling, I tell you. The first bosskill for me in the DS series.  So I ran to the door. I tried to open the door. My Deprived pushed. It creaked. And right when I thought it would open...  The game crashed.  I will recommend this game after they fixed the graphic issues and the random crash issues.  Until then, better save yourself a hell lotta trouble.  Yours, Pinky

First, I consider this to be a sitting VR game. As such, I don't expect to be twirling around to get the right view of the marble. So, I go into this game knowing that they will control the camera so that I will be mostly looking at the right spot. With that mindset, I'm being taken for a ride. I enjoy that. A virtual rollercoaster with 3D views and am able to turn my head. Maybe they could have had us control the camera in some way. I don't want to, I'm busy controlling the marble. I liked marble madness and it's that type of game. For me, the 3D VR enhances the experience. The camera control isn't perfect and it's not always where I want it, but it's doing a better job than I would if I had to juggle all the tasks. Sometimes, it's a little abrupt where I wish it wasn't. Also, the marble breaking when I mess up always makes me ill, so I close my eyes after it starts falling off the cliff until after the sound. So, overall, I've enjoyed my time with the game and like the VR experience. If you like marble madness and you like VR and you like riding on rollercoasters, you'll probably like it. I'm going to be showing my family the game this Christmas and I think it will be a hit.

HunieCam Studios is somewhat unusual, I would call it an arcade-tycoon game.   Compared to other management games: The game play is a lot like casual management games, almost similar to a clicker game. Drag and drop and click to rush, repeat. The actual economics of it can be a bit tricky, though. Without a solid strategy you could easily flounder and fail and be mocked by the sex fairy. With bad luck in the random elements you can have a much more difficult run.  Compared to HuniePop: the voice acting is the same, but there is much less of it. The humor is the same, but there is much less of it. The dirty bits are pretty much nil, only what you see in the previews on the loading screens.  Would I recommend HunieCam? YES, because it goes on sale for a nice price. It's not a game you should spend $20 on, but for under $4 I enjoy it. It's not demanding, you can play it for a time waster. It's fun to replay and build cam studios around various themes or kinks or fetishes. It's not really great wank bait, but for a few bucks it's a nice little game.

So Espaco Muerto Dos gets some flak for attempting more action and less horror then its predecessor, Dead Space 1, but honestly I thought that was the way to go because Dead Space 1 wasn't particularly scary to begin with. At least in Dead Space 2 they decided to ditch the constant jump scares and focus more on gore and body horror, which fits the narrative better.  All the guns and combat mechanics make a return, and they're as enjoyable as they were in Dead Space 1. Now, however, you can fly in zero gravity which is pretty cool I guess. I still thought the Dead Space 1 jumping system was better because there was always the threat of getting hit mid-jump and flying out into space. Now you have jets! Ok, cool, don't have to worry about that anymore, I guess.  And now, Isaac has a voice, unlike in Dead Space 1! This is be single best improvement over the first game, in my very very humble opinion, because now we can actually *gasp* relate with him as a character! However, the story is just as bad as the first game's, and at no point did I ever have any idea what was happening or why.   Also the game kept pushing the phrase 'Make Us Whole' as if that was to Dead Space 2 what the gurgling sound was to The Grudge, and it just came off stupid. It's not a scary phrase. Wasn't really well done either, like you'll be in an elevator and a ghost will appear and  MAEKASOLE .  Overall, though, Dead Space 2 is a really enjoyable experience. Combat was tight and engaging, there were a lot of diverse and interesting areas, and some very cool, very memorable 'WTF JUST HAPPENED?!?' moments too. This is probably the best Dead Space game( followed by Dead Space 1 then Call of Duty 3: Dead Space) and it's definitelly worth playing!

Tropico 3's soundtrack is 20x better than Tropico 4.  Gamewise, Tropico 4 is a better game.  The problem with Tropico is there is no penalty for going into excessive debt(meaning beyond 20k in deby), other than not being able to suppress a revolution if you do not have an already existing army of appropriate size.  Maybe this is linked to the Keynesian attitude of the US...  On top of that, the inflation rates of the 'print money' mandate are extremely exaggerated(a permanent 30% increase? really? you arent even going to compare it to real GDP or total assets?). The percentage increase of buildings should gradually go up for the next 3 years, then decline gradually for 3 years afterwards.  I think that there should be a pentalty for excessive debt(-20k or more in debt), and that penalty should be that you get to choose between a foreign nation either sending paid rebels to your island(which also, consequently gains respect with the nationalists) OR you forfeit a building asset to a foreign power and dont pay the employee salary(and consequently lose respect with nationalists).  That seems like a more interesting system since there is no (in-game) penalty for excessive debt.  I am sort of conflicted by this game because Tropico 4 is definitely a more fascinating game(more in-game options.  But Tropico 3 has a much more interesting sound-file set(especially music-wise... but other aspects of the sound were better too, such as the reactions of characters when you would click on them).

This game is easily the best value in terms of hours per dollar I've ever had in a (non-free) game. It often goes into the sub-$3 range on sales and you could literally play for hundreds of hours without getting bored. It's got something for everyone: building, exploration, combat, rare loot. The graphics are retro, but they'll never be dated. Best of all, the developers have a consistent track record of updating and improving the game with new content.   It's not perfect. It could use much better mod support, world generation sometimes seems buggy, and the 2D form factor sometimes feels like a straightjacket. But for what it is, it's at the top of the class.

I loved the game when I started playing it, but as stupid as this complaint sounds, the game needs a way better tutorial.  It is a well made game, visually looks wonderfull, solid mechanics, but the combat can get really repetative and just frustrating.  Save games There should be no save game option in any game mode, but the easyest, as this just makes the game pointless, as soon as you lose somebody, you are going to load the game. That would have made the game more challenginig and fun, but also rage inducing, but in a more fair way.  Aiming It works on a percentage based accurecy system, where the higher percentage gives you the higher chance of hitting a target and that is where most of my frustration comes from. Even with a 95%, I had 3 people miss in a row and caused my entire team to die because of this. Also, how far is close, medium or long range, iIm just guessing.  Enemy movement Ground units, cool, they work normaly, but flying units, those are horrible. I get that they can move around in a space without issues, but being in a building and instantly be on the roof, ignoring walls and such... that is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t. I can take many things as game mechanics and this dosent bother methat much, but when this is something you can not enticipate ever, it makes me frustrated waymore than it should.  Obstructions You have your high walls and low walls, simple and they protect you depending on there the enemy is fireing at you, sure.... but when you or the enemy just straight shoot through the walls, no... thats not how cover works. Some times, the cover explodes, sometimes bullets ignore cover, most of the times, when you think you have a safe shot, you dont and it just breaks the logic of how things should work. Walls cant be trusted, especially when some walls explode and some dont.  If the tutorial was made to give more than a few examples on how players interract with the enviroment, so that I can learn the mechanics better, I would be happy and call my failures my fault, but when I legit dont understand how things work, I rather give up.

I won't go on and on with this one, but let me just say that before Frictional Games' Amnesia: The Dark Descent became synonymous with fear, they were perfecting their craft with the Penumbra series.  Black Plague is the second of their three-part Penumbra series, but in this man's humble opinion it tops even their most successful of games.  Yes, I said it. It's better than Amnesia. Now before you get to the hate let me just say that I adore Amnesia. It introduced me, as it did so many others, to Frictional Games' work. But once I finished it up I wanted more. I was lucky enough to see this package come up during one of Steam's classic Halloween sales and decided to snag it. For some reason or another, I started Black Plague first, and I couldn't have made a better choice.  This game has it all. From the atmosphere that breathed down my neck, tingling my spine and darkening my thoughts to the gameplay immersing me into the world, this game hits all the right notes. But this one does what I hope Soma (Frictional Games' next title) will do again - make a great story.  I won't spoil anything, but let me just say don't let the atypical setting of a mental asylum fool you. It's much more than that, and giving this game enough of your time to find out why is well worth the investment.  I highly recommend this game.

After playing it for 145 hours, I can say that it was really worth my time so far. I don't know why the Metacritic Score is pretty low, but it's probably because the reviews were from back when the game was... well... not so interesting.  It's a third person shooter with parkour-elements, filled with missions around defending, assassinating a boss, exterminating a level and so on.  There is a gigantic list of weapons, all of them can be further be changed in appearance, while mods will change how they behave. A Flamethrower can turn into a Gasthrower, heavy melee weapons can turn into fast weapons of destruction. There are also different warframes - pretty much classes in other games. Each Warframe has 4 Skills, most of them very useful, others very situational.   Weapons and Warframes can either be crafted via ingame money and resources or bought with real money. Crafting will also take real time to complete, from half a day with most weapons to 3 days when finalizing a new warframe. It's clearly a way to get you to buy stuff, but as soon as you have some missions under your belt, the waiting time isn't really that annoying.   Just as a warning: The game isn't easy at the beginning. You will die often and hard. But after that initial hurdle, the game gets really good.  My only problems so far are the high prices of some things you can buy and that there are only 4 enemy factions so far.  You should check it out if you like third person shooters. You should also check it out if you liked Borderlands, as it is somewhat like that with Leveling up and modifying weapons. It is free 2 play, but you won't get slapped with a paywall until you're like 20 hours into the game. And by that you will probably know if you want to play on or not.

The game's not for everyone. Probably one of its biggest flaws is the repetitive gameplay. But, with so many frames, weapons, and mods to customize your loadout, it's easy to overlook the repetitive feeling of the missions. Of course, it's only repetitive if you decide to really get into it, as with any other game. There are seriously 20+ frames (characters) and counting, and hundreds of unique weapons. By unique, I mean one of the draws of this game is the insane amount of interesting weapons to choose from. There's a bow made specifically for decapitating enemies, some living weapons (one of which sprays acid at enemies), there's a sword and shield made out of physical 'bones' and energy, and recently they added in a god damn spartan laser looking thing called the opticor. Everything is color customizable, and while this is a F2P game, you can trade with other people for the paid currency (platinum). Once you get into void missions, you can sell your extra prime parts for platinum, which lets you buy pretty much whatever you want (cosmetic items, double mod capacity on gear pieces, color pallettes, extra slots for weapons and frames, etc). In short, the game feels a bit grindy after a while, story is so far a mystery (with some clues by the devs), and pvp is not really all that great (this is mostly a pve game, just saying). But there are enough frames and weapons to keep you busy for a loooong time. Just keep in mind that this game is about super-powered robot ninjas fighting and flipping around through space. As long as that sounds fun to you, the game will be fun to you.

I followed the developer's blog like a hawk for the better part of a year, and in that time, my excitement began to wane. It was not because the game wasn't shaping up properly - I just have a way of obsessing over something for a time, then moving on. Well, the wait was finally over, and I was almost tempted to let it slip by. Then, recalling my past enthusiasm over the title, I decided that $20 was worth the risk and clicked 'add to cart.' That was one of the best buys I have ever made on Steam.   I cannot get enough of this game. I admit that due to a hectic work week, I have not been able to invest as much time as I would like into the game. Still, from the moment I completed the outstandingly crafted tutorial, I knew this was a favorite. It brings back all the fond memories I cherish of the glory days of Stronghold. It truly captures everything I love about this genre. If you are on the fence, allow me to kindly push you off. Buy this and don't look back. It is deceptively complex, and you will quickly discover that violence is not necessary to create a tense, pins-and-needles game of survival. Granted, you will experince most of what the game has to offer within the first 10-20 hours of play, but don't let that deter you. There is a mind-boggling amount of room to expand your village and conquer the elements for years (both in-game and literal) to come.

This game really touched me. It has been a long time since a game made me cry, and this one finally broke that.  This game is a pure work of art. It is the nearest to perfection a game could come. It is hard for me to actually pinpoint what part of this game I loved the most, because everything was equally amazing. The story, the artwork, the gameplay, and the music is all so well-blended together, and clearly all underwent a great deal of iteration. None of the elements have any kind of weird disconnection one typically sees in games that were created by large teams.  The only negative thing I can think of is that I had a screen freeze in predictable locations on some levels, but that was brief and none of it happened in intense moments, so I shrugged it off. Its probably my computer anyway.

This game is basically everything I wanted from it. Captures the Souls combat formula perfectly in 2D, incorporates aspects of metroidvania games to fill in the rest of the mechanics, and just feels well put together (especially for such a small team) whilst not being overly short or simplistic. I've played all 3 of the main Dark Souls installments and perhaps it's just familiarity causing it, but I found this legitimately more challenging at many points than Dark Souls 3 was. Level design is excellent (and would probably appeal to people who preferred Dark Souls 1's looping paths and interconnectivity and abundance of shortcuts) and the platforming aspect isn't neglected either; there's some neat tricks introduced as you go deeper. The price of entry seems fair, I could see myself spending multiple playthroughs at likely over 10 hours each (2-3x more for the first, NG+ an option) with this. Strikes me as very replayable with as many interesting build options that the game gives you. Magic isn't awful like it has been for the last 1.5 Dark Souls games either, so there's that. Virtually anyone could run this, and I've not heard of many technical issues (more than could be honestly said for some of its inspiration). All in all I had a lot of fun and the similarity to niche games many of us have previously enjoyed helps to both highlight where it does well in emulating the thrill of said games, but also shows off the ways that it feels like an entirely new experience divergent from those games.   Really all I could complain about here is enemy grab attacks. Mostly unfun in the Souls games and mostly unfun here too when they come in to play, but that's just a select few enemy types that often have avoidable mechanics if cautious.

Good God this game is amazing. Combine the immersive storytelling of Skyrim, impactful/world-altering choices of Mass Effect (except your choices matter in the end, too, not just for the first two thirds of the story), and strong gameplay mechanics similar to that of dark souls (not as praiseworthy, but stronger than any other comparable rpg), and this is what you get. World is filled to the brim with amazingly crafted story and side quests and characters you will care about. Also gwent - I've never cared for mini games within rpgs before, but I found myself enjoying this a lot more than expected.   The amount of content squeezed into this one $60 game would cost you hundreds elsewhere, spanning multiple sequels and dlcs. They are making dlc for this game, but instead of the current business model of cutting out chunks of games to sell to you later, these dlcs will add to an already more than complete experience. I intend to sink hundreds of hours into this game before I'm done with it, replaying to see how different choices affect the world. Why go outside when you have this beautiful game in front of you?  Just give them your money already.

Massive battles between thousands of men in the fields of Germany; Minor skirmishes along the border of Austria to secure your lands; and giant, continent spaning empires duking it out for control of Europe. This is a Dark Souls game. I'll just stick to how it stands out, as by now most people are roughly aware of the nature of the series.  The environments are gorgeous. Absolutely gorgeous and I've been wowed by them a lot. The enemies are trying to bash ye fookin 'ead in and the game takes you to places from the previous installments, fused in a creepy and eerie way. If you're familiar with the previous games in the series and sit to think on it a little, it goes into extra nightmare fuel territory, on top of the general feel. While I'm somewhat annoyed by how the game made greatshields quite the liability compared to Dark Souls 2, I can't deny that it manages to stay true to the Dark Souls formula while subtly altering it in quite a few ways.   Aside from the obvious focus on challenge and the need to git gud, Dark Souls 3 manages to find a decent balance between convenience and challenge. You still feel like you're banging your head against the wall, but not quite to the level I remember from Demon's Souls and Dark Souls the First. In fact, Dark Souls 3 is in a precarious position, as it's both friendly and unfriendly to newcomers. While it's well worth to play the other games, this installment actually has one of the smoothest difficulty curves in the series. That's most likely caused by a well-rounded approach to enemy placement. It felt like every area had more variety to it than previous games. This way, the game tries to force you to adapt, as it's hard to stick to the 'raise shield and smack them' approach that works very well in the previous games.  Obviously enough, the key thing is liking this kind of games: if you don't enjoy banging your head against the wall and prefer easy cheevos raining upon you, the game isn't for you. The difficulties you face make every success fulfilling, however.

Ever really want to recommend a game but you just can't?  This is that game. It's beautiful, it's murder-tastic, it has tombs to raid and skills to discover--and the main storyline took me twenty hours to finish.  There are very many things to praise about this game. Like the previous reboot, it's gorgeous. Frozen, snow-choked mountain passages give way to the broken spires of yet another lost kingdom in a forgotten corner of the earth. The plot is a cannibalism of Indiana Jones (The Last Crusade), but not in such a completely boring way that it isn't fun to play. Lara is once again slowly learning to fill out the titular tomb raider role, becoming the combination archeologist, parkour expert, free climber, and murder machine we all know and love. The background the game is playing with for a plot is another interesting combination of fantasy and psuedo-history, in this case the Russian gulags of the early to mid twentieth century and a re-named, re-hashed version of the grail myth.  If I got a little tired of QTE, I certainly didn't get tired of the scenery and the backdrop.  My issues with the game, other than with the price tag and playable hours, had to do with several technical issues including almost continuous sound glitches, a few poor interface issues, and the sheer muscle exhaustion of having to stab buttons for rolling, very frequent QTE.  This is where I talk a bit about value--the work that went into writing, modeling, coding, marketing, and all the other necessary skills in getting a game to market is of obvious value. I understand, as well, that the studio must recover the cost.   However, from the customer end of it, I am deeply uncomfortable with the $50+ pricetag for twenty hours of game play. The marketed expansion pack, having to do with Baba Yaga, adds another 10 or so hours, another $10, and something like twelve achievements. I have a total of thirty one hours, primarily in order to finish getting all the collectables, because I'm an achievement nerd.  I have no issues with the pacing, in terms of story. The problem was not that the story dragged, the problem was that there was not enough story to justify the price tag. When I buy a full-price game, I expect anywhere from 30-50 hours of gameplay. I tend to get through stories faster than most, but I was deliberately slow making it through this one because the in-game map (which would show collectables) did not work for me about half the time I spent playing, so I slowly explored areas using survival senses to try and miss as few collectables as possible.  Had I focused on the story, I'm fairly certain I could have finished the game in something like sixteen to eighteen hours, which is far too short for a full price game.  The game has many, many merits, but I cannot recommend it until it goes on sale. If I were assigning a price, I would not wish to pay any more than $30 or so for the amount of time spent playing it.

In Short: An improvement of the business mechanics seen from Recettear.  Long Ver: Recettear, a Japanese indie darling that focuses on being a merchant, years later we now find a very similar game inspired by Recettear.  In Al Fine, you play as Loius, a young man swindled by a Sprite named Alice to pay back for breaking a supposingly precious 'gem'.  But there is no giant debt hanging over our heads but in place are the guild taxes which occurs a few times per month and when there is a challenge, you have a few days to upped over your opponent's profits.  The shop is affected by what types of customers which are affected by decorations, by weather (sunny, rainy, cloudy) affecting the numbers of customers, by type of items (food *rot by days*, equipment, merch) or by the usual...price. But within price, there are more restrictions as customer's budget are not affected by your rank like in Recettear (aka the level). Only by market trends will the price change, allowing you to sell more.  While in the shop, using Alice you can restock items as long as you have it in the warehouse. Although, I have not found anything to improve her speed in the game so far (5 hours of playing).  Improving the shop will cost you your merchant points that are gained via selling in the guild. I do find that its best to pile a few upgrades rather than 1 upgrade as 1 doesn't affect too much into the shop and you save time.  Speaking of time, just like Recettear...you will pass the time by just visiting places. Some places will give you information to be used much later in the game.  There is 1 huge con to this game, the inability to go into dungeons. You can hire an adventurer to do so but its just bassically assigning them away missions which success depends on their level and equipment. The game does not have any feature to change an adventurer's equipment so far...  Another con would be the character design, the illustrator seems to lack basic anatomy lessons but in return makes it very detailed. There is just something annoying about their necks and collarbones... Interestingly there is actually another illustrator on this game as you can see on the merchants portraits via rank which are superbly better than the character designer (unless its somebody introduced in the game).  Storywise, I do feel there is far more characterization with Alice than Louis...I can't really say anything more as I'm in Chapter 2. The game does not show anything on romance, only friendship.  The music so far is pleasant and it works within the game. But is it memorable? No.  Overall: If you're hungry for more selling mechanics aka Recettear, this might scratch that itch. I did enjoy the game with my 5 hours and it does have some difficulty to it to gain maximum profit within a short time. (The game does tell you whether you're good or terrible). There are more games inspired by Recettear but those games are sadly to be release so...so far away. It either 75 or 78/100 would be my rating.  Currently: 29 hours = Finish. It might be shorter but I was trying to get rank 1 in the Merchant rankings. Its actually pretty easy to fall from rank 1 to rank 3 or 4 after just 1 day of terrible profits. Also found out that seasons also changes the trends of items so the difficulty increases.

Theres something about this game that keeps me coming back again, and again. And its not just the fact that its still being updated with content after five years.  There's something about Terraria that just feels special. I can load up a new world and make it look like however I'd like it to. I can make dirt holes into houses. I can make tree houses, light houses, castles, winter cabins, asian pagodas, underwater science stations, pirate ships, blimps, wizard towers, fishing huts, and any other home that exists outside of this list.  I can invite others into these houses and customize each one for them. A bankers vault for the Merchant, medical facility for the nurse, shady cantina for the arms dealer, or a party club for the resident party girl. Or I can make them live in dirt holes in the ground.  I can create a new character and outfit them however I choose. They can dress up for halloween, wear cool armor, or run around as any number of birdmen.  I can speed run through the obstacles laid before me. Or take my time, and invent a whole new world to build a storyline around. Or I can hide in dirt holes in the ground.  I can do whatever I want in Terraria. And I can do it at my own whimsical pace. And it makes me want to come back again and again and again. If you haven't joined me already, I'd highly suggest doing so.  Come make your own dirt holes, and fill them with whatever your heart desires.

app_name: The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings Enhanced Edition
review_text: CD Projekt RED really hit it out of the park with this one. Even though some complain the combat being a little dogdy and unresponsive, The Witcher 2 is truly a sight to beseen and a immersive experience. You're a really missing something out if you call yourself a RPG fan or PC gamer (and even if you're not).  The game spands over 3 chapters including a prologue and epilogue. During your time you'll find youself doing fun, action-packed, interesting, immersive and CHALLENGING quests. Especially the latter really characterizes this game if you want to play this game. Though it's not THAT hard. You just have to use your brain - to find out what combinations work and what potions to use at what time. You have to prepare and you never go head on into a 1 vs 8. NO! You tactically plan your doings and that's what this game really excels at (among other things). I haven't find out a bad quests yet and I have played through it twice - there are really a lot of incentives for you to do so. This game oozes of atmosphere and choice, like a real RPG should. Your choices feel like they have an imense impact on the story (and they do). The RPG-elements are suberb and it handles really well with the themes of racism and sex. The latter is really well done, though some times there are a little too many boo**** if you fully explore this game, if you no what I mean. Graphically the game is a technically marvel! It's jaw-dropping even. And if we talk about combat, the animations and gamepley are suberb. BUT the targeting system and responsiveness can be a little tricky. That being said, it's so much fun, finishing your foes in brutal and new ways every time. Overall the game is a no-brainer and one you can replay many times just to see the different outcomes. The major as well as the minor. Especially if you're the guy that saves people the first time around and the second time leave evil doers to the hands of even more evil doers! Buy it and support the awsome developers, you really should appreciate! They really care about their player-base! + the game is often very cheap and if you buy it on  GOG.com  (their store), it's DRM-free! Peace out!

Where to start? This is a pretty good game. The story is top notch for Transformers. It's interesting without being overly complex or trying to be something more than it is. The co-op is gone, making the campaign more story driven, though this makes the campaign seem gimmicky at times. The ability to buy and upgrade your weapons is a really nice feature.   The graphics are really detailed. Transformers have a nice glossy look. The locations are more varied. The music is pretty epic and times and always sets the mood for the action. The voice acting is great.   The Escalation or 'horde' mode is remade and well done. The waves only go to 15 but you have multiple tries to complete, which MAY make it to easy for some, but there is always hard mode. Everyone in the lobby can choose their own difficulty without effecting everyone else.    Now we get to where most people spend their time on this game. Multiplayer. This game has some SERIOUS balance issues. There's always something over powered and annoying you have to deal with. And the players are VERY annoying. Everything from kill stealers and campers (on the convoy map ALL you ever see are jets camping at the top) and the heavy classes dominate everything. This game is just not suited for competitive play. If you want a good competitive game, go for War for Cybertron. The PvP in this game is just for joking around.   I give this game a 7/10. It has a great singleplayer experience and fun co-op.

I'm really not the one to write negative reviews about anything, but this game sure deservers a hell of a warning. Since I know that many of you have this game (bundles/sales/free giveaways) I just want to spare you the trouble of ever starting this below-par platformer.  First I would like to start with the good things, because it's not all that bad. Shooting is fun. Humour is OK. Political conotations are appreciated. 'Brain bugs' are a decent mechanic all around.  Now with the bad. Level design which is so tightly crammed leaving you no room for outmaneuvering your enemies - meaning the games is linear as linear gets. Really, no paths to chose whatsoever. Puzzle sequences, which are actually not puzzle sequences at al as all you need to do is move some boxes around - going from A to B, then from B to A, then from A to B again, try to jump up a few times and flick the switch that leads to C... Checkpoints, which will often leave you between a rock and a hard place. Lagg in online co-op. Sometimes gamebreaking (probaly not everyone has experienced this, but we did).  Co-op was the thing I really hoped will save this game from it's mediocrity. But it didn't. Infact, co-op is probably the worse segment of Rocketbirds. Why? You see, there is a unique mechanic that is essential in co-op mode, the one featuring one player jumping on-top another mostly used for reaching high ledges.   There are two major issues with this. The first one is that it is not optinal - you just jump next to your partner and you are automatically on-top of him, which leads to HUNDREDS of falls, fails, repeats, backtraking instances. Second one is that there is virtually no room in those tightly squeezes corridors for this mechanic to actually work properly, leaving you with a sorrow grim on your face time and time again. You know the game is bad when you and your partner laugh about how bad this really is.  If you allready own this game, sure, give it a try, why not. You might like it, you might actually love it for some strange reason. If you do not own this game, you are not missing out on anything. Even for 7,99€ (in the time of writing) you can find dozens of better and more polished platformers in steam store.

app_name: Fallout 3 - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: Out of any company that could've gotten the rights to the Fallout franchise it had to be Bethesda, the company notorious for dumbing down the Scrolls series, Fallout was once an intricate RPG that focused on character progression, decision making that actually had an impact on the world around you, deep interaction with NPC's and using tactics to overcome tough disadvantages.  Fallout 3 is nothing more than an action game in an open world, with barely any RPG elements at all besides for upgrading your players stats everytime you level up, which have no consequence as to how your game plays out seeing as it's so damn easy in any case, as you basically role play as Superman which allows you to put little regard in thought when it comes to building your character.  The decision making, which is an extremely massive part in every true RPG's soul, has been narrowed down to 'Good guy/Bad guy' decisions rather than offering you multiple options, each which have a consequential impact minor or big that make you feel more involved with the world. The plot, another important aspect of RPG, is as generic and un-inspired as they come, a 12 year old could've written this.  In Fallout 3 there is NEVER any sense of danger, ever, you can traverse from one end of the map to the other with ease, unlike the older games in which travelling was a major risk and each encounter felt like it could be your last. I really hope they're using a better team for Fallout 4 because if it's anything like this dumbed down generic open world FPS it's going to stink, if you really want to play a modern Fallout, I'd suggest you play New Vegas, they attempt to fix most of the issues in this mess otherwise you're better off playing Fallout 1 and 2.

So, you like zombies? Shooting them, I mean.  Well, here's the game for you. It retains the beautiful sniping mechanics of sniper elite with aim adjustment for wind and velocity/distance, you get a satisfying go at popping thousands of zombie heads in gory slow motion (something that you can toggle off in case it ends up boring you after the 4600th kill).  That isn't all you'll be doing, though. Featuring iconic WW2 weaponry with their own benefits and drawbacks, you have plenty of choice when it comes to dealing with the hellish hordes that the führer has summoned. Not everyone is an expert sniper and you can still have a grand old time mowing down the flesheating freaks with a machine gun.  Set at the end of WW2, the nazi war machine is failing and Hitler pursues a desperate method of salvaging his war. An ancient nepalese relic recovered by the Thule society reanimates the slain wehrmacht to devour the enemies of the Reich! It's up to our hero, Karl Fairburne, the great Russian bear of a man Boris Medvedev, Dr. Schweiger and redeemed(?) nazi Herman Wolff to wipe the undead scourge off the streets of Berlin before it spreads to the rest of the continent!  That's it for the plot, really. It's vaugely elaborated upon in the followup Nazi Zombie Army 2 but not by much. If you expect a plot, play something different but it's good for what it is, playing up the nazi occult thing in a way I've only seen the Wolfenstein games explore.  Mechanics wise, it's a third person shooter in which you counter hordes of sluggish zombies (no running here, except for the suicide bombers!) but it's not that easy. Unlike games like left 4 dead, you go down in just a couple of hits and the numbers that spawn in co-op can seem endless at times. But hey, that's what grenades are for!  Single player and multiplayer are pretty much the same deal but with more players come more of a challenge. More enemies will spawn to hinder you and something you thought was a breeze when playing alone can become a nightmare. The levels themselves are largely remade levels from the sniper elite game, approached from different angles and merged in interesting ways. You'll kind of know your way if you played the main game.  Enemy variety includes regular zombies who are just your typical flesh eating monster, groaning and shuffling about as well as quicker moving stripped skeletons who are very good at overwhelming their opposition, forcing stationary snipers to move about.   The previously mentioned suicide bombers appear in the middle of regular zombie hordes to catch you off guard and rush you and sometimes you're faced with eerily cackling zombie snipers, leaping from building to building to take shots at you while you're trying to handle the horde in front.   There's also the trooper zombies who are gigantic, slow moving behemoths with machine guns, shooting right back at you and demanding dozens of shots to the forehead to finally drop.  Finally, there's the occult general. A very rare, scripted boss creature that summons hordes of every variety and protects himself with a magical barrier of floating skulls, encouraging a different kind of headshot.   That's enough about that. Gameplay wise, it's good for what it is. Hardly a captivating masterpiece of storytelling to share with your grandkids someday but an entertaining go through a strange, strange setting.  I don't believe in scores so I rate this game as fun, entertaining but capable of being a lot more than what we're given. Maybe we'll see the formula brought to perfection in sniper elite Afrika's version of the zombie nightmare?

I enjoyed this expansion a great deal. It did a great job of bridging the gap between BG 1 and BG 2. The graphics, considering it was running on the old infinity engine, were excellent. The story was enjoyable however I did have a small complaint in that it was very linear. This is excusable considering the entire point of the game is to connect to specific story points in the series but I still feel it could have been done better. Another minor complaint was how little it effects anything in BG 2- only a small amount of items will show up in random places later. My favorite part of SOD was the NPC's. Traveling again with Boo (and his medium sized terrestrial friend Minsc), Viconia and other series favorites- actually voiced by their original actors- was a real nostalgic joy after so many years. The new characters too were a lot of fun, Captain Corwin and M'Khiin being my favorites.  Obviously there are some strong opininions and disagreements about this game but for me personally it was well worth playing. I think any old school fan of Bioware who loves their modern titles like Mass Effect and Dragon Age will find this Beamdog expansion fits right in.

8.5/10 Overview: First Person Melee/Shooter  The Good:  + Graphics- both on an artistic and technical level everything looks fantastic. Every environment spread across 17 levels feels distinct. The only downside is the developers went heavy with the bloom lighting.  + Combat is fun and satisfying. The game gives you a variety of different weapons at your disposal. The katana, your main weapon is usually your go to weapon when things start to go bad. It slices and dices enemies to bits. You also have guns that are slowly acquired through the game. The first half of the game seems to be more reliant on your katana and when the larger enemies show up is when your guns start to become more useful. You also have a variety of spells and sword techniques that can give you the upper hand in combat. No matter how you choose to dismember your enemies, you will always have a good time.  + The game features a very robust upgrade system. Money is used to upgrade your weapons. Each weapon has 3 upgrades. Ki crystals are used to upgrade your spells. Each spell has 4 upgrades. Lastly as you defeat your enemies you gain experience which is used to upgrade your passive abilities and sword techniques.   + Secrets are scattered throughout every level, some secrets are a throwback to the original game  + The story starts off very weak however the more it starts to reveal itself the more interesting it gets. The story is told through comic book style cut scenes that look beautiful. Throughout the game you are accompanied by a demon named Hoji who at first is really annoying but starts to grow on you.   + The game is very polished. Even with all the explosions and all the effects happening my game barely dropped below 60fps.   + New Game plus to start the game over with all your upgrades and weapons.   The Average:  You have the ability to dash forward. If you dash on top of a steep staircase you will die. This gets really frustrating as you will be dashing all the time and this will probably be the cause of a lot of needless deaths. The developers said this bug/'Feature' cannot be fixed.  As much fun as the game is, it starts to wear itself out in the last few levels. Once you've seen all the enemies and upgraded all that you need, fighting the same hordes of enemies over and over again starts getting boring. Luckily the game ends before it really becomes a bigger issue.   This is one of those rare first person shooters where playing with a controller is preferable. The reason being is that you have to input directional inputs to cast your spells and I found it easier to do with a controller than a K/M. In addition the game relies more heavily on melee combat than guns so precise aiming is never really a concern.   Some of the later levels can get a bit confusing.   I felt the last boss was underwhelming. I guess I was expecting something more.    Conclusion  This game is a remake of an old school FPS however this is not an old school fps. Levels are mostly linear with only one way to progress while throwing large amounts of enemies at you for you to dispose. If you enjoy fighting large groups of enemies while you level up your abilities than you will have a good time with the game. Overall this is definitely worth checking out and a very underrated game that deserves a bit more attention.

I rate this game  90 / 100  on my book.  the best game I ever played in a long time. I had more fun playing this game than Metal Gear:V Phantom.   Pro's :  - Optimize to the MAX :   Graphics is beutiful and detailed. I love the day and night cycles, Sand Storms. it really makes   you want to seek some shelter or any hole in the ground when the Storm is out.   - Big Open World:   its big, its open, and dry like your mom's Vagi.. no really, this game is amazing. I had the most fun driving in first person with big V8 engine block on the hood, while hunchback is in the back playing with himself. using nothing but shotgun.    - Customization to the MAX:    so many parts to mix and match to build your own Death Car. you can also customize Max.  unlocking different types of armor he can wear / different shotgun you can use.   - Combat: CAR   Car combat is fun. so many ways / tactics depends on how you setup your Magnum Opus. is it fully armored plowing death machine?, or nimble fast spikey running around in circles on your enemy while you rip the wheels off them with your harpoon? or little bit of both. there's a trade off on having heavy ram and armor - obvious the speed anf handling, while having less armor and ramming power gives you speed. also, don't forget having the right tires.    - Combat: Max on foot   He is a one bad motherf.. don't let that leg brace fool you, his combat is fluid and brutal.   - Dinki:   your own personal mine sniffing dog. - you get your interceptor back when you beat the game. although personally having 2 big gas tank sticking out in the back of the car is not my cup of tea, for that is the first thing I aim at when chasing an enemy.   - Missions:  Quests, Faction, and the Convoy route for every zone is great. I enjoy doing them, did not feel like a grind at all.    Con's :   - Air baloon  its a one time use only, not sure why. so if you miss marking any important land mark while you are up there, you are on your own.   - No Flip-overs   your car is basically a Cat - always lands on its feet err wheels. my personal preference, I want consequences for driving recklessly off cliff.   - The Enemy StrongHold Bosses are the same  - Big Hulk-like ,Masked, with heavy melee that moves the same as the last. ok, so 1 of them is a black guy, and they all have different paint on them but all in all, they are the same. I just wish they give every bosses different looks and weapons/ fight mechanics.    - Where's the Gore?  I'm not sure why they didn't add the heads blowing up / ground beef style like Gears of war. or guts hanging out when you blast them with your shotgun., or heads being chopped off? C'mon WB -- its MAD MAX.   Overall, the game is simply amazing. you get past the no brains splatter when you get to shove a knife on some ones face, or when you get the slo-mo '300 spartans' combat-like-effect - as you slam your enemy's face on the wall.    I enjoy the attention to detail. from the little spurt of gass/flame that leaks out of your muffler and set the ground on fire for a bit, to the heat waves that comes out of your big V8 engine block. the explosions and smoke from the wreckage you create are beutifull to look at. The Story is bleak but fits in perfectly with the whole world of MaX .. there is no happy, lubby dubby ending.

It's been many years since System Shock 2 came out. Overshadowed by the admittedly superior Half-Life, SS2 still stands today as an exceptional fusion of FPS and RPG elements, with a heaping helping of atmosphere. Trapped on a half-functioning spaceship populated by murderous mutants, it shows its age and yet... I still come back to it. I still return to the halls of the Von Braun, trembling as I turn every corner even when I have them mapped out.  Mod packs exist to improve the graphics and skins of the original game, and you can tweak the configuration to limit or remove certain aspects. The game has its flaws -- if you go straight for standard and heavy weapons the game is fairly easy -- but it still holds its own particularly in comparison to more recent, weaker shooters.

Description: -Inspired by the old 2d fighting games. Skullgirls is one of the best out there. It follows the path of currently 9 girls on their quest for the infamous Skullheart. The skullheart will fill them with their darkest and deepest desires. Having to fight their way to get it. You can fight in a match consisting of a player party of 1,2, or 3 people. Each varying assist moves and balanced strength depending on how many people you choose to fight with.  Controls: -Standard arcade format, closely similar to street fighter, with low,mid, and high punches and kicks. -Compatability with keyboard, console controler, or arcade joystick. -Easy to learn, even for casual gamers not used to fighters. As most characters share the same combinations for blockbusters and special moves.  Pros: -Fast pace -Fun for high skill and low skill players -Unique artstyle and music -Unique and original characters with their own personality  Cons: -Not many players online to find a match with. If you're looking for a match it's easier to go into ranked matches, but this isn't reccomended for low skill/casual players.  -Servy laggy at times -Not a huge character selection as compared to Mortal Kombat or Street Fighter or Marvel Vs. Capcom -There isn't a reliable combo breaker, you can literally be infinite combo'd after someone hits you once. -Fighting games aren't for everyone, it is a dying breed of games and the player-base will always be low. So find/make friends who own this game to always have someone to play with.

If you haven't bought this already, buy it.  Pros:   The dungeons are different every time.  Tons of characters with very different playstyles (ex the default character has to move to the beat, another one can move to his own beat, one more has infinite bombs, etc.)  The music will get you. I literally cannot get the music out of my head.  Pretty easy to understand. You only use the arrow keys.  Daily Challenge (if that's your thing). Every day a random dungeon is set up and you get one run to score as high as possible. There's a global leaderboard.  Pop in your own music and play to its beats.  Cons:  If you don't like rhythm games, well you won't like this.   It is very VERY hard at first. You meet lots of enemies who each have their own movement and attack patterns. You will probably die a lot, and it can be frustrating. There are a lot of items that can make the game either a (relative) breeze or very hard if you don't have them. I don't really know if it's a con or not, but it was frustrating.  End of list.    TL;DR  This is my GOTY.

I find myself in a bizarre situation. On the one hand, Deus Ex: The Fall isn't all too terrible. On the other hand, there is no mention anywhere of this being an episodic game. You simply play for a little bit and then reach a 'To Be Continued...' screen. It feels a bit cheap.  Cheap is easily the best word to describe this. Don't bother wasting $5 on this. I got it for around $2 and feel a little cheated. Graphics don't make a game, but holy crap is this a bit of a mess. Character models barely animate, there is clipping abound and performing takedowns almost always results in the world disappearing around you before forming back. I also got stuck in a box very early into the game.  As for the actual mechanics, there are a bunch of differences compared to Human Revolution, but the iOS origin of the game makes that understandable. What isn't so forgivable is that none of your keybindings show up on the screen. Everything is left as if you were still playing on an iPad. At least the tutorial voice recognizes your keyboard and mouse.  The level design is also entirely forgettable. This is pretty much a greatest hits of what the Deus Ex series has to offer. Nothing new ever happens. You hack terminals and computers, you stealth takedown enemies or blast them away and you crawl through vents. There is even a requisite nightclub, as if the first game made a permanent blueprint that can't be escaped.  I don't necessarily mind the chopped down areas, but there isn't much in the way of freedom. You definitely have choices in how you tackle the objectives at hand, but the AI is so shockingly stupid that you often wonder if stealth even matters. I often just ran straight at people and attacked them, barely taking a scratch.  The music is the standout feature here. The voice acting is hit and miss, but you can easily forget the characters are even talking when you hear the music. It may be a bit too short (the samples loop quite a bit), but it sounds far better than Human Revolutions did. It fits the tone of the game very well.  I also have a love/hate relationship with the store system. Instead of having to go to a merchant or even terminal, you simply buy what you want at any given moment. This turns moments where you get outnumbered or cornered by a robot into trivial affairs. I suppose trying to aim sucks on iPad, but it's flawless on PC.  So I could take the bad if there were at least a complete game here. As it stands, I feel like I only got half an experience. It wasn't devoid of fun, but you could spend your money a lot better. Human Revolution routinely goes on sale for $5 and you could even get the first two Deus Ex games in a pack for $5. Doing either option would be a much better investment then dealing with this drek.  3/10

When you first make a character in Hero Siege and begin on Act I, you'll find it difficult. You'll find the game frustrating. But, once you overcome the restlessness that comes along with starting a new game and find yourself settled with the game, prepare to become fully infatutated with Hero Siege. Hero Siege has a lot of great points, as well as bad, which you can easily find out either by reading reviews on the game or buying it and finding out wether you dislike/like the game there. Don't let the reviews sway your mind to only take one look at the game and either buy or ignore the game. See for yourself thoroughly, wether or not you want the game, because Hero Siege isn't your average game.  As I said before, Hero Siege has its good and bad points, the things that determine its quality. Some good things about Hero Siege is that it twists RPG elements in a good way. For example, normally in quests for RPGs, you'd have to travel through multiple environments, defeat a couple of enemies, or retrieve something important to the quest giver in order to receive a reward that you already knew you'd get (for the most part). But, in Hero Siege, you'll never know what you'll get. Maybe some new armour, maybe a buttload of coins. (I find the mystery in that enticing.) Your reward varies, wether or not you did something difficult. Adding on to this, a lot of quests in RPGs have you running around and traversing multiple stages/environments, which again, is different from Hero Siege, where your quest and everything needed to fulfill it is right on one screen from your quest-giver to your objective(s) in the quest.  As I said before, though, Hero Siege also has bad things about it. For example, it's hard to get into at the start, as I said before. Trying to play with friends is a pain, and if you run on a toaster and find out that the game only allows the FPS to go up to 60, you'd die by the pain that is lag. As well as the fact of all the bugs that involve relics and the stages that can knock you into a gate that you will never be able to leave.  But y'know, Hero Siege is a perfectly good hack 'n slash in my opinion, though I have almost no experience with games like these outside of the game itself. Besides, all these reasons that represent Hero Siege's 'quality' doesn't really matter all that much because it doesn't matter wether in some peoples' eyes it's a good game or not. What you think of the game is what matters. I recommend this game because that shows what I think of Hero Siege.

At the moment I saw this game I knew I am going to like it. So far I have spent 17hour of playing this game I have completed 3 of 4modes game offers and get 11 of 12archivemenents. Now I feel competent to write few lines about it.  Game is remake of games released few years ago for iOS and Android. Despite developers call it remake game is simplistic lacking any advanced feature, setting and unfortunatelly being short even in comparison with other budget titles. On other other site game is very adictive and fun to play which is what matters.  Players take control of rocket power formula which resembles that one from Trackmania Nation. Rocket engine is powered by nitro which is restored when next checkpoint is passed in modes Platform and Time Attack. In Puzzle/Collector mode turbo is refilled automatically when not used. With rocket engine equiped car can reach crazy speed, fly and maneuver(brake, steer, rotate) in the air.  First of game mod is tutorial with 5 track where players have chance to familiarise yourself with control.  Second mode is Platform with 25tracks. Tracks are arranged from simple where rocket engine is used just to speed up cars, to track where rocket engine is used to overcome huge distances between distant part of track. Harder track in this mode require lot of practising to reach finish line.   Thirt mode is Time Attack (11tracks). Rocket engine here serves just like turbo to speed up cars. All track are easy to drive with few jump. Surplus of nitro make it mode feel totaly different from platform. Instead of precise aircontroled flight player control formula in tight corners whith even remind me Wipeout/F-Zero games.   In both previous game modes players have change to compare your results with other BUT only when track is finished with personal best. This is promist to be fixed. Game would certainly benefit from possibility to see records of other players anytime. Also developers implemented so called 'asynchronous multiplayers' into these two modes. Game download ghosts of players with similar time and let them compete with it. Again unfortunately game lack here becouse new ghost are downloaded only when track is reload which result in playing with much slower and therfore uselles ghost all the time.  Last mode is Puzzle/Collecter (8 maps). Objective is to colect 5stars on every maps. Starts aren't placed directly on track but on objects around tracks. This mode require lot of air maneuvers and restarts. Players face with challange to not just overcome huge distances but also significant changes in direction in comparsion with other mode where just slight corrections in the air are needed. Unfortunatelly camera doesn't work so well in JCS and any controled stunts are literally impossible. Fast camera rotation really doesn't help.. Therefore this mode utilise fixed camera which make game feel like beta but helps in crazy maneuvers. Personally this is the only mode I haven't finished. It's not really fun to play FOR ME. This mode also face it premiere in PC version and was created by developers of PC version. Hopefully other feature and content we might see for this game will push game further..  It's very disapointing that game doesn't include multiplayer and level editor. Personally think that editor would be much usefull. What would we play in that multiplayers without new tracks? For example game Trials Evolution:Gold Edition has functional editor and ingame sharing system which push game to endless of hours of gameplay for those who want and multiplayer which non of PC players ever make to work present NO problem in long term. With multiplayer and editor this game might threaten possition of Trackmania which took direction to LOT of feature, realism, graphic and addictive gameplay was lost in the way..  Hope I will see more 'PC wotrhy' sequel in few years.  7/10 and must have for those who loved Trackmania before TM2 release.

The idea behind FarSky is simple: Underwater survival. The scope is small, with limited build options and equipment tiers, but the goal is clear and most importantly: achievable in just a few hours. This is not long-term survival, but a short and condensed package of the things we enjoyed in Minecraft. The ambiance is great too. The graphics are nothing to rave about but the worlds have a beauty to them, and are densely populated with marine life, both tasty and hostile. With randomised maps and a hardcore mode to beat, there's replayability, but sadly no multiplayer. It's $6 in the current humble bundle, which is well worth it if you fancy a quick survival fix.[6.8]

I pre-ordered this game so I owned/played it on release. At that time, the main story was unfinishable. I remember reading forums saying 'don't play storyline just play sandbox.' Yeah I was ♥♥♥♥♥♥.  Since then the developers have improved this game by leaps and bounds. You have to give the devs credit for sticking with it and not abandoning ship. If you expect this game to be an X3 clone then you will be disappointed; however, if you want something fresh and change doesn't scare you then give it a try.  If you are like me and played it at release, got extremely ♥♥♥♥♥♥, and uninstalled it never to install again, you might give it another try. Keep in mind it is not perfect, but you will be surprised how much better the game has become! IMO the game is now playable and quite fun.

Reminiscent of the Balders gate series. Good game, well worth full price, but just falls shorts of being a classic.  In a way it is a shame, as, whilst the Balders Gate series were extremely good, they weren't the pinicle of Interplay's achievements. It would have been nice to see Obsidian aim higher. Fallout, Planescape Torment, Mask of the Betrayer,and Bloodlines were huge steps forward from Balders Gate, and to be honest going back to Balders Gate is a step back, even though it is better than many games produced since then.  The plot is OK, the lore is OK, the dialogue is OK, the combat is actualy slightly revolutionary - with in theory compromises having to be made. No one character can be everything. However, really it just means you have tanks and ranger dps. Not that exciting in practice. Combat can be challenging sometimes, but there are one or two attacks that are overpowered (such as confusion and dominate) and if those aren't used it is a breeze usually. Traps are annoying and very silly. They are disproporiate in terms of damage and they often instakill parties who are not sneaking all the time in dungeons.  It is an above average CRPG.

39,517 reviews on Steam. Overall. Overwhelmingly Positive. Other developers need to take note of CDPR's business model.  Respect the gaming community. Respect the customer. Don't insult their intelligence. Love the game You are making. Profit. Even though I intended to buy this on the PC/Steam, I still bought the full game plus season pass on PSN just to support the developer. But I've also for some reason played it more on the console than on my PC. Can't really tell a huge difference between maxed out PC settings @ 1080p and the PS4 version.  The amount of respect I have for CD Projekt Red is unparalleled by any other developer. They deliver over and over again, pure gaming perfection and quality. This game is surely an interactive computer entertainment masterpiece.

Pros:    Story: Wow. This game has an immense, immersive…I need another “I” word…INCREDIBLE story to it. Almost all of the quests have a depth behind them. It can be little crap things that lead right into a huge line that you would have never even known existed if you hadn’t talked to that little village person (there’s no giant exclamation mark above the heads of people). I stumbled upon many of them just wandering around the world. It’s…crazy. I’ve been humbled and taken back by how awesome it is. I’m not even talking about the main story, which in itself is pretty legit. No, enthralling. It’s storytelling at it’s best and honestly, all games in this category need to step it up if they want to compete with future witcher games.  The sheer amount of armor, weapons and items is staggering. You can upgrade a ton of things including your horse! There are so many designs to find and craft (treasure hunting) that you’ll be drawn into that aspect just to find them all. Things to do: You will get caught up in so many things you won’t know what to do with your time in game. It’s encompassing. Side quests will pop up everywhere from you running around while you’re just exploring or while you’re already on a quest or checking out the local board in a town, ect. Witcher contracts, horse races and treasure hunts, oh my!  Gwent! – This is a side game, but really it should be a stand-alone game. It’s in-depth and I found myself wanting to challenge anyone and everyone that I could to a couple rounds of Gwent just to win a card that I didn’t have.  Funny lines batman! The devs included references from media culture in the game. For one, I dare you to find all the Pulp Fiction quotes.  The world is crazy big and there is so much to explore. If you’re like me, you’ll love to just venture around and check things out on your map that might look like something interesting even if they have nothing to do with a current quest. The environment is fantastic to look at! It really shows with whatever atmosphere you’re in too. It might be in a pitch black cave or on the top of sprawling snow capped mountains. It all looks beautiful! No matter what you heard about dumb downed graphics (because of the console versions), the pc version is awesome.     Cons:    No matter what graphic setting you put the game on I still can’t get out of the game unless I ctrl + alt + delete. Alt + tab does nothing. I have two monitors, so that might have something to do with it? I’ve tried all the settings (yes, even windowed mode) and I still can’t get it to leave the screen without pressing those said keys.  Combat is just…well, it could use some work. It’s sad to say since it’s a main part of the game. There’s only a handful of things that you can do. You have your fast and your strong attack with your swords. Those each have a combo. Then if you “spec” into a certain skill tree you can have a special attack with the fast attack and/or strong attack. That’s it as far as the swords.  Magic skills seem to lose the value of what being a witcher is supposed to mean. They don’t seem to do enough vs. what your swords can do.  Counter attacking needs some help. If you have multiple enemies attacking you, just roll out of the way, If you don’t, you just get hit. It has no assassin’s creed combat value where it actually knows that multiple people are attacking you.  There needs to be more skill slots. That or they need to change the amount of points you get. You’ll end up just putting points in a tree and just going with that. There’s no hybrid builds really.  The game is riddled with bugs. From quests to talking to people to just trying to look at a loot items, you might stumble upon a bug or you might not at all. Don’t get me wrong, these are not game breaking bugs, but they can be very annoying. I can go into detail if you need.  No bug fixing by the devs? There was an update 9 days ago from this posting, so it seems they are doing something… This could just be me since most of the bugs that I’ve experienced haven’t been fixed yet.    Conclussion:    I could probably talk more about various things, but this pretty much sums it up. The game is definitely worth it’s weight in gold. Even if you haven’t played any of the other Witcher games, you’re sure to love this one!

Never have I seen a more compelling and heart wrenching tale from any other game. The way the narrative strives to socially commentate on all of the world's worst issues and it's incredibly accurate depiction of war and poverty is only the beginning of why this game deserves Game of the Year Edition 1995. My particularly favourite scene was the delivery of a very powerful line by Putt Putt as he says 'I'll have some Cheese Squigglies, please'. You can just tell how much emotion the voice actor put into this role considering Putt Putt has been suffering from PTSD since the aftermath of 'Putt Putt Joins the Parade'. Simply incredible stuff.  Oh and how could I not forget the romance that develops between Zanzibar the Hippopotamus and Sammy Seal. Sensational. 3 thumbs up. 6 stars. WouldPlayAgain/10
